actions.thought: 9d3h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and no high cards. UTG in a 6-handed game requires a tight opening range to avoid playing out of position post-flop with marginal hands. Folding preserves stack for better spots early in the tournament.

Elon (UTG) has folded, and action is on me in middle position with ATo (AdTh). In 6-max this is a standard open: strong enough to raise for value, take initiative, and pressure the blinds/button while not inflating to an awkward size. I’ll use a small but non-min open to reduce multiway limps and keep my range credible.

I am holding 4d 2h in the Cutoff, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing an open raise to 250 from Middle Position (FiveTwo), I have no equity to justify calling, and this hand is too weak to use as a bluffing candidate for a 3-bet this early in the tournament. Folding is the only standard play.
3-7 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even with button position, calling a raise with this trash hand is unprofitable. Clear fold.

I am holding a medium pocket pair (8c 8h) in the Small Blind. FiveTwo has raised to 2.5x from Middle Position. While calling is an option for set mining, playing out of position against both the raiser and potentially the Big Blind is disadvantageous. I will 3-bet to 850 to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and thin the field. If called, I have a strong hand that can flop well, and if 4-bet, I can re-evaluate based on sizing.

I’ll fold. 9♥7♣ off is a weak, poorly-connected hand with no suit or strong blockers, and I’m out of position. Flash’s sizable 3-bet to 850 after FiveTwo’s open makes this at least a three-way pot if I call — a terrible spot for 9-7o. Pot odds to call (~750 into ~1950) look tempting superficially, but my equity multiway against an open+3-bet range is far below what’s needed, and a 3-bet bluff here isn’t attractive (two players still in, deep stacks, few blockers). Early in the tournament I should preserve chips and avoid marginal, dominated, or awkward multiway spots, so fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a small blind 3-bet after opening MP, ATo offsuit is too often dominated by SB’s value range (AQ+/TT+), and it doesn’t realize equity well even with position because we’ll make a lot of second-best top pairs. With no read in Hand 1 and plenty of better continue candidates (suited Ax, broadways, pocket pairs), I’ll avoid inflating a 100bb pot with a marginal offsuit Ace.

UTG in 6-max with Q4o is well below an opening range. It’s dominated when we make a queen, has poor playability, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus multiple players with deep stacks. Preserve stack and wait for stronger open spots.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (AKo) in Middle Position, which is a premium starting hand. With 100 big blinds deep, the standard play is to open-raise for value. A raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is a standard size to build the pot while preventing opponents from seeing a cheap flop with marginal holdings. There is no reason to limp or fold here.

78o is a weak speculative hand facing a middle position raise. With players behind me who could squeeze, and holding an offsuit connector that doesn't play great in 3-bet pots, folding is the correct play here.

I am on the button with Q3s. Pro raised to 2.5x from middle position. Q3s is a weak suited hand that is significantly behind a standard opening range from middle position. While I have position, calling would likely lead to being dominated or forced to fold on most flops. 3-betting is an option for a bluff, but Q3s lacks the blockers (like an Ace or King) that make for better 3-bet bluff candidates. In a tournament setting, especially this early, I prefer to fold marginal hands against strength.

Pocket tens in the SB facing a MP open to 250 is a clear 3-bet spot. I want to take the initiative, isolate the opener, and deny equity to speculative hands that play better multiway or OOP. I’ll 3-bet to 850 total (≈3.4x the open) — large enough to get fold equity and narrow ranges but not so huge as to overcommit out of position. If called I’ll c-bet appropriate textures and re-evaluate to a 4-bet or shove only against polarizing, large 4-bets; I’ll fold to extremely strong sizing that represents tighter premium ranges.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind after a middle position open-raise. My hand is 9h7s offsuit, a speculative hand at best with poor playability out of position. Pot odds are approximately 2.6:1 (calling 750 into 1950), requiring ~28% equity, but 97o has very low equity against a typical SB 3-bet range (e.g., QQ+, AK, some bluffs), likely 20-25%. Postflop OOP with this hand is disastrous on deep stacks. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (AKo) and am facing a 3-bet to 850 from the Small Blind. I have position on the raiser. While 4-betting is a viable option to apply pressure, calling allows me to play a pot in position against a wider range, keeping in dominated hands like AQ/AJ and smaller pairs that might fold to a 4-bet. At 100bb depth, flat calling the 3-bet is a standard, lower-variance line that leverages my positional advantage post-flop.

I flopped a set (TsTh on Td Tc 5h) and I'm heads-up versus the MP opener who cold-called my 3‑bet. His continuing range contains overpairs, broadways and medium pairs — many of which will call a reasonable value bet. The board is fairly dry with virtually no strong draws, and I'm out of position, so I should take the initiative to extract value and charge any backdoors. I size to ~50% of the 1800 pot (900) to get calls from worse pairs/AX/broadways without folding them off, while keeping flexibility for turn play and preserving a healthy stack to maneuver.

I hold Ace-King offsuit on a paired Ten-high board (5h Td Tc). The pot is 1800 (pre-bet) and Minni has c-bet 900 (half pot). This board texture is very dry and static. While I have missed the flop, my hand has significant showdown value as Ace-high, beating all of Minni's semi-bluffs (like AQs, KQs, or lower suited connectors) and chopping with opposing AK. I am in position, which allows me to re-evaluate on the turn. Folding top of my range for missed hands (AK) to a single standard c-bet is too exploitable, and raising isolates me against overpairs or trips. Calling is the most prudent play to keep the opponent's bluffing range in the hand while getting a good price (25% pot odds) to see a turn card.

I actually have quads on the turn (I hold Ts Th and the board is Td Tc 5h 6h) — essentially the nuts. Pro called my flop bet, which puts him on a range that contains sets (55), medium pairs/overpairs and broadway/Ax hands; many of those hands will happily call a large turn bet. I want to keep the initiative and build the pot while charging any heart or straight draws, so I’ll bet for maximum value rather than check/induce. A pot-sized bet (3600) maximizes value extraction from sets and overpairs while still leaving chips behind to get more on the river. If he raises or shoves I’ll be calling — quads beat virtually his entire continuing range — and if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river for another value action.

I hold Ace-King offsuit and have encountered significant aggression from the Small Blind (Minni). Following a pre-flop 3-bet and a half-pot flop c-bet on a paired board (5h Td Tc), Minni has now fired a pot-sized bet (3600) on the turn (6h). This sizing is extremely polarizing, representing either a strong made hand (Overpairs like AA/KK/JJ or Trips with AT) or a strong semi-bluff (hearts). With only Ace-high and drawing nearly dead against Minni's value range, calling a pot-sized bet is negative EV. Preserving my stack (still >80 BBs) is prioritized over hero-calling this early in the tournament.

I am in UTG position with King-Nine offsuit (K9o). This is the earliest position at the table, requiring the tightest range. K9o is significantly below the standard opening range for UTG in a 6-max game (typically 15-20% of hands, e.g., 77+, ATs+, KJs+, QJs+, AJo+, KQo). Playing this hand often leads to being dominated by better Kings or Aces and playing out of position post-flop. With a stack of 82.5 BB, there is no need to take unnecessary risks. The correct play is to fold.
KTs is a solid opening hand from middle position. With Pro folding UTG, I'm effectively first to act. Standard raise to 2.5-3x with this hand to build the pot and isolate or take blinds.

I am in the Cutoff with 4h 2h. Claude has opened for 250 from middle position. 42s is a very weak hand that does not play well against a standard opening range, even with position. Calling or 3-betting here would be unnecessarily risky and likely negative EV in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the button with A♠ J♣ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand that wants to seize the initiative. Claude opened to 250 from middle; there are still the SB and BB to act but with deep effective stacks (≈117 BB) this is a good spot to 3-bet for value and fold equity rather than just calling and letting the blinds dictate action. I make a standard-sized 3-bet to 1000 total: big enough to isolate Claude and price out many speculative hands, but not so large as to commit my stack. Plan: play post-flop aggressively on A-high/dry boards; re-evaluate if I face substantial resistance (a large 4-bet) and fold AJo to a polarized 4-bet from a tight opponent.

Facing a 4x 3-bet from the button after a middle position open-raise. My hand is J6o, offsuit and uncoordinated, with poor playability and low equity against likely strong ranges from both Claude and Minni. Positionally disadvantaged in the small blind. Early in the tournament with deep stacks (~100BB), no need to speculate with trash. Standard fold to preserve stack.

We’re in the big blind facing an MP open and a Button 3-bet to 1000. With 9d4s we have very poor playability and are out of position versus a strong, narrow range. Calling 900 more to win 1400 gives decent immediate odds, but we’ll realize equity terribly and be dominated often; a 4-bet bluff is also unattractive with no blockers and shallow-ish stacks. Best decision is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni on the button with KTs. Button 3-bets can be wide, and KTs has good playability with potential for straights, flushes, and top pair. Getting about 1.87:1 on a call (750 to win 1400). Being out of position is a disadvantage, but the hand plays well enough postflop. Folding is too tight, and 4-betting bloats the pot OOP with a hand that plays better as a call. Calling to see a flop.

Flopped a gutshot straight draw (need Q for Broadway). Board of A-J-9 hits Minni's 3-betting range well. I'm OOP as the caller, so checking is standard here. My hand has some equity but not enough to lead. Will check and evaluate Minni's action.

I flopped two pair (A♠ Jc on A–J–9 rainbow). This is a very strong hand vs Claude’s cold‑call range (AX, Jx, broadways, medium pairs, suited connectors). The only real worries are sets (AA/JJ/99) and the open‑ended straight draws (Q‑T, T‑8, etc.). With virtually no flush draw present I want to extract value and deny equity to straight draws. A 50–70% pot continuation bet is ideal: charges draws and gets called by worse Aces/Jacks and some broadways. I’ll bet 1,400 into the 2,150 pot (~65%) to get value/protection while keeping options open. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll continue for value on safe turns and pot‑control or check‑call if a straight‑completing card shows; if he check‑raises large on the flop I’ll treat it as a very strong range (likely a set) and re‑evaluate (leaning to folding to an oversized raise, calling a smaller raise to get to showdown).

I have KTs with a gutshot straight draw (need Q for Broadway). Board is A-J-9 rainbow which hits Minni's 3-bet range hard (AK, AQ, AJ, KK, QQ, JJ all possible). I'm getting 4950:1400 or about 3.5:1 on a call. With just 4 outs (~8.5% on turn), I need roughly 11:1 implied odds to continue. Even with backdoor possibilities, this is too thin. Minni's c-bet sizing of 1400 into 2150 is substantial. Folding is correct - chasing a gutshot OOP against a 3-bettor on an A-high board is burning chips.
KJs is a solid hand from UTG - suited broadway cards with straight and flush potential. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB. This is well within my UTG opening range.

I am holding Q6o in middle position. Claude has raised from UTG to 250. Q6o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against an early-position raising range. There is no reason to defend or 3-bet here. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

7♣2♦ is one of the weakest starting hands. Claude opened UTG to 250, and there are still the button and both blinds to act — I’d be out of position and likely dominated. Calling 250 into a 400 pot (~38% pot odds) with almost no equity versus an UTG raising range is unjustified, and a bluff 3‑bet from this spot is too risky with no meaningful blockers. Fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with J7o facing a UTG raise to 2.5BB from Claude, who has shown aggression in previous hands. J7o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by likely strong UTG ranges like pairs, broadway, suited connectors. Deep stacks (98BB), no position advantage justifies calling or 3-betting. Pot odds don't warrant calling (calling 250 into 400 is 38% pot but equity vs UTG range is low ~30-35%). Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Facing an UTG open (6-max) from the small blind with 9c4c, we’re dominated too often and will be forced to play out of position versus a strong range, with the big blind still able to squeeze. Even getting a decent immediate price, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a low-EV defend. Best is to cut the loss and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with J3s facing a standard 2.5x open from Claude in UTG. Although I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550, requiring ~27% equity), J3s is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a strong UTG opening range. My equity realization will be low due to the positional disadvantage and the likelihood of being dominated. Against an early position open, this is a clear fold.

I am UTG with T3o, which is a very weak hand. In a 6-max game, opening this hand from the earliest position is mathematically incorrect and would lead to significant losses over time. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Middle position with Q8o is a marginal holding. There are four players yet to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB) and I’m deep (≈129 BB), so I don’t want to invest chips in a likely multiway pot out of position with a hand that’s often dominated. Limping is passive and a small open invites callers and 3-bets; playing postflop OOP with a single high card and weak kicker is a tough spot to extract value. Early in the tournament I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot/position to open. Fold.

J4o is a trash hand with poor playability and dominated by any raising range. In cutoff with two folds ahead, a steal could be considered with better cards, but this hand lacks equity and post-flop potential. Button FiveTwo has shown aggression previously and may defend wide, while blinds are deep-stacked. Pot odds poor for calling limp (not an option anyway). Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament.

Folded to me on the button, but 34o is near the bottom of playable holdings: it has poor high-card value, is easily dominated when we pair, and doesn’t realize equity well if the blinds defend or 3-bet. With no blockers and no strong read that SB/BB are over-folding, opening risks getting played back at or having to navigate postflop with a trash range. I’ll pass and preserve stack for better steal candidates.

I am in the Small Blind with J2o, which is a very weak hand. The action folded to me, creating a Blind vs Blind scenario against Claude. While BvB ranges are wide, J2o is at the bottom of the range and plays poorly out of position post-flop. With a healthy stack of 81 BBs, there is no need to force a play or attempt a steal with such low equity cards, especially against an opponent who has shown activity. The cost to complete is small, but the reverse implied odds and playability are terrible. Folding is the disciplined decision.

UTG in a 6-handed game with J♥2♠ — a weak offsuit holding with terrible kicker and limited playability. From UTG my open-raising range should be tight; limping just invites pressure and raises from the active opponents behind (we've seen multiple large preflop and postflop raises in prior hands). With no structural reason to gamble (deep stacks aside), calling 100 or opening with this hand is unnecessarily risky and likely -EV. Fold and wait for a better spot in position.

Js5c is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity against any reasonable calling or raising range, and no blockers or suited connectivity. In middle position preflop with no action ahead (UTG folded), this is a standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in a tournament with aggressive opponents observed in prior hands.

Cutoff is a good steal spot, but 6♠3♣ offsuit is too weak to profitably open against three players left who have shown willingness to 3-bet and apply pressure. Limping is also poor because we realize equity badly and invite isolation. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with Kd 6s. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, K6o is a marginal hand, typically near the bottom of or outside a standard opening range in a 6-max game without antis. Additionally, the Big Blind (Flash) showed aggression in Hand #1 by 3-betting pre-flop, suggesting they may defend their blind aggressively. Without the incentive of an ante to justify a wider steal range, and given the potential for resistance, folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better spots.
Pocket tens is a premium hand, especially heads-up against just the big blind. Everyone has folded to me in the SB. I should raise for value - standard 3x raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7d 4d. The Small Blind (Claude) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more to see the flop. In a blind vs blind battle, ranges are typically very wide. 74 suited is a speculative hand with some playability, and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. Given the 2:1 pot odds and my position, calling to see a flop is a standard and profitable play.

I have pocket tens on a K-8-4 board with two spades. Flash called my preflop raise from the BB. The King is a bit concerning but I still have an overpair to most of the board, and I hold the Ts for a backdoor spade draw. Standard c-bet for value and protection against draws. Betting around 1/3 pot.

I have bottom pair (fours) on a K-8-4 board. Claude, in the Small Blind, led for a small 1/3 pot bet (200 into 600). Given the wide range Claude likely has in a blind-vs-blind scenario and the favorable pot odds (4:1), folding bottom pair to a single small bet would be too tight. I will call to see how the turn develops, keeping the pot small and utilizing my position.

Turn paired the 8, which is actually good for me - less likely Flash has an 8. I have an overpair with TT, and hold the Ts blocking some flush draws. Flash likely has Kx, a flush draw, or a weaker pair. I should bet for value against draws and weaker hands, and for protection. Betting around half pot.

In this blind-vs-blind battle, I defended my Big Blind with 7d 4d and called a small flop bet after hitting bottom pair. The turn 8h pairs the board, which is a problematic card for my specific holding; the pairing of the 8 reduces the relative strength of my pair of fours against Claude's range. Claude has now fired a second barrel of 500 into the 1000-chip pot (making the total pot 1500). While I am getting 3:1 pot odds, my hand has very little potential to improve on the river, and Claude's range as the pre-flop and flop aggressor contains many hands (Kings, eights, or pocket pairs) that have me significantly outclassed. I will fold to avoid further losses.

UTG in 6-handed game with 65s, a marginal suited connector. Early position requires a tight range. Opponents are aggressive based on prior hands (frequent raises and 3-bets). Deep stacks, early in tournament (hand 7/150), better to wait for stronger spots to avoid difficult post-flop situations OOP. Standard play is to fold.

We’re first-in from middle position in a 6-max lineup with four players left to act (CO/BTN plus aggressive blinds). T7 offsuit is too weak to open profitably here and plays poorly when called or 3-bet (dominated by better tens, weak connectivity). Limping is also low-EV and invites isolation. Best is to preserve stack and wait for stronger spots.

I hold Qh 5s in the Cutoff. This is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand (Q5o). While the Cutoff is a stealing position, the players remaining behind me include Claude (active on Button) and Minni (aggressive 3-bettor in the blinds). Opening here would likely result in being 3-bet or played back at, forcing a fold. With 81 BBs, there is no need to take marginal spots with trash hands. Folding is the standard and correct play.

AKo on the button is a premium hand. Three players have folded, leaving only the blinds. This is an easy raise situation - I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to 250 to build the pot and put pressure on the blinds.

Holding 6s 3h in the Small Blind against a Button raise is a trivial fold. This hand is extremely weak, has poor playability, and I would be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Conserving chips for better spots is the priority here.

Facing a button open to 250 (pot 400; I must call 150) with 8♣5♥ in the big blind. 8-5 offsuit is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position versus a button opener. With deep stacks the hand has low implied odds and high reverse implied odds if I pair a low card. A 3-bet would be reckless and a call puts me in difficult OOP spots for marginal equity. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

6-max UTG (effectively lojack) with T7 suited is a marginal but playable open when deep. We have good playability, can realize equity in position vs most of the table, and can fold comfortably to strong 3-bets. Opening also helps us avoid being overly tight early and gives us chances to pick up blinds/antes (pot already 150). I’ll use a standard small open size to reduce risk while maintaining fold equity.

I hold 3h 4c in Middle Position facing a standard open-raise to 250 from UTG (FiveTwo). 43o is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em with very poor playability and equity, especially against a typically strong UTG range. With four players left to act behind me, calling or 3-betting would be a losing play. The standard and correct decision is to fold immediately.
93o is a very weak hand. Facing a UTG raise, this is a clear fold - no reason to defend with such poor holdings out of position relative to the raiser.

I have Ace-Queen suited on the button, which is a very strong premium hand. FiveTwo opened from UTG to 250. While UTG ranges are typically tight, AQ suited is well ahead of a standard opening range. Raising (3-betting) allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and build value while I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. A standard 3-bet size in position is around 3x to 3.5x the initial raise, so I will raise to 850.

Fold. I’m in the small blind with 7♣5♠ facing an UTG open to 250 and a button 3‑bet to 850 (I must call 800 into a 1250 pot). 7‑5 offsuit has very poor equity vs a BTN 3‑bet range and the original raiser’s range, and I will be out of position post‑flop. Calling requires ~39% equity to break even and in a likely multiway pot this hand will be dominated or have little implied odds. A re‑raise would be committing chips with a very weak hand and little fold equity. Preserve the stack and fold to the 3‑bet.

Facing an UTG open from FiveTwo to 250 and a BTN 3-bet from Flash to 850. My 6s7h is a weak offsuit connector with poor playability OOP in the BB. Pot odds are 1250:750 (~37.5% equity needed), but 67o has low equity (~25-30%) against a credible BTN 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, broadways, suited Ax/connectors). FiveTwo likely folds as in Hand 1, leaving me OOP vs aggressive Flash heads-up deep-stacked. Squeezing with 67o is suboptimal due to poor postflop playability and blockers. Folding preserves stack early in tournament (Hand 8/150).

We opened UTG with a marginal suited connector and now face a Button 3-bet. Even getting ~32% immediate pot odds (600 to win 1850), T7s performs poorly versus a typical BTN 3-bet range against an UTG open, and we’ll also realize equity badly out of position in a 3-bet pot (low SPR, lots of dominated top-pair situations). This hand is also a poor 4-bet bluff candidate (no blockers, too often forced to fold to a jam). Best play is to let it go and choose better blocker-heavy hands to defend/4-bet in future versus Flash’s pressure.

I am holding 4s 8d in the UTG position. This is an extremely weak hand (unsuited, disconnected, low ranks) and I am in the worst position at the table with five players left to act. There is no strategic merit in playing this hand. Folding is the only correct decision.
36 offsuit is a trash hand in middle position. Clear fold - no reason to put chips in with this weak holding when there are still 4 players behind me.

I am holding a premium pocket pair (Tens) in the cutoff. Since the action has folded to me, I will open with a standard 3x big blind raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. This hand is strong enough to play for a large pot, especially in a 6-max format.

I’m on the button with Qh9d facing a cutoff open to 300. Pot is 450, call is 300. Q9o is a playable hand in position against what should be a fairly wide cutoff opening range. With ~127 BB effective stacks and two players (SB/BB) still to act, a light 3-bet is too speculative — it bloats the pot and risks 3- or 4-bets or multiway calls out of position. I’ll call to use position post‑flop, realize equity when I hit top/second pair or draws, and apply pressure selectively on favorable turns. If I hit the flop I’ll look to extract value; if I miss I’ll often float and try to take it away or fold to heavy action. Therefore: call 300.

Q<2d is a trash hand offsuit with poor playability, especially out of position in the SB facing a Cutoff raise from Flash (who has been aggressive) and a Button call from Minni. Pot odds are ~3:1 (250 to call into 750), but equity against their ranges is likely under 25%, with dominated queen and no straight/flush potential. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

We’re in the big blind with AdAs versus a CO open (to 300) and a BTN flat, so this is a prime value 3-bet spot. Calling under-realizes our equity OOP and invites multiway play; AA wants to build a pot and isolate. Versus open+call, a larger sizing is best to charge dominated hands and deny BTN a good price to peel in position. If we get 4-bet, we’re happy to continue (often by jamming given stacks).

I am holding a strong pocket pair (Tens) in the cutoff. FiveTwo has made a significant squeeze raise from the big blind after my open and Minni's call. While TT is a premium hand, 4-betting here often commits me to a stack-off against a range that might include JJ-AA or AK. By calling, I can keep the pot manageable, utilize my position on FiveTwo post-flop, and see how the board develops. I am getting decent odds to call, and although Minni is still to act behind me, folding is too conservative for a 6-max game.

I’ll call 1300. On the button with Qh9d facing a BB 3‑bet to 1600 (pot 3550), Q9o is too weak to 4‑bet — a light 4‑bet here bloats the pot and commits me against two opponents (and I’m likely dominated). Calling keeps me in position on both players, lets me realize equity and exploit favorable flops, and gives reasonable pot odds (~3.7:1, ~21% equity needed). Plan: proceed on flops where I hit (pair/strong draws), float and apply pressure selectively, and fold to heavy turn/river action when missed. A fold would be too tight; a raise is overly aggressive with this hand and multiway action.

We 3-bet pre and got two callers, so we’re still the range leader. This flop is coordinated (4-straights and pair+draws exist), but AA remains an overpair that wants value and protection versus hands like 88–TT, 7x/6x that peeled, and straight draws (98/84/43). Multiway I prefer a more “honest” c-bet that denies equity without overinflating versus sets/2p/straights; ~1/3 pot accomplishes that and keeps worse hands in. If we face a raise, especially multiway, we can proceed cautiously because raises here skew strong.

I have an overpair (Tens) on a low, coordinated board of 6-5-7. FiveTwo made a significant squeeze pre-flop and is now leading out for approximately 1/3 of the pot. While this board texture is somewhat dangerous because it connects well with a lot of calling ranges (like 89s, 55, 66, 77), my Tens are likely ahead of FiveTwo's overcard continuation bets (AK, AQ). Raising here might only isolate me against hands that have me beaten (JJ-AA or sets), whereas calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how Minni, who is still to act behind me, reacts. Given the pot odds and the strength of my overpair, folding is not an option, and calling is the most flexible play to navigate this three-way pot.

Call 1600. The pot is 8050 so I’m getting ~5:1 immediate pot odds (need ~17% equity). Q9o has reasonable combined turn+river improvement (pair outs + 8s for the 5-6-7-8-9 straight — roughly 30–40% to hit something by showdown) and I’m on the button, so I can realize equity and use position to float or extract value on a favorable turn. A raise into two active callers would bloat the pot and is too risky with no pair; folding here would be overly tight given the price. Plan: call, evaluate the turn — fold to heavy turn/river action unless I improve (pair or the 8); if I pair or complete the straight I’ll start extracting value or raise.

Turn completes/creates a ton of straights (any 9 or 4, plus hands like 98/43 were already made on the flop), and we’re multiway after both players called the flop—so betting mostly gets action from hands that have us crushed while folding out a lot of the worse one-pair/underpair hands we’d like to keep in. With SPR < 1, I’d rather check and let them reveal strength: we can call some reasonable sizing if it looks draw-heavy, but we preserve the option to fold versus a big shove + strong line that’s heavily straight/sets.

The turn 8c is a very dangerous card as it completes a four-card straight (5-6-7-8). Any 9 or 4 now has a straight. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor and flop bettor, has checked, which could indicate they are wary of the board or are trapping. Minni is still behind me. My pocket Tens are now a vulnerable overpair. Betting here is risky because I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold and will likely only be called or raised by hands that beat me (straights, sets, or two pairs). Checking allows me to see how Minni acts and potentially see a river card for free or at a lower cost, preserving my stack in a very coordinated board.

I’ve completed a 9-high straight with Qh9d on 6c-5d-7h-8c — a very strong made hand in this spot. Very few hands beat me on the turn (mainly the narrow 9+10 combos that make a 10-high straight); most of the cutoff/BB calling/3‑betting ranges contain lots of one-pair hands, pocket pairs and other holdings that will happily call a value bet. Both opponents checked to me, which makes it less likely they hold the precise combo that outkicks my straight, so I should extract value rather than give a free river. I’ll bet about half the pot to get called by worse hands (7x, 6x, 5x, pocket pairs, single-pair/weak two-pair) while keeping the sizing reasonable against potential check-raises — pot is 9,650, so a ~4,800 bet leaves me with ~4,750 behind. If I face a large check-raise or shove I’ll re-evaluate (a big raise could indicate one of the very few hands that actually beat me), but the +EV line here is to bet for value now.

On 6-5-7-8 in a 3-bet pot multiway, AA is now just one pair on an extremely straight/two-pair heavy texture. After we check and CO checks, BTN fires 4,800 into 14,450, a line that’s heavily weighted to made hands (any 9x/4x straight, sets, two pair) with very few credible bluffs—especially given she called pre and called the flop multiway. Versus that value range we have almost no clean improvement (an A doesn’t beat straights; we’re mostly hoping to chop on a 4/9), and there’s still a player behind who can wake up with a straight and further crush our equity. Calling also effectively commits our remaining stack. Best play is to preserve chips and fold.

The turn 8c is an extremely coordinated card that completes several straights (any 9 or 4). Minni, who called a pre-flop squeeze and a flop continuation bet, is now leading for 4,800 into a three-way pot after the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) checked. This sequence strongly indicates she has connected with this board, likely holding a straight, a set, or at least two pair. My pocket Tens are now a weak overpair and effectively a bluff-catcher. While I have a gutshot to a higher straight (if a 9 falls), the risk of being drawing dead or significantly behind Minni's value range is too high. Given the pot odds and the high probability that my hand is second-best, I will fold to preserve my remaining 7,000 chips for a better spot.

65 suited is a speculative hand that plays poorly from UTG. While it has some playability as a suited connector, opening from the earliest position with 5 players behind is too loose. Better to fold and wait for a stronger hand or better position.

I am in middle position with 8c 5s, which is a very weak hand. Standard strategy dictates folding such trash hands in this position as they have poor playability and low equity against typical opening or calling ranges from the remaining players.

7♠2♦ is the absolute worst hand — no real playability. I’m in the cutoff with the Button and both blinds left to act; raising to steal with this hand is reckless and calling 100 to see a flop puts me out of position and often dominated or drawing dead. I have a big stack, so there's no need to gamble; preserve chips for better spots. Fold preflop.

Preflop on the button with 2h4d, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. It's folded to me, but even in position, this hand has zero playability postflop and is a standard fold against blinds who can defend wide. Preserving stack early in the tournament (hand 10/150) by avoiding speculative calls or opens with unplayable holdings maximizes EV long-term. No stealing value here given the hand strength.

Folded to me in the small blind, so this is a classic steal spot. K4o isn’t strong, but the king blocker helps reduce BB’s strongest continue/3-bet holdings, and heads-up I want to apply pressure rather than limp and play passively out of position. I’ll use a standard SB open size that keeps my range consistent and gives good fold equity; if Pro 3-bets, I can comfortably fold.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3bb open raise from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). This is a Blind vs Blind scenario where ranges are typically wide. Although 6c 5d is a marginal hand, I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600), requiring only ~33% equity. My hand has approximately 35% equity against a standard top-50% stealing range. Crucially, I will have a positional advantage post-flop, which allows me to better realize my equity and potentially float or bluff if the opponent shows weakness. Given the stack depth and position, calling is a +EV play compared to folding.

This flop (8d-4h-7d) connects well with BB’s defending range, so I don’t want to blast it. But with bottom pair plus the Kd blocking strong diamond continues and giving me backdoor flush potential, a small c-bet is a good compromise: it folds out a lot of overcards/high-card floats, denies equity, and keeps the pot manageable if called. If I get raised, I’m generally folding this hand; if called, I’ll often slow down on many turns.

I have flopped the nut straight (holding 5-6 on a 4-7-8 board). The board texture includes a flush draw (two diamonds), which poses some risk, but in a Blind vs Blind scenario, the opponent's c-bet range is very wide, including many bluffs and marginal hands. The opponent bet small (1/3 pot). Raising here would likely fold out all their air and bluffs, which I want to keep in the pot to extract value on later streets. Calling disguises the strength of my hand and gives the opponent a chance to improve to a second-best hand or fire another barrel on the turn. I am in position, which gives me control over the pot size later. Therefore, a call is the best play to maximize expected value.

The 9c is a dynamic turn that improves a lot of BB’s continuing range (more 9x pairs, two-pair/straight possibilities like T6/65, and it keeps diamond draws live). With only bottom pair (4s) and no real ability to barrel for value, betting mostly just builds a pot where we get called by better and raised off our equity. I’ll check to control pot size and realize equity, and then decide vs sizing (typically defend vs small stabs, fold vs big pressure).

The turn card (9c) improves my hand to a 9-high straight (5-6-7-8-9) on a board of 8d-4h-7d-9c. While higher straights (J-T or T-6) are possible, the opponent's check on the turn after c-betting the flop suggests they may be pot-controlling with hands like sets, two pairs, or overpairs, or perhaps gave up on air. Given the wet board texture with a flush draw and potential straight redraws, checking back is risky as it gives a free card that could beat me or kill the action. I should bet for value to extract chips from sets (99, 88, 77) and two-pair combinations that might call, while also denying equity to flush draws and gutshots. A bet of 600 (60% pot) is a strong value size that targets their calling range effectively.

Pro’s turn stab is only 600 into 1600, so I’m getting ~2.7:1 (need ~27% equity). With a made pair (4x) I beat all his overcard floats and some diamond/straight-draw pressure hands that called the small flop c-bet, and this sizing is consistent with a lot of one-and-done probes. Raising doesn’t make sense with this hand (worse won’t continue much, better will), so the best line is to bluff-catch once and re-evaluate river—generally folding to heavy river pressure on bad runouts and considering a call on bricks vs smaller bets.

River goes check to me (to call 0). With Kd4c on 8d-4h-7d-9c-Th I only have a weak pair of 4s. Betting can’t really get called by worse (missed diamond draws and pure overcards will mostly fold), while a lot of Pro’s turn-raise range that now checks (7x/8x/9x, Tx that decided to pot-control, occasional slowplays) beats me and can comfortably call or even raise. Since Pro’s check also contains plenty of missed diamonds/semi-bluffs, the highest-EV line is to take the showdown and win versus those hands rather than turning this into a low-quality bluff.

I hold a straight (6-7-8-9-T) on a board of 8d-4h-7d-9c-Th. The river Ten puts four consecutive cards on the board (7-8-9-T). This makes the board extremely dangerous; any Jack makes the nut straight (Jack-high), and any 6 results in a chop. Betting is thin value at best, as worse hands like sets or two-pair will almost certainly fold fearing the straight, while any hand containing a Jack will beat me (and likely raise). Since the opponent checked, taking the free showdown is the optimal play to realize my equity against sets and pairs without risking chips against the top of their range.

I am in the UTG position with Seven of Hearts and Eight of Diamonds. This is a weak connector hand that is generally not strong enough to open from early position in a 6-max game. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position against potentially stronger ranges.

I have K♠4♠ in middle position facing a raise (to 200) — to call is 100 into a 150 pot. K4s is a marginal, often-dominated hand vs a raiser’s opening range and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. Pot odds and implied odds don’t justify investing chips here, and a 3‑bet bluff from this hand is thin against competent opponents. With a healthy stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better, cleaner spot rather than calling or bloating the pot with a weak K‑4 suited.

Qs6c is a weak offsuit hand, not in the standard cutoff RFI range (typically 20-25% hands like QJo+, suited connectors 76s+, but Q6o is too weak with poor playability and kicker). Deep stacks (97bb), early tournament (hand 11/150), unopened pot but remaining players include aggressive ones like FiveTwo on button and Claude in BB. Limping is suboptimal; raising risks playing OOP postflop with a marginal hand. Best to fold and wait for better spots.

Three players have folded and we’re on the button with position and decent blockers (Ace). A4o isn’t strong, but it’s a profitable open in a 6-max spot to pressure the blinds, win the dead money, and play in position when called. I’ll use a standard steal size that isn’t too big but still applies pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with AQo, a premium hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. This player has opened frequently but has also folded to 3-bets twice previously (Hand #1 and #8 to a size of 850). Playing AQo out of position by just calling is passive and invites the Big Blind to squeeze or call, leading to a multi-way pot where I am out of position. 3-betting for value and isolation is the superior play. It targets the wide Button opening range, denies equity to the Big Blind, and potentially takes down the dead money immediately, which has proven effective against this opponent. I will raise to 900, a standard 3-bet size (approx 3.6x) that applies pressure to the 48bb stack of the opener.

I have QQ in the BB - a premium hand. FiveTwo opened to 250 and Pro 3-bet to 900. With pocket queens, I should 4-bet here for value. QQ is ahead of most 3-betting ranges. A 4-bet to around 2400 puts pressure on both opponents and sets up a potential all-in if Pro 5-bets. I'm committed to calling a shove with QQ.

We opened A4o on the button as a steal, but now we’re facing a 4-bet to 2400 after a 3-bet from the SB. With only 4800 total, calling 2150 creates a tiny SPR where we’ll be forced into high-variance decisions and often be dominated when we make an ace. A 5-bet jam isn’t credible here (Claude’s 4-bet range is heavily weighted to QQ+/AK) and won’t generate enough fold equity. Pot odds don’t justify a call versus that range, so we cut losses and preserve the remaining stack.

I 3-bet to 900 with AQo from the Small Blind against a Button open, but the Big Blind (Claude) has put in a cold 4-bet to 2400. A cold 4-bet from the blinds is an extremely strong line, typically representing a range of QQ+, AK, and potentially JJ. Against this value range (JJ+, AK), AQo has approximately 30-33% equity, and against a tighter range (QQ+, AK), equity drops to ~24%. I need to call 1500 into a pot of 3550, offering roughly 29.7% pot odds. Being out of position and likely dominated by AK or crushed by AA/KK/QQ, continuing is negative EV. Preserving the stack early in the tournament is prioritized over playing a marginal spot against immense strength.

Utg with 3♦7♠ offsuit is a clear fold. The hand has very poor equity and playability from first to act and will be dominated or face multi-way action. Players behind have shown frequent aggression and re-raises in earlier hands, increasing the chance of getting 3-bet out of position. I won't limp or open-raise with this; preserve stack and fold.

T4o is a trash hand with no high card value, suitedness, or straight potential worth playing. In middle position after UTG fold, facing blinds (pot 150, to call 100 BB), standard play is to fold and wait for better spots. No reads suggest opponents are weak enough to justify a limp or raise. Preserve stack for premium hands in this multi-way tournament.

Unopened pot from the cutoff, but K4o is too weak and dominated to profitably open at 48bb, especially with an aggressive SB (Claude has shown large 3-bets) and a capable BB (Flash has 3-bet before). The steal value (150) doesn’t justify the frequency we’ll face 3-bets/calls and have to play poor equity/inferior kickers postflop. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold 7-2 offsuit on the Button, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although everyone has folded to me and I have position, the blinds (Claude and Flash) have shown aggression in previous hands (Claude 4-betting Hand #11, Flash 3-betting Hand #1 and Hand #8). Opening with zero equity is not profitable here. I will fold.
Folded to me in the SB with A2o. This is a clear open vs just the BB. A2o is well ahead of BB's defending range and stealing the blinds is +EV. Standard raise to 250-300.

I am in the Big Blind with Td 4d. Claude (Small Blind) raised to 250. This is a blind versus blind situation where Claude's opening range is likely very wide. I have position on him for the remainder of the hand, and I need to call 150 to win a pot that will be 500 (3.33-to-1 odds). Given the suitedness of the hand and the positional advantage, this is a standard defend in a tournament setting.

I have A2o on a Js-Qc-3h board. I completely missed this flop with just ace high. This board texture hits Flash's BB defending range well - lots of Qx, Jx, and broadway hands. A c-bet here is essentially a bluff that will get called by better hands. Better to check and potentially see a free card to hit an ace, or give up if Flash bets.

I am in the Big Blind with 10d 4d on a flop of Js Qc 3h. This board heavily favors a standard pre-flop raising range, but Claude (Small Blind) has checked to me. While I have very little equity here (only 10-high and a backdoor diamond draw), checking back is the most prudent play. Claude might be checking a marginal hand like a weak Jack or a pocket pair to control the pot, or he could be giving up. By checking behind, I see a free turn card and avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand while maintaining the ability to potentially bluff later if the board texture changes or if he continues to show weakness.

Turn brought the 9d. Flash checked behind on flop, suggesting weakness or a marginal hand. I have ace-high which has some showdown value. Betting as a bluff is risky since Flash could have floated with any piece of this board. The 9 could have improved hands like 98s, T9s. Best to check and potentially win at showdown with A-high or give up if Flash bets.

On the turn, the 9d has significantly improved my hand, giving me an open-ended straight draw (8 or K completes the straight). Claude (Small Blind) has now checked twice on a board that traditionally favors a pre-flop raiser's range (Q-J-x). This persistent weakness suggests he either has a marginal hand looking for a cheap showdown or has completely missed the board. While I could semi-bluff here to try and take the pot down immediately, checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit a very disguised straight on the river. If I miss, I can still evaluate a bluff on the river if Claude checks a third time. This line minimizes risk while keeping the pot manageable.

River completes the board Js-Qc-3h-9d-5h. Flash has checked every street which signals weakness - likely Kx, weak Ax, or complete air. I have ace high which has showdown value against their checking range. Betting here is risky: better hands won't fold and worse hands won't call. Best to check and potentially win at showdown with A-high.

The river is the 5h, which is a complete brick. The board is Q-J-9-5-3 and I have 10-high. Claude (the pre-flop aggressor) has checked for the third consecutive time. This line of check-check-check from the Small Blind almost always indicates a weak hand or a total airball, likely something like low pocket pairs (66, 77, 88), A-high that missed, or suited connectors that failed to connect. Since I cannot win this hand at showdown with 10-high, I need to turn my hand into a bluff. A small-to-medium sized bet of 300 (60% of the pot) should be enough to fold out his weak holdings and missed high cards, as he has shown no interest in this pot since his pre-flop raise.

Flash checked flop and turn, now makes a small 300 bet into 500 on river. This delayed betting pattern often indicates thin value rather than a bluff - Flash likely has a pair (Jx, 9x, or even 5x) and is extracting value. My ace-high loses to any pair. Getting 2.67:1, I need to be good ~27% of the time, but Flash's line strongly suggests a made hand. Fold is correct.

72 suited is a weak hand with poor playability and low equity multiway from early position (UTG). In a 6-handed tournament, standard play is to fold trash like this to preserve stack and wait for better opportunities. No reads or dynamics from history justify entering the pot here.

AcQs is a strong, high-playability open from middle position in 6-max. We want to build the pot with an equity edge versus callers, take initiative, and pick up blinds frequently. A standard 2.5x open keeps our risk controlled with a ~48bb stack while still pressuring the blinds and button.

I hold A3o in the Cutoff facing a raise to 2.5x BB from FiveTwo in Middle Position. While FiveTwo has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets in previous hands (Hands #1, #8, #11), A3o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated by the opener's calling range. Additionally, there are active players behind me (Claude on Button, Flash in SB) who could wake up with a hand or squeeze. The risk of 3-betting with this holding is slightly too high given the players left to act, and calling is not profitable due to reverse implied odds. Folding is the prudent play to preserve my healthy stack (83 BB).

I have 5h8h on the button facing a min-raise from FiveTwo. This is a suited one-gapper with decent playability in position. FiveTwo has a shorter stack (4550). Calling is reasonable here to see a flop in position - folding is too tight for a suited hand on the button, and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand strength. I'll call and play postflop with position.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. There has been a raise to 250 from FiveTwo and a call from Claude. While the price to call is only 200 chips, Queen-Seven offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against two opponents, one of whom has shown aggression. I'm likely dominated by a better Queen or just have poor post-flop playability. Folding is the standard disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Call to 250 total (call the 150). K♠T♣ is marginal but playable from the big blind and this call is cheap — 150 into a 650 pot gives ~18.8% break-even equity. There are two opponents: an MP open (FiveTwo, ~45bb) and a button caller (Claude) who will often have a wide calling range. A 3-bet to isolate is an option, but I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with KTo and risk a shove/call from the shorter stack or a sticky call from the button. Plan: see the flop; continue only with top pair/two pair or strong draws, and avoid bloating the pot with marginal hands against aggression.

Check. I have K♠T♣ on 8♦-4♠-5♠ — no pair, only weak backdoor spade equity and two overcards. Pot is 800 and two opponents are still in (FiveTwo opened, Claude called), so c-betting multiway with a marginal hand is low EV and risks getting called or check‑raised by hands that already beat me (pairs, 8x/5x/4x) or by real spade draws. I called preflop planning to see the flop and only continue with top pair/two pair or strong draws; this flop doesn’t meet that criterion. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. If facing a small turn bet I can call as a bluff‑catcher/backdoor equity and re-evaluate; if I pair a king/ten or hit a substantial draw I’ll start betting for value or applying pressure, otherwise I will avoid bloating the pot and fold to large aggression.

3‑way on 8♦4♠5♠ is a board that connects well with the BB/caller ranges (pairs, 5x/4x, 76/65, and many spade draws), and our AcQs is just two overcards with only a thin backdoor flush via Q♠. A c-bet will get called a lot and also exposes us to check-raises while we have poor equity when played for a bigger pot. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our overcard equity, and lets us barrel later on favorable turns (A/Q/K, or some spades) when ranges narrow.

I flopped two pair (8s and 5s) on 8d4s5s - a very strong hand. Board has a spade flush draw. Both opponents checked to me in position. I should bet for value and protection against the flush draw. Betting around 60-70% pot (500-550) to extract value from draws and weaker hands while protecting my equity.

I hold K♠T♣ on 8♦-4♠-5♠ — no pair, only two overcards and a very slim two-card backdoor spade possibility. Claude (button) bets 500 into a 1,300 pot. Calling 500 to win ~1,800 requires ~27.8% equity. I have roughly 6 outs to pair by river (≈24% to hit at least one pair) and only ~2% to hit a runner-runner spade flush — combined effective improvement odds are still below the break-even threshold.

Worse, I’m out of position to Claude (and the preflop raiser FiveTwo is still in), so even if I hit a single pair I’ll often face difficult decisions versus pocket pairs, 8x/5x/4x, or stronger button holdings. Raising as a bluff OOP is poor here and could force an awkward shove/call against shorter stacks. My preflop plan was to continue only with top pair/two pair or strong draws; this spot doesn’t meet that criterion. Preserve stack and fold rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

Claude’s 500 stab after we check in a 3-way pot can still be fairly wide (one-pair hands, some draws, and some pure floats). With AcQs we have ~6 overcard outs and some chance to win later when he gives up, plus we need to defend our checking range so he can’t profitably bet any two. The price is close, and raising would bloat the pot with a hand that has no real draw and risks getting shoved on. I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn—continue on A/Q (and some favorable runouts), and be prepared to fold to strong barreling on bricks.

Turn pairs the 4, which doesn’t improve our AcQs (still just ace-high with ~6 clean outs) and also gives Claude more value combos (4x trips) while his flop-stab range still contains plenty of hands that may slow down in position. With SPR ~2.25, betting risks getting called by 8x/5x and raised by trips, forcing us into a high-variance bluff line with poor equity. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets us realize our overcard equity when he checks back, and we can respond based on sizing if he barrels (defend vs smaller bets, fold to strong pressure).

I have two pair (8s and 5s) on 8d4s5s4d. The 4 pairing doesn't hurt me much - I still have a strong hand. FiveTwo checked to me again. They could have a flush draw, a weaker 8, or possibly hit the 4. I should continue betting for value and to charge draws. Betting around 55-60% pot (~1000) is good sizing to extract value while not overcommitting against possible trips.

We’re facing a 2nd barrel on a paired board with only A-high and no real draw (no flush draw, just 6 overcard outs that aren’t always clean vs 99+/boats/trips). Calling 1000 to win 3800 needs ~26% immediate equity, but the bigger issue is stack-to-pot: if we call, we’ll have ~3050 behind into ~4800, letting Claude apply maximum river pressure (very likely jam) and we won’t be able to continue unimproved. That makes the turn call largely a 1000-chip donation unless we expect him to check river very often, which is unlikely given his aggression line. Raising/shoving is also poor: our blockers (Qs) reduce some spade-draw bluffs, and his continuing range (8x/5x/4x/pairs+draws) won’t fold enough. Preserve the ~40bb stack and fold.

6-handed but still first to act with ~40bb. K3o is too dominated to open UTG and not a good limp/call candidate either (plays poorly multiway, makes weak top pairs, and invites isolation when we’re out of position). Preserve stack for better spots.

I am in Middle Position with King-Deuce suited (Kh2h). This is a weak speculative hand. With four players left to act behind me, including the aggressive stacks, opening this hand is loose and unprofitable in the long run. I risk being dominated by better Kings or facing 3-bets that force me to fold. My stack is healthy (83 BB), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks. The standard and correct play is to fold.
A4o in the cutoff with two folds in front. This is a decent hand to open from late position. Standard raise to 2.5x to steal blinds or isolate. Only 3 players behind me.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of eights (8c, 8d), which is a strong starting hand. Claude, the cutoff, has raised to 250. Claude has been fairly active in previous hands, suggesting a wide opening range. With a medium-sized stack of 7200, I want to take the initiative and isolate the raiser. A 3-bet here allows me to define the strength of Claude's hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If called, I have position for the rest of the hand. I will raise to 750.

Facing a button 3-bet to 750 after the cutoff opened to 250, I'm in the small blind with A♣3♦ (offsuit) and already have 50 posted. A3o is highly vulnerable to the button's 3-bet range (many better aces and broadways) and I'll be out of position to both the button and the big blind for the rest of the hand. Calling 700 to play a bloated pot requires ~38% equity (700 / 1850) which A3o won't reliably have vs this range, and a raise here is thin and risks getting isolated or committed against stronger holdings. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Preflop in the big blind with Tc7d, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a strong 3-bet range from Flash on the button after Claude's open from cutoff. Pot is 1150, to call 650 gives poor implied odds even if I hit, and facing two aggressive players with position postflop. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no value in calling or 4-betting this trash.

A4o facing a 3-bet from the button. Flash has shown aggression with 3-bets previously. While pot odds are decent (need to call 500 into 1150), A4o plays poorly out of position post-flop. This hand is at the bottom of my opening range and doesn't have the playability or equity to profitably continue against a 3-bet. Folding is disciplined here.

I am in UTG position with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo). In a 6-handed game, ATo is a marginal opening hand from Early Position (UTG). It is easily dominated by better Aces (AJ, AQ, AK) and plays poorly out of position if called. There are five players left to act behind me, including aggressive opponents like Claude and the chip leader Minni on the button. Furthermore, the Big Blind is somewhat short-stacked and could shove, forcing me to fold. Opening here creates reverse implied odds. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a stronger hand or better position.

Q3 offsuit in middle position is a clear fold. This hand has poor playability and is dominated by most hands that would call or raise. With 4 players still to act behind me, the risk of running into a stronger hand is high.

I am in the Cutoff with 7s 4s. While the hand is suited, it is a two-gap connector that is generally too weak to open for a raise from this position. With aggressive players like Minni on the button and a short stack in the Big Blind, I expect to be called or re-raised frequently. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to avoid putting chips at risk with a marginal hand.

I’m on the button with Ad3c — an Ace with a weak kicker but excellent position and folded action to me. This is a perfect spot to open-raise for fold equity rather than limp. Elon (SB) has been fairly passive and often folds; FiveTwo (BB) is active but not so deep (≈39.5 BB) that I should be overly cautious. I choose a standard 3x BB open to 300 total. That sizing (300) gives good fold equity, keeps the pot manageable if called, and is a clear steal attempt from the button. Plan if called: use position to C-bet many dry, Ace-high or uncoordinated boards, and control the pot on dangerous textures; fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I haven’t improved. Plan if 3-bet big: fold A3o (dominated and poor kicker); consider calling only vs a small isolation 3-bet depending on sizing and opponent. Overall goal: pick up the pot preflop or play postflop in position with initiative.

94o is a complete trash hand with no playability, straight draw potential, or suitedness. Facing a button raise to 3x from Minni, who has shown aggression in prior hands, this is an easy fold from small blind. No position after the flop, and calling 250 into 450 pot gives poor implied odds even if I hit. Preserve stack for premium spots in tournament.

Button open to 3x tightens my big blind defense range. T6o is too weak/dominated and will realize equity poorly out of position at ~40bb effective, so calling leaks and 3-betting is too ambitious as a bluff this deep. Preserve stack for better defend/3-bet candidates.
J7o is a weak offsuit hand from UTG, the earliest position. This is not in a standard opening range - too weak to open from this position as I'll be out of position against 5 players. Easy fold.

I am in the middle position (MP) with Ace-Five offsuit. In a 6-max game, this hand is generally too weak to open from this position as it lacks good post-flop playability and is easily dominated by better Aces. Given the stack depths and the presence of active players like Minni behind me, I will choose the conservative route and fold.

Cutoff with K♠2♥, 190bb effective. UTG and middle folded; button, SB and BB still to act. K2o is a marginal, unsuited hand — playable in position but weak to 3-bets and domination by Kx/Ax from the blinds. With deep stacks and aggressive players behind (FiveTwo and Pro), I don’t want to open‑raise and invite a 3‑bet out of position with a poor kicker. I'll call the 100 to see a flop in position, keep the pot manageable, and decide based on texture (continue if I hit a K or get a strong draw; fold to significant aggression otherwise).

Preflop on the button with KJo offsuit after UTG and MP folds, and cutoff limp. Excellent position with a strong hand that plays well postflop. The pot has 250 with only 100 to call, but raising isolates the limper, builds the pot with dead blind money, and takes initiative. Minni's limp from cutoff suggests a wide range. Standard isolation raise sizing of 4x the BB (400 total) balances value and fold equity against blinds who defend wide but often fold to button raises. My deep stack supports this play.

We’re in the small blind facing a 4bb isolation raise from the Button over a limp, with the big blind still to act and us guaranteed to play the hand out of position. K9o performs poorly in this spot: calling creates an awkward, dominated situation versus Elon’s calling range and invites the BB to come along; 3-betting turns a marginal hand into a high-variance bluff from the worst position with no strong blockers beyond a single K. With 39bb, preserving stack and waiting for better SB 3-bet candidates (suited Ax/Kx, broadways, better offsuit Kx) is higher EV.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 2d, facing a raise to 400 from the Button (Elon) who is isolating a limper (Minni). The pot is 650 and I need to call 300. Although my hand is suited, 9-2 is extremely disconnected and one of the weakest hands in the deck. Playing this out of position against an aggressive Button raiser and a potentially calling Cutoff is a losing proposition. My equity is insufficient to justify the call, even with decent pot odds. Folding is the only prudent option.

Elon’s sizable isolation raise to 400 after I limped turns this into a spot I should not continue. K♠2♥ is a weak, frequently dominated hand with a poor kicker and I’ll be out-of-position post‑flop. Calling 300 into a 650 pot (~32% pot odds) requires equity I don’t have versus a typical button raising range (lots of Kx/Ax and broadways). 3‑betting as a bluff is unattractive with deep stacks — I don’t want to build a big pot OOP with this holding. Fold and wait for a better, cleaner spot to invest chips.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with 3c 6d, which is an extremely weak hand. Playing garbage hands out of position is a recipe for losing chips, especially this early in the tournament. There is no strategic reason to open or call here.

A♦9♣ in middle position is a playable ace with good post‑flop potential. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop — I want to take the initiative and avoid a multiway pot where my Ace-high equity is diluted. A standard 3xBB-ish open is appropriate: large enough to exert fold equity against the blinds and the button, but small enough to preserve postflop maneuverability with deep stacks (I have ~190bb). Opponents behind include a shorter button (FiveTwo 3,900) who may call or squeeze, and deeper SB/BB who will defend occasionally; if I face a big 3‑bet I can release A9o, but versus single callers I can play postflop aggressively. Therefore I will raise to 300 (total).

Q℃ 6♠ is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential worth pursuing. Facing a standard 3x raise to 300 from Minni in middle position, who has shown aggression in prior hands. In cutoff position, but calling OOP with this hand offers poor implied odds and postflop disadvantage. No reason to 3-bet bluff or flat. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

On the button versus a likely wide Minni open, A2o is too weak to flat profitably (often dominated when we make top pair) but works well as a light 3-bet because we block strong Ax and can win the pot preflop or take initiative in position. With 39bb effective, a non-all-in 3-bet keeps fold equity high while preserving maneuverability postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with KJo facing a raise to 300 from Minni (MP) and a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo (BTN). FiveTwo is short-stacked (starting roughly 30bb) and has shown aggression previously, but a cold call here is very dangerous out of position against a 3-bettor and an initial raiser left to act. KJo is frequently dominated by 3-betting ranges (AK, AQ, KQ, JJ+). 4-betting is too risky given the stack depths and players involved. The prudent play is to fold.

Holding 3h9h in a 3-bet pot. This is a weak suited hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet range. I'm out of position and would need to call 800 into a 1350 pot. The implied odds aren't great either since FiveTwo only has 3000 behind. Easy fold.

I opened to 300 with A♦9♣ and the button (FiveTwo, ~3,000) 3-bet to 900. I'm out of position heads-up to the flop and he still has ~2,100 behind. A9o is a marginal hand here — often dominated by Ax and behind against a 3-betting range. Calling 600 into a 1,350 pot gives only ~31% equity to break even, and I risk facing a shove from a shorter, aggressive button which puts me in a tough decision. A 4-bet bluff (to 1,500) unnecessarily escalates the pot with a hand I don't want to get involved with deep or in a shove spot. Given stack sizes, position and hand strength, the correct +EV play is to fold and wait for a cleaner spot.

UTG in a 6-handed table with Qc8d — an early-position, marginal offsuit queen. This hand is often dominated and has poor post-flop playability out of position. Calling 100 (limp) turns it into a multiway pot and invites raises/squeezes; raising to 200 would be an open with a weak, easily 3-bet hand against several aggressive players at the table. With deep stacks but plenty of active raisers behind, folding and waiting for a better spot maximizes long-term EV.

KJo is a strong hand from middle position in a 6-handed tournament setting with deep stacks (98BB). Standard play is to open raise for value and to build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop. Minni (UTG) folded, so no early action. Raise to 3x the BB (300 total) to get value from worse kings/jacks and suited connectors, while maintaining fold equity against blinds and later positions. FiveTwo is short-stacked (43BB) in CO but unlikely to have premium without raising over. Avoid limping as it invites multiway pots with a non-speculative hand.

Facing a 3bb open from middle position, QTo offsuit is too easily dominated (QJ/QK/AQ/KT) and doesn’t realize equity well, especially with the Button and blinds still able to squeeze. With 43.5bb effective, there’s no need to take a thin, reverse-implied-odds spot; I’ll wait for better hands/steal positions.

I hold 5c 6h (off-suit connectors) on the Button. Elon has raised to 300 from Middle Position. While I have the advantage of position, 65o is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a standard opening range. Calling 300 (3BB) is too expensive for this speculative hand, and calling invites squeezes from the blinds. There is no strategic merit in entering this pot with this holding at this stage of the tournament.

T9o in the SB facing a MP raise to 300. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play well out of position. With Flash still to act in the BB who could squeeze, and being OOP for the rest of the hand, T9o isn't strong enough to defend here profitably. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6s 2s. Elon raised to 300 from middle position. While the hand is suited and I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450), 62s is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Against a standard opening range, I have roughly 30-32% equity, which is right on the border of the pot odds, but the lack of post-flop playability and the risk of being dominated or forced to fold on most flops makes folding the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

UTG in 6-handed play with AcTc (suited A10), a strong hand with good equity, playability, and suited connectivity. Standard opening range includes ATs from early position in tournaments with deep stacks (99.5BB). Raising builds the pot, denies equity to weaker hands, and takes initiative. 3x BB (300) is a standard sizing based on observed opens (250-300 common), balancing aggression without overcommitting.

Elon opens UTG to 3bb, and 9♢3♠ offsuit is far too weak to continue profitably from middle position versus a strong UTG range. Calling puts us in multiway trouble with dominated, disconnected holdings, and 3-betting would be pure spew with our stack depth. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Four offsuit (Q4o). The player in UTG has raised to 300 (3BB). Q4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and is dominated by the UTG opening range. There is no strategic merit in calling or raising here. This is a trivial fold.

A8o on the button facing a UTG open of 3x. This is a decent hand with position. Not strong enough to 3-bet for value against UTG range, but definitely playable. Calling to see a flop in position is standard here.

I am in the Small Blind with Td 7s. Elon (UTG) raised to 3x, and Claude (Button) called. T7o is a very weak hand to play out of position against two players, especially a UTG opener. Even with the pot odds offered, I would be playing a multi-way pot out of position with a hand that is easily dominated and has poor post-flop playability. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I’m in the big blind with 4♠6♣ facing an UTG open to 300 and a button call — pot is 750 and it costs me 200 to call. Calling 200 into a 750 pot would require roughly 21% equity to break even (200 / 950 ≈ 21.1%). 4-6 offsuit is a very weak, often-dominated hand and versus two opponents my true preflop equity is well below that (likely ~10–12%), so a call is -EV. A 3-bet bluff (min-raise to 500) is also unattractive here: I have no meaningful blockers, I’d be out of position postflop, and a small isolation raise will usually be called or re-raised by better hands. With deep stacks I can wait for much better spots, so the correct play is to fold and conserve chips.

Post-flop OOP as pre-flop raiser on a dry low board (652 with two diamonds) that connects well with my UTG range (Ax, pairs, broadways). AcTc holds Ace-high equity against button calling range, blocks top Ax, and has backdoor club flush/straight potential. C-bet thin for value/protection vs. draws (diamond flush draw, wheel/78 straight draws) and floats, denying equity while keeping pot manageable. Half-pot sizing (approx 375-400) balances range, observed in prior hands.

I have A8o on a 6d 2c 5d board against Elon's UTG range. This is a low, dry board that misses most of my range but also misses a lot of his raising range. I have position, ace-high (which could still be best), and backdoor straight potential. Elon's half-pot c-bet could be a standard continuation with overcards. Floating here in position is reasonable - if he checks turn, I can take it down with a bet; if he fires again, I can fold. Getting ~3.8:1 on the call.

Heads-up on turn as preflop raiser (IP effectively), AcTc gives ace-high with nut club flush draw (four clubs to any club completing ace-high flush) after 5c pairs the board. Strong equity (~35-45% vs calling range) against Claude's button calling range (pairs like 77-JJ, 65s/54s/43s, diamond draws, Ax weak). Board low/paired favors my range; previous flop c-bet called suggests he has showdown value or draw. Betting extracts value from weaker made hands (trips unlikely, overpairs, mid pairs), weaker draws (diamond FD now less clean), floats; protects vs his equity (e.g., 43 straight, 78). Check risks giving free card to complete draws/straights or induce bluff but weaker with position. Pot-sized bet (1000 into 1550 ~65%) balances value/protection/semi-bluff, denies equity, builds pot for river shove potential if check-called. Deep stacks allow; observed Claude folds to aggression sometimes (e.g., Hand 3,12). Standard continuation.

Facing a turn bet after calling flop. Board paired with 5c. Elon (UTG) has now fired twice into me. My A8o is just ace-high with no pair, no significant draws. Against an UTG range that opened and is double-barreling, my ace-high is rarely good. The board pairing gives him more bluffing opportunities but also means any pair he has is ahead. Getting ~3.5:1 isn't enough when I'm likely behind most of his range. Disciplined fold - can't profitably call two barrels with just ace-high.

6-handed UTG with ~44bb, J7o is too weak to open profitably. If we raise, we’re often dominated when called/3-bet and will be forced into tough postflop spots out of position. Limping invites isolation with little playability. Preserve stack for better opens/spots.

I am holding 8c7c (suited connectors) in Middle Position (Hijack) with 6 players remaining. While this hand has good playability and implied odds, opening from this position is marginal, especially with aggressive players like Claude (Cutoff) and deep stacks like Minni (SB) left to act behind me. The risk of being 3-bet or playing a multi-way pot out of position outweighs the potential benefit of stealing the blinds or hitting a strong flop. A disciplined fold preserves my healthy stack (81.5 BB) for better spots.

Q3o is a weak hand, even from the cutoff position. With 3 players still to act (Button, SB, BB), this hand is at the bottom of any reasonable opening range. It has poor playability and will frequently be dominated. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

I am on the button with 76o. While this can sometimes be used as a steal hand, both players in the blinds (Minni and Elon) have been active and hold significant stacks, making a steal attempt riskier. Given the offsuit nature of the connectors and the likelihood of being called or 3-bet by the aggressive blinds, I will choose the disciplined path and fold this hand.

I have A♠ K♣ in the small blind and everyone folded to me. This is a premium hand — I want to take the initiative and isolate the big blind rather than limping out of position. A standard 3x open (300 total) balances fold equity and pot control: it’s large enough to discourage some blind-defenses but small enough on these deep stacks to keep worse hands in and let me play postflop. Raising to 300 means I add 250 more chips (300 total committed), leaving ~18,250 behind. Plan: if called I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and control the pot when I miss; if Elon 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (often call or 4-bet depending on sizing and player tendencies).

Preflop in BB facing a late position SB raise to 300 after folds around the table. Minni's range from SB is very wide, often attempting to steal blinds given history of similar raises (e.g., Hand 15). QcTs is a strong defensive hand OOP with straight potential, good playability, and equity against a wide range. Pot odds are excellent (200 to call into 400 pot). Calling preserves stack flexibility in this early tournament stage while keeping pot small OOP. 3-betting risks bloating pot OOP with a marginal hand against an aggressive opponent; folding is too tight.

I have A♠K♣ on 3♣ A♣ 5♣ — top pair with the best kicker and K♣ gives me a 4-card club draw. This is a textbook continuation-bet spot: I want value from worse A-x and smaller pairs and I need to charge club draws that have a lot of equity. A ~2/3‑pot bet (≈400 into 600) denies equity to many draws while still extracting value and leaves plenty of chips behind to fold or continue if Elon shows heavy strength (likely a made flush). I’ll bet 400 to both protect and get value.

Facing a 400 bet into 1000 pot on a flush-heavy flop 3c Ac 5c with Qc Ts, giving me the nut flush draw (9 clean outs) and positionally disadvantaged OOP vs aggressive SB Minni with wide range. Pot odds offer ~22% equity (calling 400 into 1400), but nut FD has 35%+ equity vs typical aggro continuation range (Ax, broadways, sets, weaker draws). Raising as semi-bluff exploits fold equity on scary board, builds pot for when we hit nuts, denies equity to weaker draws/pairs. History shows Minni aggressive but folds to 3bets postflop sometimes. Raising to 1200 (3x her bet) sizes well for value/fold equity balance, preserving stack (10500 deep). Calling risks multiway or tough turn OOP; folding discards too much equity.

I have A♠K♣ on 3♣ A♣ 5♣ — top pair with the best kicker plus a 4‑card club draw. I c‑bet 400 and Elon raised to 1,200, which is polarized toward made flushes (two clubs), sets (33/55), A3/A5 two‑pair and very strong Ax/club hands. Even so, my hand has good realizable equity and I block several of his best combos (As removes some A‑x, Kc removes many club combinations). Calling 800 more to see the turn is cheap relative to the pot (800 to win ≈3,000 → ~26.7% break‑even), and my equity versus a realistic raising range should be at or above that thanks to the backdoor club outs and best kicker. Raising here would bloat the pot out of position and play horribly against the hands that actually beat me; folding top pair + draw is too nitty.
