Plan: call the 800, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the turn — proceed for value on safe cards, and be prepared to fold to large turn aggression if a club hits and Elon continues heavy.

I have A♠K♣ — the turn K♥ improves me to two pair (Aces and Kings). Elon’s flop raise polarized his range into made clubs (two clubs = a flush), sets (33/55) and strong A‑3/A‑5 two‑pair combos (and some bluffs). The K turn is a “safe” card for me: it folds many of the hands that were ahead on the flop (A3/A5) and makes my hand ahead of a large portion of his raising range, while still losing to sets and any made club flushes. With pot = 3,000 and stacks deep, I want to extract value from Kx and worse Ax hands and charge single‑club hands that still have a river club out. A ~2/3‑pot turn bet (2,000) hits that goal — it gets value from worse hands, denies equity, and keeps the pot controllable. If Elon responds with a large, polarized raise (which is exactly the line a made club flush or a set would take), I will give serious weight to folding; if he just calls or gives a small raise I can proceed to the river and re‑evaluate. Bet 2,000.

Nut flush draw (9 outs) on turn with position vs Minni's aggressive SB range after she donk-bet flop continuation and called my semi-bluff raise on scary flush board. Kh blank doesn't complete many new draws, preserves my equity. Pot odds excellent (2000 into 5000 = ~29% needed; FD has 35%+ vs polarized range of Ax/sets/draws). Calling keeps pot controlled OOP? Wait, but I have position (BB acts last postflop). Raising risks commitment with reverse implied odds vs made flushes/sets; her history shows calling aggression (e.g., Hand 2,9). Call realizes equity, sets up jam if club hits (huge implied), or check-fold blanks. Stack depth (9300) allows flexibility vs her 15k.

I have A♠K♣ — two pair (Aces and Kings) on 3♣ A♣ 5♣ K♥ 4♥. Pot is 7,000 and Elon (BB) raised the flop and just check‑called my turn bet before checking the river. His flop raise polarizes toward made clubs and sets, but his turn call and river check show some caution/weakening — his range contains a mix of hands that still beat me (two‑club flushes, sets/trips) and a meaningful number of hands I currently beat (A3/A5 two‑pair on the flop that my K turned behind, weaker Kx, and other Ax/Kx combos). I want to get value from those worse Ax/Kx hands while keeping the sizing small enough to induce calls and avoid bloating the pot into obvious flush/set monsters.

Betting is the exploitative line: make a modest value bet to extract from A3/A5 and Kx while giving myself room to fold to heavy aggression. I choose ~3,000 (≈43% of the pot) — large enough to charge worse hands but not so large that every marginal hand folds or that a raise commits me without clear reads. If Elon check‑raises big or shoves, I’ll assume a very strong, polarized range (flush/trips) and give that action respect (I would fold to a large polarized shove); if he calls, I expect to be ahead of a good portion of his calling range and win a decent pot.

Facing a 3000 bet into a 10000 pot on the river (needing ~23% equity). My Qc Ts missed the nut flush draw entirely and holds just queen-high with no pair or straight. Minni's line is strong: wide SB preflop raise, small flop donk on flush-draw heavy board, calls my semi-bluff raise (suggesting Ax, sets, or strong clubs), large turn donk after Kh (pairing top possibly or strong hand), and now large river donk after blank 4h. This polarizes her range to value (flush like Kc/Qc/6c etc., AcX with good kicker, two-pair, sets) where I have near-0 equity. Bluffs are unlikely given her aggression post-flop raise call and repeated donks. Pot odds insufficient vs her value-heavy range; folding preserves 7300 stack (73BB) in early tournament (hand 20/150) for better spots. Calling risks stack without showdown value.

I am in UTG position with 2s 4s, a very weak suited one-gapper. With 81.5 BBs, I have a healthy stack but being out of position against 5 opponents with such a weak hand makes this an easy fold. Opening ranges from UTG should be tight (top 15-20%), and this hand falls far outside playable criteria.
Q8o in middle position is a weak hand. With 4 players behind me including the button and blinds, this is a clear fold. Not strong enough to open raise from this position.

I am in the Cutoff with 53s. While suited connectors and gappers can sometimes be used as steal hands, 53s is a bit too weak for a standard opening range in this position, especially with an aggressive player like Minni on the button and two relatively short stacks in the blinds who might shove. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have A♥K♥ on the button — the best non-pair starting hand. Only the small blind (Elon, 7250) and big blind (FiveTwo, 4250) remain to act. I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds and play the hand in position. A standard 3–4x BB open works well here: big enough to gain fold equity against blind defend ranges but small enough to get value-calls from worse hands (AQ, KQ, suited broadways, some pairs). I’ll raise to 400 total. Plan: if called, continue aggressively in position (c-bet favorable flops, extract when I pair up); if I face a sizable 3‑bet, re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent — call small 3‑bets, fold or shove vs very large 4‑bets depending on stack and read.

Preflop in SB with T6s facing a button raise to 400 after three folds. Minni on button has a wide range but is aggressive based on history. T6s is a marginal suited hand with some playability, but calling OOP commits 350 more into a 550 pot (pot odds ~1.57:1) without great implied odds or postflop position. Folding is standard to avoid difficult spots OOP with a speculative hand. Not strong enough for a 3bet bluff given Minni's stack and aggression. Preserve stack for better spots in multi-hand tournament.

Button is opening large (4bb) but with position and a big stack, so her range is still wide. A7o is a reasonable BB defend: we have an ace-blocker, decent top-pair potential, and folding too often here lets her profitably pressure our shorter stack. 3-betting would bloat the pot out of position versus an aggressive opponent who can call/4-bet a lot; calling keeps SPR workable and lets us realize equity with a hand that can make strong top pairs and some nut-flush potential is absent but still fine.

Heads-up BB vs BTN on 3h-3c-6h, this paired low flop hits my BB defending range well (lots of 3x/6x/small pairs) and doesn’t strongly favor her high-card opening range. With A7o I’m mostly in a bluff-catcher category: I have ace-high with a small amount of backdoor potential (runner-runner hearts via 7h), but no immediate equity to build a pot. Checking keeps her range wide, lets her stab with overcards, and allows me to respond based on sizing (continue vs small c-bets, fold vs big pressure) without bloating the pot out of position.

Flop: 3h 3c 6h. I hold A♥ K♥ — no pair but the nut heart draw (9 outs) plus two overcards. BB called my 4x open so his range is wide (pocket pairs, 6x, broadways, suited hearts, connectors). This is a textbook spot to take the initiative: I have a lot of equity and can fold out weak pairs and hands with little showdown value. I’ll make a sizable continuation bet (~2/3 pot) to charge draws and get value from worse hands while keeping control of the pot. Plan: if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn and continue barreling when I improve (or target turn cards that favor my range), but I’ll be cautious to fold to large check-raises that look like sets/two-pair.

BTN’s 4bb open is still fairly wide, and on 3-3-6 (two hearts) this is a board where she’ll c-bet a lot of unpaired overcards to take it down. Versus the ~2/3 pot stab, I’m getting a price that allows me to continue with A-high: I can already be ahead of her broadway/air, I have two overcards to the 6, and I retain some backdoor potential (running hearts, plus turn A/7 improving to a pair that can win at showdown if she slows down). A check-raise with A7o is too high-variance and puts me in rough spots when called, while folding is likely too tight versus an aggressive c-bettor. I’ll call once and re-evaluate on the turn based on card and sizing.

Turn 9s doesn’t improve Ad7h (still just ace-high with no real draw), and with an SPR around ~1.7 leading would either bloat the pot with a hand that can’t stand pressure or invite a raise that forces a fold. This paired board still allows BTN to barrel a lot of air, so checking keeps her range wide and lets me respond based on sizing (fold vs big turn barrel, potentially continue vs very small/blocker sizing).

I have A♥K♥ on the button. On the flop I had the nut heart draw + two overcards; the turn (9♠) missed the heart but I still have 9 hearts + 3 remaining Aces + 3 remaining Kings = 15 outs (~15/46 ≈ 32.6% to improve on the river). FiveTwo called my preflop raise and my flop c‑bet, then checked the turn — that line is consistent with a wide range of weak-to-medium pairs, some heart draws, and occasional single‑card 3s/trips. Checking here surrenders fold equity and lets them see a free river; betting gives me a chance to fold out underpairs and charge non‑heart draws while still retaining decent equity if called. The pot is 1,984 and FiveTwo’s stack is ~3,383 (effective stack vs him), so I’ll size up to apply meaningful pressure but not commit unnecessarily. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (1,300). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I still have ~33% to improve and will re‑evaluate on the river (value bet if I pair or hit the heart; check or pot‑control/dump small if a scary card falls). If he check‑raises large I’ll give weight to folding (he’d represent trips/very strong made hands).

We’re BB vs BTN on 3-3-6-9 with Ad7h: still pure ace-high with essentially no draw (only a backdoor that’s gone). Minni’s turn barrel is ~65% pot, and if we call we’ll have ~2.1k behind into ~4.6k, meaning she can profitably jam most rivers and we’ll be forced to fold almost always unless we spike an A/7—outs that are not clean because her double-barrel range contains plenty of Ax and pairs. Check-jamming needs her to fold a very large portion of her betting range, but with her stack depth and this sizing she can comfortably call with any pair/9x/overpairs/draws, so fold equity isn’t reliable. Best EV is to preserve the 3.3k stack and let this one go.

Pocket 3s from UTG in 6-handed. Small pocket pair with set-mining potential. Standard open raise to 2.5x is appropriate - I have good stack depth (107BB) for implied odds if I hit a set. Folding is too tight, limping is weak. Opening to 250.
Holding 9d 5c in middle position against an under-the-gun raise from Claude is an easy fold. This hand is well below the threshold for a playable range in this situation.

I'm in the cutoff with A♠6♠ facing an UTG raise to 250. A6s is playable but marginal vs an UTG opening range — often dominated by better A‑x — and I'll be out of position against the opener if we see a flop. The call is small relative to my 23k stack (≈1%); calling preserves stack depth, keeps the pot controllable, and lets me realize equity with a suited ace or flush/straight draws. 3‑betting light here would be an aggressive OOP play that risks creating tough postflop decisions against a likely stronger range, and folding would be too tight given the cheap price. Plan: call the 250, see the flop, and proceed cautiously — play aggressively only when I hit or have clear equity/draws, otherwise fold to heavy action.

T6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (likely strong range) and a call from Minni (potentially wide but still adding heat), multiway pot preflop is unattractive even on the button. Pot odds of calling 250 into 900 (after call) offer insufficient equity against their ranges. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in a long tournament.

We’re in the small blind with 62o facing an UTG open and a cutoff flat. This will go multiway often (BB still to act), we’ll be out of position with a hand that realizes equity terribly and is dominated when it makes pairs. Despite getting a tempting price, the implied odds are bad and we’ll burn chips defending too wide with a ~33bb stack. No viable squeeze/bluffing candidates here. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 4h facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG and a call from Minni in the Cutoff. The pot is currently 650, and I need to call 150 to see the flop, offering me pot odds of approximately 18.75%. However, K4 offsuit is a very weak hand, particularly against an Early Position opening range which is typically strong. Additionally, playing out of position against two opponents with a hand that suffers from domination issues (if I pair the King) and has poor playability is highly unprofitable. Despite the attractive pot odds, the low equity realization and reverse implied odds make this a clear fold.

Flopped an underpair with 33 on 9-2-5 rainbow. Dry board that doesn't connect well with a cutoff calling range. I should c-bet small as the preflop aggressor - this board favors my range and a small bet can take it down often. Around 1/3 pot is sufficient on this dry texture.

Claude (UTG) c-bet very small (220 into a 870 pot). After his bet the pot is 1090 and calling 220 to see the turn requires ≈16.8% equity (220/(1090+220)). I hold A♠6♠: currently just ace-high but with backdoor spade possibilities and decent chance to pair an A or a 6 by the river (combined ≈24%). I’m in position, so a cheap call lets me realize equity, float to take the pot on a blank turn, or fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve. A raise here would be an unnecessary bloating of the pot with a hand that’s often dominated and can create tough decisions vs a re‑raise. Folding is too tight given the price and the positional advantage. Plan: call the 220, see the turn, and proceed based on opponent’s action and whether I improve.

Turn brought the Qc, an overcard that likely helps Minni's calling range (Qx hands now pair up). My pocket 3s are just an underpair with minimal showdown value. Minni called my flop c-bet showing some interest. Double-barreling here would be turning my hand into a pure bluff, which isn't ideal with a pair that has some showdown value against missed draws. Check and evaluate - if Minni bets, I can fold cheaply.

Claude opened UTG and c‑bet a tiny amount on the flop then checked the turn — that line looks like weakness or pot control rather than a strong made hand. I hold A♠6♠ but the turn Q♣ missed me: the backdoor spade is dead and I only have ~6 outs to pair by the river (~13% equity), so I’m not betting for value. In position on a dry board with the preflop raiser showing weakness, a small turn bluff is the right play to fold out lots of his missed hands (Kx, Jx, many underpairs and overcards). I’ll make a modest bet (≈1/3–1/2 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping risk small — if he raises I’ll release, if he calls I can decide on river based on the card.

I have pocket 3s, an underpair on 9-2-5-Q. Minni bet 400 into ~1090 after I check-called flop. The Qc turn likely improved their range (Qx hands now have top pair). My 33 has almost no showdown value against their betting range. Getting about 3.7:1 odds but I only have 2 outs to improve. Minni has been aggressive (as seen in previous hands) and their turn bet after calling flop shows strength. Time to give up.

I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position with 7-3 offsuit. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and is a standard fold from early position, especially with several aggressive players left to act behind me.

Facing a single limper (to call 100) with Kc8d in middle position, I don’t want to play a marginal K‑8 offsuit multiway out of position. I’ll raise to take the initiative and try to isolate the cutoff limp — a modest 4x BB-sized raise (to 400) gives good fold equity versus the blinds and discourages a multiway pot while still keeping the size manageable against potential short‑stack shoves. With my very deep stack I can navigate postflop pressure if called, and I can fold to heavy action if a player behind jams with a very strong range. Calling 100 would invite too many opponents and folding is too passive with this stack; raising to 400 is the best exploitative line here.

Preflop in cutoff facing a middle position raise to 400 (4BB) from Minni who has a massive 23k stack and has shown aggression in prior hands. My hand K4o is weak: king-high offsuit with poor playability, dominated by any king or better raiser range. Pot odds are poor (calling 400 into ~950 effective pot if called, but with Button, blinds behind), and position is not enough to justify entering with this trash hand against a deep villain. Multiple players to act behind, including button and blinds. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

Minni opens to 4bb from middle position with a big stack and has shown plenty of aggression. With 8♠2♦ offsuit (very poor equity, dominated when we hit an 8, no connectivity/suitedness), calling 4bb invites a difficult spot with the blinds still to act and weak postflop playability. A 3-bet bluff is unnecessary with this exact hand and our ~33bb stack. Best stack-preserving decision is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd Ts facing a 4x raise (400) from Minni in Middle Position. Minni is the chip leader and has been active, but a 4bb open represents a significant price to pay to see a flop out of position with a marginal off-suit hand. JTo is easily dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, or pocket pairs. Calling would leave me playing a bloated pot out of position against an aggressive big stack. The prudent play is to fold and preserve my 80BB stack for better spots.

T3 suited is a weak hand. Facing a 4x raise from Minni in MP, I need to call 300 into a 550 pot. T3s doesn't play well postflop against a likely strong range, and my hand lacks connectivity and high card value. This is a clear fold from the BB.

UTG in 6-max with Q♠J♠ — a strong, suited broadway hand that belongs in my open-raise range. I want to build the pot and narrow the field, so I’ll open to a standard ~3x the BB. 300 total meets the min-raise, gives fold equity, and reduces multiway spots against the capable players at this table. With deep stacks I can continue postflop with position considerations; if I face a 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate based on opponent and sizing (call or fold vs tight 3-bets, consider 4-bet vs aggressive/shorter stacks). Raise to 300 to take the initiative.

73s is a very weak hand, suited but low and gapped. Facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has a huge stack (23k+) and plays aggressively as seen in previous hands (e.g., frequent raises and value bets). UTG range is strong (premium pairs, big aces, etc.). My stack is 7250, pot 450, calling 300 OOP with trash is -EV. No position advantage, speculative hand needs multiway pots or better implied odds, which aren't here. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

We’re facing an UTG open from the chip leader with 33bb and an offsuit, disconnected hand (87o). Against a reasonably strong UTG range, this is dominated too often and has poor playability at this stack depth (reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs). A 3-bet bluff is unattractive versus a big stack likely to continue, and flatting doesn’t realize equity well enough. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with Ac 2c facing a 3x raise from the chip leader Minni in UTG position. While A2s has potential (nut flush, wheel), it is easily dominated by stronger Aces in a UTG opening range. Minni has been very active and aggressive recently, accumulating a large stack, which allows them to apply pressure. Calling 3BBs with a marginal hand invites squeezes from the blinds and puts me in a potentially difficult post-flop spot against the aggressive deep stack. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my 80BB stack for better opportunities.

I have pocket Aces (AA) - the best starting hand in poker. Minni opened to 300 from UTG and I'm in the small blind. This is a clear 3-bet spot for value. I want to build a pot and get action from worse hands. A standard 3-bet size here is around 3x the open, so approximately 900-1000. I'll raise to 950 to extract value while keeping some hands in.

I am in the Big Blind with 2d 3c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni opened from UTG and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. Given the strength indicated by a 3-bet from the blinds and the poor quality of my hand, there is absolutely no reason to continue. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Claude (SB) 3‑bet my UTG open to 950. Q♠J♠ is too strong to fold and has good equity/playing potential against a mixed SB 3‑bet range, especially given Claude’s shown willingness to 3‑bet and the stacks (I’m 23.5k vs his ~9.2k). The pot is 1,350 and calling 650 (950 total committed) gives me roughly 32–33% pot odds — reasonable for QJs vs a range that includes bluffs and broadways. I’ll call and see a flop to realize equity and use my deep stack to continue selectively; 4‑betting out of position here is too thin and a fold would be overly passive vs a likely wide-ish 3‑bet range.

I have pocket Aces on a 4c-7h-9s board - relatively dry with no flush draws. I 3-bet preflop and Minni called. My AA is almost certainly still the best hand. I should c-bet for value to get calls from worse pairs, draws (like 86, 68, T8), and potentially hands like KQ/AK that might float. Betting around 60% pot (~1200) is a good sizing to extract value while keeping in Minni's weaker range.

I opened UTG and called Claude’s 3‑bet, so I’m already 950 in. On 4♣7♥9♠ Claude C‑bets 1,200 into a 3,200 pot — calling costs me 1,200 more (total commitment 950+1,200 = 2,150). I have Q♠J♠: two overcards and a backdoor spade possibility (but not a one‑card flush draw). The call gets ~27% pot odds and QJ♠♠ has roughly the equity needed against a mixed 3‑bet/c‑bet range that includes bluffs and broadway hands. Raising OOP here would be too thin and polarizing; folding surrenders good equity. Plan: call 1,200 (total 2,150), see the turn, continue if I pair or pick up a meaningful draw; be ready to fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss.

I have AA on 4c-7h-9s-9h. The 9 paired on the turn which is somewhat concerning - Minni could have 9x hands that just made trips. However, AA is still ahead of most of their calling range (TT, JJ, 88, AK floats, etc.). Minni has been aggressive and called my 3-bet and flop c-bet, but checking here looks weak and lets them realize equity for free. I'll bet around 55% pot to continue extracting value from worse pairs and draws while keeping the pot manageable if they have trips.

Board pairs on the turn (4♣ 7♥ 9♠ 9♥) and Claude has shown a very strong line: SB 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet on the flop and now a turn raise. The pot is 6,800 and calling costs 2,400, so I need ~26.1% equity to break even (2,400 / (6,800+2,400)). Q♠J♠ has only ~6 outs to pair on the river (3 queens + 3 jacks) -> ~13% raw chance, and many of those hits still lose to trips/sets or better that Claude’s line represents. Raising as a bluff (min‑raise to 4,800) OOP into a very strong line is too risky — I’ll either get called by better or face a shove where I’m crushed. Fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

UTG with 4s7h offsuit, a complete trash hand lacking connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. In early position at a 6-handed table, this is an automatic fold to avoid playing out of position with marginal equity against likely raises from aggressive opponents observed in prior hands. Preserve stack for better spots.

6♠2♠ is far too weak to enter from middle position with several active players behind (including aggressive stacks on the Button and in the blinds). Limping is -EV and opening would be overly loose given our 33bb stack and the likelihood of getting played back at or called multiway. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with Ad 5h (Ace-Five offsuit). The action has folded to me. While having an Ace in late position can be a candidate for a steal raise, there are aggressive players remaining. Claude is on the Button and has shown frequent aggression (e.g., Hands 11, 24). Minni in the Big Blind is the chip leader and very active. A5o plays poorly post-flop, especially if called by the Button, and folding to a 3-bet would be a waste of chips. Given the 80bb stack depth, I can wait for better spots or better steals when the players behind are tighter. Folding is the prudent play here.

On the button with Q7o after UTG, Middle, and Cutoff all folded. This is a great spot to steal the blinds. Q7o is a decent stealing hand with position. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) as a standard button open.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit. Claude, on the Button, has opened with a raise to 250. J3o is a very weak hand and does not play well out of position, especially against an active player like Claude. Defending the small blind with this range would be unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 2.5bb button open to 250 from the button while I’m in the big blind with Td9h. With deep stacks (215bb) this is a hand with good playability and straight potential but it’s marginal for a 3-bet bluff out of position. Calling 150 more (250 total committed) gives me ~27% pot odds (150/(400+150)) and the hand will have enough equity vs a typical BTN opening range to justify a defend. Plan: call and see the flop, play cautiously OOP — continue with top pair, strong draws or two-pair+/sets, fold to heavy turn/river pressure when missed. A fold is too tight given the price; a 3-bet is too exploitable with this offsuit connector.

Check. I have T♦9♥ on J♥8♥2♥ — no pair but a huge drawing hand: an open‑ended straight draw (7 or Q) plus a four‑heart situation (one heart in hand with three hearts on board). That’s 15 unique outs (~32% to hit the turn, ~54% by the river). Out of position and with deep stacks, I don’t want to bloat the pot by betting OOP; I’ll check to induce a c‑bet and realize equity. Plan: check‑call a standard small/medium c‑bet to see a turn, take a free card if Claude checks back, and only escalate if I hit (heart/straight) or get proper pot odds vs aggressive raises — fold to extreme turn/river pressure if I completely miss.

I have Qh giving me a strong flush draw on this monotone 8h-Jh-2h board. Minni checked to me, and I have position. This is a great semi-bluff spot - I have fold equity plus equity when called with my queen-high flush draw. Betting around 60% pot for value/protection and to build the pot if I hit.

Call. With T♦9♥ on J♥8♥2♥ I have an open‑ended straight draw (7 or Q = 8 outs) plus the nut flush draw (9 hearts) with two overlapping cards (7♥, Q♥) — ~15 unique outs (~32% to hit on the turn, ~54% by the river). Facing 350 into a 900 pot: calling 350 to potentially win ~1,600 requires ~22% equity to break even, and my draw comfortably exceeds that. Stacks are deep (I ~21.4k, Claude ~11.8k) so implied odds favor realizing equity. I’m out of position, so raising as a semi‑bluff is unnecessarily bloating the pot and can be exploited; a flat call controls the pot size and keeps worse hands in. Plan: call the 350, see the turn, and if I hit a heart/straight or pair I’ll proceed aggressively; if I miss and face heavy turn pressure I’ll fold.

Turn: A♣ on J♥ 8♥ 2♥. I hold T♦9♥ — no pair but an OESD (7 or Q) plus a four‑heart redraw. Straight outs = 8 (four 7s + four Qs), heart outs = 9, overlap (7♥, Q♥) = 2, so 15 unique outs to the river -> 15/46 ≈ 32.6% to hit. Pot is 1,250 and Claude opened and c‑bet the flop; I called to realize equity. With no bet to me on the turn I should not bloat the pot OOP with a semi‑bluff: the Ace is a scary card for his range (Ax, two‑pair combos) and a lead invites a raise that would put me in a marginal shove/fold spot with only ~33% equity. Checking keeps pot control, preserves my equity to see a free/cheap river, and lets me call a reasonable bet but fold to extreme aggression. Plan: check and either take a free river if he checks, or call a small/medium turn bet to see the river; if I hit the straight/flush I will bet/raise for value on the river, if I miss I will fold to heavy river pressure.

I have Qh for the second-nut flush draw (only Kh beats me). Board is 8h-Jh-2h-Ac. Minni has checked twice now - if they had a made flush, they'd likely have bet or check-raised by now. The Ac is a good card to barrel on as it's a scare card. I should continue my semi-bluff with strong equity. Betting ~65% pot to put pressure and build the pot for when I hit.

Call 800. Pot is 2050; calling 800 requires ~28% equity (800 / 2850). I have T♦9♥ on J♥8♥2♥ A♣ — an OESD (7 or Q = 8 outs) plus the nut‑flush draw (9 hearts) with 2 overlapping cards (7♥, Q♥) → 15 unique outs. That’s 15/46 ≈ 32.6% to hit the river, which exceeds the pot‑odds, so a call is +EV. Claude’s line (BTN open, flop c‑bet, turn bet on the Ace) shows meaningful strength (Ax, Jx, pairs/sets), so raising OOP as a semi‑bluff is risky — it invites 3‑bets and commits me with only ~33% equity. With deep stacks and good implied odds I control the pot by calling and realizing equity. Plan: see the river; if I hit the straight/flush I will extract value (bet/raise); if I miss and he barrels big or jams, fold; if he checks, re-evaluate for a small value bet or check-back depending on the river texture.

I turned a made flush on the river (9♥ with board 8♥ J♥ 2♥ A♣ A♥). This is a very strong showdown hand but it’s not the absolute nut flush — higher hearts (K♥/Q♥) or full‑house/quads combos (any A with a paired/ranking board card, or pocket J/8) can beat me. Claude’s line (BTN open, small flop c‑bet, turn bet on the A, then checking the paired A on the river) looks like a wide range that contains many Ax, single pair J/8 combos and medium pocket pairs — many of which will call a modest value bet but only a few beat my flush. I want to extract value from those worse hands while not bloating the pot against the relatively rarer full‑house or higher‑flush combos. Betting roughly one‑third to one‑half pot gives the best chance to get called by worse hands and keeps the pot manageable if he check‑raises. I’ll make a modest value bet of 1,000 chips. If Claude check‑raises large or jams I’ll assume the range is heavily weighted to full houses/quads or superior flushes and will fold; if he calls, I expect to be ahead vs his single‑pair/Ax/Jx holdings.

The river Ah completed my flush - I now have the Queen-high flush (Qh). Only Kh beats me. Minni's small bet (1000 into 2850) looks like either a thin value bet with trips/smaller flush, or a blocking bet. My Q-high flush is extremely strong here. Given Minni's small sizing, I should raise for value to extract from weaker flushes and Ax hands. A raise to around 2500 prices in weaker flushes and trips while not over-committing if somehow facing Kh.

Board A♣ A♥ J♥ 8♥ 2♥ — I hold T♦9♥: a made heart flush (A,J,9,8,2) but not the absolute nut (K♥/Q♥/T♥ beat me), and paired aces on board introduce the possibility of full houses/quads. Claude’s line (BTN open, small flop c‑bet, turn bet on the A, then check‑raise the river) is polarized to value and a small subset of bluffs. Pot is 6,350 and I must call 1,500 more — calling 1,500 to win ~7,850 requires ≈19% equity to break even. Unconditionally the combos that beat me (full houses/quads + higher hearts) are limited — rough combinatorics give full houses/quads on the river at only a few percent and higher‑heart combos in the low double digits; even after weighting for Claude’s aggression I estimate his range beats me less than or around the break‑even threshold. Folding here is too tight; raising is thin and would commit me into obvious full houses/quads or get called by higher hearts. Plan: call the 1,500 (total committed 2,500) and evaluate showdown; if Claude jams over this call I’ll give credit to a full house/quads or dominant heart and fold to extreme shove.

6-handed UTG with T7o is a clear under-open: dominated by the calling/3-betting ranges behind, poor playability postflop, and we’re not deep enough to justify opening marginal offsuit connectors. Given the table has shown plenty of aggression (frequent 3-bets) and our stack is only ~33BB, preserving chips for better UTG opens is higher EV than limping or raising and getting played back at.

I am in Middle Position with J5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. The action has folded to me. With 80 BB deep and players behind left to act, this hand has no playability and is not suitable for opening. The standard play is to fold immediately to conserve chips.

9h2h is a weak suited hand. While I'm in the cutoff with only 3 players behind, this hand has poor playability - the gap between 9 and 2 is too large for straight potential, and it's dominated by most hands that would call. Not worth opening with this hand.

Action has folded to me on the button. King-seven offsuit is a standard hand to open-raise for a steal in this position. While not a powerhouse, King-high is frequently ahead of the range the blinds will defend with, and I want to put pressure on the remaining two players to win the pot pre-flop or take the lead into the flop.

I’m in the small blind with 7♣4♣ — a weak suited hand that plays poorly out of position. Flash opened to 250; the pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to complete (250 total). Calling puts me OOP vs the button (and likely the big blind if he defends), often in dominated or multiway spots with little equity. 3-betting would be a fancy bluff with low fold equity and unnecessary risk given my stack and remaining hands. I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Facing a button raise to 250 from Flash after folds including SB. Pot 400, to call 150 (about 27% pot odds, but positionally BB defends wide). K7s suited is a strong defensive hand with king blocker, straight/flush potential, and good playability postflop. Vs button openers who are often wide, calling is standard to realize equity OOP rather than folding equity thin with a 3bet. Raising risks going heads-up with a marginal hand vs potential 4bet. Flash has shown aggression but also folded to pressure; however, call keeps pot small and sees flop.

Flop Qh 2d Ah completely misses my K7s: king-high with no pair, no flush draw (spades don't hit), no straight draw. Board is extremely wet with nut flush draw (hearts), broadway straight draws, and strong Ax/Qx pairs likely in villain's button range. OOP first to act in BB vs BTN, standard is to check for pot control. Plan to check-fold to any reasonable c-bet as equity realization is poor heads-up OOP on this texture, and king blocker doesn't outweigh the reverse implied odds. Donk betting is suboptimal: no value and poor as bluff on scary board where villain floats wide but calls/raises light. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament.

As the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace-high and Queen-high flop favors my range significantly more than the Big Blind's range. I will place a standard continuation bet of approximately 45% of the pot to capitalize on this range advantage and force folds from weaker hands that Elon might have defended with, such as small pairs or suited connectors that missed the board.

Flop Qh 2d Ah completely misses K7s: king-high with only backdoor spade draw, no immediate equity. Board is draw-heavy (nut flush draw, broadway straights like KT/JT), favoring Flash's wide button range which includes many Ax, Qx, suited connectors. OOP in BB facing half-pot c-bet (250 into 800), equity realization is low heads-up; villain cbets frequently for value/protection but will continue strong with better hands. Calling risks bloating pot OOP with reverse implied odds and poor playability on scary texture. Raising as bluff is thin: no fold equity vs polarized range, exposes to re-raises. Folding preserves 7000 stack for superior spots in tournament; K7s defense pre was realization play, now cut losses.

I am in UTG position with a very weak hand, 6d 9s (9-high offsuit). In a 6-handed game, the UTG opening range should be relatively tight (pairs, broadways, strong suited connectors). 69o is well outside any standard opening range. The prudent play is to fold immediately to conserve chips.

Pocket Aces - the best starting hand in poker. I'm in middle position with Pro already folded. I need to raise for value here. Standard open raise of 2.5-3x BB is appropriate. I'll raise to 250 to build the pot while encouraging action from worse hands.

I am in the cutoff holding 8s 6c. Claude has raised from middle position to 250. This is an offsuit, non-connected hand that is far below the range required to continue against an open raise, especially with three active players behind me (Minni, Elon, and FiveTwo). Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I'm on the button with 8♣ T♦ facing a standard middle-position open to 250. With ~177bb effective and two players (SB and BB) still to act, this is a clear spot to keep the pot small and use position. T8o is too weak for a value 3-bet and not a great candidate for a bluff 3-bet (a min-raise to 400 would just invite squeezes or shoves from the blinds). I will call 250 to see the flop and plan to play post-flop using position: continue when I hit top pair/good draws or apply pressure selectively, otherwise fold to strong resistance.

Preflop in the small blind with 2s6h, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing a raise from middle position (Claude, who has been aggressive) to 250 and a call from the button (Minni). Pot is 650, to call 200 more (total 250 from me). This gives poor implied odds even if I hit, and position is bad postflop. BB still to act. Defending this wide risks chips unnecessarily in a tournament spot where survival and stack preservation matter over 150 hands. Standard fold to avoid spew.

We’re getting a very good price in the BB: 150 more to win a pot that will be 800, needing ~19% immediate equity, and 7♥5♥ has playable multiway equity with suitedness/straight potential. With only ~32bb, I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand via a 3-bet, but folding is too tight given the odds and implied value when we flop draws/two pair. Call and proceed cautiously postflop.

Multiway on Q♠9♦4♦ our 7♥5♥ has essentially no made hand and no real draw (only very weak backdoor hearts/straight possibilities). Out of position versus the preflop raiser, donk-bluffing without equity is too spewy, and checking keeps the pot small while letting Claude bet his strong range. Plan: check and mostly fold to meaningful pressure, continuing only versus very small sizing with good turn potential.

I have pocket Aces on a Q94 board with two diamonds. This is a great spot to c-bet for value. My overpair is very strong here and I want to extract value from Qx hands, pocket pairs, and diamond draws. Both Minni and FiveTwo called preflop, so they could have Queen-x hands or suited connectors. A bet of around 60% pot (~500) balances value extraction while not scaring away weaker hands.

Pot is 1300 and Claude has bet 500 on Q♠ 4♦ 9♦. I’m on the button with T♦8♣ — I missed the flop but have ~10 outs: 4 J’s to make the 8-9-10-J-Q straight and 3 tens + 3 eights to make a pair. Probability to improve by the river is ≈38%, while the break-even equity to call is ~28% (500/(1300+500)). With position and acceptable pot odds this is a +EV call. Raising would be too thin (I lack fold equity and can get crushed by Qx/sets); folding would give up too much equity. Plan: call to see the turn, continue if I pair or make the straight (or pick up a backdoor diamond), and fold to strong turn aggression if I miss.

On Q♠9♦4♦ with 7♥5♥ we have no pair and essentially no real draw—only very thin backdoor hearts and two-runner straight possibilities. Versus a bet into two players and a call, our immediate equity is far below the ~22% needed to continue, and turning this into a bluff (call or check-raise) is poor given the multiway dynamics and this texture (strong Qx/9x and many diamond/draw combos continue). Preserve the remaining stack and fold.

Three diamonds on board now but I hold the Ad, giving me the nut flush draw plus my overpair. If Minni made a flush, it can't be the nuts since I have the Ad. I should continue betting for value against Qx hands and weaker pairs while also charging draws. If Minni has a small flush, I have 9 outs to improve to the nut flush. Betting around 55% pot to continue extracting value while controlling the pot size.

Claude bets 1,000 into a 2,800 pot — I must call 1,000 to continue. Break-even equity = 1000 / (2800+1000) ≈ 26.3%. My hand T♦8♣ on Q♠-4♦-9♦-2♦ has the following clean/unique river outs: nine diamonds (completing a flush), three remaining tens, three non-diamond jacks (straight), and two non-diamond eights — total = 17 unique outs. 17/46 ≈ 37% raw chance to improve on the river, comfortably above the 26% required. Some of those outs are “dirty” (a diamond river can also help Claude if he already holds two diamonds), but his line and relatively small turn sizing make a wide/mergeable range plausible. Raising here is unattractive — I lack sufficient fold equity and would be vulnerable to 3-bets or call-downs by Qx/sets/flushes. Folding surrenders too much equity. With position I can realize my equity and control the river: call 1,000 now; if I hit a clear value hand (flush, clean straight or pair that looks best) I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll often check and fold to big river aggression, bluff selectively only on very favorable runouts.

River brought the Kh, no fourth diamond. I have AA with the Ad blocker. Minni has been calling flop and turn - likely has Qx, a busted diamond draw, or possibly a small flush. The K could have given them two pair if they had Kx. With my Ad blocker, any flush they have isn't the nuts. I should bet for thin value - Qx hands will likely call. Betting around 45% pot to extract value while controlling my risk if they have a flush or two pair.

Board: Q♠-4♦-9♦-2♦-K♥. My hand T♦-8♣ is just Ten-high — I missed all of my river outs. Claude opened preflop and barreled flop (500) and turn (1000) and now puts out a smallish river bet (1700 into a 5500 pot). To call 1700 I need ≈23.6% equity (1700/7200). Against Claude’s line his range is weighted to made hands (Qx, 9x, Kx, sets, maybe completed diamonds if he held them earlier) and also some thin value/second-pair hands; the combos where I beat him (pure air or rare bluffs/missed two-card diamonds) are a minority. I also lack meaningful blockers to his primary bluff combos, and raising as a bluff with only high-card is too risky and can be exploited. Given the required equity and my low actual showdown equity versus his continuing range, this is a fold — preserve stack and wait for a spot with real showdown value or fold equity.
K6o from UTG is too weak to open. This hand is easily dominated by better kings and doesn't play well post-flop. Standard fold from early position.

I am holding Ace-Six of spades in the Hijack position. A suited Ace is a strong starting hand in a 6-max game and is a standard open from middle position. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and apply pressure to the remaining players, especially the shorter stacks like FiveTwo.

I have pocket aces (Ac Ah) — the strongest preflop hand. Flash opened to 300 from MP; I'm in the cutoff with three players left to act (Button, SB, BB). I want to 3-bet for value and to thin the field, not just min-raise (500) which would invite multiway callers and speculative hands. I will raise to 1500 total: large enough to charge weaker opens and isolate Flash, yet leaves maneuverability postflop and puts uncomfortable decisions on the shorter SB (3033) and the Button/BB stacks. If I face a shove I’ll commit with AA; if I get called I’ll continue to extract value postflop.

On the button with 8hTh suited connectors, facing an MP open-raise to 300 and a large CO 3-bet to 1500. Excellent position for speculative hands like this. T8s has strong playability, good equity vs typical 3-bet ranges (JJ+, AK, some bluffs ~35-40% heads-up), and huge implied odds given deep stacks (Minni 144BB, Flash 73BB, my 69BB). Immediate pot odds to call 1500 into 1950 current pot are ~1.3:1 (43% needed, but equity + position + implied make it profitable). SB (30BB) and BB to act, but button allows control postflop. Minni aggressive historically (frequent raises, wins pots), sizing large suggests polarization but call extracts value when we hit. Folding too tight early in tournament; 4-bet too thin with this hand. Standard call.

We’re facing a large 3-bet to 1500 and a cold call behind, and we’re out of position with A7o and ~30bb. Calling creates a very low SPR spot where we’ll be effectively committed with a dominated ace and poor realization multiway (and the BB can still wake up/squeeze). Jamming has little fold equity versus a strong, committed range given the 3-bet sizing and cold call. Best play is to cut losses and preserve the 30bb stack for better spots.

I hold Q3o in the Big Blind. The action facing me is significant: a raise to 300 from Flash, a 3-bet to 1500 from Minni, and a cold call of 1500 from Elon. My hand is extremely weak and dominated by the strong ranges implied by this action. Committing 1400 chips (about 18% of my stack) with Q3o in a multi-way 3-bet pot is a clear losing play. I must fold immediately.

I originally opened with Ace-Six of spades, which is a standard middle-position raise. However, Minni has 3-bet to 1500, and Elon has cold-called that 3-bet from the button. A cold-call of a 3-bet usually signifies a very strong range (like pocket pairs or strong broadway cards). My Ace-Six suited is likely dominated by their ranges (AK, AQ, AJ, or high pairs). Calling 1200 more would commit nearly 20% of my stack to a pot where I will be out of position against Elon and facing a strong 3-better in Minni. Given the action, the best play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up to my 3-bet on a fairly dry 9s-5s-7c flop and I hold AA — still the best hand. I want to charge spade draws and worse pairs (9x/7x/5x, KQ type hands, small pocket pairs) while denying correct odds to continue for free. A solid c-bet of ~2/3 pot accomplishes that: it gets called by draws and dominated pairs, folds some air, and keeps us in position to extract more on safe turns. I’m prepared to call a shove (AA is committing vs almost any shove here). Bet ~2300 into the 3450 pot to build value and protect.

Heads-up on the flop in position with T8h on 9s5s7c board. I flopped a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to the 6 for 5-6-7-8-9), plus backdoor heart flush and straight potential, and the T is a live overcard against some of Minni's range. Minni's CO 3-bet range (JJ+, AK, AQs+, some bluffs) gets hit by sets, two-pair combos (97s, 95s rare), top pair (AJ+, KQ unlikely), but also misses with overpairs, AK/AQ. Her large 2300 bet (~70% pot) into 3300ish pot is polarized: value (sets, 97, 75?) or bluffs/draws (spade draws, air). Pot odds are excellent: 2300 to call into 5750 (28.6% equity needed). Gutshot alone ~17% vs random (but less vs nuts), but + position, implied odds (Minni 10k+ behind, my 3.1k committed post-call but SPR low), and she may fire turn weak. Folding kills playability from preflop call; raising semi-bluff risky vs deep aggro Minni who may shove wide. Call to realize equity, control pot, and stack-off profitably when ahead later.

I have AA on a fairly dry turn (9s-5s-7c-Td) heads-up vs Elon who called my 3-bet pre and my large flop c-bet. His line suggests a range of medium pairs (9x/7x), pocket pairs, and spade draws — TT/99/77 are possible but not dominant. With the pot ≈8050 and Elon short (≈3150), my goal is to extract value from worse pairs/draws and to charge any spade/straight draws while putting maximum pressure on his short stack. A ~half-pot turn bet (~4000) accomplishes that: it denies correct odds to many draws, gets called or shoved by most of his value/draw combos, and wins the pot immediately when he folds. If he shoves I will call — AA is a favorite versus his plausible shove range and the pot odds/implied value justify calling. Bet 4000 to charge draws and extract value.

Heads-up on turn with T8h on 9s5s7cTd board, holding top pair (Tens) with 8 kicker after pairing the blankish 10d. This improves from flop gutshot (to 6 for straight) + backdoors to a decent made hand with showdown value and still some straight outs (6 for 6-7-8-9-10). Minni's line (CO 3-bet pre, large flop c-bet on drawy board, large turn bet ~50% pot) is polarized: strong value like sets (55/77/99), two-pair (97s/57/59s), combo draws that improved, or bluffs/spade draws/overpairs/A-high continuing aggression. Facing all-in shove equivalent (3150 to call into 12050 pot = ~4.8:1 odds, needing ~17% equity), which TPTK easily crushes. My equity vs her range is strong: ahead of all bluffs/draws (~40-50% her range given history of aggression, e.g., Hands 9,20,21 where she bets big), live vs many floats/overpairs/AK/AQ (8 outs to straight + some runner-runner), only crushed by sets/Two-pair (~20-30%). Great price realizes equity, no fold equity needed since pot-committed; folding post-flop call leaks value early tournament with deep effective stacks (her 8k behind). Her sizing suggests trying to deny equity but pot odds crush; standard call-off profitably.

I am dealt 6h 7c in the UTG position in a 5-handed game. While these are connectors, they are offsuit and extremely weak for an opening hand from early position. My stack of 7300 (73 BB) is healthy, but there is no reason to risk chips with a marginal hand like this, especially with aggressive, large-stacked players like Minni and Claude behind me. Standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG+1 with pocket Aces — the strongest possible starting hand. There are 4 players to act behind, including a short-stacked Button (FiveTwo, ~30bb) who is likely to shove or isolate, a medium SB (Pro) and a big-stacked BB (Claude) who can 3-bet. I want to build the pot and isolate the short stack while keeping post‑flop options and not bloating the pot unnecessarily. A standard 3x BB open is appropriate from this early position: large enough to get value and to discourage some callers, small enough to allow comfortable 4-bet or call decisions if I face action. If the button shoves I’ll call with AA; if I face a 3‑bet from a deep stack I will 4‑bet for value or commit depending on size. Action: raise to 300 total (3x BB).

Minni is opening to 3bb in a 5-handed game and has shown an aggressive, wide preflop approach. On the Button with Q9o and ~30bb, this is a reasonable defend in position: we realize equity well, can float/pressure on favorable textures, and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand that doesn’t want to face a 4-bet jam. Calling also keeps our range wider and lets us fold comfortably if one of the blinds squeezes large.

I hold 93o in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from Minni (UTG+1) and a call from FiveTwo (Button), I am out of position against two players with a hand that has very poor playability and low equity. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a weak holding. Folding is the only correct play.

I have pocket Kings - a premium hand. Minni raised to 300 and FiveTwo called. With KK in the BB, this is a clear 3-bet for value. I want to build the pot and potentially isolate. A standard 3-bet sizing here would be around 3-4x the original raise. I'll make it 1100 to extract value from worse hands that may call.

I have AA in UTG+1 and already opened to 300; Claude (BB) 3‑bet to 1100. Folding is not an option — this is pure value. I want to 4‑bet to build the pot and isolate the short‑stacked button, while charging Claude’s 3‑betting range and preserving post‑flop maneuverability. A sizing of ~4x Claude’s 3‑bet is appropriate here: big enough to extract value and get folds from many hands, but small enough to allow FiveTwo to shove (which I’ll call) and to avoid overcommitting unnecessarily. I’ll 4‑bet to 4400 total. If Claude 5‑bets big or jams, I’ll re‑evaluate but I’ll be strongly inclined to commit with AA.

We’re facing a BB squeeze to 1100 followed by a UTG+1 4-bet to 4400, and we only have 2733 total. Calling is effectively an all-in and we’ll often be up against very strong ranges (Claude’s squeeze is already strength-heavy; Minni’s 4-bet over that is typically QQ+/AK at minimum, even for an aggressive player). Q9o performs poorly versus those ranges and is frequently dominated; we’d need roughly ~32% equity given the pot odds, and we won’t have it in this configuration—especially with Claude still able to continue and further reduce our equity. Best play is to fold and preserve the remaining stack for better spots.

KK facing a 4-bet from Minni. This is a standard 5-bet jam spot with pocket Kings. Minni has been aggressive throughout the tournament, and KK is way too strong to fold here. The only hand that beats me is AA. With effective stacks and pot size, I'm committed. Jamming for value against QQ, JJ, AK and potentially wider from an aggressive player.

I opened to 300 and 4‑bet to 4,400 with AA after Claude 3‑bet to 1,100. Claude has now 5‑bet/raised to 17,280. My stack is 18,987 so calling costs an additional 12,880 (leaving ~6,107 behind). The pot is 22,030 now; if I call the total pot will be ~34,910, so I need ~36.9% equity to break even. AA is far ahead of any realistic 5‑bet/all‑in range (KK/QQ/AK and occasional bluffs), so folding is not an option. Reraising to the minimum or shoving would commit nearly my entire stack for little extra benefit — Claude has already shown massive strength and likely is effectively all‑in — so I’ll just call and take my strong equity. Action: call to 17,280 total.

I'm already all-in with KK after the preflop 5-bet jam. The Ace on the flop is scary as Minni could have AA, but I'm already committed. With To Call: 0 and my stack at 1100 (remaining after going all-in), I just check and see the runout.

I’m already effectively committed — I called Claude’s massive preflop raise and To Call is 0, so there’s no further chip I can put in. On A♥ 3♣ 5♦ I hold A♣ A♠ — trips (a set) of Aces, an extremely strong hand. Quads on the flop are impossible and only rare made straights (2+4) or future board pairs giving a full house could beat me. Since I have no chips to bet and folding is not an option, I take the forced action and check (0).

I'm essentially all-in from the preflop 5-bet with KK. The pot is 34910 and I have only 1100 left. To Call is 0, so I just check and hope my Kings hold against Minni's range. The Ace on board is concerning but I'm committed.

I have Ac As on A♥ 3♣ 5♦ 7♦ — a set of Aces, essentially the nuts against any realistic 5‑bet preflop range. This hand is heads‑up vs Claude (who 5‑bet preflop and now only has ~1,100 behind), so my priority is to extract his remaining chips. A small value bet sized to his remaining stack will almost always induce an all‑in call from hands I beat (AK, KK, QQ, etc.) while denying a free river that could (in the rare case) turn a full house for very unlikely holdings. Betting 1,100 gets his stack committed with minimal additional risk and maximizes EV — if he folds I pick up extra chips, if he calls I'm a dominant favorite. I avoid overbidding since he can't call more than his remaining chips and there's no benefit to bloating the pot further.

I have KK with only 1100 chips left facing a 1100 bet into a 36010 pot. I'm getting over 32:1 pot odds. This is a trivial call - I'm completely pot committed after the preflop 5-bet jam. Even if Minni has AA, the pot odds make folding mathematically impossible.

4-handed UTG with 27bb, 8♥2♥ is too weak to open profitably into a capable BTN/SB and a massive BB stack who can punish with wide 3-bets/isolations. Limping is also bad because we invite an iso and play a dominated hand out of position. Best stack-preserving, +EV line is to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

I am on the Button with T6o, a very weak hand. Action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal blinds, the Big Blind (Minni) holds a massive chip lead (42k vs my 7.8k) and has been playing aggressively. Opening light into a deep-stacked, active opponent is risky. There is little value in trying to steal here with trash cards given the risk of being 3-bet or called by the big stack. Folding is the disciplined and standard decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 5c, which is a very weak hand. The action has folded to me. While it only costs 50 more to call, Minni in the Big Blind has a massive stack and has shown significant aggression in previous hands (especially hand #29). Playing a weak hand out of position against an aggressive big stack is a recipe for losing chips. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 4-handed game with 8s 7c. Although the table is short-handed, 87o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. With a massive chip leader in the Small Blind who has shown aggression and a short stack in the Big Blind who might shove, opening this hand is essentially burning chips. The standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. While it is a 4-handed game and position is valuable, this hand is slightly too weak to open, especially with the chip leader (Minni) in the Small Blind and a short stack (FiveTwo) in the Big Blind who may look to shove. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I'm in the small blind with 7♦6♣ and both UTG and the button have folded — it's heads-up against FiveTwo in the big blind who is relatively short (~26 BB). 7-6o is marginal but has enough postflop playability heads-up to be included in an opening range. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the short blind, and avoid giving him good pot odds to see a cheap flop by limping. A modest 3x open to 300 (total) balances fold equity and build-up: it folds out many hands, isolates the short stack, and keeps the sizing economical for me. If FiveTwo jams I will evaluate his shove range but can comfortably call or fold as appropriate given his tendency to shove wide with a short stack; if he just calls I'll proceed cautiously out of position, c-betting appropriate flops and folding to strong resistance.

Minni is opening from the small blind and should be wide. With T9o in the big blind getting 200 to win a pot that will be 600 (need ~25% equity), this is a clear defend. A 3-bet is awkward at ~26bb effective (would commit too much and play poorly if shoved on), and a shove is too thin versus a calling range that will include many better hands. Best EV is to call and realize equity with a connected hand and position postflop (acting last).

I raised preflop and this is a dry Q♥‑8♣‑3♥ board. I have 7♦6♣ — no pair but an open‑ended straight draw to a 5 or 9 (8 outs, ~31% to hit by river). As the preflop aggressor I should take the initiative and apply pressure with a semi‑bluff that also has real equity. The pot is 600; a ~1/2‑pot c‑bet keeps the sizing economical, folds out many worse hands (unpaired overcards, small pairs, weak draws) and preserves fold equity against a short stacked big blind. I will bet 300 more (total committed 600). If FiveTwo jams all‑in I will fold — we’d need roughly ~41% to call profitably vs that shove and our ~31% equity doesn’t justify a call. If he simply calls I’ll proceed cautiously out of position on the turn (evaluate card — barrel if it improves our story or check for pot control if it brings Q/8/paired cards).

Blind-vs-blind Minni is opening very wide and this 1/2-pot c-bet can contain a lot of air and marginal one-pair hands. With T9 we have a clean gutshot to the J (and some backdoor heart potential with Th), plus we’re in position and can realize equity / apply pressure on good turns (J, T, 9, heart turns when she slows down). A flop shove needs a very high fold frequency and gets called by too much of her range (Qx, 8x, hearts, pairs), so the higher-EV line is to call and keep her bluffs in while preserving the option to fold to a big turn barrel on bricks.

I c‑bet the flop as a semi‑bluff but the turn (Qd) pairs the board and completely changes the texture. I have 7♦6♣ — no pair and with one card to come my clean outs are limited (three 7s + three 6s + three 8s = 9 outs → ~9/46 ≈ 19.6% to make a single pair on the river), and many of those river pairs will still lose to Qx, 8x or pocket pairs that called on the flop. FiveTwo is short (≈1,833 behind) so any bet here is likely to induce an all‑in; calling a shove would require roughly 1,833 / (1,800+1,833+1,833) ≈ 33.6% equity to break even — well above my realistic equity. I’ll take the low‑variance line: check for pot control and either see a free river or fold to a shove. If FiveTwo bets small I can consider a call to realize equity, but I will fold to a large jam. Checking preserves my huge stack and avoids committing chips with a very weak hand and insufficient equity.

Turn pairs the queen (Qh 8c 3h Qd) and Minni checks. With Th9c we still only have a gutshot to the J (plus thin pair outs), and our stack is ~1 pot behind. Stabbing here risks getting check-jammed by the big stack (a common leverage spot blind-vs-blind), forcing us to fold and lose a large chunk of our stack. Checking back realizes our equity for free and keeps our remaining stack intact for better spots; we can value-bet if we spike a J or consider bluffing rivers that check through again on favorable runouts.

Board: Qh‑8c‑3h‑Qd‑Kd. I hold 7♦6♣ — no pair, nothing improved on the river. FiveTwo called my flop c‑bet and checked the turn, so his calling range contains many hands that beat or are very close to beating me (Qx, Kx, 8x, pocket pairs, or medium showdown hands). Betting the river would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity against a player who called the flop and is short (1,833); he can easily shove and force me to fold, and I have essentially no equity if he jams. With a large stack and no reason to risk chips with a worthless hand, I take the low‑variance line and check to showdown. If he bets, I will fold to any large shove.

We have no showdown value with T-high on Qh-8c-3h-Qd-Kd after calling flop and it checking through turn. Minni’s double-check line contains a meaningful amount of give-ups/missed hearts and random air that will fold, plus some marginal pairs that may still fold to a credible river stab. Jamming needs >50% folds and risks getting looked up or check-jammed by a chip leader; checking back realizes nothing. Best risk/reward is a small blocker-style bluff that targets her air while keeping our stack intact and letting us comfortably fold if she check-raises.

I checked the river with 7♦6♣ and nothing improved. FiveTwo called my flop c‑bet and then checked the turn, so his line is weighted heavily toward real hands (Qx, 8x, pocket pairs) with some missed heart draws mixed in. He now bets 400 into a 2200 pot — calling would cost 400 to win ~2600 (≈15.4% pot odds). My hand has essentially no showdown value and my realistic equity vs his calling range is well below that threshold, so a call is +EV negative. Raising would be a pure bluff and risks a larger shove from a short stack. With a big stack and no reason to gamble on a marginal, low‑equity spot, I fold and preserve chips for better situations.

I am dealt 8-4 offsuit in the first position (UTG) in a 4-handed game. This is a very weak starting hand with no connectivity or high-card value. Given the stack sizes and the fact that there are many hands left in the tournament, there is no reason to open or play this hand from this position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with A♥Q♣ — a strong, position hand against only the blinds. I want to open and take the initiative: get folds, isolate the short-stack SB, and still have plenty of postflop maneuverability. A raise to 900 (total) gives meaningful fold equity vs the SB/BB, is consistent with table sizing tendencies, but doesn’t overcommit me or needlessly invite huge 3-bets. If I face a large 3-bet or shove I’ll re-evaluate then; otherwise I expect to play position postflop for value and pressure.

Minni’s 9bb open is very large, which tightens her range and gives me a bad price from the small blind. 8♦3♦ has poor raw equity versus a stronger opening range, and calling 850 would leave me ~27bb in a bloated pot out of position with dominated/low-flush potential and tough postflop decisions. A shove would be for ~35bb total and is likely to get called at a frequency where my equity is not sufficient, plus the BB can still wake up. Best is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

The opponent Minni (Button) has opened for a very large size of 900 chips (9 big blinds) into a 50/100 pot. While A6o has reasonable equity against a standard button opening range, calling a 9x raise out of position with a marginal offsuit Ace is extremely unprofitable. The pot odds are poor (requiring ~43% equity), and the reverse implied odds are high if I hit an Ace and am dominated. I preserve my ~77bb stack for better opportunities.

I’m UTG with A♦9♣ in a 4-handed game — wide-ish ranges, so this Ace is strong enough to open. I want initiative and to avoid a multiway limp where A9o loses value, while also isolating the shorter button. A standard 3xBB open to 300 gives healthy fold equity without committing too many chips from my very deep stack. Plan post‑flop: c-bet favorable ace/high or drawy boards, control the pot on ragged boards, call small/standard 3-bets to play postflop for value and fold to large polarized 3-bets or shoves unless I have a clear read calling is +EV.

4-handed, Minni’s UTG open is likely fairly wide, and we have position. A9o is playable but can be dominated if we bloat the pot with a 3-bet at ~36bb (a 3-bet/fold would cost a big chunk of our stack). Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity on the button, and lets us proceed cautiously on A-high flops while folding comfortably if the blinds squeeze big.

I hold Q7o in the Small Blind. The action began with an UTG raise to 300 and a Button call. Although the pot odds are roughly 4:1, playing Q7o out of position in a multi-way pot is a losing proposition due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. My hand is easily dominated by the ranges of the UTG opener and the Button caller. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ks 3s. Minni opened to 300 from UTG and FiveTwo called from the Button. The pot is currently 750 and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me pot odds of 3.75-to-1. Ks 3s is a decent suited hand that plays well enough in a multi-way pot for this price, and calling closes the pre-flop action. While I'll be out of position, the price is too good to pass up. I will call and see a flop.

I have flopped the King-high flush on a very coordinated board (Js Qs 9s). While the Ace-high flush is the only hand currently beating me, I am in a very strong position. In a multi-way pot, it is standard to check to the pre-flop raiser (Minni) to see if she continues with a bet. If she bets, I can evaluate whether to call to keep her range wide or raise to extract value from hands like a single Ace of spades, sets, or straights. Checking also allows me to control the pot size if an Ace of spades appears on a later street.

I opened UTG with A♦9♣ and got two callers (Button and BB). Flop J♠ Q♠ 9♠ gives me middle pair (9 with an Ace kicker) but the texture is highly dangerous: two overcards to my nine and three spades on board. Against two callers, a c‑bet is thin — many hands that called preflop will have spade combinations (some already have a made flush with two spades, others have strong Qx/Jx or big draws) and will happily continue. I want to control the size of the pot with a marginal, vulnerable pair and gather information. Plan: check to keep bluffs and single‑spade draws in, call small bets to extract value from worse pairs/draws, but fold to large, polarizing bets or shoves that represent a made flush/top pair. If checked through I’ll look to value bet or barrel on safe turn runouts (Ace/9 or non‑spade low cards); fold to heavy aggression that indicates a made spade/monster.

Multiway on a monotone J♠Q♠9♠ board, our A♣9♦ is just bottom pair with no spade and very poor ability to barrel (we don’t block flushes, and both opponents can have many spade combos). With two players behind and decent showdown value, betting mostly builds a pot where we’ll face awkward calls/raises and have to overfold turns. Checking keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity, and lets us re-evaluate on the turn (betting only on favorable cards / if checked to again).

I flopped a King-high flush with Ks 3s. The turn Jc pairs the board, which is a bit of a scare card as it makes full houses and quads possible (JJ, QQ, 99, QJ, J9). However, the flop checked around, which reduces the likelihood that an opponent is trapping with a monster, though it's not impossible. I have a very strong hand and need to extract value from trips, smaller flushes, or even a straight. I will lead out for about half the pot to see where I stand and build the pot.

Turn J♣ pairs the board — my A♦9♣ is now two pair (Jacks and Nines). That’s a much stronger hand than on the flop but still vulnerable to Jx (trips/full houses), Qx (two pair J&Q), pocket sets and any made spade flushes. Flash’s bet (500 into a 1,450 pot) is small and looks like a probe / thin value bet rather than a polarized shove. Out of position, I want to control the pot size while keeping worse hands and bluffs in. Calling 500 gives very good pot odds to continue, extracts value from one-pair hands and weaker two-pair/9x hands, and lets me reevaluate on the river. Plan: call now, then on the river value-bet safe cards and fold or proceed cautiously if heavy action indicates trips/full house/flush.

We have Jacks and nines (two pair) on a paired, monotone board. It’s a decent made hand with some showdown value, but raising is unattractive: worse hands (some Qx/9x) often fold, while better hands (made flushes, any Jx, Q9, boats) continue and can put us in a bad stack-off spot with ~33bb. Getting 1950:500 (need ~20%) in position is too good to pass, and calling keeps the pot manageable while letting us realize equity (including a few strong improving rivers like another 9). On most rivers we can check back if it checks through, and we can comfortably fold to large river aggression on blanks when ranges skew strong.

I have a King-high flush on a board with four spades (Js Qs 9s Jc 4s). I have the second-best possible flush, trailing only the Ace of spades (As). While the board is paired, making full houses possible, the flop checked through, which reduces the likelihood that an opponent is holding a monster like QQ or 99. On the turn, I led out for 500 and received two callers, which suggests they likely have a Jack, a spade, or a straight draw. Now that the fourth spade has arrived on the river, I want to extract value from a Jack or a lower flush. A bet of 1000 into a pot of 2450 is a sized for value, as it is large enough to get paid but small enough not to scare away a trip-Jacks type hand. If I am raised, I will have to carefully consider if I am facing the Ace of spades or a full house.

Board: Js Qs 9s Jc 4s. I hold A♦9♣ — two pair (Jacks & Nines) but I have no spade. Flash’s line (check/call on the three‑spade flop, small turn bet, small river bet) is consistent with medium/strong spade flushes, Qx (which makes J&Q two pair), or thin value. There are several hands that beat me (any spade, any Q, pocket 9s/Js for full house/quads), but the river sizing is small. Calling 1,000 into an effective pot of ≈4,450 requires only ~22.5% equity and is cheap relative to my 40k stack. Raising would polarize my range and get me crushed by the many spade/Q combos; folding would concede too much for minimal savings. I’ll call to reach showdown and fold to any large raise, since a big reraise would strongly represent a flush/full house.

River is J♠ Q♠ 9♠ J♣ 4♠ (4 spades) and we hold A♣9♦: two pair (JJ99A) with no spade. Flash bets into two players and Minni cold-calls with us still to act—this is a very strong, value-heavy line on a 4-flush board. Most one-spade holdings are now flushes, and both players’ actions strongly weight them toward having a spade (or a full house), while the portion of their ranges we beat (worse two pair like Q9, some thin/value-bluff hands) is too small. Even though the pot odds are ~18%, calling risks 36% of our stack in a spot where we’re very often drawing dead. Preserve the 27.8bb stack and fold.

UTG 4-handed we can open wider, but 7♠2♣ is still the absolute bottom of the range: poor playability, dominated when we pair, and no strong straight/flush potential. With ~28bb effective and opponents who can apply pressure (especially big-stacked blinds), limping or opening just burns EV. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with Jd9d, a suited one-gapper that has good playability and potential to make straights or flushes. With the action folded to me, this is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play the hand in position. The player in the Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and aggressive, but folding this hand on the button is too tight. A standard raise size of 2.5bb (250 chips) puts pressure on the blinds while minimizing my risk if I face a 3-bet.

I hold KTo in the small blind against a button raise from Pro. In a 4-handed game, King-Ten is quite strong against a button opening range, which is typically wide. While I could call, playing out of position against both the button and an aggressive big blind (Minni) is difficult. I will 3-bet to 850 to seize the initiative and potentially end the hand immediately. This also forces the big blind to fold most of her range and isolates Pro if he chooses to continue.

2♥5♠ is a clear muck. I’m facing a re-raise to 850 after the button opened to 250; pot is 1,200 and I must call 750. Calling requires ~38.5% equity (750/(1,200+750)) — far more than 2‑5o has vs a typical 3‑bet range (pairs, broadways, Axs, suited connectors). Playing this out of position against an aggressive line is a losing proposition, and a raise would be a large, unnecessary bluff into multiple deep stacks. Preserve the 39k stack and wait for a real spot — fold.

I hold Jd9d on the Button. After opening to 250, the Small Blind (Flash) 3-bet to 850. The pot is now 1200 (including the BB fold). I need to call 600 more to see a flop. The pot odds are 1800:600, or 3:1, requiring roughly 25% equity. J9s is a suited one-gapper that plays well in position, retaining equity well against strong ranges (around 30-35% vs a tight range like TT+, AQ+). Given my positional advantage, stack depth (approx 75BB effective), and the attractive price, calling is the optimal play. I will call to see a flop and leverage position post-flop.

On this flop of 9h-Qh-Th, I have middle pair (tens) and an open-ended straight draw. However, the board is extremely dangerous as it is monotone hearts and I do not hold a heart in my hand. My opponent's range for calling a 3-bet from the button includes many suited broadways and connectors that could have already flopped a flush, a straight (KJ), or a better pair with a flush draw. Since I am out of position and the board is very 'wet', I will check to see how the opponent reacts. Betting here risks being raised and forced off my equity, whereas checking allows me to control the pot and potentially realize my straight draw if the turn is safe.

I hold Jd9d on a very wet monotone board of [9h Qh Th]. I have bottom pair and an open-ended straight draw (outs: 8, K). However, because the board is all hearts and I hold no hearts, my straight outs are tainted (the 8h and Kh make flushes), and I could already be drawing dead against a flopped flush. Flash (SB), the pre-flop 3-bettor, has checked to me. While his check might indicate weakness (like AK without a heart), he also has many strong hands in his range that check for pot control or to trap on this texture. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff that likely only folds out hands I am already beating or flipping with (like AK/AJ), while opening myself up to a check-raise that would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a turn card, and keep the pot manageable given the dangerous board texture.

The 2s on the turn is a complete blank. My hand (middle pair plus an open-ended straight draw) still has significant equity but is vulnerable on this monotone board. Pro's check on the flop is telling; it's less likely he has a made flush or a straight (KJ), as those hands would typically bet for value on such a wet texture. However, since I don't hold a heart, I am at a disadvantage if another heart falls or if he is trapping. I will check again to control the pot size. If he bets, I can evaluate based on his sizing, as I have many outs to improve (any Jack or King gives me a straight, and a Ten gives me trips).

On the Turn, the board remains [9h Qh Th 2s]. The 2s is a brick that changes nothing. My hand (pair of Nines + open-ended straight draw) has showdown value against unmatched overcards like AK/AJ, but is vulnerable. Flash (SB) has checked to me for the second time. His 3-bet range is strong, and checking twice on this texture suggests either a trap (flush/set) or pot control with a marginal hand (overpair/top pair scared of flush) or simply missed high cards. Betting here offers little value: worse hands fold, and better hands call or raise. If I bet and face a check-raise, I lose my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see the river card, and potentially win at showdown against missed overcards or improve to a straight.

The river Jack of clubs completes my King-high straight (9-10-J-Q-K). While the board contains three hearts, meaning any heart in Pro's hand makes a flush, the fact that both of us checked the flop and the turn makes a flush significantly less likely, as most players would bet a made flush or even a strong draw on such a wet board. Pro's passive play suggests a marginal hand like a pair of Queens, Tens, or perhaps a Jack. I will bet 900 (half the pot) to extract value from these hands. If Pro has Ace-King for a higher straight or a sneaky flush, I will have to evaluate a raise, but for now, a value bet is the most profitable line.

On the River, the board is [9h Qh Th 2s Jc]. This puts a 4-card straight on the board (9-T-J-Q). Any King (AK, KK, KQ, KJ) or Eight (88, A8) makes a straight. Additionally, the flush draw from the flop is still present. I hold [9d Jd] for Two Pair (Jacks and Nines). While Two Pair is normally strong, on this texture it is merely a bluff-catcher. 

The opponent (Flash) 3-bet pre-flop, which heavily weights their range towards strong hands like AK, KK, QQ, JJ, TT.
