The line of checking the flop and turn (pot control on a wet board) and then leading out on the river when the straight completes strongly suggests they have hit the straight (likely with AK or KK) or were trapping with a flush. Betting AA or AQ here would be thin value as they are beaten by the board's straight potential and any two pair. Given that the opponent's value range (Straights, Flushes, Sets, Better Two Pairs) crushes mine, and I unblock the most likely straight combos (AK, KK), folding is the prudent play.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 4-handed game with [As 3h]. Although 4-handed play requires wider ranges, Ace-Three offsuit is a marginal hand that often suffers from domination issues and poor post-flop playability, especially out of position. With an aggressive chip leader in the small blind and a short stack in the big blind who might shove, the risk outweighs the reward. Standard strategy dictates folding A2o-A5o from early position in this setup.

I am on the button with King-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a 4-handed game. Pro has already folded, so I only have the blinds to contend with. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to build the pot and take advantage of my position. I need to be mindful of FiveTwo's short stack (26 BBs), as they might shove over my raise, but KJs is generally strong enough to continue or evaluate based on the specific sizing of a 3-bet.

Facing a button open to 300 from Flash, I'm in the small blind with A♥4♦. Out of position and with a weak kicker, this is not a hand I want to 3‑bet light (min‑3bet to 500 would be tiny and a larger 3bet risks a shove/call from the short BB). Calling the 250 more (total 300 committed) keeps the pot manageable, gives me decent equity vs a wide button opening range (good blocker to Ax hands) and lets me see a flop to play postflop (continue for value if I hit an Ace, fold or pot‑control vs heavy action otherwise). I prioritize preserving my large stack and avoiding marginal, high-variance spots out of position, so I will call.

We’re getting a very good price in the BB: 200 more to win a pot that will be 900 (need ~22% equity). Even though 7c4c is weak and we’ll be out of position multiway (lower realization), it’s still preferable to defend rather than fold at this sizing, and it’s far too weak to turn into a squeeze with our ~27bb stack versus two deep opponents. Plan postflop is mostly check-range, continue on strong pairs/draws (flush draws/straight draws/two pair) and otherwise play cautiously.

I called preflop with A4o to see a cheap flop; on 5d-7s-3d I only have Ace-high and a single diamond (backdoor flush needing both turn+river). I'm out of position with two opponents (button opened wide, BB is short and sticky), so betting OOP to fold them is unlikely to be profitable multiway and would inflate a pot I don't want to play big without a pair. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and only continue with a call to a small bet (good pot odds) or with a pair/top pair/A improvement on later streets; I’ll fold to sizable aggression.

Multiway and out of position, this is a spot where we should largely check-range. We have top pair but a weak kicker (7c4c) on a dynamic board with diamond/straight draws; betting ourselves risks getting raised and playing a bloated pot versus stronger 7x/overpairs/sets while still not loving stack-off at ~25bb. By checking, we keep the pot manageable, allow the preflop raiser to c-bet (and potentially bluff), and we can comfortably continue versus reasonable sizing (especially against draws and worse pairs).

I missed the flop with King-Jack offsuit. The board of 5-7-3 with two diamonds is more likely to have connected with the calling ranges of the blinds than with my button opening range. Since both Minni and FiveTwo checked, and FiveTwo is playing a very short stack, a continuation bet might invite a check-shove from a pair or a draw. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keep the pot small, and see if I can improve on the turn or win at showdown with King-high.

Check. The turn paired the board (5♦-7♠-3♦ → 7♦) and I only have A♥4♦ — ace-high with a 4-card diamond draw. I’m out of position and this is a three-way pot; betting OOP will bloat the pot and will often be called by wide but stronger ranges (made pairs, sevens, two-pairs) so I prefer pot control and to realize equity. I have nine diamond outs to the flush (~19%) plus a couple of extra backdoor outs to an ace or a 4, so I’ll check and take a free river. If someone makes a small bet I’ll call for the odds to hit my flush; I’ll fold to heavy or polarizing aggression (big shove/raise) since my Ace-high is vulnerable.

Turn gives us trips (7c) on a now-paired board, which is a strong but not invulnerable hand: any made flush (two diamonds), any full house (55/33/7x5x/7x3x), and some stronger 7x kickers can have us in rough shape. Since flop checked through, ranges are somewhat capped and there’s plenty of worse that can still call (5x, 3x, pocket pairs, A-high with a diamond, straight draws like 46/64). I want to start building value while keeping the sizing modest so I don’t bloat the pot into a spot where deep stacks can apply huge pressure with flushes/boats. Plan: bet ~1/2 pot and comfortably fold to a big raise (multiway, raises here are heavily value-weighted).

On the turn, the 7d completes a potential flush and pairs the board. FiveTwo, who is playing a very short stack, has led out for half the pot after everyone checked the flop. My King-high has very little showdown value here and almost no equity against a range that includes flushes, trips, or even a basic pair of fives or threes. Furthermore, Minni is still to act behind me. Calling with just overcards in this spot is a losing play, especially given the board texture and the number of opponents. Folding allows me to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I will CALL 450. On the turn I have A♥4♦ on 5♦-7♠-3♦-7♦: I’m OOP with only Ace-high but I have a four-card diamond draw (9 diamond outs). On the turn there are 46 unseen cards, so the pure flush probability to hit the river is 9/46 ≈ 19.6%. There are also a couple of additional non-diamond Aces (and small pairing outs) that give me a few incremental % of equity, so my total effective showdown equity is roughly in the low‑to‑mid 20% range. To call 450 into a pot of 1,350 (final pot ≈ 1,800 if I call) I’m getting ~25% pot odds — this is a close, marginal decision but the bet size is small and the villain is short-stacked. I prefer to realize equity and see a cheap river rather than bloat the pot by raising OOP with a draw. Plan: call the 450, see the river, and fold to heavy/polarized shove on the river if I miss (only continue if I make a flush or if pot odds on a river jam justify a call).

I improved on the river — 4♥ pairs my 4 and I now have two pair (7s and 4s). We're heads‑up vs FiveTwo (short ~2k stack) who led small on the turn; his turn bet is ambiguous (could be thin value, a made diamond flush, trips/full house from pocket pairs, or a blocker bluff). Two pair beats a lot of his continuing range (single pairs like 5x/3x, A‑x, missed draws) but loses to diamond flushes and full houses/sets. With the pot at 1,800 I want to extract value from worse hands while keeping the pot controlled against monsters. I will make a half‑pot value bet (900). This sizing invites calls from one‑pair hands and worse A‑x, charges bluffs, and doesn’t overcommit me if he jams — if he shoves I’ll re‑evaluate (most shoves will be very strong, but short‑stack shoves contain bluffs too), but the immediate play is to probe for value now.

We river a full house (7s full of 4s) on 5d-7s-3d-7d-4h, which is an extremely strong hand in this line. After flop checks through and Minni calls our small turn stab, her 1/3-pot river lead is often a blocker/value bet (7x, 5x/4x, some straights like 65/64) or occasionally a missed-diamond bluff. The only realistic hand that has us in bad shape is specifically 7h5x (sevens full of fives), and because we hold 7c and the board has two sevens, that’s only a few combos. With our remaining stack, raising all-in is effectively a small additional amount (only +1133 over the 900), giving her great pot odds to look us up with many worse made hands, so we should maximize value rather than just call.

Board: 5♦ 7♠ 3♦ 7♦ 4♥ — my hand Ah4♦ gives me two pair (7s and 4s). Pot = 4,733; FiveTwo shoved to 2,033 total, so I must call 1,133 more. Calling 1,133 to win 5,866 (final pot) requires ≈19.3% equity to break even. What beats me? Any hand with a 7 (trips/quads/full house), any two-diamond flush, and full-house combos (33/55/44) — by card-combo counting there are only a few dozen flush combos (~36) and ~87 combos that include a 7 (plus a handful of pocket full houses), so the absolute number of combos that beat me is limited. The villain’s small turn bet and now river shove polarize him toward strong made hands, but he’s a short stack and will also shove missed draws/bluffs at a non-trivial frequency. Given the attractive pot odds (need ~19%), the relative scarcity of combos that categorically crush my two pair, and the tiny fraction of my large stack I’m risking, a call is +EV. I call the all-in (put a total of 2,033 on the river).

I am UTG with K3o in a 4-handed game. This hand is significantly below the opening range for this position, as it has poor playability and is easily dominated. Given the stack sizes and the need to maintain a solid strategy, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a weak hand. I will fold.

I’m on the button with K3o and huge depth (36,734 ≈ 367 BB). Button open-steals are very profitable here — K3o is marginal but perfectly fine as part of a wide button raising range, and I want to leverage position and big fold equity vs the SB and BB. I’ll open to a standard ~3x the BB (300 total) to avoid inviting cheap multiway flops while still keeping the size small relative to our stack. If I face a meaningful 3-bet (large sizing or shove) I’ll fold K3o — it’s dominated and not worth playing a big pot out of position. If called, I’ll use position to c-bet dry, high-card flops and exercise pot control on wet/connected boards, looking to extract value with top pair or better and fold to heavy aggression when unpaired.

We’re in the small blind versus a button open to 3bb with 82o. Even though the button range is wide, this hand is too weak and too dominated to defend profitably out of position, especially with the big blind still to act and our stack being healthy (~58bb). A 3-bet bluff with 82o is also a poor candidate (no blockers, terrible playability). Best is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 6c. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300 (3BB). The Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 to win a pot that will be 650. The pot odds are approximately 31%. While 65o has roughly 32-35% raw equity against a wide button opening range, my realized equity will be significantly lower due to being out of position with a weak, unsuited hand that rarely flops strong. Defending 65o against a 3x open is generally -EV. I will fold to preserve my stack of 67.5 BBs for better spots.

I open to 3x (300). A♣T♠ UTG in a 4‑hand game is a legitimate open — ATo is well within a 4‑max opening range and has good post‑flop playability and blocker value. My stack (~369bb) is much deeper than the others (≈58–104bb) so I can apply pressure; a 3x sizing gives decent fold equity, narrows the field, and keeps pot control versus the shorter stacks. Plan: c‑bet favorable Ace/high‑card flops, play straightforward postflop, and re‑evaluate vs big 3‑bets or shoves (call shoves only when pot odds and opponent ranges justify it).

9♠5♥ offsuit is too weak versus an UTG open even 4-handed. It has poor playability, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and calling invites the blinds in, reducing our positional advantage and realization. With no blockers and no good 3-bet bluff properties, the best EV is to conserve stack and wait for stronger/button-defense candidates.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9d 4d. The player UTG (Minni) has raised to 300 (3bb). I have a very weak hand (low suited gapper) and would be playing out of position against the chip leader who opened from early position. Even though the pot odds are somewhat inviting, my equity realization out of position is poor, and I risk being squeezed by the Big Blind. It is not profitable to defend this hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-5 offsuit. Minni, who has a massive stack and has been playing quite aggressively, opened to 300 from UTG in this 4-handed game. While the pot odds are roughly 3.25 to 1, 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against a big stack. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

4-handed UTG plays much wider; A9 suited is comfortably ahead of folding range and has strong playability (nut-flush potential, decent top-pair value) while benefiting from initiative. I’ll open-raise rather than limp to deny equity, isolate the blinds, and keep my range aggressive. Size slightly larger than min to reduce multiway without overinflating versus the big-stacked BB; plan is to continue versus reasonable 3-bets (especially in position postflop) but can fold to very large 3-bets from tighter ranges.

I hold Pocket Jacks on the Button. The UTG player (FiveTwo) opened to 250. In a 4-handed game, this is a premium hand. Reviewing history, FiveTwo has folded to 3-bets in Hand #1 and Hand #8. To maximize value, I want to keep FiveTwo in the pot with their weaker opening range rather than forcing a fold immediately. Additionally, the Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has shown aggression; flat calling on the button might induce a squeeze play from the blinds, against which I can back-raise/shove profitably. Being in position allows me to control the post-flop action effectively. Therefore, I elect to flat call.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 3h. FiveTwo raised from UTG and Pro called from the Button. While I have decent pot odds (200 to call into 650), 63s is a very weak hand to play out of position against an UTG raiser and a caller. The risk of being dominated or facing further action from Minni in the Big Blind makes this a clear fold.

I’m in the big blind with 5♦6♠ facing an UTG raise to 250 and a button call — pot is 650 and I must call 150 (total commit 250) or raise to at least 400. 5-6 offsuit is a speculative, weak hand that performs poorly multiway and out of position. Calling 150 into a 650 pot gives me ~18.8% pot-odds (150/(650+150)), but against an UTG open and a cold call from the button my actual equity with 5-6o is likely below that because many of their hands dominate mine (pairs, broadways, suited connectors). Raising to isolate would be thin and out of position versus two players with decent stacks — not a good spot for a marginal hand. With a deep stack I can wait for a much better spot and avoid getting multiway-bluffed or dominated. Fold and preserve my deep stack for higher expected-value situations.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser, I retain enough range advantage on K-Q-6 to profitably c-bet small. Ah9h has a strong overcard (A) plus nut backdoor hearts, making it a good low-cost bluff that can continue barreling on favorable turns (A, heart, or good high cards) while denying equity to Pro’s ace-highs and small pairs. I’ll use a ~1/3-pot sizing to keep my range wide and avoid overinflating OOP.

Pre-flop, I called a UTG open with Pocket Jacks on the button to play in position. The flop comes [6d, Qh, Ks], which is an exceptionally bad board for my hand as it hits the UTG opening range very hard (AK, AQ, KQ, KJ, QJ, QQ, KK). My Jacks are now effectively third pair. Although the c-bet size is small (225 into 875, roughly 25% pot), my equity against a standard UTG value range is poor, and I have very few outs (2 jacks) to improve. I am likely drawing nearly dead against top pair or better, and even against draws like AT/JT, I am vulnerable. Floating here usually leads to folding on the turn against further aggression or losing at showdown to better hands. Preserving chips is the prudent play.

The game is 4-handed, so UTG is effectively the Cutoff position. A3s (suited Ace-Three) is a standard opening hand from the Cutoff in this structure, offering nut flush potential and wheel straight possibilities. With a stack of over 60 big blinds, I am deep enough to navigate post-flop. Raising puts pressure on the blinds and the button. A standard open size of 2.5bb (250 chips) is consistent with table history.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. Pro has raised from UTG (which is the cutoff in a 4-handed game) to 250. While A6o is a decent hand in a vacuum, it performs poorly against a standard opening range from early position, especially with two players still to act behind me. Calling often leads to being dominated or playing a bloated pot with a weak kicker, and I don't believe this is a high-value spot for a 3-bet bluff given the current stack distributions. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from Pro while I’m in the small blind with 8♠7♣ (offsuit). This hand is a marginal connector with no suit and I’ll be out of position versus the opener (and possibly the big blind), making post‑flop play difficult. Pro’s UTG range is likely fairly strong, and calling 200 into a 400 pot to play multiway OOP offers poor equity and tricky implied odds — plus the big blind can still squeeze. I’m deep-stacked and can wait for a better spot. Fold and preserve chips.

Pro opens to 2.5x from UTG in 4-handed play, which is typically a reasonably wide range. Getting a good price in the big blind (150 to win 400) and 9♠8♦ has decent playability and connectivity, so we should defend rather than fold. Offsuit makes it a poor 3-bet candidate OOP versus an early-position open, so calling keeps weaker hands in and avoids inflating the pot out of position.

We defended pre with a suited/connected-ish hand, but this flop (T♠-4♣-3♦ rainbow) heavily favors the UTG raiser’s range (overpairs, Tx, broadways). With 9♥8♦ we have no pair and essentially no real draw (only very weak backdoor runouts), so leading doesn’t make sense and check-raising would be a low-equity punt. Best is to check, evaluate sizing, and continue only versus very small bets or on favorable turns if checked through.

I hold bottom pair (Threes) with an Ace kicker on a T-4-3 rainbow board. As the pre-flop aggressor in position, I have the option to c-bet, but this board texture connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, straight draws, Tx). My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable; betting risks getting raised or called by better hands, while folding out hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets or call a bet on the turn comfortably. I also have backdoor flush and straight possibilities. Thus, checking is the most stable low-variance play.

Pro’s flop check-back on T-4-3r caps his range a bit and leaves a lot of unpaired broadways/medium hands that don’t want to build a pot. The turn 2♣ shifts range advantage toward the BB: I can credibly have 2x, 22, 43/32, and other low connected holdings that Pro rarely has from an UTG open. With 9h8d I have essentially no showdown value and don’t want to give Pro a free chance to stab in position, so I’ll use this card to apply pressure with a small-to-mid donk that should fold out his air and some marginal holdings. If called, I’ll mostly shut down unless we improve or the river is a strong barrel card (notably a 5 or board pair that favors my range).

I hold a pair of Threes with an Ace kicker and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel on a T-4-3-2 board. After I checked back the flop, the opponent's turn lead (probe bet) targets my perceived weakness. While the 2c completes some straights, my hand has showdown value against bluffs and draws (like flush draws). I also have outs to improve (5 gives a straight, Ace/3 gives two pair/trips). The pot odds are approximately 29%, and my combined equity from holding the best hand currently plus drawing to a winner justifies a call.

We have 9-high with no showdown value, so checking is essentially conceding unless Pro randomly has complete air and checks back (unlikely given his turn call). The river 5♠ is a strong range card for the BB after we donk the 2♣ turn: we credibly have plenty of 6x (23456), Ax (A2345), and some two-pair/sets from defending preflop and taking this line. Pro’s turn call can still contain a lot of one-pair Tx and medium pairs that are now under maximum pressure on a 2-3-4-5-T board. I’ll fire a polar, near-pot bluff to fold out those bluff-catchers; if raised, we trivially fold.

On the river, the 5s puts a four-card straight on the board (2-3-4-5). Holding the Ace of Hearts, I have completed the Wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5). While any 6 makes a higher straight (2-3-4-5-6) and is certainly in the Big Blind's range, the opponent's pot-sized lead is polarizing. They could be valuing a 6, chopping with an Ace, or bluffing. Folding a straight in a 4-handed scenario against a wide blind defense is too exploitable. Raising achieves nothing as I only get called by better hands (6x) or chops. The correct play is to call to catch bluffs and chop with other Aces.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o) is a marginal hand to open from under-the-gun (UTG). While it's technically within a standard 4-max opening range, the table dynamics make this a fold. Minni, the chip leader, is on the button and has shown high aggression, and the two players in the blinds (FiveTwo and Pro) have relatively short stacks (43 BB and 82 BB respectively) which may prompt them to shove over an opening raise. Given my healthy stack of 104 BB and the weak post-flop playability of A2o, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have A♣ K♠ on the button — the best unpaired starting hand. With only the blinds left to act (SB FiveTwo 43bb, BB Pro 82bb) this is a standard open-raise spot. I want to maximize fold equity and get heads-up or value when called while keeping postflop maneuverability, so I’ll raise to 400 (4x the big blind). That sizing charges the blinds’ wide defending ranges, keeps pots manageable vs deeper stacks, and gives a clear plan: continuation-bet favorable flops, proceed cautiously on highly coordinated boards, and call an effective shove from the SB (AKo is often flipping or favorite vs shove ranges). If I face a large 3-bet from Pro I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and player tendencies, but opening to 400 is the +EV play here.

Button opened large to 4bb and we’re in the small blind, out of position, with a hand that’s frequently dominated when called (K8o) and doesn’t realize equity well. Flatting invites the big blind in and puts us in tough multiway spots; 3-bet bluffing into the chip leader is high variance given they’ve shown willingness to continue in big pots. With ~43bb, this is a low-EV defend—better to preserve stack for stronger SB 3-bets/defends.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 3c, a very weak hand (bottom 10% of hands). Minni on the Button has raised to 400 (4BB), which is a large sizing compared to a standard 2.5x-3x open. The pot is currently 550 chips, and I must call 300 more to see a flop. This offers me pot odds of roughly 1.83:1, requiring about 35% equity to call profitably. 93o performs poorly even against a wide button range, likely having less than 30% equity, and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the poor hand strength, the large raise size, and the positional disadvantage, folding is the correct play.

UTG in a 4‑handed game with pocket 7s is a clear open — good equity, set value, and a hand you want heads‑up or HU against the shorter button. Limping or just calling (100) invites multiway action and makes postflop play harder. Standard open here is ~3x the BB to build value and apply pressure to the Button (43 BB) and the blinds. I’ll raise to 300 total to take the initiative. If I face a small 3‑bet I can continue or call for set‑mining depending on size; I’ll fold to an enormous 3‑bet/shove unless price is correct.

4-handed, UTG is effectively the cutoff and Minni has been opening wide and applying pressure. On the button with Ks9c we’re a bit dominated versus a tight range, but in position with a good price (300 to win 450 preflop, plus implied odds) this is a reasonable defend. I don’t want to 3-bet bluff here: if called we’re often in dominated Kx spots and bloat a pot versus the chip leader while risking a sizable chunk of our 43bb stack. Calling keeps ranges wide, lets us realize equity with position, and we can comfortably fold if the blinds squeeze big.

I hold Pocket Queens (QQ) in the Small Blind, facing an UTG raise from Minni (300) and a call from FiveTwo on the Button (300). This is a prime spot for a squeeze play (3-bet). Flat calling would be suboptimal as it invites a multi-way pot where I would be playing out of position. I want to charge the opponents to see a flop, isolate the raiser or the caller, and build value with a premium hand. Given the raise and call, there is 750 in the pot. A standard out-of-position squeeze size is roughly 4x the original raise plus the dead money, or around pot-sized. Raising to 1200 is appropriate; it puts significant pressure on FiveTwo's 40BB stack and challenges the wide range of the chip leader, Minni.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9h4c. Minni opened from UTG, FiveTwo called on the Button, and Pro squeezed to 1200 from the Small Blind. This is an extremely weak hand to play against significant pre-flop aggression. Given the 3-bet from Pro and the potential for Minni or FiveTwo to re-raise or call, folding is the only viable option to preserve my stack.

I opened UTG to 300 with 7♣7♠ and the SB 3‑bet to 1,200 (pot 1,900; to call 900). 77 is a good hand to play heads‑up for set value, but calling here invites multiway action (the Button can still act) which sharply reduces implied odds. With my very large stack and only three opponents, I prefer to 4‑bet‑min to isolate and take the initiative: a raise to 2,100 pressures light 3‑bets and the Button, often wins the pot immediately, and if called I’ll be playing relatively in position vs the SB with a hand that has solid set value. I will respect a big 5‑bet/shove and fold if Pro commits a large overbet — I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal pair vs what looks like a very strong range. Therefore raise to 2,100 to isolate or take the pot now.

This is now a 4-bet pot (Minni 300 -> Pro 3-bet 1200 -> Minni 4-bet 2100) and we’re sandwiched with K9o. K9o performs very poorly versus any reasonable 4-bet range and is heavily dominated by the KQ/KJ/AK portions, and if we call we’ll have ~1,9k behind creating a tiny SPR where we’re often forced into committing chips with marginal top-pair or just folding after investing a huge chunk. There’s also a high risk Pro jams over the top, and we can’t profitably continue. A shove bluff isn’t attractive either: our blocker is weak (only a king), and both villains’ ranges after this action are strong enough that we get called too often. Best play is to cut losses and preserve the ~40bb stack for better spots.

I hold Pocket Queens in the Small Blind. The action has been intense: Minni (active chip leader) raised, FiveTwo called, I 3-bet to 1200, and Minni 4-bet to 2100. Minni is known to be extremely loose-aggressive, having previously played 39o for a massive pot. The 4-bet size is very small (min-click), which offers great odds to call, but playing OOP with an SPR of ~1.1 is tricky. Given Minni's maniacal tendencies, their range is not limited to KK+, and they could easily be clicking buttons with much weaker holdings or air. Flat calling invites difficult decisions on Ace or King high boards where I might get bluffed or out-drawn. Shoving (5-bet all-in) maximizes value against their wide range, denies equity to overcards, and simplifies the decision tree. Against this specific opponent, getting 70bb in pre-flop with QQ is a profitable play.

Fold. I opened UTG with 7♣7♠ and 4‑bet to 2,100 to isolate the SB after his 3‑bet to 1,200. Pro’s massive re‑raise to 7,025 (I must call 4,925 into a 9,525 pot) requires roughly 34% equity to make a call profitable (4,925 / (9,525+4,925) ≈ 34%). Versus a polarized 5‑bet/overbet range (AA/KK/QQ/AK ± rare bluffs) pocket sevens are well below that threshold. Calling would commit a large chunk out of position with a marginal hand and poor implied odds — I preserve my deep stack for better spots. Fold.

4-handed UTG plays much wider than full ring, and AQs is a clear value open that also blocks strong Ax/QQ+ continues. With ~40bb, raising (rather than limping) builds a pot we can win with initiative and keeps our range protected. I’ll use a standard-but-not-bloated open size that pressures blinds while limiting how awkward it gets versus a 3-bet given our stack depth.

The opponent FiveTwo opens to 2.5BB from UTG (effectively CO in 4-handed play). While I have the Button, Ace-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by an UTG opening range (AT+, pairs). Calling invites the blinds to squeeze or overcall, and the hand plays poorly post-flop. 3-betting is an option as a bluff since I hold an Ace blocker, but FiveTwo has a stack of ~38BB and might be committed or shove, forcing a fold. Given my healthy stack depth (>100BB), there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

I have 2d 6h in the Small Blind, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has opened for a raise from the UTG position in this 4-handed game. Given the lack of strength and the fact that I would be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 and I'm in the big blind with Td4h — a weak, unsuited hand and out of position. The pot is 400 and it's only 150 to call, which gives ~27% equity required; that's cheap enough to defend the blind heads-up versus a single raiser. I don't want to 3-bet bluff out of position with this holding, and FiveTwo's ~38BB stack means they still have a reasonable opening range. I will call the 150 (250 total committed) to see a flop and then fold to heavy aggression unless I hit a meaningful pair or draw; re-evaluate on the flop.

I have Td4h and flopped a small pair (4s on board) on a very dry J-4-3 texture. FiveTwo checked the flop, which either shows weakness or a slowplay; I'm out of position and this is a marginal made hand that wants to keep the pot controlled. I will check to induce action and realize my showdown equity cheaply — I plan to call reasonable c-bets (small-to-medium sizes) but fold to large, polarizing bets/raises that represent sets/top pair/overpairs. If checked to on the turn I'll re-evaluate with the turn card and opponent's stack dynamics.

Minni checks from the BB on a J-4-3 flop that we connect with extremely well: we have the nut flush draw (and two overcards), plus we retain a strong range advantage as the preflop raiser. A c-bet here applies immediate pressure to BB’s air/weak pairs, starts building a pot for when we improve, and keeps our line consistent with our value hands (AJ+, overpairs) on this texture. I’ll use a medium-small sizing to get folds without bloating the pot unnecessarily versus a big-stack opponent who can check-raise.

I defend here by calling 250. I flatted preflop with T♦4♥ and on this dry J♠-4♠-3♥ board I’ve paired the 4 — a marginal but legitimate showdown hand against a wide UTG c-bet. FiveTwo’s 250 into a ~800 pot is a small continuation bet, giving me ~23–25% pot odds to call, and it’s likely they’re stabbing with a fairly wide range rather than only sets/top pair. Raising out of position with bottom pair is unnecessarily thin and folding after defending the blind would be too tight. I’ll call to see a turn and re-evaluate: plan to check-call small-to-medium turn bets for value/bluff-catch, but fold to large, polarized aggression (or an all-in shove) unless I improve.

I will check. I have T♦4♥ — a small pair (fours) on an otherwise very dry J♠-4♠-3♥-9♦ board. FiveTwo opened UTG and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn; that line is consistent with either pot-control top pair/overpairs or a wide/medium c-bet that missed. Betting out-of-position with bottom pair here is thin for value (many better hands will still call; many worse hands will fold) and needlessly bloats the pot against a capable opponent with ~35BB. Pot control and realizing showdown equity is the higher-EV line: check to see a river, call reasonable river bets as a bluff-catcher, fold to large polarized aggression unless I improve, and re-evaluate on the river (look for spot to pressure given my large stack if FiveTwo shows further weakness).

Turn goes check to us on J♠-4♠-3♥-9♦ after BB check-calls flop. With A♠Q♠ we still have the nut flush draw plus two overcards, so we’re happy to semi-bluff: we can fold out a lot of BB’s flop floats/weak pairs (3x/4x/underpairs/some 9x) while building a pot for when we hit. Sizing should create real pressure but keep our stack geometry clean; ~2/3 pot sets up a near pot-sized river jam when called and denies equity to hands like T8/87/overcard-peel types.

I have T♦4♥ — bottom pair on a dry J♠–4♠–3♥–9♦ board, out of position vs an UTG opener who c‑bet the flop and now fires a modest turn bet (700 into a 1750 pot). Calling 700 requires ≈22% equity (700 to win ~3150), and my pair of fours has reasonable showdown value vs a fairly wide continuation range. Raising OOP with this marginal hand is thin and likely to commit me to an unfavorable large pot; folding now surrenders good pot odds. I will call to realize equity and keep the pot controlled — plan to fold to large, polarized river aggression if I don’t improve, but call small river bets or proceed if I pair up/improve.

I’m OOP with T♦4♥ on J♠–4♠–3♥–9♦–J♥: second pair (fours) on a river that paired the jack. FiveTwo opened UTG and followed a small flop c‑bet with a modest turn bet — a line that can be wide but also contains hands that beat me (any J, 9x, or pocket pairs >4). The river makes my hand much more vulnerable (any single jack now has trips), and betting thin for value here is unattractive: it will fold the worst hands I beat and get called (or shoved) by hands that dominate me. With the effective stack dynamics (FiveTwo is relatively short), a bet risks inducing a shove and a very uncomfortable call with only a small pair.

Pot control and realizing showdown equity is the higher‑EV line: check and let FiveTwo show whether he wants to bet. If he checks back I win when he missed; if he fires a small river bet I can call for pot odds as a bluff‑catcher; if he jams a large, polarized shove I’ll fold. I’ll check to get to showdown without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

River pairs the jack, we miss the nut flush and have only A-high. After BB check-calls flop and turn, a big portion of Minni’s range is Jx (now trips) plus some slow-played boats; those hands will happily check to induce and then call/jam over any river bet. While there are missed spade draws that we beat, holding A♠Q♠ blocks a lot of the natural missed-draw combos, so our fold equity on an all-in triple barrel is lower than it looks and the downside is tournament-ending. With no need to turn this specific blocker-heavy hand into a high-variance bluff, I’ll take the free showdown and win when Minni has missed draws.

Current hand is 43/150, 4-handed. I am UTG (effectively Cutoff) with A5o and a stack of 107BB. In a 4-handed game, the opening range widens, but A5o remains a marginal hand, typically at the very bottom of or outside a standard opening range. The table dynamics include an aggressive chip leader in the Small Blind (361BB) and a short-stacked Big Blind (27BB) who might be incentivized to shove over an open. A5o plays poorly post-flop and is easily dominated. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve the stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 4h 3h, which is a speculative low suited connector. While usually a candidate for an open in late position, I must consider the remaining players. Minni in the small blind has a massive chip lead and has shown significant aggression in previous hands. FiveTwo in the big blind is relatively short-stacked (~27 BB), making them prone to shoving over a standard open. Given my stack size and the risk of being forced to fold to a 3-bet or a shove, I will play conservatively and wait for a stronger opening hand.

I’m in the small blind with KcJc — a strong, playable hand heads-up. Button and UTG folded so it’s just me vs FiveTwo (short stack ~27bb). I want to take the initiative, isolate the short-stacked BB and deny them good odds to see a cheap flop. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) gives me fold equity, builds a pot when I’m likely ahead, and remains small enough that if FiveTwo shoves I can comfortably call for position/EV (KJs has decent equity vs a shove range). If called I’ll play postflop for value and leverage our postflop edge and deeper stack.

Facing a 3bb small-blind open with 27bb effective, 8♢5♣ offsuit is a low-playability defend OOP. Although the immediate pot odds are decent (200 to win 400), realization is poor versus Minni’s likely aggressive c-betting/barreling, and we’ll frequently make dominated one-pair hands with little ability to continue. With our stack size, preserving chips for higher-equity defend/3-bet spots is higher EV than taking a marginal, high-variance call here.

I am dealt pocket threes in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. While a small pair, in short-handed play, this is a reasonable opening hand to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially set-mine if called. My stack is healthy (over 100 BB), allowing for standard opening play. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take control of the pot early.

UTG (Flash) opened to 300 from early position — a fairly strong raising range. My hand 2♥7♥ is the worst suited hand: very low absolute strength, often dominated, and with almost no playability post‑flop. Calling 300 requires ~40% equity to break even heads‑up (and worse multiway), which 2♥7♥ does not have versus an UTG range. A 3‑bet bluff is unnecessarily high variance here with blinds still to act and stacks deep; I should conserve chips for better spots on the button. Fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Flash opens to 3x from UTG in a 4-handed game (still a relatively strong opening range). From the small blind we’ll be out of position and frequently play a multiway pot with Pro in the big blind, which reduces our equity realization. 9♠2♠ is too weak to defend for 2.5bb more at this stack depth, and it’s not a viable 3-bet (too low equity, poor blockers, and 27bb is too deep to profitably jam). Best is to preserve the 27bb stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit, a very weak hand. The UTG player (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb). Even though I am closing the action and getting reasonable pot odds (calling 200 to win 450), T2o performs very poorly against an UTG opening range and will be difficult to play out of position. The hand is not strong enough to defend. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

UtG in a 4‑handed game with Js6d offsuit — a very weak, easily‑dominated holding. I’d be out of position against the Button, SB and BB; calling 100 to see a flop invites a multiway, OOP pot with poor equity and many hands that dominate me. A min‑raise to 200 to attempt a steal is thin — both the Button (2,691) and the blinds (10k+ stacks) can easily call or 3‑bet and put me in a tough spot. With a healthy stack (36,284) there’s no need to gamble here; preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

4-handed and folded to the button, Q7o is strong enough to open as a steal. With ~27bb, I want to apply pressure and pick up blinds/antes (dead money) while keeping the sizing small to reduce loss when the blinds 3-bet and to keep my range wide. Limping gives up initiative and lets the big stacks realize equity too easily. If I face a sizable 3-bet, I can comfortably fold this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with A2o facing a Button raise from FiveTwo who has a relatively short stack (~25BB). Although I am getting reasonable pot odds to call, A2o plays poorly out of position and is often dominated by the opener's Ax range. Furthermore, 3-betting as a bluff is risky against a 25BB stack that might shove light. Conserving chips and avoiding marginal spots out of position is the prudent play.

I hold Ace-Queen of clubs in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a 4-handed game. FiveTwo, the button, has raised to 225. FiveTwo has a relatively short stack of approximately 25 big blinds. I will 3-bet to 750 for value. This size exerts significant pressure on a 25 BB stack and is a clear value raise against a button opening range. If FiveTwo chooses to shove, I have a comfortable call given the strength of my hand and the pot odds.

Flash’s 3-bet size (to 750) gives a tempting price in position, but Q7o is heavily dominated by the value and semi-value hands that make up even a fairly light BB 3-bet range (AQ/KQ/QJ/QT, better suited queens, broadways). With ~25bb effective, calling creates a low SPR spot where we’ll too often make second-best top pair or be forced to fold postflop after investing more. It’s also far too weak to 4-bet (shove or small) as a bluff given removal and equity. Preserve the stack and move on to better defend/4-bet candidates.

4-handed but still first to act with ~25bb, Js3o is too weak and dominated. Limping creates tough multiway spots out of position versus bigger stacks, and opening risks getting 3-bet/jammed and having to fold equity too often. Preserve stack and wait for better open/steal candidates.

I am on the Button with 6c 7h (off-suit connector). While the Button is a strong position to steal blinds, 67o is generally below the standard opening threshold, often being a fold. The Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has been active, increasing the risk of a 3-bet or a difficult defense to navigate. Given my healthy stack of over 100 BBs, there is no need to force action with a marginal hand. Folding is the prudent long-term play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd Qh. Both the UTG and Button have folded, leaving me heads-up against Minni in the Big Blind. Kd Qh is a premium hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. I will raise to 350 to take control of the pot and build value, though I must be wary of Minni's large stack and aggressive history.

I’m in the big blind with Q♦4♦ facing a 350 SB open (pot 450, 250 to call). With deep stacks (I have 36k) and only the SB in action (button folded), this is a clear defend spot. Q4s is marginal but has backdoor flush/straight potential and reasonable equity vs a likely wide SB stealing range. Pot odds for a call (~250 to win ~700) are ~3.8:1 (need ~21% equity) — I clear that. 3-betting to 600 would be thin with this holding and unnecessarily bloats the pot; folding is too tight. I’ll call 250 (total 350) to see a flop, using my positional advantage post-flop to realize equity and control the pot (c-bet or check/raise lines depending on the board).

On a paired board of 5s 5d 3d, I have King-high with two overcards. This board generally favors the pre-flop aggressor as it is less likely the Big Blind has many fives in their defending range. I will place a continuation bet of 350, which is half the pot, to represent a strong hand and take down the pot immediately or define Minni's range. If she calls, I will re-evaluate based on the turn card.

Call 350. I have Q♦4♦ on 5♠-5♦-3♦: a Q-high diamond flush draw (9 outs). The pot is 1,050 and calling 350 gives me ~25% break-even equity, while my flush draw (plus the chance to pair the Q) has roughly ~35% to hit by the river — calling is +EV. Flash’s bet is small (likely a wide c-betting range), so I prefer to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable out of position rather than bloating it with a thin raise. Plan: see the turn — if I hit a diamond I’ll extract value or apply pressure; if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously (check/call or fold to heavy action depending on villain’s line).

I have turned top pair with a King kicker on a board of 5s-5d-3d-Qc. This is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle. I'm ahead of most of Minni's range, which includes middle pairs (66-JJ) and diamond draws. I will bet 800 (approximately 57% of the pot) to extract value and protect against the flush draw. If Minni has a 5, I am in trouble, but the Queen is a great card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor.

Board: 5♠ 5♦ 3♦ Q♣; my hand Q♦4♦. The turn pairs the board with a queen, so I now have top pair (Qx) with a weak 4-kicker plus a four-card diamond combo (two diamonds in my hand and two on the flop) — I still have 9 outs to a flush. Flash opened preflop, c-bet the paired flop and now barrels the turn; his range is wide (overcards, Qx, medium pairs, occasional 5x/trips and some bluffs/draws). The pot is 2,200 and he bets 800; calling 800 into 2,200 requires roughly 800/(2200+800) ≈ 26.7% equity to break even. My hand (top pair + diamond outs) has reasonable equity vs a continuing range and is often ahead of pure bluffs and small pairs. Raising here is thin — I will often get called by better Qx or 5x and won’t gain much fold equity — so the prudent, +EV line is to call and keep the pot controllable. Plan for the river: if I hit a diamond I will extract value; if the river is a blank and Flash fires a big, polarized shove I will fold to strong river aggression (since trips/strong Qx are plausible); if he checks or bets small I will call or raise for value depending on the card. Call 800 to see the river and re-evaluate.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a 5-5-3-Q-9 board. The river 9 is largely a blank, as the diamond flush draw missed. Throughout the hand, I have been the aggressor and Minni has called every bet. Her range likely consists of weaker Queens (QJ, QT), middle pairs (66-JJ) that didn't believe my flop bet, or perhaps a slow-played 5. Given the blind-vs-blind dynamic, my top pair is very strong. I will bet 1500, which is half the pot, to extract value from her 'sticky' calling range. If she raises, I will have to carefully consider if she's been slow-playing trips, but a bet remains the best way to get value from her likely holdings.

Call 1500. The river bet is small (1,500 into a 4,500 pot) — I’m getting ~25% pot‑odds (1500/(4500+1500)=25%). I have top pair (Q♦) with a weak 4 kicker; that’s vulnerable versus big Qx and trips with a five, but it still has reasonable showdown equity against the wide, small‑ball line Flash has taken (preflop open, c‑bet flop, turn barrel, small river stab). That line contains a mix of value (Qx, occasional 5x/trips, some two‑pair) and bluffs/missed diamond draws or overcards. I also block some strong queen/diamond combos by holding Q♦ and two diamonds, which reduces certain two‑pair and diamond‑bluff combinations. Raising here is thin — I’ll get called by better queens and often run into trips or shove‑raises — while folding gives up value to bluffs and medium pairs. So I’ll keep the pot controlled and call to reach showdown (and fold only to a large shove or very polarizing action).

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with J4s. While suited hands have some potential, J4 is disconnected and weak. Opening from first position with such a weak hand is generally unprofitable, especially with a deep-stacked aggressive player in the small blind and a short stack in the big blind who might shove. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with 7c 4d, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is a potential stealing position, Minni (SB) has a massive stack and has shown aggression throughout the match, and FiveTwo (BB) is quite short-stacked and likely to defend or jam. Given the low quality of my hand and the risk of being shoved on by the short stack or called by the big stack, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my healthy chip count.

Short summary: I have 7♠8♠ in the small blind and it’s heads-up against a short-stacked big blind (~2,366 chips ≈ 23.7bb). A small raise to the minimum (200 total) maximizes fold equity vs a wide short-stack pushing/folding range, isolates the short stack, and keeps the pot controllable. If he folds I scoop the 150-chip pot; if he calls I’m playing a strong suited connector heads-up; if he shoves I have reasonable equity and acceptable pot odds to call. Raise to 200 to apply pressure and extract value from a short stack.

Minni is opening very wide from the small blind and we’re getting a great price (100 to win 300). JTo has solid equity vs a steal range and plays well postflop in position?—we’re out of position, but still have enough stack depth (~24bb) to realize equity and avoid turning a marginal hand into a high-variance 3-bet shove that gets called by dominating hands (AJ/KJ/QJ/AT+). Flatting keeps weaker hands in and lets us navigate flops cheaply.

Flop: 4h 3h 9h; I hold 7s8s. I have no made hand and no heart flush — only a very weak backdoor straight (need both turn and river) and 6 outs to pair by the river (~24% to hit at least a pair). The BB is short-stacked and called my small preflop raise, so his defending range is heavy in hearts and small pairs; this texture disfavors a continuation bet because my fold equity is low and I’m frequently up against made hands (a flush or a 9/pair). Pot control is important — I don’t want to build a big pot with essentially air. I’ll check to keep the pot small, allow bluffs to show themselves, and call small bets to realize equity, but fold to large shoves since calling an all‑in (~2266) only needs ~17.6% equity to breakeven and I’m usually well behind. If he checks back I can see a free turn and re-evaluate; if he bets small I’ll call cautiously; if he jams I’ll fold.

Minni’s SB min-open is very wide and her flop check on a monotone board signals a lot of air / give-ups (broadways without a heart, random offsuit junk). With J-T no heart we don’t have a great hand to face pressure, but we do have two overcards and a reasonable stab candidate: a small bet should fold out a big chunk of her range and denies equity to her overcards that checked behind. I’ll use a small sizing to keep risk low; if she raises, we can comfortably fold given we have no heart/real draw.

I checked the flop to keep the pot small with air. Board 4♥‑3♥‑9♥ and I hold 7♠8♠ — no hearts, no pair, only backdoor straight possibilities and 6 outs to hit a pair by the river (~24% two‑card equity). FiveTwo is short-stacked and defended wide preflop, so his 150 c‑bet can be a small value bet, a blocker bet with a heart, or a bluff. Calling 150 into a 550 pot gives ~21.4% pot odds (150/(550+150)), which is roughly in line with my ~24% chance to hit by river, and calling preserves pot control while allowing worse hands/bluffs to continue. I won’t inflate the pot with a raise from air; if he jams his short stack on top of this bet I will fold because I’m usually behind vs a made heart or pair. Action: call 150 to realize equity and keep the pot manageable.

I hold 7♠8♠ and the turn brings 8♥, so I’ve just made middle pair (8s) with a 7 kicker. The problem is the board is extremely wet for me: 4♥‑3♥‑9♥‑8♥ — any heart in FiveTwo’s wide defended range is already a flush. He bet small on the flop, which is consistent with lots of hearts, small pairs or thin value bets from a short stack. My hand is vulnerable to flushes and to any 9x or better — not a hand I want to build a big pot with. There is currently no bet to call, so the correct line is pot control: check and let him commit bluffs or show aggression first. If he makes a small turn bet I can call to realize equity and see a river; if he jams his short stack I will fold because calling that shove (~2.1k) would require roughly ~43% equity vs a flush‑heavy range, which I don’t have. If he checks back I get a free river to re-evaluate. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and avoid getting bloated by flushes.

Turn brings the 8h making a 4-heart board. With JcTs we have no heart and only an open-ender (7/Q), so our showdown value is essentially zero and our bluff story is weak because Minni’s flop check/call on a monotone board heavily weights her toward having at least one heart. On this texture, many players will also check a made flush to induce, and if we bet we risk getting called often (or check-raised) with very little fold equity. Taking the free card maximizes our equity realization and keeps the pot manageable with our ~18bb stack, while preserving the option to value/bluff on favorable rivers (7/Q non-heart, or board-pairing runouts that reduce her single-heart confidence).

Check. On the river I only have second pair (8s) with a 7 kicker on a very hostile board: 4h-3h-9h-8h-Tc. There are four hearts on the board, so any heart in FiveTwo’s wide defending range is an automatic flush; he called preflop, made a small flop bet and then checked the turn, which is consistent with a lot of hearts, small pairs, or thin value hands. My hand is a vulnerable showdown catcher — it wins vs some weak pairs and missed hands but loses to flushes, 9x, tens, and any trips/sets. With the pot only 700 and me having a very deep stack compared to FiveTwo, the prudent play is pot control: check and take it to showdown rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand. If he bets small I can call to beat bluffs/weak pairs; if he jams a large portion of his short stack I’ll fold. So I check to avoid getting exploited and to let stronger holdings or bluffs show themselves.

River comes Tc on a 4-heart board. With JcTs we have a pair of tens but no heart, so we’re either comfortably ahead of her non-heart give-ups/pairs, or drawing dead vs any single-heart hand (a flush). Given Minni’s flop check/call, her range is still heavily weighted toward having at least one heart, and on this texture worse hands (9x/8x/4x without a heart, random air) will often fold to even a small bet because the flush is so obvious. So betting is a classic reverse-thin-value spot: we mostly get called by better and fold out worse. Best play is to take the free showdown and realize our equity.

I am dealt 8s 2d in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands and offers no significant potential for straights or flushes. With a short stack like FiveTwo in the blinds who might be looking for a spot to shove, there is no reason to attempt a bluff or enter the pot with such a poor hand. Folding is the only sensible move.

Button with A♠5♠ (offsuit A5). In 4-handed play the button open-range should be wide — A5o is a clear open to seize initiative and leverage positional advantage. A 3x standard raise to 300 (total) applies pressure to the small blind and big blind, reduces multiway chaos, and isolates one opponent more often. FiveTwo (SB) is relatively short (2,766) and may shove; that is fine — we get good pot odds and A5 has reasonable equity vs a wide shove. If Pro (BB) 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to heavy 3-bets given the weak kicker), but I’m happy to open and play postflop in position with initiative. Therefore I raise to 300 to maximize fold equity and playability.

Button open is likely wide 4-handed, and pocket 2s has enough raw equity plus strong implied odds when we flop a set versus Minni’s deep stack. 3-bet/folding would risk a large chunk of our 27bb stack with a hand that doesn’t want to play a bloated pot OOP, and a shove needs unrealistic fold equity. I’ll flat to keep the pot manageable, accept that BB can squeeze (we fold to big pressure), and look to realize equity/stack them when we hit.

I am in the Big Blind with K3o. The Button raised to 3bb and the Small Blind called. The pot odds are roughly 22% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 900). While K3o has raw equity close to this number against wide ranges, my equity realization will be poor playing out of position in a multi-way pot with a weak offsuit king. I suffer from reverse implied odds (domination) and lack playability. It is a disciplined fold despite the price.

OOP in a single-raised pot versus a wide button range, this flop heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (lots of overpairs/top pairs) and our 22 has very poor equity and realization unless we spike a deuce. There’s no profitable lead here and no need to inflate the pot with an underpair. I’ll check my entire range and be prepared to fold to most meaningful c-bets, only considering continuing versus very small sizing if I think I can realize some showdown/bluff-catch equity.

I raised from the button with A5 and the flop (9♦ T♣ 6♠) is very dry and misses most of both ranges. FiveTwo (SB) defended preflop with a relatively wide, somewhat short-stack calling range — many of those hands missed this flop and can be folded by a c-bet. I have decent showdown value (ace‑high) and a blocker to some Ax combos, so a standard continuation bet is the exploitative play. I’ll bet about 300 chips (~40–45% pot) to apply pressure and keep the pot controllable if called. If he jams all‑in for his ~2,500 remaining chips I will fold because A5 has poor equity versus a shove range that will be rich in pairs and tens; if he simply calls I’ll use position to reevaluate on the turn and either barrel favorable turn cards or pot-control/check back if we don’t improve.

Minni’s 30% pot c-bet is likely very wide, but with 22 on 9-T-6 rainbow we have almost no equity, no overcards, and essentially no backdoor draws. Calling 300 to “see one” is mostly lighting chips on fire because (1) we rarely improve (only 2 outs), (2) we’re out of position with a low SPR, so we won’t realize much of whatever thin showdown value we have, and (3) Minni can comfortably barrel turns and force us off the hand. Check-raising/shoving needs unrealistic fold equity. Best is to preserve the remaining 25bb+ stack and wait for a better spot.

Kc6d offsuit is too weak to open UTG in this 4-handed spot. With two players to act behind — a short-stacked Button who can easily shove and a deep-stacked big blind who defends wide — calling or min-raising will likely put me out of position in a multiway pot or force tough decisions against aggression. The 100-chip call is inexpensive but the hand has poor postflop playability and is often dominated. Preserve the healthy stack and wait for a better opportunity — fold.

4-handed and folded to on the Button, we should be opening a wide range to pick up blinds and play in position. Th4o is weak and won’t realize equity well in bloated pots, so I prefer a small steal size that keeps risk down while still applying pressure. If either blind 3-bets, we can comfortably fold at this stack depth (≈25bb) rather than defend a dominated, low-playability hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ts7s, facing a 2.25x raise from the Button (FiveTwo). While T7s has some playability, defending from the Small Blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. Additionally, the Big Blind (Flash) has a large stack and is yet to act, increasing the risk of a squeeze. FiveTwo has a short stack (23BB), making implied odds less attractive. Given the marginal hand strength and poor position, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 3s, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has raised to 225 from the button. Although the pot odds are roughly 3:1 to call (125 to win a total pot of 500), 83o is a difficult hand to play out of position and has poor equity realization. Given that FiveTwo is a relatively short stack, I would rather not bleed chips with junk hands and will wait for a better opportunity to engage.

4-handed ranges are wider, but 5-3 offsuit is still too weak from UTG—poor playability, dominated frequently, and it invites isolation from the deeper stacks, putting my 26.6bb stack in awkward spots. With no incentive to defend my UTG position by limping, the best stack-preserving, EV-maximizing decision is to fold and wait for a better open/steal spot.

In a 4-handed game, K4s is a marginal opening hand from the Button. While it is often a standard raise to steal the blinds, the Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader with over 300BB and has shown significant aggression and willingness to defend. Opening a weak King into a loose-aggressive massive stack creates a high-variance spot where I am often dominated or forced to fold to a 3-bet. With a healthy 100BB stack, I can wait for better opportunities to engage the chip leader rather than playing a weak kicker out of position if the SB calls or in a bloated pot against the BB.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The action has folded to me. I have a stack of 13525 chips. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the blinds immediately. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard sizing in this position.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind with Ad9d in the big blind. The pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 (total 300) — I’m getting ~3:1 pot odds, so I only need ~25% equity to justify a call. A9o has useful blockers (the Ace) and performs well versus a typical SB opening range. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by making a small 3-bet; a large 3-bet is an option in some spots but isn’t necessary here. I’ll call to realize equity, keep weaker hands in, and see a flop where I can play top-pair hands aggressively or exercise pot control on bad boards. If the flop is favorable (Ace or dry board) I’ll continue for value; if it’s very wet or I face heavy aggression I’ll fold or pot-control as appropriate.

I have middle pair (fours) with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry flop. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni has checked to me, a continuation bet is standard to protect my hand and extract value from straight draws like 67 or 78, or even Ace-high hands. A half-pot bet of 300 should help define where I stand in the hand.

I have top pair (9s) with the best kicker (A) on a very dry flop (5h 4d 9c). Villain was the preflop opener and made a small 1/3-pot continuation bet (300 into a 900 pot) — that sizing usually represents a wide, top-heavy c-bet range and gives me good fold equity and value-raising opportunities. Out of position I prefer to charge draws and extract thin value from worse pairs (weaker 9x, 5x, 4x) and A-x hands rather than simply check-calling and letting villain realize equity for cheap. I’ll raise to 900 total (a ~3x the c-bet), which is large enough to charge backdoor draws and get value from worse hands while keeping stronger hands’ bluffs and thin calls in range. Plan: if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn — bet for value on favorable cards (A, blank) and control the pot or fold to heavy aggression if he shows strength (check-raise or large barrel), since a check-raise often denotes sets/two-pair in this spot.

I am facing a raise from the Big Blind on a relatively dry 9-5-4 rainbow board after my continuation bet. While I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker, this hand is extremely vulnerable and difficult to play out of position against an aggressive opponent like Minni, who has a significant chip lead. Her raise represents a range of hands that likely has me beat or has significant equity against me. Given the need to preserve my stack for the remaining hands in the tournament, calling a raise with only bottom pair here is a low-expectation play. I will fold and look for a more profitable situation.

At a 4-handed table, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff. ATo (Ace-Ten offsuit) is a standard opening hand from the Cutoff, whereas it might be a fold from UTG in a 6-max or 9-handed game (as seen in Hand #15 where I folded it). With 100BB effective stacks for most and a short stack in the Big Blind (26BB), a standard open raise is the best play to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds (though out of position against the button). I will raise to 225, a slightly smaller size often used by pros to risk less while maintaining fold equity.

I am on the button with 9c 7d, which is a weak hand. Pro has opened for 225 from UTG. While I have position, 97o does not play well against an UTG opening range, and with FiveTwo sitting on a short stack of about 25BB in the Big Blind, there is a significant risk of being squeezed or facing a shove. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I’m in the small blind with K♣8♣ facing a UTG open to 225 (pot 375, I must put in 175 more). K8s is playable but marginal to 3-bet out of position — 3-betting invites the short stack (FiveTwo, 2,566) to shove or the opener to 4-bet, and a minimum raise to 350 won’t buy enough fold equity. Calling is cheap (175 into a ~550 pot after my call; ~32% break-even equity) and preserves flexibility. Plan: see the flop and continue with a king, pair, or club draw; fold to strong, committed aggression if I miss. Therefore I’ll call and keep the pot manageable.

We’re getting a very good price (125 to win 550) and can realize equity multiway with a pocket pair. With ~25BB effective for us, a squeeze/3-bet would commit too much of our stack with a hand that performs poorly when called, while calling keeps the pot manageable and lets us play for a set or favorable textures.

I called preflop with K♣8♣ intending to see a flop and only continue with a king, a pair, or a club draw. The flop 3h‑5h‑6h is a wet, monotone heart board that completely misses my clubs and gives strong equity to any heart, small paired hands or sets from the opener/BB. With two opponents and no backdoor outs for me, a c‑bet would be a thin bluff likely to be called by the short stack or to run into a hero call/raise from the preflop raiser. I’ll check to keep the pot small and preserve my stack — folding to committed aggression, and potentially looking for a better spot to take the pot on later streets if checked to.

Monotone low flop (3h5h6h) multiway is very likely to connect with at least one player’s calling range via a heart. With 44 we have an open-ender (2/7) plus a small pair, but no heart to continue confidently versus aggression. Leading here is mostly a bluff into two opponents (including the UTG raiser still to act) and risks getting raised off our equity by made flushes or strong AhXh holdings. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets us realize our equity when checked through, and allows a profitable check-call versus reasonable sizing given our draw and price.

I am in position (relative to the blinds) in a multi-way pot on a monotone board [3h, 5h, 6h]. I hold [Ac, Td], giving me Ace-high with no heart. This texture heavily favors the defending ranges of the Small Blind and Big Blind, who are more likely to have small pairs, two pair, straights, or made flushes. C-betting into two opponents on this texture with no equity (no flush draw, no straight draw) is a losing play; I will only get called by better hands or strong draws, and I fold out hands I am already beating. Checking back is the standard line to control the pot and re-evaluate on the turn.

Preflop I called with Kc8c intending to see a flop and only continue with a king, a pair, or a club draw. The flop (3h-5h-6h) completely missed my hand — it’s a very wet heart board that favors any heart combinations (any two hearts already have a flush) and small pairs/sets from the raiser. I checked the flop to control the pot. The turn (2♦) is a blank for me: I still have no pair, no heart and no realistic backdoor. This is a three‑way pot and the short stack (FiveTwo) can shove, so bluffing here is thin and high‑variance. With a large stack and no showdown value, I’ll check to keep the pot small and preserve chips; fold or concede to meaningful aggression and re‑evaluate on the river if checked to.

Turn gives us a made straight (2-3-4-5-6). With both 4s in hand, opponents can’t have any straight, so the only real hand that beats us is a heart flush. Since the flop checked through multiway, there’s a good chance we can now extract value from overpairs/one-heart hands and smaller pairs while also forcing flushes to reveal themselves via a raise. I’ll use a small-to-medium donk size to get called by non-flushes and to cheaply fold if we face significant aggression (we have no outs versus a made flush).

On the turn, the board is [3h, 5h, 6h, 2d]. I hold AcTd (Ace-high). The pot is 675. FiveTwo (Big Blind) bets 300 into the pot. I checked the flop, showing weakness. The board is very coordinated with a possible flush and a 4-card straight on the board (2-3-5-6, needing a 4 for the straight). FiveTwo's lead often indicates a piece of the board (pair, straight, or flush) or a semi-bluff with a heart. With only Ace-high, no draw to the flush or straight, and another player (Minni) still left to act behind me, I have very low equity and no reason to continue. This is a clear fold.

Situation: heads‑up vs the short stack (FiveTwo) on the turn holding K♣8♣ on 3♥‑5♥‑6♥‑2♦. Pot 975, I must call 300. Evaluation: the board is very dangerous (three hearts) and my hand has no pair, no heart, no realistic backdoor — so I have little intrinsic showdown value. However the turn bet is small relative to the pot (300/(975+300) ≈ 23.5% break‑even) and FiveTwo is short‑stacked, so he can be betting wide to probe/steal. Raising here is unattractive: I have almost no fold equity, I’d simply invite an all‑in from a 2k stack, and I have no strong blockers or improvement to justify building the pot. Folding is too weak given how cheap the call is and how wide his turn range can be. Plan: make the inexpensive call to see the river and keep the pot controllable. If he shows heavy action (a shove or a large river shove) and I haven’t improved I’ll fold — only continue for value if I pair or the river meaningfully changes the read. Action: call 300.

Check. The river (3h‑5h‑6h‑2d‑3d) pairs the trey and massively improves many hands — any 3 is now trips, any 5/6 becomes two pair, and flushes were already live on the flop. I hold K♣8♣ with no hearts and no pair — effectively zero showdown value and no meaningful blockers. Betting here simply invites a short‑stack shove (FiveTwo has ~2,141) or a call from hands that already beat me; I have almost no fold equity and would be committing chips with a losing hand. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets him bluff‑shove so I can fold, and avoids unnecessary loss when my hand is so weak. If he checks back I realize a tiny showdown equity; if he bets I will fold to heavy action (shove) and consider a call only to light pressure-sized bets, but I won't build the pot.

River pairs the 3, which is a big relative hand-strength shift: our 6-high straight now loses not only to any made heart flush (2 hearts in hand) but also to any 3x that improved to trips, plus boats from slowplayed sets (66/55/22/33). Minni’s turn call range contains a lot of these hands, and her river check can easily be a trap/pot-control with a flush or trips on a paired board. Betting only gains extra chips when called by worse; if she folds, we win the same amount as checking, and if she check-raises we must fold and burn chips. With position and a decent showdown hand, the highest-EV/lowest-variance line is to take the free showdown.

I am holding 3s 8c in the UTG position. This is an extremely weak hand and does not justify an opening raise or a call from early position with three players left to act. Standard strategy dictates folding such junk hands to preserve chips.

On the button with 6♣T♠ — a marginal but perfectly playable hand in position. Only the blinds are in (pot 150) and both opponents have shown willingness to fold/defend; I want to seize initiative and apply fold equity rather than limp into a multiway pot. A standard 3x open to 300 sizes up pressure on the short(er) SB (33 BB) while keeping the pot manageable against Pro (101 BB) who can 3-bet. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet or shove; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and realize equity when I connect, folding to significant resistance when I miss.

Button is opening wide 4-handed and we’re in the SB with 88 at ~34bb. Flat-calling invites the BB to overcall/squeeze and leaves us playing a tougher, higher-SPR pot out of position. A 3-bet both isolates the opener and creates a low SPR where 88 can comfortably play for stacks on many flops (and we deny equity to overcards). If Minni 4-bets jams, we’re generally priced in with a hand this strong versus a typical aggressive button reshove range.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s6s. The Button (Minni) opened to 300, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 900. FiveTwo is short-stacked with only ~1,600 behind, meaning implied odds are very poor. Additionally, Minni is still left to act behind me and could 4-bet. My hand is a weak suited connector that requires high implied odds and multi-way action to be profitable, neither of which are favorable here given the stack depths and the 3-bet pricing. Cold calling a 3-bet with 64s out of position is a losing play.

I opened to 300 on the button with T♠6♣ and FiveTwo (SB) 3-bet to 900. T6o is marginal but plays well in position; the 3-bet size is relatively small and FiveTwo is fairly short (~25 BB), so they can have a wide, sometimes light 3-betting range. Calling 600 more (900 total committed) keeps the pot manageable, preserves my postflop positional advantage, and lets me realize equity or apply pressure on favorable flops. I don’t want to 4-bet-bluff this hand and folding here would be too tight given pot odds and position. Plan: call, use position to c-bet favorable textures, and fold to significant resistance or a large 4-bet/shove from the SB.

We 3-bet pre and flop an overpair (88) on a very low board in a 3-bet pot. With only ~2516 behind into 1900 (SPR ~1.3), we’re effectively committed and want to deny equity to Minni’s wide button range that contains lots of overcards, gutshots, and club draws. Jamming maximizes protection and still gets called by many 5x/33/44/66-77 and strong draws that have the right price, while simplifying the hand out of position.

Fold. On T♠6♣ with a 2♣-3♣-5♠ flop I have only 7 clean outs (three sixes + four fours) — ~27.8% to improve by the river. Facing a 2,516 bet into a 4,416 pot requires about 36.3% equity to break even on a call (2,516 / (4,416+2,516)), so a call is -EV. FiveTwo’s line (preflop 3‑bet then a large flop raise) looks polarized toward value (sets, two‑pair, strong made hands or strong club combinations), and raising as a bluff from this holding is too risky and unnecessary given my deep stack. Preserve chips, fold, and wait for a better spot in position.

I have 3♠5♥ UTG — a very weak, offsuit, unconnected hand with poor playability and no blockers. I’m first to act facing a standard 100 to-call (min-raise to 200) and will be out of position against three opponents, some of whom have shown frequent aggression. Multiway pots drastically reduce the hand’s equity and there’s no reason to invest chips with this holding; I’ll preserve my large stack for a better spot.

Minni has folded and we’re on the button with position against the blinds. T8o isn’t a premium, but it’s well within a profitable button opening range 4-handed, especially with fold equity versus blinds and the benefit of initiative. With 44bb, we can apply pressure without committing too much; if we face a big 3-bet from the blinds we can comfortably fold. I’ll use a standard small steal size to keep risk controlled while still generating folds.

I am in the Small Blind with As4c facing a Button raise to 2.5bb from FiveTwo. A4o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, often dominated by better Aces or facing difficult decisions on connected boards. While 3-betting as a bluff is an option due to the Ace blocker, FiveTwo is the short stack (40bb) and may be willing to commit or call in position. Calling is unattractive due to the positional disadvantage. The standard and disciplined play is to fold and preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q♥ 8♠. The player on the Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. With the Small Blind folding, I only need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of roughly 3.6 to 1. Q8o is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a wide Button opening range, especially given these favorable odds. Although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the price is too good to fold. I will call and see a flop.

I have missed the flop completely with Queen-high and no real draws. The board (Kd-9h-7d) is quite favorable for a Button raising range, which often contains many Kings and high cards. Being out of position, there is no merit in leading out with a bet here. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor and most likely fold if they put in a bet, as I have very little equity to continue.

We’re the preflop raiser in position and flop comes K-9-7 with two diamonds. With T8o we have an open-ended straight draw (6/J outs) and can leverage fold equity versus BB’s whiffed overcards and weaker hands while building a pot for when we hit. A small c-bet keeps risk controlled, denies equity, and sets up turn barrels on good cards (6/J/A/Q or certain runouts) while allowing us to comfortably fold if we face a big check-raise.

I have completely missed this flop with Queen-high and no significant draws. While the bet from FiveTwo is small (less than 40% of the pot), I have no equity to continue in this hand out of position. I already checked with the intention of folding to aggression, and since I have no pair or even a back-door flush draw, the most disciplined play is to fold and conserve my chips.

4-handed UTG still needs discipline. 8♠3♠ is too weak to open profitably into competent, deep-stacked blinds (especially Minni as big stack who can 3-bet and pressure us). Calling is not an option preflop, and an open risks getting played back at with a hand that realizes equity poorly. Preserve stack for better steal spots and stronger suited/gapped connectors.

I am on the Button with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o). In a 4-handed game, this hand is strong enough to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds, especially when folded to. My position allows me to control the pot post-flop if called. A standard raise size of 2.5BB (250 chips) is appropriate to apply pressure to the blinds while keeping the pot manageable.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 3c, which is a very weak starting hand. Pro has opened for 2.5bb from the Button. While I'm getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 400), 9-3 offsuit is not a hand that plays well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the tournament goal of maximizing stack size, there is no reason to defend such a weak range against a Button open. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 5♣2♦ — the worst offsuit hand. Button opened to 250 (SB folded), leaving me out of position head‑to‑head. Calling 150 more would require ~27% equity to break even (150 to win a 550 pot), and 5‑2o has almost no playable equity vs a BTN opening range and will be dominated often.
