A light 3‑bet bluff here is unnecessary and risky with deep stacks and poor post‑flop position. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I hold King-Ace offsuit (AKo) in the UTG position. In a 4-handed game, this is a premium starting hand. My stack is roughly 100 big blinds. I will open the pot with a standard raise to 2.5bb (250 chips). This size allows me to build the pot for value while keeping my range balanced and not committing too many chips out of position against the deep-stacked chip leader in the blinds.

Holding Ten-Five offsuit on the button, I'm facing an UTG raise from Pro. This hand is far too weak to call or 3-bet with, regardless of my positional advantage. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

Fold. Facing an UTG raise to 250 from a likely strong opener, 5♥6♥ from the small blind is too marginal — I’ll be out of position to the raiser and the big blind and more often dominated. Calling 200 into the 400 pot requires ~33% equity to break even, and against an UTG range (plus possible action from the BB) my postflop life will be difficult. 3-betting is also a poor bluff here with such a weak holding. With a large stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot (dealer or heads-up) rather than playing a marginal OOP speculative hand.

Facing a 2.5bb open from Pro (first position 4-handed) we’re getting a decent price (150 to win 400 immediate), but T3o is near the bottom of our defend range: it’s offsuit, poorly connected, and frequently dominated when we make top pair (T-x) or second pair (3-x), creating nasty reverse implied odds out of position. With ~46bb effective and no need to take marginal, low-realization spots versus the preflop aggressor, the highest-EV/lowest-variance play is to simply release.

I am holding 9-2 offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Even in a four-handed game, opening with such a wide and weak range is unprofitable, especially from the first position to act. I will fold to conserve my chips for a more favorable hand.

Ah Kc (A♥ K♣) is a premium, high-equity hand and I’m on the button with only the blinds left to act. I want to take the initiative, build the pot when I’m likely ahead, and apply pressure to the blinds. A standard 3x BB open is natural and consistent with the table (strong enough to fold out weaker holdings but not overcommit given deep stacks). I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind). Contingency: if FiveTwo (SB, ~45bb) shoves I’ll call AKo; if Pro (BB) 3-bets to a normal isolate size I’ll consider a 4-bet for value or a call to play postflop in position depending on their sizing.

Minni is opening very wide on the button, but Q5o is near the bottom of any reasonable small-blind defend range and plays poorly out of position with dominated top-pair issues. Calling needs solid equity realization and we also risk the big blind squeezing, which makes our 250 call even worse. A 3-bet bluff is not attractive with this hand/stack because Minni can comfortably call in position and pressure us postflop. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh Jd. The Button (Minni) raised to 300 (3BB). I need to call 200 into a pot of 450, offering me excellent pot odds to defend against a wide button opening range. KJo plays reasonably well post-flop and is ahead of many hands in a standard button stealing range. Given the stack depth (100BB), a 3-bet is an option but calling closes the action and controls the pot size while out of position. I will call to see a flop.

I missed the flop completely on an Ace-high board (5c Ac 8d) which heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Being out of position with just King-high and no draws, checking is the only standard play. I intend to fold to a continuation bet as my hand has very little equity or playability.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♥K♣) on a fairly dry flop (5♣ A♣ 8♦) and I was the preflop aggressor. Pro defended the big blind, so his range contains many Ax, pocket pairs and broadways — hands I want value from and hands I want to charge. I’ll continue for value/protection with a standard ~50% pot bet to extract from worse A’s, Kx and pocket pairs and to deny cheap chances to backdoor draws. Betting 325 keeps the pot manageable versus deeper stacks but is large enough to get value and fold out some equity. If he check-raises I’ll re-evaluate (likely continue against a single-size raise, fold or control the pot facing an overcommitment).

I hold King-high (Kh Jd) on an Ace-high board (5c Ac 8d). The opponent, who raised from the button, has continued with a bet of 325 into a pot of 650. The Ace interacts favorably with their range, and I have no pair and no significant draws. Calling out of position with just King-high is not profitable against a c-bet on this texture. I am likely drawing dead or very thin. The correct play is to fold.

Holding T♦8♠ UTG in a 4-handed game is a marginal, unsuited one-gapper. I'm out of position against three players — several of whom have shown frequent aggression and 3-bets in prior hands — so limp-calling invites multiway pots where this hand is often dominated and open-raising is a thin isolation attempt likely to be 3-bet. With stacks deep but opponents capable of big action, the +EV play is to conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Minni has folded and we’re on the button, so stealing is attractive, but 5♠2♣ is at the very bottom of range and plays terribly when either blind defends (dominated, poor equity, low realizability). Both Pro and Flash have shown willingness to defend/3-bet, and with ~46BB I don’t want to burn chips in a low-EV open that often gets called and forced to barrel. I’ll pass and wait for a better spot to apply pressure with hands that realize equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c3s, a very weak offsuit hand. The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Flash) covers me. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to complete, this hand has very poor playability post-flop out of position. It is at the very bottom of my range. Raising is too risky with this holding, and completing likely leads to bleeding chips on future streets. Folding is the standard and safest play.

4-handed we can open fairly wide from UTG, but 9c5s offsuit is near the bottom: poor playability, dominated often when called, and it performs badly versus 3-bets from the Button/SB. With ~45bb and no antes, the 150 in blinds isn’t enough incentive to limp or open-raise a hand this weak. Preserve stack for higher-EV steal/iso spots.

I am on the Button with 8d 2c, which is a statistically very weak hand (unsuited, unconnected, low cards). Although position is favorable, the hand strength does not justify an open raise or steal attempt, especially with deep-stacked opponents in the blinds who may defend. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold.

I have King-Ten suited in the Small Blind. With the action folded to me, I have a very strong hand for a blind-vs-blind scenario. I will put in a standard raise to 3x the big blind to attack Minni's big blind and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and high raw equity against a wide defending range.

I’m in the big blind with Ac7c facing a small‑blind open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200. Effective stacks are deep (me ~32.6k, villain ~12.4k). Heads‑up the SB’s open will be fairly wide; Ac7 has an Ace‑blocker and decent postflop playability. Folding is too tight and simply calling leaves me out of position on the flop against an aggressive opener. I want to take the initiative and generate fold equity while keeping the pot reasonable, so I’ll 3‑bet to isolate. A raise to 1000 total is a standard, constructive size here — big enough to fold many weaker opens and define the hand, but small enough to play postflop if called. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls we proceed with a strong ace and position/plan for continuation; if he 4‑bets large I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold).

I have King-Ten suited in a blind-vs-blind situation against Minni, who has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. While I am out of position, KTs is too strong to fold to a single 3-bet. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize the equity of a hand that plays very well in a 3-bet pot, without turning a high-equity hand into a 4-bet bluff against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent who may not fold often.

On this Queen-high board, I have missed completely with my King-Ten suited, although I do have one backdoor diamond draw. Minni, as the pre-flop 3-bettor, has a significant range advantage here as this board hits her 3-betting range (AQ, KK, AA, and some bluffs that might still be ahead of me like AJ or air). Being out of position against a very aggressive player with over 30,000 chips, I should play cautiously. Checking is the standard play for the caller of a 3-bet to see how the aggressor proceeds. I will check and evaluate based on the size of her continuation bet, likely folding to significant pressure since I have zero showdown value and limited improving potential on this specific texture.

I 3‑bet preflop to take the initiative and isolate the SB. Flop (4d 3s Qs) missed my Ac7c, but I have an Ace blocker and two clubs, and Flash checked. Versus a caller of a 3‑bet his range is wide but weighted toward hands that will often fold to pressure (low pairs, some broadways and weak holdings) while Qx and spade‑draws will call. I want to keep the pot manageable while extracting fold equity and denying free cards to draws, so I’ll make a small continuation bet (~1/3–1/2 pot). A bet of 700 into the 2000 pot applies pressure, preserves flexibility, and lets me fold to a sizable raise without committing a large portion of my deep stack. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (pot‑control with no pair, barrel only on favorable cards); if raised strongly I’ll presume a very strong hand and release.

On a flop of Qs-4d-3s, I have King-high with a backdoor diamond flush draw. Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop, has a range that significantly favors this texture, containing all the top sets, overpairs (AA, KK), and strong top pairs (AQ). Although she is betting small (700 into 2000), I am out of position with very little immediate equity and poor playability on future streets. Given Minni's history of aggression and her large stack, she is likely to continue applying pressure on the turn. Calling with just two overcards and a backdoor draw in a 3-bet pot is a losing play long-term; I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am in UTG position (first to act in 4-handed play) with King-Five suited (K5d). While ranges widen in short-handed play, K5s is still a marginal hand to open from first position, especially with a very deep-stacked and aggressive player (Minni) in the Small Blind and a short stack in the Big Blind. The hand is easily dominated by better Kings and faces reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and prudent play to preserve the stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Jh 8c. This is a speculative hand that is marginally playable in a four-handed game, but given the tournament situation and my stack size of 11,725, I want to maintain some pressure. Minni (the big stack) has been quite active and aggressive from the blinds in previous hands, and FiveTwo is relatively short-stacked at 4,466. Raising here might invite a 3-bet from Minni or a shove from FiveTwo. While folding is safe, a standard raise can often steal the blinds. However, J8o is weak enough that if I get re-raised, I'm forced to fold. Looking at the table dynamics, Minni is playing a lot of hands aggressively. I will fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my position, as preserving chips is important in this high roller format.

K♥T♠ is strong enough to open from the small blind heads-up against the big blind — calling 50 is too passive and keeps a weaker range in the hand. With the button and UTG folded I'm isolating the big blind and taking initiative. The BB has ~44BB so he will defend fairly wide; a standard 3x sizing gives good fold equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily out of position. Plan: raise to 300 to isolate; if called I will play postflop aggressively on A/K/T/Q-high flops (frequent c-bets) and proceed more cautiously on coordinated/paired boards or against strong resistance (large check-raises or 3-bets). If the BB 3-bets large I will evaluate based on sizing and tendencies — call manageable 3-bets to play postflop, fold to very large 4-bets/squeezes.

We’re in the BB facing a 3x SB open. Although the immediate price (200 to win 600 if we call) looks decent, 7♣3♥ offsuit has very poor playability and realization OOP, especially versus an active, deep-stacked SB who can apply pressure across streets. A bluff 3-bet isn’t attractive with this hand/stack depth because we’ll get called too often and have no good continuations. Best EV is to conserve chips and wait for a better defend/3-bet candidate.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) is a strong enough hand to open from under-the-gun (which is effectively the Cutoff). My stack of 11,725 (117 BBs) allows me to play aggressively. Minni is the chip leader and has been very active, so I want to set a standard opening size to define the range. Raising to 300 is a standard 3x open that should isolate the field or take the blinds down.

Flash opened UTG to 300, which from an early position is a fairly strong range. My hand (9♦5♣) is a weak, unsuited disconnected holding with poor equity and poor postflop playability against an UTG opener. Calling 300 would likely put me in a multiway pot (SB and BB still to act) where this hand performs even worse, and a tiny min-raise to 500 is not a credible isolation bluff vs an UTG range and risks unnecessary action. With a healthy stack I should conserve chips for better spots — fold and wait for a hand with more playability or position advantage.

We’re in the small blind 4-handed, so ranges are wider, but calling a 3bb UTG open OOP with Ts6s is a losing, dominated spot—especially because BB gets a great price and can come along, leaving us in a tough multiway pot. Flash has shown a fairly aggressive preflop style, so a light 3-bet works well here: we leverage fold equity, often win the dead money immediately, and when called we at least have suited equity and initiative. If Flash 4-bets, we can comfortably fold with no regret given our hand and stack depth.

I am in the Big Blind with Js Th. The action started with a UTG raise to 300 from Flash, followed by a 3-bet to 1100 from FiveTwo in the Small Blind. FiveTwo is playing with a relatively short stack (3366), so their 3-bet represents a very strong range (likely premiums) that commits a third of their stack. JTo performs poorly against a UTG opening range and even worse against a SB 3-bet range. Additionally, Flash is still left to act behind me and could 4-bet. The pot odds do not justify a call with this hand strength, and implied odds are low due to the short effective stack of the 3-bettor. Folding is the only viable option.

I opened UTG for 300 with A8o, which is a standard play in a 4-handed game. FiveTwo, who is very short-stacked with only about 33BBs total (3366 chips), has 3-bet to 1100 from the Small Blind. A8o does not play well against a 3-bet range, especially when facing a player who is likely committing a significant portion of their stack. Calling 800 more out of position against a polarized range or a strong linear range is usually a losing play with this hand. Given FiveTwo's stack size, they are likely looking to get it in or have a very strong hand. Folding is the most disciplined and correct move here to preserve my stack.

UTG with T♠7♦ is a weak, unsuited, poorly connected hand — easily dominated and difficult to play OOP against the Button, SB and BB. With 4 players to act behind (including two deep stacks who can 3‑bet) and only a small incentive to limp/call 100, this is a clear fold. Preserve my large stack and wait for a better spot or position to open-raise.

Button vs blinds after UTG folds is a prime steal spot. A3o has an ace blocker that reduces the chance the blinds can continue with strong Ax/AA, and it has decent playability in position (can make top pair, wheel equity on low boards). With our ~49bb stack we want to pick up the dead money without overinflating the pot against deeper stacks, so a standard but not oversized open works well.

Opponent FiveTwo raises from the Button. While FiveTwo has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets in previous hands (Hands 1, 8, 45), holding A6o in the Small Blind is a marginal spot. Calling is generally -EV due to being out of position with a hand often dominated by the opener's calling range or better Ax. A 3-bet bluff is plausible given the opponent's history, but A6o plays poorly if called. Following a standard solid strategy, folding weak offsuit aces from the Small Blind against a Button open is the prudent play to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Tens (Tc, Th), which is a very strong hand against a button open from FiveTwo. FiveTwo has shown a tendency to open wide from late positions and has folded to 3-bets several times in previous hands (Hand #1, #11, #45). I will raise to 850 to take the initiative, build the pot for value, and potentially win the hand immediately. If FiveTwo calls, I am in a strong position, and if he shoves, I will have a tough decision but Tens are generally worth going with against a 46BB stack from the button.

Flash has shown a tendency to 3-bet aggressively, and this is a classic BTN vs BB dynamic where ranges are wide. With Ah3s we have an ace blocker (reducing AA/AK/AQ frequency), position, and some playable equity (top-pair potential plus wheel/backdoor possibilities). At ~46bb effective, a 4-bet bluff becomes awkward (too big to 4-bet/fold comfortably, and a shove gets called by a strong range we do poorly against), while folding is too tight versus his likely wide 3-bet range. Calling keeps his bluffs in, realizes our positional equity, and lets us navigate postflop with a manageable SPR.

On a board of 5s-9h-Jc, my pocket tens are currently second pair. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage, as my range includes overpairs like AA, KK, and QQ, which FiveTwo likely doesn't have after just calling the 3-bet. However, the Jack is an overcard. With an SPR of roughly 2.3, I want to put out a continuation bet to maintain initiative, protect my hand against overcards like Ace, King, or Queen, and get value from smaller pairs (66-88) or straight draws (like T8, though I block that). A bet of 700 (40% of the pot) is large enough to charge draws and small enough to allow me to get away if he raises and shows significant strength.

Flash’s small (~30% pot) c-bet in a BTN vs BB 3-bet pot is very likely a high-frequency range bet. With Ah3s we have position, an ace-high that can already be best versus his unpaired broadways, plus backdoor spade equity and some runner-runner straight possibilities. Folding would overfold versus this sizing, while raising (especially with our low SPR) effectively turns into a shove that gets called by a lot of hands that still have strong equity against us (overpairs and even many two-over hands), so we don’t generate enough fold equity. Calling keeps his bluffs in and gives us a chance to win either by improving (A/spade runouts) or by taking it away when he checks later streets.

The turn Ten of Diamonds is an excellent card for me, as it gives me a set of Tens. While the board is now quite connected (completing possible straights like KQ, Q8, or 87), the majority of FiveTwo's range consists of Jacks, nines, or straight draws that will still call a bet. With a Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of approximately 1.05 (3316 remaining in his stack vs. 3150 in the pot), I am committed to this hand. I will bet 1500 to extract value from his Jacks and draws, intending to call if he shoves or to shove myself on most river cards.

Flash’s turn sizing is again small (~32% pot), consistent with a high-frequency double barrel in this BTN vs BB 3-bet dynamic. While Ah3s has no real draw on this runout, ace-high can still be best versus his unpaired broadways (AQ/KQ/AK-type hands) and some low-equity bluffs that continue with this sizing. Folding would likely overfold versus that range, and raising/shoving is poor because he’d be getting a very good price (only ~1.8k more) and will call with most made hands and even many draws. Calling keeps his bluffs in, lets us realize our showdown value when he gives up, and preserves the option to jam rivers when he checks with a capped/missed range, despite our shallow remaining stack.

I have a set of Tens on a board of 5s-9h-Jc-Td-2s. The river is a complete brick. While straights like KQ or 87 are possible, they are unlikely given the betting line, and I am ahead of almost all of FiveTwo's calling range, including AJ, KJ, QJ, and J9. With FiveTwo having only 1816 chips left and a pot of 6150, I have a clear value bet to put him all-in. Even in the rare case he has a better hand, the pot odds and the strength of my hand make this an automatic commit.

We’re getting a very good price: call 1816 to win 9782, so we need only ~18.6% equity. Flash’s line (BB 3-bet, small/medium barrels, then a small river shove for our remaining stack) is consistent with trying to maximize fold equity with missed broadways and other air that continued with his range. Ah3s is near the bottom of our range, but it has relevant blockers to some of his strongest value (AA/AJ/AT), and ace-high will beat his unpaired hands that take this sizing. Given the pot odds and Flash’s demonstrated aggression/bluff potential, folding would overfold here; we call off.

I am on the Button with 3h 2c (3-2 offsuit), which is statistically one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite being in position 3-handed, the hand has virtually no playability or equity to justify an open raise, especially against deep-stacked opponents in the blinds who have shown aggression. Folding is the only standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 5h. Pro has folded on the Button, leaving only me and the chip leader, Minni, in the Big Blind. 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand. Given Minni's large stack and active play style, trying to steal the blind or playing out of position with a marginal hand is not worth the risk. Folding is the standard professional play here to preserve my stack.

On the button in a 3-handed game, 98o is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. Although it's offsuit, its connectivity gives it some playability post-flop. I'll raise to 300 to try and take the blinds or play the hand in position against a defender.

Facing a standard button open to 300 while I’m in the small blind with Q♠7♦. This hand is marginal and often dominated by the button’s opening range; it also plays poorly out of position. Calling 250 to see a flop will likely create a multiway/OOP scenario (the big blind can still act) where my post‑flop maneuverability and equity are limited. A small raise here would be a thin bluff and risks bloating a pot out of position against two players. With a deep stack and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better, positionally advantaged spot is the right long‑term play.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 9s facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Flash). Minni (SB) folded. I have 100 chips already posted. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650 total after my call. This gives me pot odds of roughly 30.7%. 6s 9s is a suited one-gapper that defends well enough against a wide Button opening range, having about 38-40% equity. While out of position, the suited nature and straight connectivity make it a standard defend (call) rather than a fold or a 3-bet bluff at this stack depth (97bb).

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with 6s9s on a 7s8c4d board. While this board connects well with my defending range, standard strategy is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flash) rather than leading out. This allows me to see his action. If he bets, I have a very strong semi-bluffing candidate for a check-raise or a profitable check-call. Checking protects my overall range.

I have top pair on a relatively connected but dry flop. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Pro has checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play here for value and protection. My top pair of eights with a nine kicker is likely ahead of Pro's big blind defense range, which includes many high cards, smaller pairs, and gutshot straight draws. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot (400 into 650) should clarify where I stand while charging drawing hands.

I hold 6s9s on a 7s-8c-4d flop, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needs a 5 or 10) and a backdoor flush draw. I checked to the pre-flop raiser (Flash) who bet 400 into a pot of 650. The pot odds are 400 to call for a total pot of 1450, which is approximately 27.5%. With 8 outs to the straight, my equity is roughly 32% by the river, giving me direct odds to call. Additionally, this board texture favors my Big Blind defending range, so I can profitably continue. A check-raise is an option as a semi-bluff, but calling is lower variance and keeps the opponent's bluffing range in the hand while realizing my equity for a good price out of position.

On the Turn, the board is 7s 8c 4d 4h. I hold 6s 9s, giving me an open-ended straight draw. The 4h pairs the board, which doesn't significantly change the dynamic relative to the flop action. Having check-called the flop, standard play out of position is to check again to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donking) here is generally suboptimal as I don't have a value hand that needs protection or extraction, and I want to realize my equity for free if the opponent checks back. I will check and evaluate based on their action.

The turn card is a four, pairing the bottom of the board. This is generally a neutral or slightly positive card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, but it doesn't change much of the hand's dynamic. I still hold top pair (eights) with a decent kicker. Pro has checked again. While I could bet for value against a seven or a straight draw, checking behind serves two purposes: it controls the pot size with a marginal made hand and protects me against a potential check-raise. It also allows me to induce bluffs on the river from missed draws or weaker hands. My hand has significant showdown value, and checking ensures I get to the river to see Pro's final action.

I hold 6s9s on a board of 7s 8c 4d 4h 2d, leaving me with 9-high after missing my open-ended straight draw. My opponent (Flash) raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, but checked back the turn. This line often indicates a marginal made hand (like a pair of 7s, 8s, or a pocket pair) or a strong Ace-high engaging in pot control. While I have no showdown value and will lose if the hand goes to a check-down, bluffing is risky here. Flash's check-back on the turn suggests he has a hand he wants to take to showdown cheaply, and he is unlikely to fold a pair to a river bet on this relatively dry runout. Additionally, representing a 4 or a straight is difficult given I checked the turn myself. Therefore, the highest EV play is to check and fold to a bet, preserving my stack rather than firing a low-probability bluff.

On the river, the board is 7-8-4-4-2. I hold top pair with an eight and a nine kicker. Pro has checked to me for the third consecutive street (counting the flop call). After I checked back the turn for pot control, checking back the river ensures I realize my showdown value and win the pot against missed draws, small pocket pairs, or a seven. While a small value bet might be called by a seven, the risk of facing a check-raise from a slow-played four or a superior eight makes checking the most prudent play to maximize my long-term stack.

Three-handed and on the button with 4♠3♥. This is a marginal but playable hand in position — I have the advantage of acting last post-flop and plenty of fold equity versus the blinds. Pot is 150 and to call is only 100, but open-raising from the button in a 3‑player game is standard to pick up the blinds and seize the initiative. I’ll make a standard-sized open to 300 (3x BB) — small enough to keep my range wide and not overcommit, but large enough to apply pressure. Plan: if both fold I take the blinds; if called I’ll play aggressively in position and c-bet favorable flops; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold this weak holding rather than get tangled out of position.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 3s facing a raise to 300 from the Button (Minni). My hand is very weak (low unsuited connector/gapper) and I would be playing out of position against the chip leader. The pot odds are not sufficient to justify calling with a hand that has poor playability and low equity realization. The risk of the Big Blind squeezing is also present. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Pocket eights are a very strong hand in a three-handed game, particularly when facing a standard button open from Minni. I have a healthy stack and want to build the pot for value while also exerting pressure on the button's wide opening range. A 3-bet to 1000 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop, or play a sizeable pot with a strong pair if called.

I opened to 300 as a standard button steal, but Flash’s sizeable 3-bet to 1000 turns this spot into a three‑bet pot. I have 4♠3♥ — a very weak, often‑dominated hand with poor raw equity versus a typical 3‑bet range. The pot is 1,350 and calling 700 requires ~34% equity to break even; 4‑3 offsuit is well below that. Flash has shown frequent preflop aggression and willingness to commit postflop, so there’s little reason to gamble chips here with such a weak holding. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot to leverage position and stack depth.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with [6s 8c]. My stack is 91 BB. The two opponents in the blinds cover me significantly and have shown aggression (3-betting pre-flop). 86 offsuit is a weak hand, below the standard opening threshold even for the button in a 3-handed game, especially against active big stacks. There is no need to try to steal here with trash; I will fold and wait for a better hand.

In a 3-handed game, holding King-high in the Small Blind when the Button has folded is a standard opening situation. K6o is well within the range of hands to raise for value and to attempt to take down the blinds immediately. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind.

Flash (SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Q♠2♣ — a weak unsuited hand but heads‑up versus a wide SB opening this stake structure. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (pot odds ~2:1), and my stack is very deep so the risk is small. Folding here would be too tight heads‑up; 3‑betting Q2o is too thin and easily dominated or commits unnecessary chips out of position. I’ll call the 200 to see a flop, keep the pot controllable, and evaluate on the flop — continuing only with favorable cards or good equity, otherwise check/fold to aggression.

On a board of Qh-4h-Ac, as the pre-flop raiser, I have a significant range advantage. This texture heavily favors my perceived range (which includes many Ax, Kx, and high pocket pairs), while the Big Blind's calling range often consists of mid-tier pairs and suited connectors. Although I only have King-high with no draw, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet to force a fold from hands that missed the board, such as small pairs or non-heart connectors. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard sizing to apply pressure.

I have Q♠2♣ on Q♥4♥A♣ — top pair but a very weak kicker and an Ace on board. Flash opened from the SB (wide range) and made a small flop bet (300 into a 900 pot), which is a sizing that contains a lot of bluffs/continuation bets as well as thin value hands (Axs, Qx). Raising OOP with Q2o is too thin — I can get called or 3‑betted by better Qs or any A, building a pot I’m unlikely to win. Folding is overly nitty heads‑up with top pair. Calling keeps bluffs in his range, controls the pot size and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn. Pot odds are favorable (300 to call into ~1,200) — I only need modest equity and top pair has reasonable equity vs a wide c‑bet range. Plan: call the 300, and on the turn fold to big continued aggression if an Ace or other scare card appears; otherwise proceed cautiously (check/call small bets or extract value from worse hands).

On the turn, the King of Spades improves my hand to second pair. However, the board is quite dangerous with both an Ace and a Queen present. Minni has called both my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, which strongly suggests she has a range consisting of Aces, Queens, or flush draws. While I have improved, betting again might overplay my hand against an Ace. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate Minni's reaction. It also serves as a way to reach showdown more cheaply or induce a bluff from missed draws.

Flash opened wide preflop and made a small c-bet on the flop; I called with Q♠2♣ and now have top pair with a very weak kicker on a Q♥-4♥-A♣-K♠ board. Flash checked the turn, which looks like pot control or weakness — but there are many Ax/Kx combos that still beat me and a raise OOP or a thin value bet is too risky with this kicker. Betting risks getting check‑raised or bloated into a pot where I’m often behind; checking controls the pot size and lets me see a river for free or at low cost. Plan: check to keep the pot small, call reasonable river bets to realize equity, fold to large river aggression or a check‑raise on this street.

The river card completes the flush draw with the Nine of Hearts. My pair of Kings, which I picked up on the turn, has decent showdown value, but it is now behind any Ace or any heart flush. Minni has played the hand passively since calling my pre-flop and flop bets, which could mean she was on a draw that just hit or is holding a marginal hand like a Queen. By checking, I can reach showdown cheaply and avoid losing more chips to a better hand, while also allowing her to potentially bluff with missed straight draws.

Flash opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop and then checked turn and river — that sequence shows weakness or pot control. I hold top pair (Q♠) with a very weak kicker on Q♥-4♥-A♣-K♠-9♥. Betting OOP here would be thin: I get called by better Qx/Ax/Kx and risk a check‑raise or bloating a pot I’m often behind in. With the pot only 1,200 and no bet to call, I keep the pot small and take the free showdown. I will check; I’ll call small river bets to realize equity but fold to large river aggression.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. I will raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds, who have varying stack sizes.

I have K♦9♦ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must add 250). In 3-handed play the button's opening range is fairly wide and K9s is worth defending — it's suited, has decent showdown value and good postflop playability with deep stacks (I have ~328 BB). I don't want to 3-bet light and risk isolating vs the button or getting a cold 4-bet from the BB; calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves flexibility. I'll call the additional 250 (300 total committed) and evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively if I flop well, otherwise control the pot OOP or fold to heavy action.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 5s (53o) facing a 3x raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind. The pot offers me odds of 200 to win 700 (22.2%), which is mathematically tempting. However, 53o is one of the weakest hands in the deck with very poor playability and reverse implied odds, especially in a multi-way pot where I am out of position relative to the aggressor. Realizing equity with this hand will be difficult. The standard play against a 3BB open is to fold this specific holding.

I called preflop with K♦9♦ to defend a wide button opening and keep the pot manageable. Flop (J♥ 7♥ 4♠) is fairly dry but with two hearts — I have no pair and no heart draw, only K‑high and a very weak backdoor diamond possibility. Out of position vs the preflop aggressor, I don’t want to lead with a bluff or bloat the pot with essentially air. I’ll check to control the pot and let the button show their hand; plan is to fold to a sizable continuation bet (especially > ~1/2 pot) but consider calling a small probe bet to see a cheap turn if they’re firing wide. If checked to, I’ll reevaluate on the turn and only continue with real improvement (K/9/diamond) or a clear read to bluff.

I have flopped middle pair (sevens) with an Ace kicker on a Jack-high board. Minni, the small blind, has checked to me. This board texture is relatively dry, though there are some backdoor draws and a potential heart flush draw. By betting here, I can get value from weaker hands like a four, smaller pocket pairs (55, 66), or various draws, while also protecting my hand against overcards. A bet of approximately 60% of the pot (420 chips) is a standard continuation to maintain initiative and define my position.

I defended K♦9♦ preflop and checked the flop as planned. The board J♥7♥4♠ is fairly dry with two hearts; I have no pair and no heart draw, just K‑high. Flash's bet is 420 into a 1120 pot (~38% pot) — a small c‑bet that will include a lot of wide continuation bets and bluffs from a button opening range. Folding to such a small probe is too nitty, and raising OOP with only K‑high is a thin/bluff line I don't want to take against a likely calling range. Calling is cheap relative to my deep stack (~326 BB) and gives me good pot odds (~27% break‑even) to see a turn where I can continue only with real improvement (K/9/diamond) or if Flash shows clear weakness. I will call the 420 (bringing my total committed to 720) and reassess on the turn, folding to significant turn aggression unless I improve or get a reliable read to bluff.

I have K9s (Kd9d) and the turn paired the board: J♥ 7♥ 4♠ 7♣. I called a small flop c-bet to see a cheap turn, but I still have no pair, no heart/diamond draw and essentially no fold equity OOP now that the board is paired (hands with a 7 make trips; Js, pairs, or combinations can beat me, and some hands can already have full houses). Raising as a bluff OOP is thin and will be called by many better hands; calling a bet would be a losing, marginal play. There is currently no bet to call, so the correct line is to check and control the pot. Plan: check the turn and fold to any meaningful bet; if checked through to the river I will only continue with a real improvement or a credible bluffing card and sizing.

I have improved to Three of a Kind (Sevens) with an Ace kicker on the turn. This is a very strong hand. Minni checked again, indicating she likely has a hand like a Jack or a draw. I will bet for value and to charge any potential heart flush draws. A bet of 1000 into a 1540 pot is a standard sizing to extract value from a Jack or a drawing hand.

I’m OOP with K♦9♦ on J♥ 7♥ 4♠ 7♣. The pot is 2540 and Flash bets 1000 (I must call 1000). To break even on a call I need ~28% equity; I only have 6 outs to make a pair (K or 9) — 6/46 ≈ 13% — and even if I hit I can still be behind two‑pair/full house lines. Flash’s line (small flop c‑bet then a larger turn bet) is consistent with value (Jx, 7x) or well-timed continued aggression; raising as a thin OOP bluff with no real equity is likely to be called by better hands. Given the poor raw equity, lack of draws, and my earlier plan to fold to meaningful turn aggression, this is a clear fold to preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I’m on the button with 6♥8♣ (offsuit) in a 3-handed game. In three-handed play the button’s opening range is wide and position is a significant advantage, so this hand is playable to open. I want fold equity and to avoid a multiway pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll open-raise to a standard ~3xBB sizing. Raise to 300 total chips gives a credible steal attempt (discourages the very weakest calls) while leaving room to play postflop in position. If I get one or two callers I’ll use position to c-bet favorable boards and realize equity; if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold this hand, as it plays poorly against a strong reraise.

I hold 9c 2s in the Small Blind, which is an extremely weak hand (unsuited, disconnected trash). The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. Although Minni is aggressive and likely opening wide, 92o has very poor playability and equity realization, especially out of position. Calling 250 chips here would be a losing play in the long run. The correct decision is to fold immediately and preserve my stack of ~90 BBs for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh4d. Minni, the chip leader, raised to 300 from the Button. Pro folded. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.25 to 1, Queen-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive chip leader. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am on the Button with 9s 6c, which is a very weak offsuit hand (9-high). With 3 players remaining and deep stacks (my stack is ~90BB), there is no incentive to open-raise with trash hands. Stealing the blinds is not necessary here given the hand strength and tournament stage. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 6-high offsuit (6s 5c). The action has folded to me. While I could attempt a steal or complete the blind to see a cheap flop, 65o is a very weak hand to play out of position against Minni, who has a large stack and has been defending/playing aggressively recently. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the safer and more professional play here.

I am on the button with Qc6c in a three-handed game. Suitability and being in position make this a standard opening hand. I will raise to 300 to pick up the blinds or play the hand in position against the blinds.

Flash (button) opened to 300 in a 3‑handed pot. I’m in the small blind with A♠8♦. This hand is worth defending — it has the ace blocker and decent top‑pair potential — but it’s offsuit and I’ll be out of position postflop. I don’t want to 3‑bet OOP with a medium, vulnerable kicker and create a big pot against two deep stacks. Calling keeps the pot manageable, defends my blind against a wide BTN open, and lets me evaluate the flop (c‑bet value on A‑high boards, pot‑control or check on dry misses, fold to heavy aggression unless I improve). If the big blind 3‑bets big preflop I can fold; if he folds I’ll play heads‑up versus Flash. Call 250 more (300 total committed).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o). Flash raised to 300 on the Button, and Minni called in the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700 chips (300 + 300 + 100), and it costs me 200 chips to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.5 to 1 (or about 22% equity required). My hand has significantly better equity than 22% against a Button opening range and a Small Blind calling range. Although I will be out of position relative to the original raiser (Flash), closing the action in a multi-way pot with an Ace is a standard defensive play. Squeezing (raising) is too risky with this hand strength and stack depth, and folding is too tight given the price. I will call to see the flop.

I have A♠8♦ on 8c–Jh–3c: top pair with the nut kicker, which is a reasonably strong hand in a 3‑way pot but vulnerable to Jx and two‑club combinations. Flash (BTN) opened wide preflop and Pro (BB) called, so both opponents have fairly wide ranges that include Jx, worse 8x, medium pairs, and two‑club combos that have a flush draw (if they hold two clubs they have ~9 outs ≈36% to make by the river). I want to both extract value from worse hands and deny correct pot odds to club draws. Betting builds the pot when I’m ahead and charges draws; checking lets the opener take the initiative and can give free cards.

Action: bet for value and protection. Size = half the pot (450 into 900). If I get called (one or both), I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn: check to control the pot on non‑scary cards and re‑evaluate if a Jack or a club appears. If I face a significant raise OOP, I’ll treat it as strong (Jx/set/flush) and fold unless the raise looks like a small bluff I can reasonably call.

I am in the Big Blind with [7d Ah] on a flop of [8c Jh 3c]. Minni (Small Blind) has led out (donk bet) for 450 into a pot of 900. I am sandwiched between Minni and the pre-flop raiser (Flash) on the Button. Minni's lead into two opponents usually indicates a hand with some equity, such as a pair or a draw. My Ace-high has poor playability here; I have no flush draw and only very remote backdoor straight possibilities. Furthermore, Flash is still left to act behind me and could raise. Floating here with just Ace-high and the Ah blocker is not profitable given the multi-way action and the board texture. The most prudent play is to preserve my stack of ~86BB and fold.

I raised pre-flop with Qc6c and both blinds called. The flop of 8c Jh 3c gives me a flush draw and one overcard. Minni has led out for 450 into a 900 pot (a 'donk bet'). Given that I have a flush draw (roughly 35% equity) and am in position, I have the direct pot odds (requiring 25% equity) to call. Calling allows me to realize my equity and potentially take the pot away on later streets if Minni checks, or get paid off if I hit my flush. Raising is an option to exert pressure, but calling is safer and keeps the pot manageable while I'm drawing.

I have A♠8♦ — a pair of eights with the Ace kicker on an 8♣–J♥–3♣–Q♠ board. After my flop bet Flash (BTN) called, so his calling range is wide and contains Jx, Qx, pocket pairs, and club draws. The turn Q is a dangerous overcard: it gives many of his plausible flop calls (Qx, some two-pair/JQ combos) a clear advantage over my hand. I’m first to act heads‑up, so betting here would bloat the pot with a marginal, easily dominated hand and expose me to check‑raises or large bets by better pairs.

Decision: check and control the pot. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, lets me re‑evaluate on the river, and avoids building a pot I’m likely behind in. Plan after this check: if Flash checks back we go to showdown/control the river; if he bets small I’ll call for pot odds against draws and worse pairs; if he fires a large bet or a check‑raise I’ll fold most of the time (he will be representing Q/J/two‑pair/sets). This preserves my stack and keeps post‑river options flexible.

On the turn, the Queen of Spades gives me top pair with a 6 kicker, along with my flush draw. Minni, who donk-bet the flop, has checked. While I have improved to top pair, my kicker is weak. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and ensure I see the river for free to potentially complete my flush or reach showdown with top pair. Betting might overplay the hand if Minni is trapping or has a better Queen (like AQ or KQ), whereas checking allows me to realize my equity and potentially catch a bluff on the river or value bet if I improve.

Check. I have second pair (8s) with the ace kicker, but the river paired the board with a second Jack (8♣ Jh 3♣ Q♠ Js), which makes many of Flash’s plausible flop-calling hands (Jx -> trips, Qx, pocket 8s -> full house) now beat me. Flash’s line (wide BTN open, called my flop bet, checked the Q on the turn) is consistent with Jx/Qx/medium pairs or missed club draws — a mixed range that often beats my hand on this river. The pot is only 1,800 while both stacks are deep; betting here risks bloating the pot with a marginal holding and opens me to a value raise where I’m usually crushed. Checking keeps the pot small and forces Flash to show aggression first; if he leads a small river bet I’ll call for pot odds against bluffs/missed draws, but I’ll fold to a large value bet/raise representing trips/full house. Given the texture and line, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play is to check and go to showdown (or call a modest bet, fold to big one).

On the river, the board has paired Jacks, making my hand Two Pair, Queens and Jacks. Minni, who led on the flop, has checked for the second consecutive street. While my hand has improved to Two Pair, it is marginal for value betting. A bet is unlikely to be called by a worse hand (perhaps only a weaker Queen or an 8), and I would likely be called or raised by any Jack (Trips) or a Queen with a better kicker (AQ, KQ, Q10, Q9). By checking back, I ensure a showdown and minimize the risk against better hands while capturing the pot if my Queens are good.

I’m on the button with 3h4c — a very weak offsuit hand. With deep stacks (my stack 31,639 ≈ 316bb) and only the two blinds to beat (Pro 86bb, Flash 196bb), opening from the button is profitable more often than limping or folding. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB): big enough to apply pressure and pick up the 150-chip pot often, but small enough to avoid overcommitting. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet (this hand has poor equity), but if called I’ll use position to play postflop cautiously (c-bet favorable low/connected flops, check/fold to heavy aggression on bad boards).

In the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit facing a 3x raise from the Button (Minni), I am in a difficult position. The hand is weak, easily dominated, and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Minni has a significant chip lead and has been aggressive. While the raw equity might be borderline against a wide button range, the realization of that equity from the Small Blind is low due to being out of position and the threat of the Big Blind squeezing or overcalling. Calling 2.5 big blinds is too expensive for this marginal hand. The prudent play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the Button. Although the price to call is relatively small (200 more to win 450), J3o plays poorly post-flop and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given that there are only 3 players left and I want to preserve my stack for better opportunities, folding is the most prudent play.

I am on the Button with 5s9s in a 3-handed game. My stack is 86 BBs, while my opponents have significantly larger stacks. 59s is a marginal suited two-gapper. While opening ranges on the button in 3-handed play are wide, this hand is at the very bottom of that range. Given the aggressive nature of the opponents (Minni and Flash have been active) and the risk of being 3-bet or floating out of position against deep stacks, the prudent play is to fold and wait for a hand with better playability or high-card strength.

I am in the Small Blind with K4o. In a 3-handed game, this is a marginal hand. While King-high has some showdown value, K4o is weak and difficult to play out of position if the Big Blind (Minni) calls. Minni has the largest stack and has shown a willingness to defend her blinds and play aggressively post-flop. Since I have been raising frequently in recent hands, I anticipate she might look for a spot to fight back. I will fold here to maintain a solid image and avoid a potentially difficult situation against the chip leader with a mediocre hand.

I have Ace-Seven of Hearts on the button. This is a very strong hand for a 3-handed game. I want to raise for value and to seize the initiative. Since it's a 3-way game, I'll make it 3x the big blind (300 total) to exert pressure on the blinds.

I'm in the small blind with pocket 6s facing a button open to 300. I have deep stacks (31,789) so set-mining is profitable and the cost to see a flop is small (I need to add 250 more). 3-betting out of the small blind with a medium pair is unattractive — a min re-raise to 500 is too small to credibly isolate and a larger 3-bet would bloat a pot OOP and create difficult spots versus either opponent. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves implied odds to hit a set, and lets me fold cheaply on dry boards if I miss. I'll call the 300 total (i.e., put 250 more).

I am in the Big Blind with Kh8h facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind call. The pot is currently 700 chips, and I need to call 200 chips to see the flop, giving me excellent pot odds of 3.5 to 1. K8 suited is a strong enough hand to defend from the Big Blind, especially given the multi-way action and deep stacks (85 BB). It has flush potential and reasonable high-card strength. Squeezing is an option but risky against two opponents, so a call is the most prudent play to realize my equity.

I called preflop to set-mine with 6c6h and the flop comes A♠ 4♥ T♥. This is an A-high two-heart board where my pocket sixes are an underpair to both A and T and I have virtually no meaningful draw (only a backdoor). I’m out of position against the button and the BB and we’ve got three-way action — betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a hand that is behind most of the opponents’ continuing ranges and create difficult spots if raised. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and see how the opponents act. Plan: call a small continuation bet to try to see a turn (especially to set-mine), fold to a large raise, and if I improve to a set or the action checks through re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold Kh8h on a flop of As4hTh. This gives me the second nut flush draw. The pot is 900. Minni (SB) has checked. As the Big Blind in a multi-way pot, and with the pre-flop raiser (Flash) yet to act behind me on a board that heavily favors their range (Ace-high), donk betting is not advisable. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the button bets, I have a strong enough draw to continue (call or potentially check-raise depending on sizing), and if they check, I happily take a free card to try and realize my equity.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace and a heart flush draw. In a three-handed game, this is a very strong hand. Both opponents have checked the flop, indicating they likely do not have a strong Ace or are playing cautiously. I will place a continuation bet of 450, which is half the pot, to extract value from weaker hands like a Ten, a Four, or various draws, while also protecting my hand and building the pot.

I’m OOP with 6♣6♥ on A♠ 4♥ T♥. I called preflop to set-mine, and Flash’s 450 flop bet is a small-to-medium c-bet rather than an isolating shove. 3-betting or check-raising OOP with an underpair would bloat the pot when I’m behind most of the button’s continuing range, so I’ll stick to the plan and call to see a turn. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves my implied odds to hit a set, and lets me fold cheaply to a large raise or turn shove. If I improve to a set or the action checks through I’ll re-evaluate; if I face significant aggression without improvement I’ll fold. Action: call 450.

I hold Kh8h on a flop of As4hTh, giving me the second nut flush draw. The pot has grown to 1800 chips after Flash bet 450 and Minni called. I am facing a bet of 450, offering me pot odds of roughly 4.5 to 1 (getting 20% on a call). With a flush draw, I have approximately 36% equity to improve by the river, and roughly 19% to hit on the turn, which justifies a call based on direct odds alone. Raising here is risky given Minni's call (showing strength) and Flash's c-bet into two opponents; it would bloat the pot out of position without a made hand. The best play is to call, closing the action and seeing a turn card for a good price.

I’m first to act on the turn with 6♣6♥ on A♠ 4♥ T♥ J♣. I missed — I’m an underpair with no meaningful draw and I’m out of position against two opponents whose continuing ranges contain many A/T/J and broadway hands that beat me. Leading or check-raising OOP in a three‑way pot would bloat the pot with a weak hand and put me in very difficult spots facing aggression. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and avoid bloating the pot OOP. Plan: fold to any sizable turn shove or large bet, consider calling only very small turn bets for pot‑odds, and re-evaluate if action checks through to the river or I improve to a set.

I hold Kh8h on a turn board of As 4h Th Jc. The pot is 2250. Action checked to me in the Big Blind after Minni (SB) checked. I have a very strong draw: a King-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (a Queen makes the Broadway straight). This gives me roughly 12 outs. Despite the strong equity, leading (donk betting) into the pre-flop raiser (Flash) who c-bet the flop is risky and unbalanced. The board texture favors the aggressor's range (Ax, broadway cards). Checking is the standard play to induce a bet or see a free river. If Flash bets, I will likely have the odds to call. Checking allows me to realize my equity with minimal risk.

On the turn, I have top pair with an Ace and the nut flush draw. Both opponents checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet. The Jack of Clubs is a slight scare card as it could complete a straight for KQ or improve Jx hands, but my overall hand strength remains very high. I want to continue betting for value against weaker Aces, Tens, and straight draws. A bet of 1100 (approximately half the pot) allows me to maintain the initiative and build a pot that I have a great chance of winning, while also providing protection against random river cards.

Fold. I set‑mined preflop and called a small flop c‑bet to try to hit a set, but I completely missed on the turn. There are only 2 outs to make a set on the river (2/46 ≈ 4.35% equity). The pot after Flash’s 1,100 turn bet would be 4,450, so calling 1,100 requires ~24.7% equity to break even (1,100 / 4,450). My raw equity to improve is far below that, and I’m OOP in a three‑way pot against ranges that include many A/T/J and broadway hands that already beat me. Raising as a bluff OOP here is a losing line and would bloat the pot with a very weak hand. With deep stacks I wanted implied odds preflop, but on the turn with one card to come and virtually no fold equity, folding is the best +EV play to preserve chips for better spots.

On the turn, the pot is 3350 (including Flash's 1100 bet) and I need to call 1100. This offers me pot odds of approximately 24.7%. My hand [Kh 8h] on the board [As 4h Th Jc] gives me a King-high flush draw (9 outs) and a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight (Any Queen, 3 non-heart outs). Total outs = 12. With 46 unknown cards, my equity is 12/46 = ~26%. Since my equity (26%) is greater than the pot odds required (24.7%), this is a profitable call. Additionally, implied odds exist if I hit the straight or flush against an aggressive opponent who has double-barreled.

I arrived at the river with a missed flush draw and missed straight draw, holding only King-high on a board of As 4h Th Jc 7d. The pot is 4,450 chips. My opponent, Flash, has shown significant strength by raising pre-flop and firing bets on both the flop and turn into multiple opponents. The river card (7d) is a brick that does not improve my hand or likely change the board texture significantly to scare the opponent. Leading out as a bluff is low-percentage because my line (check-call, check-call) strongly signals a draw or a marginal made hand, and an aggressive opponent with a large stack is likely to call with any top pair or better. Checking allows me to get to showdown for free if the opponent gives up on their own bluffs, though I must be prepared to fold to any bet as King-high has negligible showdown value against a value betting range.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Sevens) on the river. My opponent, Pro, has check-called both the flop and the turn and has now checked to me on the river. This passive line strongly suggests a one-pair hand, most likely an Ace with a weaker kicker or a Ten. While some two-pair combinations (AJ, AT) or a straight (KQ) beat me, I expect those hands would often lead out or raise at some point to protect their equity on such a coordinated board. By betting approximately half the pot, I am targeting value from weaker Aces (like A9, A8, A5) that are likely to call one last bet. If raised, I will have to evaluate the strength of the raise, but a value bet here is the most profitable play against a range capped at one pair.

I arrived at the river with King-high (Kh8h) on a board of As 4h Th Jc 7d, having missed both my flush draw and my gutshot straight draw. The opponent, Flash, has demonstrated significant strength by opening pre-flop and betting all three streets (triple barreling) into multiple opponents. The river bet of 2225 into a pot of 4450 offers me roughly 3:1 pot odds, but my hand has virtually zero showdown value against a range that includes strong Aces, two pairs, sets, and the completed broadway straight (KQ). Calling with King-high hoping for a pure bluff is losing poker in this spot given the heavy action. Representing the straight with a bluff-raise is also ill-advised as my passive line (check-calling) heavily implies a draw that missed or a marginal made hand, and I am unlikely to fold out value hands. Preserving my remaining stack is the correct move.

3-handed on the button with K♣ J♦ — a very playable, positionally-advantaged hand. In this spot I want to open a wide, standard 3-handed stealing range to exploit position and get heads-up or isolate the SB. Flash (BB) is deep and capable of 3-bets, Pro (SB) is shorter (≈67bb), so I’ll size to discourage cheap flops and pick up fold equity while leaving myself plenty of postflop maneuvering room. A 3x open to 300 accomplishes that: it’s large enough to make marginal blinds fold and not so large as to commit me unnecessarily if I face a big 3-bet. If I’m called I’ll play aggressively in position; if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and ranges (call or fold vs jam depending on odds). Amount is total chips to commit preflop (includes the current blinds).

I hold Pocket Nines in the Small Blind facing a Button raise from the chip leader. 3-handed, this is a strong hand well ahead of the opener's range. I have two main options: 3-bet or call. A 3-bet to ~1000 would isolate the opener and take initiative, but playing a large pot Out of Position (OOP) against the deep-stacked chip leader carries significant risk, especially if faced with a 4-bet. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, keeps the opponent's range wide (including weaker hands I dominate), and gives me the flexibility to navigate post-flop or set-mine. Given my stack depth (67 BB) is the shortest of the three, a lower-variance approach of flat-calling is prudent to preserve my stack while realizing my equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c7c. Minni (Button) opened to 300 and Pro (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 700, and I need to call 200 more. This gives me pot odds of 4.5:1. 97s is a strong speculative hand that plays well in multi-way pots. Given the excellent price and the playability of suited connectors, calling to see a flop is the most mathematically sound and strategic play. Raising (squeezing) is unnecessary and high-variance against the chip leader and a short stack, while folding would be far too tight given the odds.

I have flopped a set of Nines (Middle Set) on a dry rainbow board of Ah-4d-9s. This is a monster hand. The Ace on the board interacts very well with the pre-flop raiser's (Minni) range, making a continuation bet highly likely. Checking is the standard optimal play here to induce bluffs or extract value from Ax hands. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out the opponent's air and define my hand strength too early. My goal is to maximize value, and the best way to do that is to let the aggressor bet.

I have middle pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board. Since I'm out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and the small blind has already checked, checking is the most prudent action. I want to see how Minni reacts to the flop before committing more chips. My hand has some showdown value but is crushed by any Ace. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a turn for free.

I opened to 300 preflop and both defenders checked to me on A♥4♦9♠. This is a three‑way pot and the board heavily favors callers’ ranges (Ax, small pairs, 9x, some broadways) more than my K♣J♦, which has no pair and very little backdoor equity. Multi‑way continuation bets have poor fold equity and often get called; betting here risks bloating the pot out of position against hands that dominate me or will call down. I prefer pot control and to realize my equity in position — check and evaluate the turn. If checked to again I can still barrel a safe turn card or take a free card to showdown; if an opponent leads I’ll reassess based on sizing and tendencies (call a small probe, fold or raise as a polarized play only on appropriate runouts).

I turned a full house (Nines full of Aces) on a board of Ah-4d-9s-As. The flop was checked through, which suggests the Pre-Flop Raiser (Minni) likely does not have a strong Ace, or is trapping. The turn Ace is a card that interacts heavily with ranges; if an opponent held a weak Ace, they now have trips and will likely bet for value if checked to. If they have air, checking gives them an opportunity to bluff representing the Ace. Betting out likely folds out all hands that don't have an Ace or a robust pocket pair. To maximize value and potentially induce bluffs or bets from trip Aces, checking is the optimal line.

The board has paired the Ace, making it less likely that an opponent holds one since both players checked the flop. My pair of Nines is currently the second-best pair. By checking, I am controlling the pot size and looking to reach a cheap showdown. Since I checked the flop with this hand, checking the turn maintains my range's deception and avoids over-committing against a potential slow-played Ace or a better kicker.

Check. I have K♣J♦ on A♥4♦9♠A♠ — no pair, no meaningful redraws, and this is a three‑way pot. Both opponents checked the flop and turn, which could be weakness but also could be a trap with an Ace or a medium pair; multiway bluffing on a paired‑Ace board has very poor fold equity. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position and get called by most hands that beat me (Ax, 9x, pairs) while folding only the worst holdings. I’ll control the pot and see a free river card; if faced with a lead I’ll fold to large pressure (or call a very small probe only if the sizing and opponent suggest lightness). If checked to on the river I’ll re‑evaluate based on the river card and opponents’ tendencies (small blocker bluff on a safe card or check down if action remains passive).

I hold Nines Full of Aces on a board of Ah-4d-9s-As-3d. The flop and turn were checked through by all players, which is extremely passive given the board texture. This strongly implies that neither the pre-flop raiser (Minni) nor the Big Blind (Flash) holds an Ace, as they would likely have bet for value or protection on earlier streets. Their ranges are likely weighted towards underpairs (KK-TT, 88-55) or air. If I check, it is highly probable the river checks through, and I lose the opportunity to extract value from these mid-strength hands. A small value bet is the best play to target pocket pairs that might hero-call, thinking I am stabbing at the pot. A size of 400 (just under half pot) is small enough to induce calls from weaker pairs while extracting value.

I have a pair of Nines on an Ace-Ace-Nine-Four-Three board. Given that both the flop and turn were checked by all players, it is highly unlikely that anyone is holding an Ace. Pro's lead on the river for 400 into a 900 pot (offering me 3.25:1 odds) looks like a value bet with a smaller pair or a bluff to steal a dry pot. My Nines represent a very strong hand in this specific line of action. While Minni is still to act behind me, her checks also suggest she doesn't have a piece of the board or a slow-played monster. I will call to see the showdown, as my hand is likely the best here and the price is right.

Board: A♥ 4♦ 9♠ A♠ 3♦. I hold K♣ J♦ — no pair and only K-high (one diamond doesn't make a flush). This is a multiway pot (both villains checked flop and turn) so my bluff equity is very poor. Pro's small river probe (400 into ~1700) and Flash's call look like thin value or showdown-calling hands (A-x, pocket pair, 9x, or stubborn middling hands). Raising to 800 would rarely fold out better hands and would risk extra chips with almost no fold equity; folding surrenders the pot when a cheap call can realize any tiny equity I have. The pot odds are roughly 400/(1700+400) ≈ 19% to break even — calling is inexpensive and the correct low-variance line to see the showdown. I will call the 400.

I am on the Button with Pocket Sixes (6s, 6c). In a 3-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. I want to play the pot in position. A min-raise or slightly larger is standard here; I will raise to 250 chips (2.5 BB) to put pressure on the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if I get called.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 2h. While the hand is suited, it is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker. Pro has raised from the button to 2.5bb. Although I am getting 2:1 on a call, playing 7-high trash out of position against the original raiser and a potentially aggressive Big Blind is a recipe for losing chips on later streets. With a healthy stack and many hands left in the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a weak range of cards.

I’m in the big blind with T♥5♥ facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, 150 to call). In 3-handed play the button’s opening range is wide and T5s has reasonable equity plus backdoor heart/straight potential. The call requires ~27% equity to be +EV and this hand has enough playability to realize that equity post‑flop. I’ll defend here rather than 3‑bet with a marginal hand out of position — call to see a flop and proceed only with meaningful pairs/draws or use pot control if I miss.

I flatted preflop with T♥5♥ for playability and hit top pair on 4♣-9♠-T♦. In 3‑handed play the button’s open is wide, so his continuing range contains many worse tens, 9x and broadway hands that will call a reasonable bet. Betting for value and protection is the right play here — I want to charge worse pairs and deny correct odds to draws/overcards. I’ll size up around half the pot to ~300 chips (~55% pot): big enough to get value and fold equity, but small enough to keep dominated hands in. Plan: if called, re-evaluate the turn and extract more value or control the pot; if he check‑raises large I’ll give strong credit to two‑pairs/sets and proceed cautiously (fold to a committed shove or re‑evaluate to a small raise).

I hold Pocket Sixes (6c, 6s) on a flop of 4c-9s-Td. The board texture is quite connected and hits the Big Blind's defending range well (containing many Tx, 9x, and straight draws). Minni leads out (donk bets) for 300 into a pot of 550. My hand is currently an underpair to the top two board cards. Against a value range (Tx, 9x, two pair), I am drawing very thin (2 outs). Against draws (QJ, J8, 87), I am flipping at best or behind. Given the board texture and Minni's lead, it is difficult to continue profitably with a low pair that has little potential to improve. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with T4 offsuit. This is a very weak hand, and while it might be tempting to steal the blinds, Pro is relatively short-stacked and might look to shove over an opening raise. Given the high blinds and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Holding 3♣5♦ in the small blind is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with very poor post‑flop playability. The button folded so I’m heads‑up versus the big blind (Pro, 7,800). Calling 50 to see a flop is cheap but will put me out of position with a hand that rarely realizes enough equity and creates difficult decisions against a competent opponent. Raising here would be a thin bluff and unnecessary given deep stacks and a solid big blind to target later. Preserve my 30,639 stack and avoid a marginal spot — fold and concede the posted SB.

Open with a standard button raise. A♣3♣ is a strong speculative/value hand in 3‑handed play — good nut‑suit potential and lots of postflop playability in position. Both opponents have shown frequent preflop activity but are not ultra‑tight, so a 3x BB open (300 total) applies pressure to the blinds and gives us fold equity while keeping the pot manageable versus 3‑bets. Plan: if called, play aggressively in position — c‑bet on ace/club/connected boards, pot‑control on dry/hostile runouts. If we face a large 3‑bet (big sizing or polar 4‑bet action), fold to preserve our deep stack; we’ll consider calling smaller 3‑bets to leverage position and our suited ace.

I am in the Small Blind facing a 3BB raise from the Button (Minni). My hand, 3h5h, is a weak suited one-gapper. While it has some post-flop playability, playing out of position against the chip leader who raised 3x is generally a losing proposition. The price to call is not compelling enough to overcome the positional disadvantage. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with K8o. Minni, the chip leader, raised to 300 from the Button. In a 3-handed game, a Button opening range is typically very wide. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650, giving me roughly 31% pot odds. K8o is a strong enough hand to defend against a Button open in this spot, as it has decent equity against a wide range and I am closing the action pre-flop.

I have flopped two pair (Kings and Eights) on a board of Kd-Jh-8d. This is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. The board is quite wet with both straight and flush draws available (diamonds, QT, T9). As the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni). If Minni bets, I will likely look to check-raise to get value from weaker Kings, Jacks, and draws, while also protecting my hand from various turn cards that could complete a draw. Checking allows me to keep my range wide and potentially trap an aggressive opponent.

I opened to 300 from the button and the BB (Flash) just checked — pot is 650. Flop K♦ J♥ 8♦ is a fairly connected, high card flop with two diamonds. I hold A♣3♣: I missed but have the Ace blocker (reduces some Ax combos) and I’m last to act, so I can seize the initiative. My preflop range contains many missed broadways and A‑x hands, so a continuation bet here will fold a lot of weaker hands (Qx, Txs, small pairs) and charge diamond draws, while keeping the pot manageable if I get called by Kx/Jx/pairs. I’ll bet for fold equity but size conservatively to avoid bloating the pot with just Ace‑high — ~1/2 pot is appropriate. If I face a sizeable check‑raise I’ll give it up (I have no pair); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on turn (give up on diamond/face cards, control pot on bricks).

I have flopped top and bottom pair (Kings and Eights) on a very wet board with both straight and flush draws. This is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Since I checked the flop and Minni bet, I should now check-raise for value and protection. The board texture (Kd-Jh-8d) is highly coordinated, meaning there are many turn cards that could be problematic for my hand. By raising now, I extract value from weaker Kings, Jacks, and various draws while making it more expensive for Minni to realize her equity. I will raise to 1000, which is roughly a pot-sized raise.

I c‑bet the K♦ J♥ 8♦ flop to take the initiative, but Flash’s large check‑raise to 1000 (I must call 700, min‑raise to 1700) strongly represents value (top/second pair, sets) and strong diamond draws. I have A♣3♣ with no pair and no diamond draw — only an Ace blocker and weak backdoor possibilities — so my realistic equity versus a polarized check‑raise range is well under the ~26% pot‑odds threshold (700 to win ~2650). Raising would bloat the pot out of position with essentially air, and calling just to float is too expensive given likely turn/river pressure. Preserve stack for better spots and fold.

I am on the Button with King-Three offsuit (K3o) in a 3-handed game. My stack is 7900 (79 BBs), while my opponents in the blinds are deep-stacked (220 BB+). K3o is a very weak hand that falls outside the standard profitable opening range, even from the button, typically requiring K5o+ or better. Given that the blinds (Flash and Minni) have been active and willing to defend or raise, opening this hand is likely to lead to a negative expected value situation where I am forced to fold to a 3-bet or play a weak hand out of position post-flop if the SB calls. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 5c, a very weak starting hand. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt a blind steal against Minni (the Big Blind), Minni currently holds a large stack and has shown a willingness to defend and play aggressively in previous hands. Playing such a marginal hand out of position for the remainder of the pot is likely -EV. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with 7-4 offsuit. While ranges are wider in short-handed play, 7-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands and generally not worth an open, especially with a chip leader in the small blind (Minni) and a relatively healthy stack in the big blind (Pro) who can still play back at me. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Button (Flash) folded so this is effectively heads-up vs the Big Blind. A♣4♥ is a strong heads‑up hand and I want to take the initiative rather than limp and allow the BB to see a cheap flop with a wide range. I also have the largest stack at the table so I can apply pressure and isolate the shorter BB. A modest but meaningful raise gives good fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if I face resistance. I’ll raise to 600 total (6x the BB) to open the pot and define ranges — I can cede or pot‑control postflop if I meet aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with [5d Jc] facing a large raise to 600 (6 BBs) from the Small Blind. My hand is very weak (Jack-high offsuit). The pot odds are unfavorable, requiring me to call 500 to win a pot of 1200 (requiring ~42% equity), and J5o performs poorly even against a wide stealing range. The large raise size makes this an easy fold to preserve my stack.

On the button in a 3-handed pot with 6d2d — a very weak but suited holding. In short‑handed play the button should open a very wide range to exploit position and collect the blinds. I’ll open-raise to 300 (≈3x BB): the size gives good fold equity against the small-stacked SB and prevents the BB from seeing a cheap flop too often, while keeping the commitment reasonable given our deep stack. If I face a large 3‑bet I will fold this hand (I don’t want to get stacked off with 6‑2s); if called I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet and/or realize my backdoor flush/straight equity and proceed cautiously. Overall this is a small, +EV steal attempt rather than an attempt to build a huge pot with junk.

Facing a 3x open from the Button (Minni) while in the Small Blind with roughly 77 big blinds. The opponent is active and likely opening a wide range.
