Ace-Eight suited is a strong enough hand to continue with. A 3-bet is a viable option to take the initiative or fold out equity, but given the stack depth and being out of position against a chip leader, it risks bloating the pot or facing a 4-bet. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to realize equity with flush potential and top pair possibilities for a cheaper price. I will defend by calling.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad 8s. Minni raised from the button to 300 and Pro called from the small blind. I need to put in 200 more to call, getting 3.5:1 pot odds. A8o is a strong enough hand to defend my blind in a 3-handed game. While a 3-bet squeeze is an option, calling allows me to see a flop with a respectable hand and keeps the pot size controlled while playing out of position against both opponents.

I have flopped top pair with a mediocre kicker (Ace-Eight) on a very wet, broadway-heavy board (As Ts Jh). This texture connects strongly with the ranges of both the Button (Minni) and the Big Blind (Flash). Being out of position in a multi-way pot, leading out (donk betting) is generally a mistake as it likely only gets called by better hands or strong draws, while folding out worse hands. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser to see their action and control the size of the pot. I will check and re-evaluate based on the betting action behind me.

I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (8) is weak and the board is extremely coordinated (As Ts Jh), offering many straight and flush draws. In a three-way pot, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and the small blind (Pro) with a marginal top pair is risky. Checking allows me to see how Minni reacts to the flop and keeps the pot size manageable while I evaluate the strength of my hand against their ranges.

I raised wide from the button preflop and both opponents checked the flop — that shows weakness or pot-control rather than strong value. The flop A♠ T♠ J♥ is hostile to my actual holding (6♦2♦) — I have virtually no showdown/clear equity and no spade blocker — so this is a pure continuation-bluff spot to try to take the pot now. With two opponents I’ll use a small, credible c-bet (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping my risk low. If I face a meaningful raise I’ll fold (I don’t want to get stacked off with junk); if I get called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and give up unless the turn is very favorable for a bluff or I pick up real equity. Bet 300 to represent strength and try to take it down now.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with an 8 kicker on a wet, broadway-heavy board (As Ts Jh). The Button (Minni) has made a small continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 900. This bet size (1/3 pot) is common on this texture with a wide range, including better hands, draws, and weaker made hands. My hand is certainly strong enough to continue given the excellent pot odds (4:1) and the fact that I am ahead of many semi-bluffs and weaker Aces in the Button's range. Raising would likely isolate me against hands that beat me (AJ, AT, AK, Two Pair, Sets) while folding out hands I dominate. Folding is incorrect with Top Pair for this price. Therefore, calling is the most prudent action to keep the opponent's range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

On a very wet board of As-Ts-Jh, I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (8) is weak. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 300 into a 900 pot, and Pro has called from the small blind. The pot is now 1500, and I need to call 300, giving me 5:1 pot odds. While this board is dangerous because it hits many broadway ranges, folding top pair to a small bet with such good odds would be too tight. Raising is risky because I'm likely to only be called by better Aces or strong draws that have significant equity. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn develops. I will proceed with caution if a King, Queen, Nine, or Spade appears.

I have improved to Trip Aces on the turn with the [Ac] landing. The board is now [As Ts Jh Ac]. While trips is a strong hand, my kicker (8) is mediocre, and the board is very coordinated. The presence of broadway cards (T, J, A) means full houses (AJ, AT, JJ, TT) and straights (KQ) are well within my opponents' ranges, especially given the pre-flop action and multi-way nature. Leading out (donk betting) here is generally suboptimal as it folds out bluffs and isolates me against better hands like better Aces or boats. Checking is the standard line to pot control and induce bluffs or allow opponents to value bet worse hands (though unlikely). I will check and evaluate the action.

The turn card is the Ace of Clubs, giving me trip Aces. While this is a very strong hand, the board is extremely coordinated (As-Ts-Jh-Ac). A straight is already possible with KQ, and I am losing to any Ace with a better kicker (AK, AQ), as well as full houses (AJ, AT, JJ, TT). Given that Pro (SB) has checked and I am out of position against Minni (the pre-flop aggressor), checking is the best play. It allows me to control the pot size and see if Minni continues her aggression, while avoiding a large commitment with a weak kicker on such a dangerous board.

Check. I have no meaningful equity with 6♦2♦ and the turn (A♣) makes my flop story weaker — anyone who had an Ace on the flop now has trips and is very unlikely to fold to a turn double-barrel. Both opponents checked the turn, which could be either weakness or a trap, but I have no Ace or spade blockers to credibly represent the strong hands they can have and very little fold equity when facing two callers. Betting here risks getting raised or called by better hands; checking controls the pot (pot = 1,800) and keeps my stack intact (29,589). I’ll check to the river and re-evaluate: if the river is a clear scare card and both opponents show weakness I can consider a bluff, but I’ll fold to meaningful river pressure unless I pick up real equity or a very strong read.

I hold Trip Aces (Ah 8h) on a final board of [As Ts Jh Ac 9h]. My 8 kicker is counterfeited by the board, meaning my best five-card hand is A-A-A-J-T. I am currently chopping with any weaker Ace (A2-A7) but losing to any Ace with a better kicker (AK, AQ), any Full House (AJ, AT, A9, JJ, TT, 99), and the very likely straight (KQ). Given the pre-flop and flop action, KQ is well within my opponents' ranges. Minni checking back the turn suggests they might not have a monster, but betting the river provides little value as worse hands (like a lone Jack or Ten) will likely fold on this scary board, and better hands will call or raise. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply or evaluate a bet if one is made, potentially inducing bluffs from missed draws.

I have trip Aces with a Jack-Ten kicker (A-A-A-J-T). However, the board is extremely coordinated. Any King and Queen together make a Broadway straight, and any Ace with a better kicker (K, Q) beats me. Additionally, pocket pairs like JJ or TT would have full houses. Given the action, Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) checked the turn, which could indicate a trap or a hand that lost its value. Pro has checked every street. In a three-way pot on such a dangerous board, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and likely only to be called or raised by hands that beat me. Checking to realize my equity at showdown is the safest and most professional play here.

Pot is 1,800 and I have 6♦2♦ — no showdown value. I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop (As Ts Jh) and got called by both; the turn paired the board with another Ace (Ac) and both opponents checked. Multiway on a paired Ace board strongly favors calling ranges (Ax trips, slowplays, two‑pair/full house combos and reasonable showdown hands). My river (9h) neither improves me nor provides good blockers, so a river bet has very little fold equity and just risks getting raised or called by better hands. I check to control the pot and preserve my stack; if either opponent fires a meaningful river bet I will fold to pressure.

Current hand is J9o on the Button in a 3-handed game. While the Button allows for a wide opening range (typically top 40-50% of hands), J9o sits on the borderline of this range. Given the deep stacks of the opponents in the blinds (Flash and Minni) who cover me significantly and have shown willingness to defend, opening this hand carries reverse implied odds and risks bleeding chips in a spot with marginal equity. Folding is a zero-EV play that preserves the stack for better opportunities with higher playability.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Five suited. Since the Button has folded, it's just me and the Big Blind (Minni). K5s is a strong hand in a heads-up blind battle. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and build a pot with a hand that has good equity and post-flop playability.

Holding T♠J♠ in the big blind vs a 3x SB open (300) is a strong, playable heads-up hand. Flash has been opening fairly often from the small blind, so his range is wide — JTs does well against that range and has excellent postflop playability and blockers to some of his broadway combos. I don't want to limp and play a big pot out-of-position; a 3‑bet takes the initiative preflop, generates fold equity, and narrows his range. With deep effective stacks (≈29k vs 22k) I can make a meaningful 3‑bet without committing myself — I’ll raise to 1,200 total (≈4x the open). This sizing is large enough to force folds from many weak holdings and keep worse hands in to extract value, and if he calls I can c‑bet favourable flops and proceed postflop with a plan; I’ll re-evaluate/fold to a serious 4‑bet shove if necessary.

I raised to 300 from the Small Blind with Ks5s, and Minni (the Big Blind) responded with a large 4x 3-bet to 1200. K5s is a hand with decent equity and playability, but playing it out of position against a significant 3-bet sizing from a deep-stacked opponent is difficult. While Minni has shown aggression, this specific sizing suggests a strong linear range or a polarized range that I'd rather not gamble against with a marginal King-high hand. I'll preserve my stack and look for better spots.

I am on the button with 5h 4s, which is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. While the button offers a good stealing opportunity, both opponents (Minni and Pro) have shown reasonable resistance and aggression in previous hands. Pro is starting to get short-stacked (80bb), increasing the likelihood of a three-bet shove over a steal attempt. Given the low equity of 54o, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

I’m the button (small blind) heads-up with Q♠8♥. In heads-up play the button has postflop position, so opening a wide range is correct. Q8o is playable and I want the initiative — a min-raise to 200 (total) pressures the big blind, keeps the pot manageable, and balances my opening range. Plan: if Pro calls I’ll C-bet appropriate flops and leverage position; if he 3-bets to a large sizing that commits a meaningful portion of stacks I’ll fold Q8o (behind range). A standard small raise here maximizes fold equity while minimizing risk.

I am in the Big Blind facing a min-raise from the Small Blind (Minni). The pot is 300 and I need to call 100 more, offering me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity required). My hand, 6s5c, has approximately 36-38% equity against a wide Small Blind opening range. Additionally, I will have position on the raiser post-flop. Given the excellent price and positional advantage, folding would be incorrect. A call is the standard play.

I have Q♠8♥ on a Q♥–7♦–3♠ flop: top pair with a decent kicker and I opened preflop (I have position). This board is very dry and heavily favors my raising range, so a continuation bet for value / protection is the right play. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot to get value from worse Qx and small pairs while denying free cards to backdoor draws, without overcommitting if I face a big raise. Plan: if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and control the pot; if he check-raises large I’ll be cautious and fold to excessive pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 6s. The board is 7d 3s Qh. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4) which gives me 4 outs (approx. 8-9% equity on the turn). The opponent (Minni) bet 200 into a pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds, which requires ~25% equity to call profitably based on pot odds alone. Although I have position, my hand strength is very low (5 high), and the Queen hits the Small Blind's raising range well. Minni is an aggressive chip leader who is likely to fire multiple barrels, making a float with a weak gutshot unprofitable. The equity gap is too large to justify a call.

I'm on the button with Q9o in a 3-handed pot — a playable hand with great positional advantage. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than just calling and playing passively out of position postflop. A standard 3x open to 300 gives me fold equity against the SB/BB, denies cheap multiway pots, and still leaves deep stacks for postflop maneuvering. Raising only to the minimum (200) is too small and calling 100 surrenders initiative. I raise to 300 to build the pot when I have position and to narrow opponents' defending ranges.

I hold [3h 7s] in the Small Blind, facing a raise to 300 from Minni on the Button. This is a very weak hand (7-3 offsuit) with poor playability and no connectivity. Being out of position against an aggressive Button opener with such a weak holding makes calling unprofitable. The pot odds do not justify a call given the hand's low equity against even a wide range. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind against a button open. Minni has been active and aggressive, but since I am closing the action and the Small Blind has already folded, calling is a standard and safe play to see a flop without bloating the pot out of position.

On a King-high board like Kh-5d-4d, the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) has a range advantage, as they are more likely to hold high-value cards including Kings. With only Ace-high and being out of position, the most prudent move is to check to the button to see their continuation bet. Leading here would likely be met with a raise or a call from better hands, so I will play defensively and check.

I opened to 300 preflop and we’re heads-up — my raising range is wide and I should continue to apply pressure. The flop K♥ 5♦ 4♦ is fairly dry but has two diamonds (draws) — it favors a preflop raiser’s range (lots of broadways and Kx). My actual holding Q♣9♠ has little showdown value and no diamond, so this is a bluff/semibluff spot where I want fold equity. I’ll c‑bet about half the pot to charge draws and fold out weaker unpaired hands while keeping the size manageable with deep stacks. If I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (fold to a sizable check‑raise; proceed cautiously vs aggression; continue barreling only if the turn helps my range or gives a good bluffing card). Action: bet ~1/2 pot to maximize immediate EV and preserve manoeuvrability postflop.

On this King-high board (Kh-5d-4d), the texture heavily favors Minni's pre-flop button-raising range, which contains all the strongest Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) and pocket pairs. My Ace-Eight offsuit has very little equity here; I have no backdoor flush or straight draws to support a float. While Ace-high might occasionally be ahead of a complete air-ball continuation bet, being out of position against an aggressive player like Minni makes it difficult to realize that equity on later streets. Calling here would often lead to folding on the turn to a second barrel or being forced into an expensive and uncertain bluff-catch. The most disciplined play to maximize my stack in the long run is to fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

I am on the Button with 4h 5s, a very weak off-suit connector (bottom 20% of hands). With a stack of 78.5 BBs, I am the shortest stack against two deep-stacked aggressive opponents (Flash and Minni). Raising here to steal the blinds is risky as they can easily call or 3-bet, and this hand plays poorly post-flop. Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 7d, which is mathematically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). Minni is the chip leader and has shown significant aggression in previous hands. While it only costs 50 more to call, playing 27o out of position against a larger, aggressive stack is likely to lead to further losses post-flop. There is no reason to risk chips with such a poor hand at this stage of the tournament.

In a 3-handed game, holding Qd7d on the button is a standard opening hand. My stack is healthy at over 200 BBs, and while Minni is the chip leader and aggressive, opening from the button allows me to exert pressure and take control of the pot early. A 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and standard for this structure.

I’m in the small blind with K♥8♦ facing a button open to 300. Stacks are deep (I have ~30k, Button ~21k, BB ~7.7k) so calling 250 more (300 total committed including my 50 SB) is cheap and sensible. K8o is a marginal hand out of position but has reasonable equity versus a wide button opening range and can hit flops where I can play for value or fold cheaply. I don’t want to inflate the pot with a 3-bet bluff (bad blockers, BB can squeeze, and I’ll be OOP postflop), and folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression when missed, continue/raise when I pair the king or make strong draws, and exploit favorable turn/river spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Js 3h facing a button raise to 300 and a call from the Small Blind. The pot is 700 and I need to call 200, offering me pot odds of roughly 22%. Although the price is attractive, J3o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially in a multi-way pot where I will be out of position relative to the initial raiser. My equity realization will be low due to the disconnected nature of the hand and the risk of domination (kicker trouble). With a stack of 77.5 BBs, it is better to preserve chips than to speculate with bottom-tier holdings.

Check. I called preflop with K♥8♦ to see a flop and completely missed — the board is Ad 7c Ah which heavily favors the button’s opening range (lots of A‑x, some 7x/full houses, etc.). I’m out of position with no pair and no realistic draw, so leading as a bluff is low EV (I don’t block Ax combos and a bet will be raised or called by many better hands). Best line is pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression, re-evaluate on the turn (if Flash checks back I can take a free card or make a small delayed stab on a safe blank; if he bets I’ll concede most of the time).

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a board with two Aces. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a continuation bet is standard here. This board texture is generally favorable for my perceived range. A small bet of roughly 43% of the pot (300 chips) allows me to extract value from weaker hands like smaller pocket pairs or high-card combinations that might call one street, while keeping the pot manageable in case she is slow-playing an Ace.

Board is A♦ 7♣ A♥, pot ~1,000 and Flash (button) bets 300 after my check. I hold K♥8♦ — completely missed. Against a button open and flop c‑bet on a paired‑ace board his continuing range is very weighted to Ax (and the occasional 7x/full). I have no real draw (only two outs to pair K or 8, which still lose to most Ax) and my equity vs his likely range is well below the ~18.8% needed given the price (300 to win ~1,300). Calling to see a turn just commits chips out of position and invites larger turn barrels I'll fold to; a raise would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and bad position. Preserve the stack and fold this spot.

Button with A♣6♠ in a 3-handed spot is a clear open-raise opportunity — position and an Ace blocker make this hand playable as a steal attempt. The pot is 150 and the call is 100; I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to take the initiative, get fold equity, and avoid giving the big blind a cheap flop. 300 is a purposeful size relative to the stacks (I have ~302xx, opponents 7.7k and 21.9k) — big enough to discourage marginal callers but small relative to my stack. Post-flop plan: versus a single caller I’ll c-bet most dry boards and proceed cautiously if I only have Ace-high; versus a sizable 3-bet from SB/BB I’ll usually fold A6o (or decide based on sizing/player), since my kicker is weak and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. Goal: accumulate chips with position while protecting my stack.

I hold Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind, a premium hand. The Button (Minni) has opened to 300, consistent with their loose-aggressive style. Out of position, I prefer to 3-bet for value and isolation rather than just call. This charges the wide range of the button and discourages a squeeze or flat call from the Big Blind. Given the opening size of 300 (3bb) and my stack depth of 77bb, a 3-bet to roughly 3.5x (1050) is an appropriate size to build the pot with a strong hand while navigating position disadvantage.

In this hand, Minni (Button) opened for 300, and Pro (Small Blind) 3-bet to 1,050. I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine of diamonds. While it is a suited hand with some playability, I am facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, who has a relatively short stack of 67BB, suggesting a strong range. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against both players, and Minni still has the option to 4-bet behind me. Given the strength indicated by Pro's 3-bet and the positional disadvantage, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Pro (SB) 3‑bet to 1,050 after my BTN open to 300. I'm on the button so I have position — that swings this decision. The 3‑bet size is moderate (≈10.5 BB) and the current pot is 1,450, so calling the extra 750 (total 1,050 committed) gives ~1.93:1 pot odds (need ~34% equity). A6o is often dominated, but the Ace blocker plus position let me realize equity and play post‑flop. A light 4‑bet invites uncomfortable all‑in scenarios against his ~6.7k stack; folding surrenders too much equity and initiative. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, play the flop in position, c‑bet most dry/A‑ or 6‑touching boards for value/protection, and fold or proceed cautiously to large aggression on later streets if I miss.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with Ace-King high on a Ten-high board (Td 4h 2d). The board texture is somewhat dry but contains a flush draw. While I have a range advantage in overpairs (JJ+), the caller (Minni) connects well with sets (TT, 44, 22) and pairs like AT or Tx. With an SPR of roughly 3, I want to exercise pot control. C-betting might fold out worse hands that I dominate (like KQ, QJ) while isolating me against better hands or check-raises. Checking strengthens my checking range (which protects my overpairs) and allows me to see a turn card cheaply or bluff-catch if Minni stabs with air. Given Minni's loose-aggressive profile, checking to induce action or control the pot size is the prudent play with Ace-high.

Pro checked after his preflop 3‑bet, ceding the initiative. The flop Td‑4h‑2d is very dry and my A♣6♠ has an Ace blocker and good showdown potential in position. A small continuation bet preserves fold equity versus his missed parts of the 3‑bet range and gets value from worse hands (Kx, Qx, small pairs, floats) while keeping the pot manageable against his ~6.7k stack. I’ll size ~1/3–40% pot to 800 into 2,200 — big enough to fold out many hands, cheap enough to call with worse, and easy to release to a significant raise. Plan if called: proceed cautiously on the turn (value bet with an Ace or improvement, check‑control otherwise) and fold to large aggression since my kicker is weak.

I am in a 3-bet pot out of position against a loose-aggressive opponent (Minni) who has called my pre-flop 3-bet. The flop is Td 4h 2d. I checked, and Minni bet 800 into a pot of roughly 2200 (about 36% pot). This small sizing is often used with a wide range including marginal pairs, draws, and air, especially when the pre-flop aggressor checks. I hold Ace-King, which is currently just Ace-high, but I have two overcards and beat all of Minni's semi-bluffs (flush draws, straight draws) and pure bluffs. Crucially, I hold no diamonds, which unblocks Minni's potential flush draw bluffs. Given the pot odds (calling 800 to win roughly 3000), I only need about 21% equity. Even against a pair of Tens, I have live outs, and I am ahead of their bluffs. Folding here would be too weak against this player type for this price. I will call to see a turn card.

I am in the Small Blind in a 3-bet pot with AhKs on a board of Td 4h 2d 8s. I checked and called a small bet on the flop. The turn card (8s) does not improve my hand, leaving me with Ace-high, but it also doesn't complete the obvious diamond flush draw. My opponent (Minni) is loose-aggressive and capable of betting draws or weaker pairs. With an SPR of roughly 1.5, I am in a 'wa/wb' (way ahead / way behind) or bluff-catching scenario. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't make sense as I fold out bluffs and get called by better. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch a bluff. Checking allows me to evaluate my opponent's action; if they check back, I have a chance to win at showdown or hit an Ace/King on the river. If they bet, I will decide based on the sizing, though I am fairly committed to calling reasonable bets given the shallow stack depth.

I check the turn. I c‑bet the very dry Td‑4h‑2d flop for 800 and got called; the 8s on the turn does not improve A♣6♠. Pro checked the turn, which looks like either a made hand (overpair/mid pair/set) or pot‑control with a marginal holding/draw. With only Ace‑high and a weak kicker my priority is pot control and avoiding a spot where I’ll be committed with a poor hand — a turn double‑barrel could fold out some hands but also invites a shove from Pro’s ~5.9k stack that I don’t want to face. Checking preserves my positional advantage, lets me realize equity, and gives one more card to decide on; plan is to call small river bets but fold to large overbets/shoves, and only press for value if I actually pair my Ace or improve.

I arrive at the river in a 3-bet pot out of position with Ace-King high on a board of Td 4h 2d 8s Th. The river pairing the Ten means my hand is effectively one pair of Tens with an Ace-King kicker. This loses to any pocket pair (which makes two pair) and any Ten (trips). My opponent bet the flop but checked back the turn, a line often indicative of a marginal pair looking for a cheap showdown or a draw taking a free card. Leading out here has no value: better hands (pairs/trips) will call, and worse hands (missed flush/straight draws) will fold. My best option is to check, hoping to get to showdown cheaply against missed draws or to evaluate if the opponent decides to bluff.

Board pairs tens on the river (Td-4h-2d-8s-Th). I hold A♣6♠ — Ace-high only with a weak kicker. Pro was the preflop 3-bettor and called my flop c-bet, then checked turn and checked the river: that line is consistent with either a made hand (T, a pocket pair/overpair) or a pot‑control/showdown call with marginal holdings. Betting as a bluff here has limited fold equity against a caller of my flop bet (they can comfortably check-call or call a small bet), and risks being raised by a made hand (trips/overpairs) which I must fold to. Pot control and avoiding a marginal bluff are the priority — I’ll check to showdown and fold to any meaningful raise. If checked through I’ll see the showdown and accept that Ace‑high will lose often but preserve stack equity.

I hold Pocket Aces [Ad, Ac] on the Button, the strongest starting hand. My goal is to build the pot and extract value. I have consistently raised to 250 chips in previous hands (e.g., Hand #74, #54, #34, #2), so raising to 250 keeps my range disguised and encourages action from the blinds who are playing deep stacks. Limping would be suboptimal as it fails to build the pot.

In this 3-handed situation, I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Pro has raised from the Button to 2.5x. While Queen-Nine is a decent hand in a short-handed game, it plays poorly out of position, especially with Minni (the chip leader) still to act in the Big Blind. Calling would likely lead to a difficult post-flop scenario or invite a squeeze from Minni. Given my healthy stack and the strategic disadvantages of my position, I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

I’m in the big blind with Jh7s facing a button open to 250 (pot 400). I only need to commit 150 more (total 250) to see a flop — that gives ~27% break‑even equity. Against a likely wide button raising range this is a defendable price heads‑up. J7o is too weak to 3‑bet out of position as a bluff and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll call to see the flop. Plan: fold to large, sustained aggression on dry boards if I miss; continue/raise with a pair, two pair, or strong draw.

The flop 4c-3c-2s completely misses my J♥7♠ — no pair, no club backdoor, essentially zero clean equity and I'm out of position versus the preflop opener. This is a textbook pot‑control spot: I don't want to bloat the pot with air OOP or bluff into a wide but often connected preflop raising range. I will check to see what Pro does. If he checks back I realize showdown cheaply; if he fires a small probe (≈1/3 pot) I may call to see a turn, but I will fold to large or sustained aggression. I'll only continue aggressively with a made hand or a strong draw on later streets.

I hold Pocket Aces [Ad, Ac] on a [4c 3c 2s] flop. This is an exceptionally strong hand for this texture: I have an overpair, the Nut Flush Draw (holding the Ac), and a gutshot straight draw (any 5 makes a wheel). The board texture is coordinated and connects well with the Big Blind's defending range (sets, two pair, straights like 56 or A5, and pair+draw combos). However, my hand has tremendous equity even against strong made hands. Checking back would be too passive given the draw-heavy board; I want to build the pot and charge inferior draws or pairs (like 4x, 5x, 6x). A bet of 300 (just over half pot) balances value extraction and protection.

I’m OOP with Jh7s on a dry 4c-3c-2s — no pair and no real draw. Pro’s 300 into an 850 pot is a ~1/3-pot probe and gives me ~26% pot odds (300 to win 1,150). My chance to hit a pair by the river (~24–25%) is roughly at the pot‑odds breakeven and Pro’s line is consistent with a wide c‑betting range here. Raising would be a pure bluff OOP with little fold equity and folding surrenders decent implied equity. I’ll call the small bet to see a turn, but will fold to substantial further aggression; I’ll only turn this into a raise/commitment with a made hand (pair+) or a strong draw.

I’m out of position with J♥7♠ on a very dry 4♣-3♣-2♠-9♦ board and there’s currently no bet to me (pot 1,150). I have no pair, no meaningful draw (only 6 outs to make a pair → ~13% to hit on the river), so I have essentially zero realized equity to build a pot OOP. Pro’s line (open preflop, ~1/3‑pot c‑bet on the flop) is consistent with a wide range, and betting as a bluff here would be thin with little fold equity. Pot control is correct: check and see what Pro does. Plan: check, fold to any substantial turn bet, only consider calling a tiny probe; if Pro checks back I see a cheap river and can reassess (bluff or showdown depending on the river card).

I hold Pocket Aces [Ad, Ac] on a Turn board of [4c 3c 2s 9d]. The pot is 1150. After betting the flop and getting called, the board remains very coordinated and dangerous. The 4-3-2 texture heavily favors the Big Blind's defending range, which includes straights (56, A5), sets, and two pairs. While I have an overpair and the Nut Flush Draw (plus a gutshot wheel draw), I want to exercise pot control here. If I bet and get check-raised, I am in a difficult spot despite my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, avoids inflating the pot against potential made straights or sets, and induces bluffs or calls from weaker hands on the river. This line is consistent with my play in Hand #85 where I checked back the turn with top pair.

I check. I have J♥7♠ — no pair, no meaningful draw on 4♣-3♣-2♠-9♦-K♠. Pro opened preflop, c-bet the flop and checked the turn, a line consistent with a wide but often value-heavy range. The King on the river improves a lot of his value combos (Kx, some Ax) and I don’t block Kings, so my fold equity is very thin. Betting as an OOP bluff here is likely negative EV; pot control and avoiding unnecessary chips in a marginal spot is correct. I’ll check and take the cheap showdown; if Pro jams or barrels a meaningful amount I’ll fold.

I hold Pocket Aces [Ad, Ac] on a final board of [4c 3c 2s 9d Ks]. I checked back the turn for pot control. The river King is a high card that doesn't complete the main draws (flush missed, though 5x straight is still possible). Minni checks to me again. Given the coordinated low board (4-3-2), the Big Blind's range contains many straights (holding a 5) or sets/two pairs. A bet here is thin; worse hands (like 9x, 4x, or missed club draws) likely fold, and better hands (straights, sets) will call or raise. Checking back ensures I win against all smaller pairs and missed draws without risking more chips in a spot where I am either way ahead or way behind.

In a 3-handed game, hand ranges are significantly wider than in full-ring games. Holding Queen-Five suited on the button is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down immediately or play it in position. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size in recent hands.

Facing a button open to 300 in a three‑handed pot with K♦7♥ in the small blind. This hand is weak and often dominated by the button’s wide opening range (Kx, broadways, suited connectors). I’ll be out of position postflop against both opponents, and calling 250 to play a ~700 pot OOP gives me marginal equity and puts me in tough spots to defend. 3‑betting as a bluff with K7o is overly risky — it will get called or 4‑bet by better hands and I don’t want to inflate the pot with such a poor holding. With a healthy stack I can wait for a better spot. Fold and preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x raise from the Button (Flash). My hand, Queen-Ten offsuit, is a strong candidate for defense given the pot odds. I need to call 200 chips to win a pot that will be 650, requiring approximately 31% equity. Against a typical wide button opening range in a 3-handed game, QTo performs well enough to justify a call. 3-betting is an option but bloats the pot out of position with a hand that can be dominated, so calling is the prudent, standard play to realize my equity.

On a flop of [Jh 2c Ad], the board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Button), who has all the strong Ax and broadway combos in their range. As the Big Blind defending with [Tc Qd], I have a gutshot straight draw to a King but very little showdown value otherwise. Leading out (donk betting) is strategically unsound here because the opponent connects so well with this board. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If they check back, I can realize equity for free; if they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, though folding is the likely response to a standard c-bet given the weak draw.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on this Ace-high board (A-J-2). This board texture is typically very good for a continuation bet from the button. I'll bet roughly half the pot (300) to represent an Ace or a strong Jack and force a fold from my opponent's air and weaker holdings, while also maintaining a backdoor club flush draw.

On the flop [Jh 2c Ad], I have a gutshot straight draw with [Tc Qd] (needing a King). The board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, connecting with their Ax and Broadway hands. Flash bets 300 into a pot of 650, offering me pot odds of roughly 24%. My immediate equity with a 4-out draw is only about 8-9% to hit on the turn and roughly 16-17% by the river. Being out of position with Queen-high and no backdoor flush potential, I do not have sufficient direct odds or implied odds to profitably call a half-pot bet. Floating to bluff later is difficult given the board favors the opponent's range so strongly. The disciplined play is to fold.

I’m on the button with Kc6c in a 3-handed pot — reasonable position and a hand with some play (king-high and backdoor club equity). Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call; I want to take the initiative, pressure the blinds, and leverage position postflop. I’ll open to 300 (3x the big blind): small enough to keep many worse hands in, big enough to get folds and avoid being trivial to 3-bets. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, use position to c-bet on dry flops, float or check-call on coordinated boards, and extract value when I improve.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 5h, facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Minni). Minni is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively. While 95s has some playability due to being suited and connected, playing it out of position against a deep-stacked aggressor is difficult. The pot odds are roughly 35%, but my equity realization will be significantly lower due to being OOP. Additionally, calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall, further complicating the spot. Conserving my 100BB stack for better opportunities is the standard play here.

I am in the big blind with 8d 3h, which is a very weak hand. Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the button. While it's only 200 more to call, playing a hand with no connectivity or high card value out of position against an aggressive opponent is a recipe for losing chips. I have a healthy stack and there are plenty of hands left to find a better spot.

I am on the Button with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o). While the Button is a strong position in a 3-handed game, K2o is a very weak hand that falls outside of a standard profitable opening range (typically top 40-50% of hands). It suffers from reverse implied odds as it is often dominated by better Kings or Aces if action continues. With effective stacks around 100BB, there is no need to get involved with marginal holdings. The most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind (Minni). While it only costs 50 more to call, Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a chip leader who has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. I prefer to wait for better spots rather than bloating a pot with a marginal hand out of position.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit (KTo). In a 3-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. My goal is to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against one of the blinds. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size, to exert pressure on Minni and Pro.

With A6s in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450), I prefer to 3‑bet rather than limp/call out of position. We’re very deep (≈279bb), A6s is a suited Ace with blocker value, and the BTN’s opening range in 3‑hand is wide — a 3‑bet gains fold equity, isolates the opener, and builds a pot where we can apply pressure post‑flop. A min‑raise to 500 is too small (invites cheap multi‑way pots); I’ll make a standard 3‑bet to 1,200 to price out callers and seize initiative. If I face a large 4‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate, but this sizing is small relative to our stack and maximizes expected value here.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 2s, facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 1200. Q2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots and against 3-bet ranges. With significant action ahead of me and a large bet to call relative to my hand strength, the only correct play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 from the button with King-Ten offsuit (KTo), a standard opening hand in 3-handed play. Minni responded with a large 3-bet to 1200 from the small blind. While I have the advantage of position, a 4x 3-bet sizing from the small blind typically indicates a strong range or an aggressive attempt to shut down the hand. KTo is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range (containing hands like AJ, AQ, KQ, and JJ+), and it lacks the post-flop playability of suited connectors or pocket pairs. Since the investment to call is significant (900 more) and I'd be playing a bloated pot with a marginal hand, I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

I’m on the button with Q♠3♣. With only the blinds to act and very deep effective stacks (I have ~283 BB), it’s profitable to open-steal often — Q3o is marginal but plays fine in position. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) to take the initiative and apply fold pressure to the blinds while risking a very small portion of my stack. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold (Q3o is poor versus 3-bet ranges); if called I’ll C-bet on many dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated/connected boards.

I hold 7c 2c in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Minni). My hand is extremely weak, despite being suited, and ranks near the bottom of starting hand ranges. Being out of position against an aggressor with a trash hand is a losing long-term play. The pot odds do not justify calling with such low equity and playability. Conserving my 98BB stack for better opportunities is the correct move.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni opened from the button with a 3x raise. While the pot odds are okay, playing this hand out of position against a strong opponent like Minni is likely to result in a loss over the long term. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold Ace-Queen suited (AdQd) on the Button, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is roughly 98 BBs. This is a clear value opening raise. I want to build the pot in position and apply pressure to the blinds. A standard open size of 2.5x (250 chips) is consistent with my previous play (e.g., Hand #86 with AA, Hand #55 with AK) and sufficient to isolate or steal the blinds while allowing me to play comfortably post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with J3 offsuit, a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. While the pot odds are roughly 2:1, J3o performs poorly against even a wide Button opening range. Additionally, playing out of position against both the original raiser and a potentially active Big Blind (Minni) is disadvantageous. Given the strength of my hand and my current stack size, there is no reason to defend or bluff here.

Pro opened to 250 from the button heads-up and I’m in the big blind with Qc8d. Pot is 400 and it costs me 150 more (250 total) to see a flop, giving ~27% immediate pot odds — cheap enough to defend. Q8o is playable vs a BTN opening range: decent chance to hit top pair and some backdoor equity, and with deep stacks I can realize implied odds postflop. 3-betting out of position with this offsuit queen is unattractive (limited blockers/fold equity) and folding is too tight. I’ll call to see the flop and proceed: continue with a queen or strong draw, otherwise play cautiously vs heavy action.

Board is 2c 9d 8s; I hold Qc8d — second pair (eights) with a decent queen kicker, out of position vs the button who opened preflop. I planned preflop to play cautiously unless I hit a queen or a strong draw; I didn’t hit a queen and this pair is vulnerable to 9x or sets, so I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position. Checking controls the pot, allows worse hands and bluffs to bet, and keeps my showdown ability vs single-barrel c-bets. Plan if Pro bets: call small-to-medium c-bets (~<=1/2 pot ≈ 250–300) for value; be prepared to fold to large pot-sized bets or raises that represent strong made hands. If checked to on the turn I’ll re-evaluate and look for cheap improvement or a spot to extract value.

I hold AdQd on a 2c 9d 8s flop. This board texture (low-medium connected) connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, straight draws like T7, 76, J10). While I have range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, my specific hand is currently just Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. Betting here risks getting check-raised or called by a range that has good equity against me. Checking back allows me to realize my equity with two overcards and backdoors, controls the pot size, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. I can comfortably call a turn bet on many cards or value bet if I improve.

I hold Qc8d and the turn (8h) gives me trips (8d+8s+8h). It checked to me — pot is only 550 and Villain (button) checked the flop and turn, so he likely has a one-pair hand (9x, 2x), a medium pocket pair, or missed broadways. Checking back would give a free river and lose value from many worse hands; I should extract value and deny a free card. A bet around half the pot (~300) will get calls from one-pair hands and medium pairs while not overcharging and folding out only the weakest hands. If he check-raises big I’ll re-evaluate (trips can comfortably call a reasonable raise and often commit vs a shove), but for now I’ll bet for value and protection.

I hold Ace-Queen suited, but missed the flop and checked back for pot control. The turn pairs the board with an Eight, and the Big Blind leads out for about 55% pot. This lead often signifies a made hand (like a pair of Nines, a pocket pair, or trips) taking advantage of my checked-back flop to deny equity or get value. My hand is currently just Ace-high with no flush draw, leaving me with only 6 outs (Aces and Queens) which may not be clean. Calling relies on hitting these slim outs or bluff-catching, which is not profitable against this line on this texture.

I am on the button with a strong starting hand (Ah 8h) in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 210 big blinds, though Minni covers me. Pro is the short stack with about 94 big blinds. Opening for a standard 3x raise is the most effective play here to capitalize on position and the strength of my hand. This allows me to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position with a hand that has good post-flop potential.

I have AA in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, to call 250). This is the best possible starting hand — I want to build the pot and isolate the opener while denying good odds to suited connectors and speculative hands. Calling is too passive and the minimum reraise to 500 is too small and invites multiway action. I 3-bet to 1200 (~4x the open) to charge calling ranges, gain fold equity, and keep the initiative. I realize I'll be out of position vs the button if he calls, but AA justifies an aggressive 3-bet here. If I face a cold 4-bet I will re-evaluate (shove or call based on sizing/opponent); if called I'll proceed to extract value with a c-bet on favorable flops.

I am holding Jc 4h in the Big Blind. The action started with a raise to 300 from the Button (Flash) followed by a 3-bet to 1200 from the Small Blind (Minni). My hand is extremely weak (unsuited, unconnected, low rank) and I am facing significant aggression. The pot odds are poor for a call, and the likelihood of winning this hand is minimal. I am out of position against a 3-bettor and the original raiser. Folding is the only logical play.

I opened on the button with a strong suited Ace (Ah 8h) and faced a 3-bet from Minni. In a three-handed game, Ah 8h is well ahead of a wide 3-betting range and plays excellently in position. Calling is the best option to keep the pot manageable while utilizing my positional advantage post-flop, especially with the deep effective stacks.

Flop is 7h 6h 9h — a monotone heart board that’s dangerous for my overpair (Ac Ad). I 3‑bet preflop and still have the initiative, so I want to both charge and protect: there are many one‑heart combos and a few two‑heart combos (made flushes) in Button’s calling range, plus some medium pairs/straight draws. Checking here gives the button free initiative and better odds to realize drawing equity; betting charges heart draws and extracts value from single‑pair hands (9s,7s,6s) and weaker broadways. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (1250 into 2500) — big enough to deny correct odds to a lot of draws and get called by worse hands, but small enough not to overcommit if I face heavy aggression. Plan: if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (continue for value/protection or pot‑control depending on the card); if he check‑raises or jams pot‑sized I’ll assume a made flush and re‑evaluate (likely fold to extreme pressure, call smaller raises based on odds and read).

I have the absolute nuts on this flop with the Ace-high flush (Ah 8h) on a 7h-6h-9h board. Since I hold the 8h, it is impossible for my opponent to have a straight flush. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading for half the pot. While I want to build a big pot, raising now on such a wet board might scare away her bluffs or overpairs without a heart. By calling, I keep her entire range in, allowing her to continue her aggression on the turn or to 'catch up' slightly with a hand she might then value-bet. This is the best way to maximize value against an aggressive opponent in a deep-stacked 3-bet pot.

Preflop I 3‑bet to 1,200 with AcAd and Flash flat‑called. I c‑bet the heart‑monotone flop (7h 6h 9h) for 1,250 and was called — that line narrows Flash to a mixture of one‑pair hands, heart combos (some two‑heart flushes, some single hearts), and straight/flush draws. The Turn Ten of Clubs is a double‑edged card: it doesn’t complete hearts but it does complete many straight draws containing an 8 and also gives anyone holding a Ten trips. Despite that, I’m still ahead of a lot of single‑pair hands and want to charge draws and extract value from worse pairs and turned tens. I’ll continue for value/protection with a sizable but non‑committing bet (~60% pot) to deny correct odds to drawing hands and to thin the field. If Flash check‑raises big (pot‑sized or shove) I’ll assume a made nut hand (flush/straight/set) and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

I hold the absolute nuts (Ace-high flush with the 8h, which blocks all possible straight flushes on this board). Minni 3-bet pre-flop and has barreled both the flop and the turn. By calling the flop, I have already begun to disguise the strength of my hand. The 10c on the turn is a relatively blank card, though it does complete some unlikely straights. Minni's 3,000 bet into a 5,000 pot shows significant strength or a committed bluff. If I raise now, I risk folding out her semi-bluffs or one-pair hands that might otherwise fire a third barrel on the river. Given the deep stacks and her aggressive history, calling the turn allows me to keep her entire range in and maximize value on the river, where I can either shove over a bet or bet for value if she checks.

Check. I have AA but the board was a monotone heart flop (7♥6♥9♥) and my button opponent flat‑called my 3‑bet preflop then called a sizable flop c‑bet and a large turn bet — a line that contains a meaningful number of made heart flushes, straights and some strong pairs. The river 3♣ is a blank for my hand (it doesn’t remove the flush) so betting here mostly gets value-called by hands that beat me (flushes/straights) and only thinly extracts worse one‑pair hands. Pot control is correct: checking lets me show down against many of his calling hands while still allowing me to call reasonable river bluffs or small value bets. Plan if he bets: call small–medium probes (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ 3.5k–4k) for good pot odds since many of those will be bluffs or worse pairs; fold to very large bets or shoves (pot‑sized or near it) which are very likely made flushes that beat my hand.

I hold the absolute nuts with an Ace-high flush (Ah 8h) on a board of 7h-6h-9h-Tc-3c. My 8h also blocks the only possible straight flush (8h-5h). Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, 3-betting pre-flop and betting the flop and turn. Her check on the river indicates she is likely in pot-control mode with a strong but non-nut hand like a set, a straight, or a lower flush, or she has given up on a bluff. To maximize value against her calling range, I am placing a large value bet of 8,500 into the 11,000 pot. This sizing is designed to extract maximum chips from her mid-to-strong hands while remaining more likely to be called than an all-in shove.

Fold. I have AcAd on a monotone-heart flop (7h 6h 9h) that turned Tc and rivered 3c. Pot is 19,500 and Flash puts me to 8,500 — I’m getting ~30.4% pot odds (8,500 / (19,500+8,500)). Flash opened the button, flat‑called my 3‑bet preflop, then check‑called a meaningful flop c‑bet on a three‑heart board and a large turn bet that completed many straights (any 8) and made trips for Tens. That line is heavily weighted toward made flushes (many two‑heart combos remain), turn‑completed straights (8x) and trips — hands that crush my AA and leave me with essentially no equity. A smaller river probe (≈≤1/3 pot) I’d consider calling for pot odds because of bluffs/missed draws, but this 8,500 sizing is large enough that the villain’s value frequency is too high to make a call +EV. Raising would only bloat the pot when I’m likely behind and can induce a shove I shouldn’t face. Preserve the stack and fold.

I'm on the button with A♣J♠ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand in a three‑handed pot. With SB (94BB) and BB (271BB) both deep, I want to seize the initiative, build value when I'm ahead, and apply fold equity against the blinds. Calling 100 forfeits that advantage and a min‑raise to 200 is too small and invites wider defense; a standard 3x open to 300 gives good pressure while leaving plenty of stacks for post‑flop play. If I get a 3‑bet I will re‑evaluate based on the sizing and who's showing strength (calling small 3‑bets, folding to big 4‑bets), and if called I'll c‑bet favorable ace‑high boards and control the pot on heavy resistance.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 6h facing a 3x raise from the Button (Minni). My stack is 94 BBs. 86o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. The pot odds (requiring ~36% equity realization) are not attractive enough to call OOP against a deep-stacked aggressive opponent. 3-betting is also ill-advised with this holding. The standard and most profitable play is to fold.

K6s is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind with in a 3-handed game, especially against a Button open. I am getting favorable pot odds of 3.25-to-1 to call the additional 200 chips. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, K6s has decent high-card value and flush potential. Given my current chip lead and the recent history where I won a large pot from Minni, I'll choose to call and see a flop rather than bloat the pot with a 3-bet.

On a board of 7d-9h-8h, I have an open-ended straight draw with my 6. This is a very connected board that likely hits a button opening range well. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. I am out of position and want to keep the pot manageable while I try to realize my equity or potentially improve on the turn.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♣J♠ and picked up a call from the BB. The flop 7♦ 9♥ 8♥ is very connected and two‑toned (two hearts) — it hits the caller’s cold‑calling range (pairs, suited hearts, connected hands) but it’s also a great spot to seize initiative as the preflop aggressor. I have naked Ace‑high with two overcards and no heart; I want fold equity to take the pot now and I want to charge heart/straight draws without committing too much. I’ll make a standard half‑pot continuation bet of 325. If he folds I win the pot. If he calls I will proceed cautiously on the turn — check most blanks and only continue when I pick up meaningful improvement (A or J) or get a clear read to bluff; if he check‑raises large on the flop I will strongly lean to folding because my showdown value is weak and I don’t want to get stacked by a polarized raise.

On a flop of 7d-9h-8h, I have an open-ended straight draw (5-6-7-8-9 or 6-7-8-9-10) using my 6. This board is extremely wet and likely connects with Minni's button opening range, but it also provides me with significant equity. Minni has bet 325 into a pot of 650, giving me 3-to-1 immediate odds to call. With 8 clean outs for a straight (approximately 16% to hit on the turn), and considering implied odds if I hit, calling is the most appropriate action. I am out of position and don't want to bloat the pot with a check-raise given the coordination of the board and the recent history of large pots played with this opponent.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds gives me top pair to go along with my open-ended straight draw. This is a significant improvement to my hand. However, the board is extremely connected (7-8-9), and any Ten or Jack completes a straight, while many of these cards are in a standard button opening range. Leading out might force folds from hands I beat but could lead to a difficult situation if raised by a straight or a better King. Checking allows me to control the pot size, see how Minni reacts to the King, and evaluate on the river. I have plenty of equity to check-call any reasonably sized bet.

I have A♣J♠ on a 7♦‑9♥‑8♥ board and picked up a flop call to my 325 c‑bet. The turn K♦ is a blank for me — it doesn’t pair my hand and it helps a lot of the caller’s calling range (Kx, two‑pairs, some slow‑played 9s/8s). I have no hearts and only ace‑high, so barreling the turn would be committing chips with very little showdown value and limited fold equity; many hands that continue will either call or raise me down. With deep stacks and a pot of 1,300, I prefer pot control and to see a river cheaply where I can apply pressure only if I improve (A or J) or if the board runs out favorably. If my opponent leads out with a large bet on this street I will fold; if we check through I’ll reassess on the river (value bet A/J, bluff selectively on safe runouts, fold to heavy resistance). For those reasons I check back the turn.

The board has become extremely coordinated with 7-8-9-K-Q. I have top pair (Kings), but my kicker (6) is very weak. The turn check from Minni after her flop bet suggests she might have a medium-strength hand like a pair of nines or a draw that she didn't want to semi-bluff. The river Queen is a scare card that completes potential two-pair hands like KQ or improves hands like AQ/QJ. There is very little value in betting here, as worse hands are unlikely to call on such a connected board, and I would be forced to fold to a raise. Checking allows me to reach showdown and potentially win against missed draws or lower pairs that Minni might check back.

I checked the turn with A♣J♠ and never improved — river Q♦ is a blank for me and actually helps the caller’s range (Kx, Qx, 9x/8x, some missed heart draws and the occasional disguised straight). Flash’s line (cold‑call preflop, call a small c‑bet on the flop, check the K on the turn and check the Q on the river) looks like a medium, non‑polar hand or a missed draw rather than a hand I can credibly extract value from. Betting here would be a thin bluff against hands that will call (pairs, K/Q) and will only fold the air I already beat; it’s also a poor value bet. With deep stacks and a small pot (1,300) I prefer to control the pot and take my showdown weight — check and show, fold to heavy action. If Flash leads out I will reassess, but absent a bet I check to showdown.

I am on the Button with Pocket Sevens (7d 7c). This is a strong opening hand from this position. My stack is roughly 94 BBs. Standard strategy dictates opening the pot to steal the blinds or play in position against them. Looking at my previous history (e.g., Hands 2, 74, 86, 92), I consistently open to 250 from the Button. This size denies cheap equity realization to the blinds while keeping the pot manageable for a mid-pair hand. I will raise to 250.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard raise. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, I should 3-bet for value and to take initiative out of position. A raise to 900 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the Button and potentially isolate or win the pot immediately.

AQs is a strong hand in 3-handed play but I'm facing an open from the button and a 3-bet from the small blind. With deep stacks I prefer to call the 800 rather than commit more preflop: calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity postflop, and keeps the button’s range involved (the button may fold to our action). A min-raise to 1550 is too small to meaningfully isolate and a larger 4-bet is unnecessary risk against an aggressive 3-bettor who can 5-bet or call with hands that dominate AQs. Plan: call to see a flop; continue aggressively when I hit an ace/queen or strong spade draw, otherwise play cautiously to fold to heavy pressure.

Pre-flop, I opened with Pocket Sevens and faced a 3-bet from the SB (Flash) and a cold call from the BB (Minni). The pot is currently 2,050 and it costs me 650 to call, offering me excellent pot odds of roughly 24%. While my stack depth (SPR ~3 post-flop) is slightly shallow for pure set-mining, the multi-way nature of the pot and my guaranteed position on both opponents makes this a profitable call. I have enough equity against their ranges and implied odds if I hit a set. Folding would be too tight given the price and position, and 4-betting turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. I will call to close the action and evaluate the flop.

I 3-bet pre-flop and got two callers. The flop (9d-6c-7h) is very coordinated and favors the calling ranges of the Big Blind and the Button more than my 3-betting range. In a 3-way pot, c-betting here with just Ace-high and two overcards is risky. Checking is the most prudent play to see how the action unfolds and to potentially realize equity if the turn is favorable.

I called preflop to keep the pot manageable with AQs vs a button opener and a SB 3-bet. The flop 9d-6c-7h misses my hand (no pair, no spade) and is fairly dry — there are some straight possibilities (5 or 8), but nothing that makes my hand strong. With two opponents and the 3-bettor (Flash) likely to have a strong range, I want to pot‑control and avoid getting raised off the hand. I’ll check and see how the others act: plan is to fold to meaningful aggression, call small turn bets to realize equity, and only become aggressive if I hit an A/Q or a strong draw on a later street.

I flopped a middle set (77) on a coordinated board of 9d 6c 7h. The pot is 2,700 chips. Both the Pre-Flop 3-bettor (Flash) and the caller (Minni) have checked to me. This board texture hits the calling ranges fairly well (straight draws, pairs), and Flash likely holds overpairs (TT-AA) or high card hands (AK/AQ). Given the wetness of the board (straights are possible with 8-5, and an 8 on the turn is a scare card), I must bet for value and protection. I want to extract value from Flash's overpairs and charge Minni's draws. Checking back is risky as it gives free cards on a board where the texture can shift rapidly. My stack is 8,500, creating an SPR of roughly 3.1. A bet of around 40% pot (1,100) builds the pot to allow for an easy shove on later streets while keeping worse hands involved.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on a flop of 9d-6c-7h. I 3-bet pre-flop and both opponents called. This board is heavily coordinated and favors the calling ranges of my opponents (middle pairs, suited connectors) much more than my 3-betting range. After I checked to the field, Pro (the button) bet 1,100 into a pot of 2,700. In a three-way pot, a bet on such a wet board usually indicates a made hand or a very strong draw. My Ace-high has little equity and no functional draws to continue. Additionally, Minni is still to act behind me, increasing the risk of being trapped or facing a raise. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation.

Heads‑up now after Flash folded on a fairly dry 9♦‑6♣‑7♥ flop. I hold A♠Q♠ — two overcards with some backdoor potential but no made pair. Pro’s bet is small (1,100 into a ~3,800 pot), so the price to see a turn is attractive (I need roughly ~22% equity). Against a typical continuation range that contains a lot of missed broadways and bluffs, AQs has enough equity to continue; raising would be thin without a pair and would risk building a big pot out of position. I’ll call the 1,100 to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn, folding only to heavy aggression unless I pair up or pick up a clear draw.

Heads‑up vs Pro on 9♦‑6♣‑7♥‑5♦ with A♠Q♠. I missed the board and there is no flush/straight completed for me (only an 8 completes the obvious straight). Pro’s flop raise showed some willingness to apply pressure — calling the flop was correct to realize equity given the small size. The turn checked to me and I still have just two overcards with no real draw. Betting now would be a thin bluff that will fold out only the weakest hands and get called or raised by many hands that currently beat me (9x, 7x, small pairs, sets) or that have decent river equity. With the effective stacks and pot size I want to keep the pot under control and use this hand as a bluff‑catcher on the river: check and evaluate the river. Plan on calling small river bets as a bluff catcher, folding to heavy aggression unless I pair up (A or Q) or the river card changes the board texture in a way that favors my range.

I hold a set of Sevens (7d 7c). The turn card is the Five of Diamonds (5d), making the board 9d 6c 7h 5d. This creates a four-card straight on the board (5-6-7-9); any 8 makes a straight. My opponent (Minni) cold-called a 3-bet pre-flop, a range that heavily includes pocket pairs (88) and suited connectors like 89s, 87s, or A8s, all of which now have a straight. While I have a strong hand, the board texture is extremely dangerous. Betting here likely only gets action from a straight that beats me or strong draws. By checking, I control the pot size, avoid being check-raised, and give myself a free chance to pair the board on the river to make a full house. If the river is a blank, I can re-evaluate, but building a big pot on a 4-liner to a straight with just a set is risky.

I hold A♠Q♠ on 9♦‑6♣‑7♥‑5♦‑2♣ — I never paired and there’s no flush, only an 8 completes the obvious straight. Pro opened/called the 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet the flop for 1,100 which I called, and both of us checked the turn. This river is too dry to bluff‑polarize profitably: betting will get called by almost all one‑pair hands that beat me and fold out only the very weakest holdings. I’ll check to keep the pot under control and present Ace‑high as a bluff‑catcher. If Pro bets a small, non‑committal size (roughly <= ~half pot) I’ll call as many of his bluffs and missed‑broadway floats fall in that bin; I’ll fold to large river jams or sizable raises that represent made hands. Checking preserves stack and maximizes my equity realization here.

I hold a set of Sevens on a board of 9d 6c 7h 5d 2c. The board contains four cards to a straight (5-6-7-9), meaning any 8 makes a straight. My opponent (Minni) check-called the flop and then checked the turn and river. While her line is passive and suggests she likely doesn't have the straight (as she would likely bet the river for value after the turn went check-check), the board texture is extremely scary for any hand worse than a straight. If I bet, it is highly unlikely a worse hand (like two pair or a lower set) calls given the four-straight board. Conversely, if I bet and am check-raised, I would have to fold. Checking back secures the pot against all worse hands and avoids value-owning myself against a trap or finding a fold. It is a standard check-back for showdown value.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce of hearts. In a three-handed game, opening requirements are wider, but J2s remains a marginal hand. Given my current stack lead and the aggressive tendencies of Minni in the small blind, I choose to fold this hand rather than risk chips on a sub-par starting hand. There is no need to force action with a weak holding at this stage of the tournament.

5♠3♣ is a very weak, unplayable hand out of position, so I don't want to build a pot or lead with a raise. However the extra to call is only 50 into a 150 pot (excellent pot odds) and stacks are deep, so folding would be overly nitty. I'll call the 50 more (total committed = 100) to see a cheap flop and play straightforwardly: check and fold to significant aggression unless I hit a pair (5 or 3), two pair, or a strong draw — otherwise avoid bloating the pot out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o. The Small Blind (Minni) has limped. Minni has been playing aggressively, frequently raising to 300 with a wide range (e.g., 86o, 95s). Her limp likely indicates a weak marginal hand or a trap, but more often it is a hand she wants to see a cheap flop with. K6o is not strong enough to comfortably raise for value, especially since I would be playing out of position in a bloated pot if called. Checking allows me to see the flop for free and realize my equity without committing more chips with a mediocre hand. This is the standard and prudent play.

I have 5♠3♣ on J♣7♠2♠ — no pair and only a backdoor spade possibility. I have 6 outs to pair (three 5s + three 3s) — ~24% to pair by the river — and only ~4% to complete a backdoor flush by river. I'm out of position against the big blind with deep stacks, so I should control the pot and avoid bluffing into a wide range. Checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold to significant aggression; I'll call a small probe if the price and read justify it, but I won't lead or build the pot with this weak hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc6h. The Small Blind (Minni) limped pre-flop and I checked. The flop is 2s-Jc-7s. Minni checks to me. I have King-high with no draw. The pot is small (200). Since I have position on the limper who checked, and my hand has some showdown value but is too weak to value bet and too strong to turn into a pure bluff given the low pot size, checking back is the optimal play. I want to see a free turn card to potentially hit a King or Six, or get to showdown cheaply.

Turn K♠ changes the hand — I now have four spades (2♠,7♠,K♠ on board + my 5♠) so a one-card flush draw (9 outs, ~19.5% to hit on the river). Pre-flop/flop I was pot-controlling and planned to avoid bloating the pot without a real draw — this turn gives me a real, strong draw worth semi-bluffing. Pot is only 200 and we're out of position, so a half-pot probe (100) buys equity: it can win the pot immediately some of the time and builds a pot to be rewarded if I hit. If Villain raises I’m folding this OOP with no pair; if called I still have ~19% to make the flush on the river and will re-evaluate. Betting ~1/2 pot balances fold equity and risk.

I have turned Top Pair on a board of [2s Jc 7s Ks]. However, the board now contains three spades, making a flush possible. Minni leads for 100 (1 big blind) into a pot of 200. Given the small sizing, I am getting 3:1 odds. While my kicker is weak and I lose to any flush or better kicker, I am ahead of bluffs (like As blocker bets) or worse pairs (Jx) that might be stabbing. Folding top pair to a single small bet is too tight. Raising would only get called by better hands (flushes, better Kings). Therefore, calling is the correct play to realize showdown value cheaply.

I semi‑bluffed the turn with my spade draw (bet 100) to buy equity and fold out some hands; Villain called. The river is 5♣, which pairs my 5♠ and gives me bottom pair (5s with a 3 kicker). Pot is 400 and there is no bet to face. Villain’s calling range on the turn is weighted toward made hands and spade draws (Kx, Jx, 7x, 2x, plus missed spade draws and scattered air). Many of those hands still beat my pair now (Ks, Js, 7s, 2s), so my pair is a weak showdown hand. Betting for thin value is unlikely to extract much — the better hands will call, the worse hands often fold — and I don’t want to bloat the pot when I’m likely behind. I’ll check to get to showdown and avoid turning this into a costly bluff; if Villain fires a small river bet (≈≤150) I’ll consider a call for pot odds, but I will fold to a larger bet. Current action: check.

On the River, the board is [2s Jc 7s Ks 5c]. I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker. The pot is 400. Minni (SB) checked to me. Given the flush potential (3 spades on board) and Minni's line (limp pre, check flop, small bet turn, check river), she likely has a marginal made hand or a missed draw. My hand has decent showdown value, but betting is thin; worse hands (like Jx or 7x) might fold, and better hands (better K, flush, two pair) will call or raise. There is little value to be gained by betting, and checking back guarantees I see the showdown for free without risking more chips in a spot where I am often either way ahead or way behind. I take the showdown.

Three-handed on the button with A♦9♣ — this is a clear open. I have position on both blinds and an ace blocker that reduces opponents' strong-A combos. With deep stacks I want to take the initiative, steal the blinds and play postflop in position against wide calling ranges. I choose a standard 3x BB open to 300: big enough to deny cheap equity to marginal hands but small enough not to bloat the pot unnecessarily if I face a 3-bet. Plan: c-bet favorable Ace-high or dry flops, play aggressively in position; fold to a large, polarized 3-bet but call a small/standard 3-bet and outplay where appropriate.

I hold 2d 3s in the Small Blind, facing a standard raise from the Button (Minni) to 300. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. Playing this hand out of position is a losing strategy due to its lack of equity and playability. The cost to call (250) is not worth the potential trouble, even with the pot odds. Folding is the only reasonable option here to preserve my stack of 12,450.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Deuce of Clubs. Minni opened from the Button to 300, and Pro folded. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, T2o is a very weak hand to defend with, especially being out of position against Minni who has been relatively active. As the chip leader, I have no reason to play low-equity junk hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold J8o on the Button. This is a weak hand, below the standard opening range for 3-handed play, especially with deep stacks (124bb) where reverse implied odds and playability matter more. My opponents (Flash and Minni) have large stacks and have shown aggression, making it risky to open light. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with T5o after the button has folded. This is a very weak hand. While SB vs BB dynamics often allow for a wider range, T5o is near the bottom of any reasonable range. Minni, in the Big Blind, has shown plenty of aggression throughout the tournament. Given my large stack and the fact that we are in the later stages of the hand count, there is no reason to play a sub-par hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with K9o, which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. I have a significant chip lead and want to maintain pressure on my opponents. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to capture the blinds or play the hand in position if called.

Facing a 3bb open from the button with 3♣7♠ in the small blind and the big blind still to act, this is a clear fold. 3-7o has very poor equity and is easily dominated by the button’s opening range; postflop I’ll be out of position and will often face difficult decisions or a 3-bet from the big blind. The call is cheap but not worth playing a lot of marginal pots with a dead hand — and a light 3-bet bluff is too risky here. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 9s. Flash, the chip leader on the Button, has raised to 300 (3BB). The pot is currently 450 (including my posted BB and the SB's fold). I would need to call 200 to see a flop. While J9o has reasonable raw equity against a wide button opening range, playing this hand out of position against the chip leader facing a 3x raise is difficult. Defending J9o typically realizes equity poorly out of position, and the larger raise size (3BB vs 2-2.5BB) makes a fold the standard play. I will preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with 9♣2♠ (9h2s) — one of the worst starting hands. Facing 100 into a 150 pot, I could limp or make a small steal, but with deep stacks and two competent blind defenders a raise will often face calls or 3-bets and put me in difficult postflop spots where this hand has virtually no equity or playability. Limping is also undesirable because I have poor postflop potential in multiway pots. Preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot to exploit position — fold preflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 2h 9d, which is a statistically very weak hand (trash). The Button (Minni) has folded. The Big Blind (Flash) is behind me. Although it only costs 50 chips to complete the blind, playing 92o out of position is generally a losing strategy, especially against an active opponent like Flash who may raise pre-flop or apply pressure post-flop. Folding preserves my stack without entering a disadvantageous spot.

I am on the Button with 5d 6h, an offsuit connector. While the Button is a strong position to steal blinds, 56o is quite weak and falls outside a standard opening range, especially against active opponents like Flash and Minni who have deep stacks. With 123 BBs, I do not need to take marginal spots. Folding is the prudent play.

I am in the Small Blind with KJs, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. The Button (Pro) has folded, so the action is on me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Minni) is a solid player, but I have a significant chip lead overall. I should raise here to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately. A standard raise size from the Small Blind is around 3x the big blind, so I will raise to 300.

Flash opened to 300 from the small blind; I’m in the big blind with 4♣7♦ (a weak unsuited hand). Pot is 400 and it’s only 200 more to see a flop (total 300 committed). With deep stacks and an aggressive opponent who opens wide here, the price is cheap and my hand has enough equity vs a wide range to justify defending. I’m out of position so I won’t escalate with a marginal 3‑bet — a raise to 500 would turn this into a bloated pot with a very weak holding. I’ll call the 200 (total 300) to see the flop and proceed cautiously: fold to large turn/river aggression on dry boards, and only continue aggressively with clear made hands or strong draws.

On this Ace-Ace-Nine flop, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor, as my opening range from the Small Blind contains more strong Aces (like AK, AQ, AJ) than Minni's Big Blind calling range. I will place a standard continuation bet of 1/3 the pot (200 chips).
