This allows me to take down the pot immediately if she missed the board entirely, which is likely given the two Aces are on the table. If I am raised, I can safely fold my King-high hand.

I have 4♥7♦ on A♣ A♠ 9♣ — no pair and no realistic draws. Flash opened wide from the SB and is firing a very small c-bet (200 into a ~800 pot). Calling 200 here is cheap (I risk 200 to win ~1,000, ~20% break-even) and keeps the pot controllable while allowing me to fold to real aggression on the turn. I’ll call to see a turn card and reassess: fold to a large bet/raise or continue only if I pick up a clear hand or a strong read on a bluff. Total committed after my call will be 500 (300 preflop + 200 on the flop).

On the turn 7h, the board remains very static. Having bet the flop and been called, Minni's range likely consists of a 9, a medium pocket pair (like 88-22), or potentially an Ace that is slow-playing. My King-high has some showdown value against her air, but very little against her calling range. By checking, I am controlling the size of the pot and potentially inducing a bluff or seeing a free river card to improve to a pair. If she checks behind, my King-high might still be the best hand or I can re-evaluate on the river. If she bets, I can comfortably fold knowing I've lost the lead on this specific board texture.

I defended a wide SB open preflop and called a small flop c-bet on A♣ A♠ 9♣. The 7♥ on the turn gives me bottom/second pair (7s) with a weak kicker. Flash's line—open, small flop bet, then checking the turn—covers a very wide range (pot‑controlling A's, medium pairs, and bluffs). My hand is behind any Ace and many pocket pairs and has little value to build a big pot. With stacks deep and the pot ~1,000, I’ll control the size of the pot and keep my options by checking the turn. That lets me see the river for free, call small river bets as a bluff‑catcher or fold to significant aggression, and avoid bloating a pot where I'm frequently behind.

The board is double-paired with Aces and has a Nine. I c-bet the flop and was called, but the turn went check-check. This suggests that Minni likely has a marginal hand like a Nine, a small pocket pair, or a busted draw. While my King-high loses to any pair, it does have showdown value against busted straight or flush draws that Minni might have called the flop with. I don't believe a bet here will consistently fold out a Nine or a small pair given the pot size and the way the hand has played out. Therefore, checking and hoping to win at showdown against air is the most stable choice.

Board: Ac As 9c 7h 2s. I hold 7d4h — second pair (7s) with a weak kicker. Flash opened wide preflop, made a tiny flop c-bet and then checked the turn and river: a clear pot‑control line that usually contains Ax (weak aces), some 9x or medium pairs, and occasional bluffs. My 7 is behind any Ace, any 9, and hands that turned sets/trips; it only beats missed overcards and pure air. Betting for thin value is low EV (villain will fold most worse hands and call with better), and a bluff here is risky against a player who showed pot control. I’ll check to avoid bloating the pot and take the free showdown. If Flash now leads a small bet (≈≤1/3 pot, ~300) I can call as a bluff‑catcher; I will fold to larger river aggression.

I am on the button with J5o. The pot is currently 150 with a 100-chip call required to stay in. While J5o is a relatively weak hand, I have position on both Minni and Pro. However, looking at the table dynamics, Minni has been quite active and Pro is relatively short-stacked compared to me. Folding J5o from the button is a standard and safe play to avoid putting chips at risk with a marginal hand in a multi-way potential pot, especially given my comfortable stack lead. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my position and chips.

Tc2h is a weak, non‑playable hand OOP. I’ve already posted the 50 SB; calling 50 more (total commitment 100) is cheap — the pot is 150 so I get ~3:1 immediate odds and can see a flop without risking a big portion of my deep stack. A raise to 200 would isolate me OOP with junk and unnecessarily inflate the pot against a BB (Pro) who defends and plays back often. I’ll call to realize equity; fold to significant re‑raise, otherwise proceed and c‑bet/fold based on flop texture and action.

I am in the Big Blind with Q6o. The Small Blind (Minni) has limped. Q6o is a marginal hand, not strong enough to raise for value confidently, and raising as a bluff risks bloating the pot out of position against a range that often calls. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free and see a flop. This is the standard passive defense against a Small Blind limp.

I missed this flop entirely. Tc2h on A♥7♦9♠ has no pair and no meaningful draws, so I have essentially no equity vs a BB defending range that includes A‑x, 9x, 7x and pocket pairs. The pot is tiny (200) relative to our deep stacks, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with junk or bluff‑barrel multi streets. I’ll check to control the pot and either see a free turn if Pro checks, or fold to a standard continuation bet (only consider a very light call to a minuscule bet).

I hold Qs6h in the Big Blind. The Small Blind limped pre-flop, and I checked. The flop is Ah7d9s, missing me completely. The Small Blind has checked to me. With Queen-high and no draw on an Ace-high board, I have very little equity and no strong reason to bluff, as my opponent's limp-check range includes many Ax and pair hands that won't fold. Checking back is the standard play to realize any remaining equity for free and avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand.

I hold Tc 2h on A♥ 7♦ 9♠ 8♥. I missed — no pair and no realistic flush (only two hearts on board + one in my hand means a flush is impossible by the river). My only clean improvements are three remaining tens (pair) or a 6/J to complete a 5‑card straight with my Ten (≈11 outs, ~24% to improve), many of which still lose to an Ace or better. The pot is tiny (200) relative to my deep stack (21,064) and Villain (BB) checked the flop, so checking to control the pot is the prudent line. I’ll check and see the river for free; if Villain then fires a normal/large bet I’ll fold most of my range (call only very small probes or if I improve), and if he checks back the river I can barrel or value-bet only when I actually pair or hit the straight.

I hold Queen-high with an open-ended straight draw (my 6 connects with the 7-8-9 on the board). The board texture (A-7-9-8) is wet and likely hits the Small Blind's limping range (middle cards). However, her check on the turn after checking the flop signals weakness, likely missing the Ace. Despite the weakness, betting is risky as she may call with any pair (7x, 8x, 9x) given the small pot, and I have very little showdown value if called. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity to hit the straight (outs: 5 or 10) on the river for free. The pot is small (200 chips), so there is little incentive to bloat it or bluff aggressively.

I have Tc2h and missed completely on the river (board A♥ 7♦ 9♠ 8♥ 8♦). I have no pair, poor blockers and my only realistic showdown outs were gone — many hands in the BB’s checking range (Ax, 9x, 7x, pocket pairs) beat me. The pot is tiny (200) relative to our deep stacks, and bluffing a paired board vs a player who defends/calls often has very low fold equity. I’ll check and conserve chips; if the BB fires a meaningful bet I’ll fold.

The hand has been checked down to the river in a limped blind-vs-blind pot. The final board is Ah-7d-9s-8h-8d. I hold Qs6h. My best five-card hand is a pair of Eights (from the board) with an Ace, Queen, and Nine kicker (8-8-A-Q-9). The opponent (Minni) has checked to me on all three post-flop streets, indicating significant weakness or a desire to get to showdown cheaply. Betting here serves little purpose; better hands (any pair, King-high) will likely call or have me beat, and worse hands (Jack-high, lower) will fold. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value against weaker high-card hands without risking chips in a small pot.

Button with 7♦4♦ vs SB (Pro) and BB (Flash). Deep stacks (~210 BB) and only two opponents — this is a spot to open a wide range. 7-4 suited is marginal but has enough postflop playability in position and benefits from fold equity. I prefer raising rather than limping to deny the big blind correct odds and to take initiative. I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300 total) to either steal the blinds or get a manageable HU pot; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this holding, while a very small 3-bet might be considered for a call occasionally, but default is to fold to significant re-raises.

I am in the Small Blind with J5o, facing a button raise to 300 from Minni. J5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Although I have a healthy stack of roughly 123 BBs, defending here is likely negative EV. Calling invites the Big Blind into the pot, leaving me sandwiched. 3-betting is too loose. The standard and most profitable play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h-6s. Minni has raised to 300 (3x) from the Button. While I am getting roughly 2.25:1 on a call, 5-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent. Given my current chip lead and the remaining number of hands in the tournament, there is no need to defend the Big Blind with the bottom of my range. Folding preserves my stack and avoids a difficult post-flop situation where I would likely be forced to check-fold on most boards.

I am on the Button with 94o, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. Although I am in position, my opponents (Flash and Minni) have significantly larger stacks and have shown willingness to defend. With 46 hands remaining in the tournament and being the shortest stack (though still deep at ~123BB), there is no incentive to open with junk hands that have negative expected value. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with JTo. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). JTo is a strong hand for a blind-vs-blind battle. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand here, or play the flop with initiative against an opponent who has shown a willingness to defend and play many pots.

I’m in the big blind with FIVE OF DIAMONDS (5d) and FOUR OF HEARTS (4h) facing Flash’s small‑blind open to 300. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (total commitment 300). With deep stacks (21,114) and heads‑up vs a likely wide SB opening range, 5‑4 offsuit has enough connectivity and equity to defend — I’m getting ~33% pot odds to see a flop. The hand is too weak to 3‑bet or isolate, so I’ll call and play cautiously out of position: fold to heavy aggression on dry high boards, continue with pairs or strong draws, and look to realize equity or apply pressure only when the texture and reads justify it.

On a flop of Qs-2h-9d, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs). As the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a bet to represent a range that includes Queens and strong nines. This continuation bet puts pressure on the Big Blind's range while allowing me to build a pot for when I potentially hit my straight on the turn or river. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard here to balance value and bluffs.

Flop Q♠ 2♥ 9♦. I have 5♦4♥ — no pair and only two‑card backdoors (very little equity). Flash opened preflop (likely a wide SB range) and now c‑bet 300 into a 900 pot (~1/3 pot). Calling 300 to win 1,200 requires ≈25% equity; my actual equity vs a reasonable c‑bet/continuation range (Qx, many overcards, pocket pairs, broadways) is well below that. A bluff‑raise OOP with no blockers and deep stacks is too risky and unlikely to get folds from better hands. I defended preflop as a speculative call, but on this dry, high texture I should fold to the flop bet and conserve chips for a better, lower‑variance spot.

I have the best starting hand, Pocket Aces. I am on the button and want to build the pot while in position. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size, which balances my range and sets up for value on later streets.

I have Q♣J♠ in the small blind facing a standard button open to 300 with the big blind still to act. With deep stacks and out of position, QJ offsuit is a defensible calling hand but not strong enough to 3-bet for value and not worth folding to a lone 3x open. Calling keeps the pot under control, preserves maneuverability postflop against a wide button-opening range, and lets me evaluate actions after seeing the flop (I can fold to a large 3-bet from the BB, continue vs single opponent, or pot-control vs aggression). I’ll call to 300 total (add 250).

I am in the Big Blind with 84o facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind call. The pot odds are roughly 4.5 to 1 (calling 200 to win 700), requiring about 22% equity. However, 84o is one of the weakest hands in poker, with poor playability and significant reverse implied odds, especially in a multi-way pot where I would be playing out of position or sandwiched. Even with the good price, the hand is too weak to defend profitably against two ranges. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I missed this flop — Q♣J♠ has no pair and only two overcards to 7♣7♥8♠, with no meaningful backdoor draw. I’m out of position against a wide button opener, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and see how Flash reacts: call small continuation bets (roughly <= ~1/2 pot) to keep the pot manageable and fold to large, polarized bets. If he checks back I can try to take it away on the turn or target Q/J turns for value.

I hold pocket Aces on a board of 7-7-8. While the paired board is slightly dangerous given Minni's range can include 7x hands from the small blind, AA is still a massive favorite against the majority of her calling range, which includes 8x, pocket pairs (99-JJ), and various straight draws. I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot to extract value and protect my hand against overcards or draws.

Board is 7c 7h 8s and I hold Qc Js in the small blind. I checked and Flash (button) bets a small 350 into a 1050 pot (~1/3 pot). I missed this flop — I have two overcards and no real backdoor flush/straight. Because the bet is small the opener is likely c-betting a wide, often weak range. Calling 350 is correct: the call requires ~20% equity (350 into a pot of 1750 after my call) and I have ~6 immediate outs to pair on the turn (≈12.8%) and about ~24% to pair by the river — the two-street equity justifies a call. Calling keeps the pot under control OOP, allows me to realize equity, and preserves options on the turn (fold to big aggression, raise/lead if I pair, or exploit a check). I should not raise here — QJos is a poor OOP bluff/raise candidate against a button opener and raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and expose me to 3-bets or larger continuing bets. Plan: call the 350, re-evaluate based on the turn card and opponent's action.

I have Q♣J♠ on 7♣7♥8♠6♦ and I’m out of position. I missed — no pair and no meaningful backdoors — so I should keep the pot small. I called a small flop c-bet which is consistent with a wide, often weak button range, but on the turn with only two overcards (≈6 outs to pair on the river, ~13%) I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP or turn this marginal holding into a costly bluff. Checking controls the pot, realizes my (limited) equity, and lets Flash show aggression first so I can make a clearer fold/call decision. Plan: check; if he bets a small probing amount I’ll consider a call depending on sizing, fold to large polarized raises, and if he checks back I’ll look to target Q/J rivers for value or bluff-shove if the river and dynamics justify it.

I have an overpair (Aces) on a 7-7-8-6 board. Minni has checked to me for the second time. While the 6 connects the board for some straights (5-4, 9-5), it is more likely she has an 8, a pocket pair like 99-JJ, or is on a straight draw like 9-10. I will bet 900 to extract value from these hands and protect against draws. AA is too strong to check back here, as I want to build the pot while I am likely ahead.

I checked the turn with Q♣J♠ on 7♣7♥8♠6♦ and Flash responded with a sizeable bet of 900 into the pot (pot = 2300 before my decision). I have only 6 clean outs (three Qs + three Js) — ~6/46 ≈ 13% to pair on the river. Calling 900 to win a 3200 pot (2300+900) requires ~28% equity (900/3200 ≈ 28.1%), so a call is -EV by pot odds. Raising as a bluff OOP with no blockers or backdoors is also unattractive — it bloats the pot with a weak hand and Flash’s line (small flop c-bet then a much larger turn barrel) can contain many value hands and isn’t likely to fold enough. My pre-turn plan was to fold to large, polarized aggression; this turn sizing qualifies. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better, more profitable spots.

I’m on the button with J♣3♥. It’s a marginal offsuit holding but I have position against only the blinds and the stacks are deep. Standard open size at this table is ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 to take the initiative and try to steal the blinds/get heads-up. If I face a real 3‑bet I’ll fold (hand is often dominated); if I get called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or exercise pot control in multiway pots. Raising to 300 maximizes fold equity while keeping postflop playability in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 3c, facing a standard raise to 300 from Minni on the Button. Although I have a deep stack (122 BB), 83 suited is a very weak hand that plays poorly, especially out of position. The pot odds do not justify calling given the difficulty of realizing equity with such a disconnected low hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6-2 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 3BB from the Button. Although I am the chip leader and the price to call is only 200 more into a 450 pot, this hand has very poor playability and realization value out of position. It is best to fold and wait for better spots to utilize my stack advantage.

I hold 2c 6d on the Button, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I have position, the hand has very little playability or potential. With two opponents remaining in the blinds who have larger stacks, attempting to steal is not worth the risk. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with a premium hand, Ace-King offsuit. Since the button folded, I am head-to-head with Minni in the Big Blind. I want to raise to build value and take control of the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate in this position to put pressure on the Big Blind.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind with T♦6♠ in the big blind and deep stacks (~203 BB), this is a clear call. The SB’s opening range heads-up is wide, and calling 200 more (total commit 300 including my BB) gets me good pot odds (~33%) and allows me to see a flop with a hand that has decent equity and good playability. A minimum 3-bet to 500 with T6o is thin — I don’t want to bloat a deep, out-of-position pot with a marginal hand. Plan: see the flop; if I hit top pair or a strong draw I’ll apply pressure, otherwise control pot size and fold to significant aggression on dry, high-card boards.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a King-high board. This is a very strong position. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board is relatively favorable for my range, I will place a continuation bet of 300 chips (half the pot) to extract value from weaker Kings, pocket pairs, or straight draws like J-10 that Minni might be holding in the Big Blind.

Board is K♥-9♦-8♠; I hold T♦6♠ — no pair but a meaningful draw: 4 clean outs to a 7 for a wheel straight and 3 tens to make a pair (some may be dirty). That’s roughly ~28% raw chance to improve by the river. Facing a small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot) I’m getting about 4:1 pot odds, so a call is correct to realize equity without bloating an out-of-position pot. Raising as a bluff here is too thin with this hand and the effective stacks are very deep. Plan: call 300, see the turn; if I miss a safe card I’ll fold to heavy turn aggression, but if I pair my T or hit a 7 I’ll come out stronger (bet/raise) to extract value or take the pot.

I still have top pair with the best kicker (TPTK) on a relatively safe turn card. The 5 of spades brings a flush draw, but it doesn't change the nuts significantly. I will continue betting for value to get called by weaker Kings, middle pairs like 9x, or to charge straight and flush draws. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot (two-thirds pot) is a standard value size to extract maximum value from Minni's calling range.

Turn: K♥-9♦-8♠-5♠, my hand T♦6♠. Clean outs to improve: 3 tens + 4 sevens = 7 outs. On the river that is 7/46 ≈ 15.2% equity. The pot was 2000 and Flash bet 800, making the pot 2800; calling 800 gives pot odds of 2800:800 = 3.5:1, so I need ≈28.6% equity to break even. My ~15% << required ~28.6%, so a call is -EV. Raising as a bluff OOP into an opponent who has shown aggressive flop/turn action is too thin and likely to get called or re-raised. With deep stacks there will be plenty of better, more profitable spots — folding preserves my stack and avoids a marginal, negative-EV river decision.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sixes (6c, 6d) in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand and standard play is to raise to gain initiative and potentially win the blinds. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 chips.

9♠3♥ is a very weak, dominated hand. Flash opened to 300 (3x BB) from the button in a 3‑handed game — I would be out of position postflop to both the button and the big blind. Calling 250 to play OOP is unattractive (calling 250 into ~700 gives ~36% pot‑equity required) and 9‑3o has far less equity versus a normal open/defend range. A 3‑bet bluff here is also a poor choice without blockers or fold equity against these deep, aggressive stacks. Preserve chips for a better spot and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 8c. Flash (Button) raised to 300. The Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450 (odds: ~31%). My hand, 98 offsuit, has reasonable playability and equity (approx 37-40%) against a wide button opening range. Given the pot odds and my stack depth (120BB), defending the Big Blind here is the standard +EV play to realize equity post-flop.

I have flopped Top Pair with a decent kicker (9h8c on 9d6h4s). The board is relatively dry (rainbow). As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flash). Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their bluffs (air/overcards) while isolating me against better hands or strong draws. By checking, I allow Flash to make a continuation bet with a wide range, which I can comfortably call. If they check back, I can bet the turn. Therefore, checking is the most +EV decision to keep the opponent's range wide.

I have flopped a set of sixes on a relatively dry board of 9d-6h-4s. My opponent (Pro) checked to me. While slow-playing is an option, there are some straight draws possible (7-8, 5-7) and I want to start building the pot immediately while I'm likely ahead. A bet of around half the pot is standard here to get value from 9x hands, middle pairs, or draws.

I hold Top Pair (Nines) with an Eight kicker on a dry rainbow board (9d 6h 4s). Facing a standard continuation bet of 350 into 650 from the Button, folding is incorrect as my hand is too strong relative to the opponent's wide range. Raising would likely isolate me against better hands (overpairs, better kickers, sets) while folding out the bluffs I beat. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's bluffing range active and re-evaluate on the turn. The price is reasonable, requiring me to call 350 to win a total pot of 1350.

On the Turn, the board is [9d 6h 4s Jc]. The Jack is an overcard to my pair of Nines, downgrading my hand to second pair. This card favors the pre-flop raiser's range (which contains many broadway combinations like AJ, KJ, QJ, JT). Leading out (donk betting) is unlikely to get value from worse hands and would likely only get action from better hands. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size. If the opponent checks back, I get to see a river cheaply. If they bet, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, as I still have a bluff-catcher but face the risk that the Jack improved their hand.

I have middle set (sixes) on a board of 9-6-4-J. The Jack is generally a good card for my range as it may have connected with Pro's calling range (like J9 or QJ/KJ). Pro has checked to me again. I want to continue building the pot to get maximum value from 9x hands, straight draws (5-7, 7-8), or any newly improved Jacks. I will bet 850 into the 1,350 pot to set up a potential shove or large value bet on the river.

On the Turn, the board is [9d 6h 4s Jc]. The Jack is a significant scare card that interacts heavily with the Button's opening range (AJ, KJ, QJ, JT). My hand (Second Pair, 8 kicker) has been downgraded from Top Pair. The opponent bets 850 (approx 63% pot). Given the texture change, I am now losing to all value hands (Top Pair+) and am purely bluff-catching. While the opponent (Flash) is aggressive, I am out of position with a marginal hand that is near the bottom of my range. I have better hands to call with (Top Pair, Sets, Two Pair). The price (28% equity needed) is not favorable enough given the likelihood that the opponent has improved or is leveraging the scare card effectively.

Three-handed on the button with K♣8♣ — a playable, suited king that belongs in my open-raise range. I want to leverage position and take the initiative: a 3x BB open (to 300 total) exerts pressure on the blinds, has good fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable if Flash (big stack, often aggressive) 3-bets. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, use position to C‑bet favorable flops and realize equity with backdoor club possibilities. 300 is a standard, size-controlled raise that balances aggression and pot control.

The opponent Minni (Button) has raised to 300. I am in the Small Blind with 7d 6d, a suited connector. Given the stack depth of 115BB and the good playability of my hand, calling is a standard and profitable play. I am getting a reasonable price to see a flop and can play well post-flop even out of position. 3-betting is an option but calling reduces variance and keeps the pot manageable against an active opener.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 6s. Minni (Button) opened to 3bb and Pro (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 700 chips, and it costs me only 200 more to call, giving me excellent pot odds of 3.5:1. While 96o is a weak hand, in a 3-handed game, the ranges are wider, and closing the action for such a small price is generally profitable to see a flop and attempt to realize some equity, despite being out of position.

On a monotone flop of Ah 7h 4h, holding 7d 6d gives me middle pair but no heart draw. This is a very precarious situation in a multi-way pot out of position. The Ace hits the pre-flop raiser's range hard, and the hearts interact well with both opponents' ranges. Leading out (donk betting) would accomplish nothing as I would likely only get action from better hands (better 7, Ace, flush, or flush draw). My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or fold to significant aggression. Therefore, checking is the standard and correct play to observe the actions of the Big Blind and the Button.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 6c on an Ah 7h 4h monotone flop. This board is extremely unfavorable for my hand as I have no hearts and no significant pair/draw potential. Pro, who is in the Small Blind, has already checked. The standard play here is to check and fold to any bet, as I have almost zero equity against two opponents on an Ace-high monotone board.

I raised preflop with K♣8♣ to use position, but this flop (A♥7♥4♥) is very bad for my hand. I have K-high with no hearts and there are three hearts on board; I cannot make a club flush because there are zero clubs on the flop (even two cards to come can’t give me the three board clubs needed). Two opponents called preflop and both checked the flop, so bluffing into a multiway pot has very poor equity. I’ll check to control the pot, see a free turn, and only continue if I pick up real equity (a K or 8) or get a very cheap bet I can call. I will fold to large pressure since I currently have essentially no showdown value.

The turn brings the 5h, putting four hearts on the board (Ah 7h 4h 5h). This is a very dangerous board texture in a 3-way pot. I hold a pair of sevens with no heart. Any opponent holding a heart has a flush. Even without the flush, I am losing to any Ax or higher pocket pair. Betting achieves nothing here as I will only be called by better hands (flushes, sets, aces) and will not fold out any hand that beats me (like a weak flush). My only viable play is to check and hope to get to showdown cheaply or fold to aggression.

The turn has brought a fourth heart to the board (5h). I do not have a heart in my hand, meaning any opponent with a single heart has a flush. Even if neither opponent has a heart, my 9-high is likely behind any pair or high card. Since Pro checked, I will also check to see if I can get to a free river, as betting into two opponents on a four-flush board with zero equity and no heart is a losing play.

Board is 4♥-7♥-A♥-5♥ and I hold K♣8♣. With four hearts on board any single heart in an opponent’s hand is a made flush. I have no hearts and no realistic blockers — my hand has almost zero showdown value. This is a three‑way pot, so bluffing here has very poor fold equity and a high chance of running into a made flush or a slow‑played strong hand. Pot is only 900 and my stack is deep enough to wait for a better spot. Best play is to check and concede the turn — fold to any meaningful bet on the river (only consider calling a tiny probe if pot odds and table read justify it).

The board shows Ah 7h 4h 5h 8h, which is a 5-card flush (Ace-high). I hold 7d 6d and have no hearts, meaning I am playing the board. The 6h would make a straight flush, and any heart 9h or higher makes a better flush than the board. In a 3-way pot, it is reasonably likely someone has a heart 9 or higher. Betting has no value as better hands will call and chopping hands (playing the board) will call. My best result is a chop. Checking gives the best chance to realize that equity for free.

The board has completed with five hearts (Ah-8h-7h-5h-4h), meaning every player has at least an Ace-high flush. I do not hold a heart, so I am playing the board. Any opponent with the 6h has a straight flush, and any opponent with a heart higher than the 8 (9h, Th, Jh, Qh, Kh) has a higher flush than the board. If my opponents have no hearts or only hearts lower than the 4 (2h, 3h), we will chop the pot. Since the action has been checked all the way through, there is a decent chance we are looking at a chop, but betting serves no purpose as I cannot represent a hand strong enough to fold out a better heart, and I don't want to be raised. I will check and take the showdown.

Board: 4♥-7♥-A♥-5♥-8♥. I hold K♣8♣. With all five community cards hearts, every player has at least the board’s A‑high heart flush — my pair of eights is irrelevant. Any opponent holding a heart can have a better flush (K♥/Q♥/J♥/T♥/9♥) and with two callers preflop there’s a meaningful chance at least one has a heart. Betting into two players on a five‑heart board is a pure bluff with almost no fold equity; it will be called by players who already have the board flush or beat it. Pot is small (900) and stacks are deep, so conserve chips: check and concede the pot. I will fold to any meaningful river bet.

Current hand is 110/150, 3-handed. I am on the Button with 6h 8h (suited one-gapper). My stack is roughly 115 BB. This is a suitable hand for opening from the Button to steal the blinds or play in position post-flop. The hand has good playability if called. A standard open size of 2.5x BB (250) puts pressure on the blinds while keeping the investment relatively low if 3-bet.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens, a premium hand. In a 3-handed game, this hand is extremely strong. Pro raised from the button, which is often a wide range. I will 3-bet to 900 to build the pot and take control of the hand from the small blind. If faced with a 4-bet, I will likely continue as QQ is near the top of my range.

3-handed: Pro opened to 250 and Flash (SB) 3-bet to 900. I’m BB with K♠5♥. Calling 800 (≈8bb) would put me into a multiway pot out of position against two ranges where my king is frequently dominated and has poor post‑flop playability. The call requires ~39% equity to break even versus the current pot odds; K5o’s equity vs a typical SB 3‑bet (and against the opener as well) is well below that. A 4‑bet bluff to 1550 is thin — I don’t have the blockers or hand strength to isolate profitably, and with deep stacks I should preserve chips for better spots. Fold and wait for a cleaner opportunity.

I opened to 250 from the Button with 6h 8h, a suited one-gapper. Flash, the chip leader in the Small Blind, 3-bet to 900. Minni folded. I need to call 650 to play a pot of 1900, requiring about 34% equity realization. While I am in position and roughly 115 BB deep, 6h 8h is at the bottom of a defending range against a large 3.6x 3-bet. Flash has a significant stack advantage and range advantage here. Without a premium hand or a stronger connector, and facing a large 3-bet, calling puts me in a marginal spot where I can easily be dominated or forced off equity. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance play.

I am on the button with Q4o. While Q4o is a relatively weak hand, I have the advantage of position and a large stack (29,036 chips). The pot currently has 150 chips, and it only costs me 100 to call the big blind's current level. However, a raise from the button is a standard play to steal the blinds or take control of the hand. Given that the tournament is nearing its end (Hand 111 of 150) and I have a comfortable lead, I will put some pressure on the blinds with a standard 3x raise. If I face significant resistance (like a 3-bet), I can easily fold without hurting my stack significantly.

Three-handed in the small blind with JJ — a very strong hand heads-up. Button (Flash) opened to 300; with deep stacks (~196bb for me, Flash ~287bb, Pro ~112bb) I want to take the initiative, get value from worse broadways/small pocket pairs and charge speculative hands. Calling 250 keeps the big blind and button in cheaply; a tiny reraise to 500 is too weak and invites multiway play. I’ll 3-bet to apply pressure and isolate the opener while leaving room to maneuver postflop. Raise to 1500 total: large enough to gain fold equity and target weaker opens, but small enough relative to stacks to continue if I face a single 4-bet. If called I’ll C-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously vs heavy resistance or large 4-bets.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 2s, facing a Button open to 300 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 1500. My hand is extremely weak (low, disconnected, though suited). Calling 1400 more into a pot of 1900 offers poor odds (requiring ~42% equity heads-up, or slightly better if Button calls, but playing multi-way out of position with trash is bad). The SB's 3-bet range is strong, and I am easily dominated. This is a trivial fold.

I raised to 300 from the button with Queen-Four offsuit, which is a weak hand used primarily for a positional steal. Minni responded with a significant 3-bet to 1500 from the small blind. While I would have position post-flop, Q4o is far too weak to continue against such a large raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot on most flops, and there is no reason to risk a large portion of my stack with a sub-par hand when I have a comfortable chip lead. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here.

Three-handed on the button with K9s and 200bb effective — this is a clear open-raise spot. K9s plays very well in position against wide blind ranges: good equity, suited, and lots of postflop maneuverability. I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total) to build the pot with position, gain fold equity, and narrow ranges. If I get one caller I’ll play aggressively in position and c-bet many favorable textures; if faced with a sizable 3-bet I’ll generally fold to a large 3-bet (especially from Flash’s large stack) but may flat small 3-bets to realize equity in position.

I am in the Small Blind holding Q8o facing a raise to 300 from the Button (Minni). Q8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Although the Button's range is likely wide, playing this hand from the worst position is generally negative EV due to the difficulty of realizing equity. Calling would also invite the Big Blind to come along or squeeze. Preserving chips by folding in this spot is the standard and prudent play.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While I am getting decent pot odds to call from the big blind (200 to win a total pot of 450), the hand has extremely poor playability and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against Minni. Given my current chip lead, there is no strategic reason to defend such a weak hand.

I am on the Button with Ten-Three offsuit (3d Ts), which is a very weak hand statistically. With 111 big blinds, I have plenty of stack depth and no need to force action with sub-par holdings. My opponents, Flash and Minni, have shown recent aggression including 3-betting, making a steal attempt with trash risky. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 6d, which is a very weak hand. Even with the action folded to me, completing the blind or raising with such low equity out of position against Minni is not a sound strategy. I will fold and preserve my chips for better opportunities.

Being on the button with a suited connector like 5s 6s in a 3-handed game is a strong opening opportunity. I have the largest stack and can exert pressure on Minni and Pro. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either take the blinds or play the hand with positional advantage.

3-handed, deep stacks, and I'm in the small blind with pocket nines — a clear value hand that plays well both preflop and postflop. Flash (button) has opened to 300 frequently here, indicating a fairly wide raising range. I prefer to 3-bet for value and isolation rather than limp/call out of position and let the button and big blind see a cheap flop multiway. A raise to 1,500 (total commitment) is large enough to generate fold equity vs weaker broadways and to build a pot when I’m ahead, but small enough that I can continue postflop comfortably against calls. Plan: if called, c-bet appropriate textures and use position/stack depth to extract value; if I face a very large 4-bet, I will re-evaluate (likely fold to polarizing shove-sized 4-bets, but can call smaller 4-bets and play postflop). Raise to 1,500 to take the initiative and maximize EV with a strong pocket pair.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8c6h. The Button (Flash) opened to 300, and the Small Blind (Minni) 3-bet to 1500. This is a very large 3-bet (5x the open). My hand is an unsuited one-gapper, which is very weak. I am facing significant strength and would be out of position against the 3-bettor. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a weak hand, and a 4-bet bluff is ill-advised in this spot. Preserving my stack of roughly 110 big blinds is the priority. The only correct play is to fold.

Minni has been using a large 3-bet sizing (5x my open) recently, which suggests either a very strong linear range or a polarized range intended to shut down the hand immediately. While 5s 6s is a good hand to play in position, calling 1,200 more into a pot of 1,900 (needing about 39% equity) is marginal against a range that likely dominates my low cards. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that we are approaching the final stages of the tournament (Hand 114/150), I prefer to fold and avoid high-variance spots with speculative hands, especially when facing large re-raises. I will continue to look for better opportunities to exert my stack advantage.

Button with 5d4d (suited, connected) in a 3-handed game — deep stacks (~206bb) and position make this a hand worth opening. Limping/calling 100 is passive and gives the blinds a free opportunity; a min-raise to 200 is too small and invites 3-bets. I want to take the initiative and use position, so I’ll open to a standard ~3x the BB (300). If faced with a large 3-bet (well above ~700–900) I’ll fold this hand; I’ll consider calling a small 3-bet (cold 3-bet to ~600) because of implied odds and post-flop playability. Postflop plan: c‑bet favorable diamond/low-straight flops, pot‑control or check/float against heavy resistance on high card, dry boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 4s, facing a 3bb open raise from the Button (Minni). My hand is an offsuit connector with very low equity and poor playability. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressor is generally a losing strategy due to the difficulty of realizing equity. Although I have a deep stack (110bb), there is no incentive to enter the pot with such a weak holding, especially with the Big Blind still to act behind me. The pot odds do not justify a call given the positional disadvantage and hand weakness. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more to see the flop. In a three-handed game, King-Ten offsuit is a strong hand and well ahead of a typical button opening range. I am getting good pot odds (2.25:1) to defend. While I could 3-bet to take control of the hand, calling allows me to play a pot in position relative to the raiser (since I'm the BB and they are the Button, though they technically act after me post-flop, the action starts with the SB/BB) or rather, it keeps the pot size controlled while I have the chip lead. I will call to see the flop.

I am first to act on a Queen-high board (Qs 9h 7c) with King-high and a Ten. While I have two overcards to the lower parts of the board and a backdoor straight draw, I don't have a made hand. Checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) reacts. I can evaluate the strength of her hand based on her betting sizing or if she checks back, which would allow me to see a free turn card and potentially improve.

Check. I was the preflop raiser but this flop (Q♠ 9♥ 7♣, rainbow) is a high-card, dry texture that heavily favors the BB’s calling range (lots of Qx, 9x, small pairs, some T8/TJ straights). My 5♦4♦ has essentially no showdown value and only backdoor diamond/straight possibilities, so a c‑bet here would be a marginal bluff that will be called too often and can commit me to a big pot with very little equity. I’ll use position to pot‑control and check/float: if Flash bets small (≈≤1/3 pot ≈215) I’ll consider calling to realize backdoor equity and take a free card on the turn; I’ll fold to large bets (>~1/2 pot ≈325) unless the turn improves me. If Flash checks back I’ll get to see a turn for free and can barrel favorable cards (a diamond or a low connector) or take advantage of later position.

I hit top pair (Kings) on the turn. Minni checked back on the flop, which suggests she doesn't have a very strong hand like a Queen or a set, or she is playing a draw cautiously. Now that the King has landed, I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential straight draws (J-10, 10-8) or flush draws that might have appeared with the second heart. A bet of around 60% of the pot should get called by marginal hands like a Queen, a Nine, or a heart draw.

Board: 9♥ 7♣ Q♠ K♥. I hold 5♦4♦ (no pair, no diamond on board -> no flush draw, no realistic straight draw). I was the preflop raiser and checked the dry flop; Flash checked back and now leads for 400 into a 1,050 pot (to-call 400). I have only 6 outs (three 5s + three 4s) → ~6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river. Break-even equity to call is 400/(1050+400) ≈ 27.6%, well above my ~13% equity. A call is not +EV. A raise as a bluff is also unattractive: I have virtually no fold equity versus a range that can include Kx/Qx/9x/7x, and I’d be turning a weak hand with no backup equity into a bigger commitment. With deep stacks I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am on the Button with a speculative hand (T8s). While this is often a standard open in a 3-handed game, the recent dynamics suggest extreme aggression from the blinds. specifically in Hand 110, I opened a similar hand (86s) from the Button and was 3-bet by Flash in the Small Blind. Additionally, Minni in the Big Blind has been 3-betting button raises frequently (Hands 111, 114). Given that I am the shortest stack and opening this hand likely leads to folding against a 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position against aggressive deep stacks, the prudent adjustment is to tighten my opening range and fold this marginal hand.

I hold pocket Tens, a premium hand in a three-handed game. With the button folding, I am opening from the Small Blind. I will raise to 300 chips to build the pot for value and to put pressure on Minni in the Big Blind, discouraging her from seeing a cheap flop with a wide range.

4♦2♠ is one of the worst hands to defend with. Flash opened to 300 from the SB; calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity. 4‑2o is heavily dominated by most of his opening range, has poor blockers and will play terribly out of position against likely c-bets. With deep stacks I’ll wait for a real hand or a better spot to 3‑bet bluff — preserve chips and fold here.

I am on the button with pocket sixes, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is the largest at the table, allowing me to apply pressure. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or set up for post-flop play with a solid pair.

Three-handed, facing a standard 3x button open to 300. I have T9 suited hearts — a strong, playable hand in short-handed play with good equity and nut-flush/straight potential. I’m out of position as the small blind, so a small/min-raise (to 500) doesn’t gain much — it bloats the pot and invites a 3-bet or a tough postflop spot. With deep stacks I prefer to see a flop and use the hand’s postflop playability: call the additional 250 for a total commitment of 300 (50 SB already posted) and evaluate based on the flop. Plan: continue aggressively on favorable flops (top pair, hearts, strong draws), fold to heavy action on dry/unfavorable boards unless I pick up good equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 6h. The Button (Flash) raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Minni) called. I am getting pot odds of 4.5 to 1 to call (200 to win 700). While the immediate odds are attractive, Q6 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially in a multi-way pot where I will be sandwiched between the two largest stacks. The reverse implied odds are significant (hitting a Queen is often dominated). Given my stack depth and the situation, folding is the disciplined choice to avoid bleeding chips in a low-equity spot.

I’m first to act OOP on a paired, dry flop (5s 8c 8d) holding Th9h — two overcards but no pair, no hearts and only backdoor, double-runner draws. I called preflop to see a flop and my preflop plan was to continue only with favorable boards (top pair/heart draws/strong draws) and otherwise avoid bloating the pot out of position. Betting here as a bluff can sometimes steal the pot, but it unnecessarily builds the pot with very little equity and leaves me crushed if I face a raise or call. I’ll check and see what Flash does: if he bets I’ll fold to sizable c-bets (I’m behind most of his continuing range) and consider a very light float only to target specific turn cards; if he checks back I take a free turn card to improve. Preserve stack and avoid a marginal OOP spot.

On a paired board of 5s-8c-8d, my pocket sixes represent a solid made hand, being an overpair to the five. Since Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here. This bet aims to extract value from high-card hands (like Ace-high or Broadway cards) that might call one street, and it protects my hand against potential overcards on the turn. A half-pot bet of 350 is a sized appropriately to maintain initiative on this dry texture.

Preflop I called with T9♥ for postflop playability. The flop (5♠ 8♣ 8♦) is dry and paired; I have no pair and no realistic backdoor flush/straight. Flash’s bet is small (350 into ~1050) — roughly 25% pot — so calling is cheap and the button’s c-bet range in 3-handed play is wide. Pot odds (~25%) are borderline with my ~20–25% equity to pair by the river, but with deep stacks I can float to a favorable turn or fold to real aggression. Raising OOP with just two overcards and no draw is a poor play. Plan: call the 350 to see a turn, fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve, and if I hit a T/9 or see clear weakness I’ll take the initiative.

I have T♥9♥ on 5♠ 8♣ 8♦ A♦. I never improved — I only have two overcards and no realistic draw (with one card to come I cannot make a 5-card heart flush), just ~6 outs to pair. The turn A is a very bad card for my equity because it favors the button’s continuing range (Ax, A‑x, 8x, many broadways) and makes thin bluffing OOP much less profitable. Pot control is the right line: check and give up to any meaningful bet. If Flash checks back I get a free river to reassess (and can barrel or value when I actually pair T/9 or see a favorable river texture). If he bets I fold to normal-sized aggression — only consider calling an extremely tiny bet for the bare odds to chase a pair.

On the turn, an Ace appears, which is a significant overcard to my pocket sixes. Minni called a continuation bet on a dry, paired flop (5-8-8), suggesting she has either a piece of the board, a pocket pair, or Ace-high. By checking back the turn, I am exercising pot control. My hand still has showdown value against her non-Ace high cards or smaller pocket pairs, but it is not strong enough to value bet now that the Ace has arrived. Checking allows me to keep the pot manageable and potentially reach showdown or evaluate her action on the river.

I improved to second pair on the river (T9 hearts vs board 5♠ 8♣ 8♦ A♦ 9♠). Flash opened pre, c‑bet small on the paired flop, then checked the Ace on turn — a wide continuing range that includes missed broadways, small pairs (5x), some A‑x and 8x. My 9 now beats a lot of his missed hands and thin pairs but loses to A‑x, 8x and any 9 with a better kicker. With the pot only 1,400 and deep stacks, the highest EV line is a small value bet to extract from worse hands and deny free showdown cards to bluffs. I’ll bet about half‑pot (700). If he calls we go to showdown; if he raises I’ll fold to his strong line (likely 8x/A8/boats).

On a board of 5s-8c-8d-Ad-9s, I hold pocket sixes. I was the pre-flop aggressor and continuation-bet the flop, but I checked back the turn when the Ace appeared to control the size of the pot and because the Ace is a significant scare card for my range. On the river, the 9s is relatively neutral, and Minni leads for 700 into a 1400 pot. This half-pot bet often represents either a thin value bet with an Ace or a bluff designed to push me off a medium-strength hand after I showed weakness by checking the turn. Given that we are three-handed and I am getting 3-to-1 pot odds, I only need to be right 25% of the time to make this call profitable. My hand beats all of her missed draws and potential bluffs. While she could have an Ace or an eight, the price is too good to fold a pair in this situation.

Three-way but SB has folded so I’m up against only the big blind (Flash). I’m on the button with J♠2♥ — a marginal hand, but in 3-handed play the button can open very wide to seize initiative and use post‑flop position. Calling 100 gives Flash a cheap flop and too good odds; a tiny min‑raise (200) invites a lot of action. I’ll open‑raise to 300 (3x BB) to take the pot initiative, deny correct pot odds to many of BB’s calling hands, and simplify postflop decisions since I’ll have position. If Flash 3‑bets large I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I can continue applying pressure or evaluate on the flop.

Minni opens from the Button, likely with a wide range. I hold King-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a strong hand that dominates much of a standard button opening range. While a 3-bet is a valid option to isolate and extract value, calling is attractive here to control the pot size while playing out of position, particularly given the stack depth (over 100 BBs). A 3-bet risks being 4-bet off a hand that has good playability and equity against the opener's range. By calling, I keep the opponent's range wide and avoid bloating the pot pre-flop with a marginal-premium hand. This also balances my calling range, as I have flatted with similar strength hands (like AQ) in previous spots.

I am holding 2-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 700), the hand has extremely poor playability post-flop, especially out of position against two opponents. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation.

The flop comes As-Th-Td, a paired board with an Ace, which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's (Minni) range as she has all the strong Aces and broadway combinations. As the pre-flop caller from the Small Blind, I am out of position with King-High and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). Leading out (donk betting) on this texture is ill-advised because Minni will likely continue with a very wide portion of her range. My standard line here is to check to the aggressor. If she bets, I will evaluate based on sizing, as I do have 4 outs to the nuts, but for now, checking is the only correct move.

I raised preflop and Pro (SB) just called, so his calling range is wide and contains many A‑x, T‑x and pocket pairs that connect very well with this flop (A♠ T♥ T♦). I hold J♠2♥ — no pair and only a remote backdoor heart — so I have virtually no showdown value and very little equity against that range. A continuation bet here will be called too often (or check‑raised by a value hand) and would commit me with no backup. I’ll check to control the pot and gather information: fold to a sizable bet, consider calling a very small c‑bet as a positional float to bluff later, and if Pro checks back I can re‑evaluate the turn with the initiative.

Pre-flop, I called with KQo in the Small Blind. The flop came As-Th-Td, checking to the raiser is standard. The opponent checked back, signaling a capped range likely consisting of weak Aces, underpairs, or air. The turn is the 8d. I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (need a Jack for the broadway straight). Leading here turns my hand into a bluff and I don't expect to fold out a weak Ace or a pair like 99/77 with a single bet. Checking allows me to potentially realize my equity for free if it checks through again, or evaluate if the opponent bets. Given I am the shortest stack, preserving chips and not bloating the pot with a marginal hand out of position is prudent.

Check. I have J♠2♥ on A♠ T♥ T♦ 8♦ and virtually no showdown value or meaningful draw. I opened preflop and SB called a wide range that contains lots of A‑x, T‑x and medium pairs that connect very well with this board. A turn c‑bet here would be a very thin bluff — it will be called by most A’s, Tens and pairs and can get check‑raised or priced in when I have almost no equity. By checking I keep the pot small, use my position to gather information, and preserve fold equity on later streets. Plan: fold to a sizable bet, consider calling a very small stab as a positional float to try a river play, and if SB checks back re‑evaluate on the river (look for a favorable card to bluff or give up).

Pre-flop I defended small blind with KQo. The board ran out As-Th-Td-8d-4h with action checking through on the flop and turn. My opponent (Minni) has shown no aggression, suggesting a range of weak Aces, underpairs (pocket pairs below Ten), or missed broadway/connectors. While I hold King-high which beats her complete air (missed draws, lower high cards), I lose to any pair. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a weak Ace or a pocket pair, as my line of checking twice doesn't represent much strength either. Bluffing into a small pot (7bb) with the shortest stack is unnecessary risk. I check to realize my showdown value against her air or fold if she bets, preserving my stack.

I have J♠2♥ on A♠ T♥ T♦ 8♦ 4♥ and both of us checked flop and turn. I have essentially no showdown value, but Pro’s multiple checks show weakness and his preflop calling range includes many unpaired broadways and underpairs that can reasonably fold a river stab. A small-to-medium bluff is profitable as long as fold equity > bet/(pot+bet) — with a 350 bet into 700 that threshold is ~33%. I estimate enough of his range will fold to make a half-pot bluff +EV here, while keeping the risk controlled if I get called. Plan: put out a credible half-pot bluff (350). If he raises big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll usually muck at showdown.

On the river, the pot is 700 and the opponent bets 350 (half pot). I hold King-high (Ks Qc). The board is [As Th Td 8d 4h]. The opponent checked back the flop and turn, which usually indicates a capped range of weak Aces, underpairs (22-99), or missed draws (QJ, J9, diamonds). By betting the river, the opponent is either going for thin value with a pair or bluffing with missed draws. Crucially, holding Ks and Qc means I block the most likely bluffs (QJ for the straight draw, KJ, Q9). Since I block the bluffs and lose to even the smallest pair (like 22) or a weak Ace, my hand has very poor bluff-catching properties. Checking three times signals weakness, but calling with King-high when blocking the opponent's bluffing range is a losing play in the long run. I have plenty of better hands (any pair, Ax) to call with if necessary.

I am on the Button with Jack-Four offsuit (J4o). This is a very weak hand with no connectivity or suited potential. Although being on the Button allows for a wider opening range in a 3-handed game, J4o falls well below the threshold for a profitable open raise or steal attempt, especially against two opponents with larger stacks who can apply pressure. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against an active opponent like Minni in the Big Blind. While the cost to call is only 50 chips, J6o rarely flops well enough to continue against aggression. Given my current chip lead and the remaining hands in the tournament, folding this marginal hand to avoid unnecessary post-flop complications is the most prudent strategy.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed game with 5-7 suited. This is a standard opening range hand for the button position. My goal is to either take down the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. Opening to 3x (300) has been my consistent sizing throughout the tournament.

Pocket jacks in the small blind vs a button open to 300 is a clear 3‑bet spot. JJ is strong enough to take the initiative, isolate the button and fold out weaker broadway hands and some suited connectors, while still having good equity vs calling ranges. I’ll avoid the tiny min‑3bet (500) which invites multiway pots and cold calls from the big blind; instead I size to a solid, but not overcommitting, 1200. This gives good fold equity, keeps post‑flop play manageable with deep stacks, and leaves room to fold or continue if met with a big 4‑bet. Action: 3‑bet to 1200.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit (A6o). The action began with Flash (Button) raising to 300, followed by Minni (Small Blind) 3-betting to 1200. Facing a raise and a substantial 3-bet (12bb), my hand is far too weak to continue. A6o is easily dominated by the calling or 4-betting ranges of the original raiser and the 3-bet range of the Small Blind. Additionally, investing over 10% of my stack pre-flop with this hand in a multi-way scenario is not profitable. The correct play is to fold.

I am on the button and originally opened with 5h7h for 300. Minni, in the small blind, has 3-bet to 1200. While 5h7h is a decent suited connector, I am facing a significant raise from Minni who has shown a tendency to play aggressively and follow through on post-flop streets (as seen in Hand #93 and Hand #114). With Pro having folded, I would be playing heads-up, but out of position to the small blind relative to the pre-flop action (though I have positional advantage post-flop). Given the tournament stage and the 4x size of the 3-bet, calling 900 more with a speculative hand like 75 suited is likely -EV in this specific spot against Minni's range. I will fold to preserve my stack and look for a better spot.

I have Q7s on the button with deep stacks (225 bb). This is a marginal but playable hand in position — it has flush/straight potential and good blocker value. Opening from the button is standard to pick up the blinds and to take advantage of position postflop. I’ll make a standard 3x-sized open to apply pressure but not overcommit: it gives solid fold equity against the SB (Pro, ~104 bb) and keeps weaker hands in so I can play postflop in position if called. Flash (BB) is the aggressive, big-stack player and may 3-bet; against a large 3-bet I’ll fold Q7s as it’s often dominated, but I can consider calling a smaller 3-bet to play postflop. Overall a 300 raise balances steal equity and postflop maneuverability.

I hold 9h 2h in the Small Blind facing a standard 3bb open from the Button. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling or 3-betting here is negative expected value. The correct play is to fold.

I am in the big blind with Q5s. Minni, who is an active and aggressive player, has raised to 300 from the button. While Q5s is a mediocre hand, folding directly to a single raise from the button in a three-handed game is often too tight. However, out of position, it is difficult to play. Looking at previous hands, Minni often raises and 3-bets. I have a large stack and can afford to see a flop if I choose, but Q5o (offsuit) is significantly weaker than suited. Since my hand is offsuit (Qd5s) and I am out of position against a button raise, folding is a disciplined and safe play to avoid unnecessary chip loss with a marginal hand.

I am on the Button with 9c 4d, a very weak offsuit hand. The blinds are 50/100 and I have a stack of roughly 104bb. Given the hand's lack of playability and the fact that the opponents (Flash and Minni) have large stacks and have been active, there is little incentive to attempt a steal with such poor holdings. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and wait for a better hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Trey of Hearts and Five of Diamonds, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Pro) has already folded. While the cost to call is only 50 chips to see a flop, I would be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand against Minni (Big Blind), who has shown significant aggression in recent hands. Given that I have a comfortable chip lead and we are approaching the final stages of the tournament (Hand 122 of 150), there is no reason to commit chips with a bottom-tier hand. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for higher-equity situations.

K7o is a standard opening hand from the button in a three-handed game. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take advantage of my position. My stack size allows me to maintain this aggressive opening range to pick up the blinds or play in position post-flop.

Qd2s is a very weak holding and is heavily dominated by the Button’s open range. I’m in the small blind and will be out of position post‑flop against both opponents; calling 250 more (to 300 total) to see a flop with almost no playability and a poor kicker is negative EV. A 3‑bet to 500 would be a pure bluff with no blockers and would lead to difficult decisions against two deep, competent stacks — not worth it here. With deep stacks I can be patient and wait for a much better spot, so I’ll conserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 4d, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button (Flash) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind folded. Although I am getting reasonable pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650), my hand has poor playability and I will be out of position against an aggressive opponent. The equity realization of 64o out of position is low, making this a negative expectation call. Preserving my stack of 10,350 is more important than defending with trash hands.

Button with 7h6s in a 3-handed spot — this is a clear open-raise candidate. I have position on both blinds and deep stacks, so I want to take the initiative rather than limp/call and let the blinds see a cheap flop. Min-raise (200) is too small and invites loose defenders; I size to a standard ~3x BB to 300 to generate meaningful fold equity while not committing too many chips. Plan: fold to a large, polarized 3-bet; call a small-to-medium 3-bet to play postflop in position; if called I'll use position to c-bet appropriate textures or pot-control. Total to put in pot: 300.

Opponent Minni is raising frequently from the button (3x open). I hold KQo in the Small Blind. This hand is well ahead of a typical button opening range, dominating many Kx and Qx hands. While a 3-bet is a valid option to isolate and take the initiative, playing 100BB deep out of position suggests a flat call is prudent to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid facing a 4-bet or playing a bloated pot without a made hand. Calling also offers a good price and disguises the strength of my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 5c, a very weak hand. Minni opened from the Button to 300 and Pro called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into 700), 85o plays very poorly in a three-way pot, especially being out of position for the remainder of the hand. With two opponents, the likelihood that my hand is dominated or will be forced to fold on most flops is high. Given my significant chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips on such a marginal holding.

I have flopped a monster hand, Queens full of Kings, on a board of Qh Qc Kd. I hold Qd Kh. The only hand that beats me is Pocket Kings (KK), of which there is only 1 combination remaining (Ks Kc). Since I have the effective nuts and am out of position against an aggressive pre-flop raiser (Minni on the Button), my goal is to extract maximum value. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (containing AA, AK, AQ, KQ, etc.), making a continuation bet highly likely. Checking is the standard play to induce a bet from the opponent. Leading out would likely fold out their bluffs or weaker hands that I want to keep in the pot.

Flop: Qh Qc Kd. I hold 7h6s — no pair, no sensible draws (there’s no way to make a 5‑card flush or a reliable straight by the river). Pro cold‑called my preflop raise, so their range contains a lot of Qx, Kx/KQ, and pocket pairs that crush my hand. Betting here as a bluff has very poor fold equity and will be called by many hands that beat me; leading into a range that’s heavily weighted toward queens/kings is unprofitable. I’ll check to control the pot and keep my stack intact. Plan: fold to any meaningful bet (I have essentially zero equity), and only consider a delayed bluff if the turn is an extremely favorable blank and villain shows weakness (checks). Amount committed: 0 (check).

I hold a Full House (Queens full of Kings) on a board of Qh Qc Kd 6h. The pot is 700. Minni checked back the flop, which is a board that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. This check-back usually indicates either a trap (slow playing a Q or KK) or a weak hand/give-up (underpairs, Ace high, air). Since I block King and Queen heavily (holding one of each, with three on the board), it is statistically unlikely Minni has a strong made hand. If I bet, I likely fold out all her bluffs and weak holdings. By checking, I induce bluffs from her air range or value bets if she hit a pair of 6s or holds an underpair like JJ/TT hoping to get to showdown cheap. I am essentially unheatable (only losing to the single combo of KK), so giving a free card is not dangerous. I will check to trap and extract value on the river or induce a bet now.

I paired my 6 on the turn but it’s still a very weak hand on Qh Qc Kd 6h. Pro cold‑called my raise preflop, so their continuing range is heavy with Qx, Kx and pocket pairs that dominate me. Betting as a bluff here has very little fold equity — many of those hands will simply call — and I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal pair. I’m in position so I can see one more card for free and control the pot size. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful bet; if checked to river I can re-evaluate (look for a blank river to bluff or check‑showdown on scary runouts).

I hold a powerful full house (Queens full of Kings) on a board of Qh Qc Kd 6h 4d. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. My opponent's check-backs suggest she does not possess a Queen or King, and likely holds a pocket pair (JJ-55), Ace-high, or complete air. Since I block the board heavily (holding a Queen and a King), it is unlikely she connects with this board. If I check, she will likely check back her showdown value hands. To extract value, I must bet. A small bet is most likely to induce a call from her weaker pairs or curiosity calls from Ace-high. I will bet approximately half the pot.

Board: Qh Qc Kd 6h 4d. My hand 7h6s gives me two pair (Qs and 6s). Pro (SB) bets 350 into a 1050 pot (~33%). Calling 350 to win ~1400 gives ~25% pot odds — cheap enough to call with a hand that beats a lot of his continuing range (missed broadways, A‑high, many small pocket pairs 22–55). Hands that dominate me (Qx trips/full houses, Kx two pair, 66/44 full houses, pocket 77+) are all possible, but the small sizing is consistent with a blocking/thin‑value bet and will be called by many worse hands. Raising risks jamming into stronger hands or folding out worse hands that pay me off; folding surrenders too much equity for a small cost. I’ll take the cheap showdown and call 350; re‑evaluate if he jams or raises further.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Eight offsuit on the Button is a standard opening hand due to position and card removal effects. Although the Big Blind (Minni) has been aggressive with 3-bets against the Small Blind recently, my tighter image may discourage a light 3-bet. With a stack of over 100 big blinds, I can comfortably raise for value and to steal the blinds. If faced with significant aggression (3-bet), I can easily fold this marginal hand without much loss. I will open to 2.5bb.

In a 3-handed game, King-Queen offsuit is a premium hand. Pro has opened from the button for 2.5x, which is a standard opening range. As the chip leader in the small blind, I want to take the initiative and either win the pot immediately or narrow the field. Calling would put me out of position against both the button and the big blind (Minni), who has shown significant aggression. By 3-betting to 900, I put pressure on Pro and discourage Minni from squeezed-calling or 4-betting light. This sizing allows me to define my range and maintain control of the pot.

I have 6h3s — a very weak offsuit hand with almost no playability. Pro opened to 250 on the button and Flash 3-bet to 900, putting me in a multiway pot out of position. The pot is 1250 and it costs me 800 more to call (my total contribution would be 900), so I need roughly 800 / (1250+800) ≈ 39% equity to break even. 6-3o has nowhere near that vs a button open + a small‑blind 3‑bet range, and I have no blockers or fold equity worth risking chips with. A 4‑bet bluff would be unnecessarily high variance and likely to be called or 5‑bet jammed by stronger ranges. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I opened with A8o from the Button, a standard steal/value raise. Flash in the Small Blind has 3-bet to 900. While I have position, A8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range, often being dominated by better Aces (AT+) or flipping against pairs. Flash has shown aggression with 3-bets recently (e.g., Hand #110, #95), but calling 650 more chips (pot odds ~34%) with a hand that suffers from reverse implied odds is risky. As the shortest stack (though still deep at ~108BB), preserving my stack for better spots is prudent. I will fold.

I am on the button with 9d 3s. This is a very weak hand. While I have the largest stack and position, 9-3 offsuit is not worth a raise, especially against opponents like Minni who have shown willingness to 3-bet or call aggressively. Given the stack depths and the hand strength, folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

QTs suited is a strong heads‑up hand — good connectedness and suitedness to realize equity. With the button folded we’re effectively heads‑up vs Pro (BB) and in heads‑up the dealer (small blind) acts last post‑flop, so raising preflop gives us initiative and post‑flop positional advantage. Pro has shown a fairly wide defense/calling tendency, so a standard 3x BB open will pick up the blinds sometimes while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. I’ve already posted 50 in the SB, so raising to 300 total (adding 250) is a clean, standard size to apply pressure and play postflop. If Pro 3‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate range and sizing; otherwise take the lead and play the flop aggressively.

Current hand is 126 of 150. I am in the Big Blind with [6d Jc]. The blinds are 50/100. Minni (SB) has raised to 300. The pot is 400, and it costs me 200 to call, offering 3:1 odds (25% equity required to call strictly on pot odds, but realizability is lower). Although I would be in position post-flop, J6o is a very weak hand that performs poorly, often dominated by better Jacks or overcards. With 107 BBs deep, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better spots rather than defending with junk hands that have poor playability and reverse implied odds. Minni is an active player, and playing a weak hand against them is likely -EV. I decide to fold.

Folded to me on the button with Kc8c and deep stacks (≈220bb). This is a playable, suited king — good position and backdoor club equity. I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than limp. A standard 3xBB open (300) gives reasonable fold equity, keeps the pot manageable, and preserves post‑flop maneuverability against both opponents (Flash is a wide defender, Pro is medium-deep). If I face a big 3‑bet I can release this marginal hand; against a small 3‑bet I may call and play position. Limping/passive play is suboptimal here, so I raise to 300.

In the Small Blind with ATo facing a Button raise from the active chip leader (Minni), I have a strong hand relative to their wide opening range. However, at 100BB deep, 3-betting risks facing a 4-bet which would force a fold of a hand with good equity. Calling keeps Minni's range wide, including dominated hands like KTo, QTo, and worse Aces, and controls the size of the pot while playing out of position. While there is a risk of the BB squeezing, flatting is a lower-variance line that realizes equity well against an aggressive opener.

Holding 9-4 offsuit in the Big Blind, I am facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 700), this hand has very poor playability and equity. Being out of position for the remainder of the hand against two opponents with a weak hand is generally a losing proposition. As the chip leader with 23 hands remaining, I should avoid unnecessary losses with trash hands and wait for better spots to utilize my stack advantage.

I am out of position on a 6h 9d 2d flop with Ace-high (As Td) and a backdoor flush draw. The standard play in a heads-up pot against the pre-flop aggressor is to check. This board texture hits my calling range reasonably well (pairs like 77-99, suited connectors), but I have no reason to lead out with my specific holding. I will check to see Minni's action; if they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, but for now, checking is the only strategic option.

I raised preflop and have position; the flop (6h 9d 2d) is very dry and largely misses calling ranges. Pro (SB) checked, so I should use my initiative to take the pot down. Kc8c has some useful blockers (the K makes top-pair combos less likely for villain) and a tiny backdoor club plan, but no real showdown value — this is a pure c-bet spot. With deep stacks I want meaningful fold equity but don’t want to build an unmanageable pot if I’m behind, so I’ll bet ~50% pot. If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll fold this unimproved hand.

I am defending my Small Blind against a Button open from a wide-range opponent. The flop of 6h 9d 2d is relatively dry but offers a flush draw. I hold AsTd, giving me Ace-high, a Ten kicker, and a backdoor flush draw. Minni bets 350 into a pot of 700 (half-pot). Against a Button c-bet range, which includes many flush draws, straight draws, lower pairs, and air (like KQ, QJ), my Ace-high has significant showdown value. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, requiring roughly 25% equity. Even against top pair, I have live outs (Aces and Tens), and I am ahead of their bluffs. Folding Ace-high here would be over-folding against an aggressive player. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the Turn, the King of Spades arrives, making the board 6h 9d 2d Ks. This card hits the pre-flop raiser's range (Button) harder than my Small Blind flat-calling range, as Minni has many Kx hands that would c-bet the flop. Being out of position with just Ace-high (As Td), I have no reason to lead into the aggressor. The standard line is to check to the raiser. If Minni checks back, I realize my equity for free. If Minni bets again, I will face a difficult decision as the King makes it likely I am behind, but for now, the only correct move is to check.

I opened preflop and continued on a very dry flop (6h 9d 2d) — Pro called my c‑bet, so his range is a lot of pairs and some broadways. The turn K♠ pairs my Kc and gives me top pair with an 8 kicker while checked to me. This is a clear value/protection spot: I want to extract from worse pairs (9x, 6x, small pockets) and vulnerable Kx, deny any unlikely two‑diamond river draws, and keep the pot manageable with position. I’ll bet ~50% pot (700). If I face a large check‑raise I can fold my marginal kicker; if called I’ll re‑evaluate the river.

I am in the Small Blind with AsTd. I called a pre-flop raise from the Button (Minni) and check-called a half-pot c-bet on a 6h 9d 2d flop with Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw.
