The turn is the Ks, making the board 6h 9d 2d Ks. This card significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which contains many King-high hands (KQ, KJ, KT, K9) that would barrel here. While I beat missed flush draws or straight draws (like QJ), I am now behind all pairs in Minni's value range (Kx, 9x, 6x, 2x) and have very little equity to improve. Minni bets 700 (half-pot) again. Continuing with just Ace-high out of position against a double barrel on a scare card that hits the opponent's range is a losing play. I have better hands in my range to defend with (Kx, 9x, sets). Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

Current tournament state: 3-handed, Hand 128/150. I am on the Button with A2o (As 2h) and 101BB stack (10,100). The blinds are aggressive, particularly Flash in the Small Blind who has 3-bet my last two button opens (Hand 125 with A8o and Hand 110 with 86s). While A2o is normally a standard open on the button in a 3-handed game, the high likelihood of facing a 3-bet from Flash makes opening this marginal hand -EV, as I would be forced to fold and lose 2.5BB. With my stack being the smallest of the three but still deep, I should preserve chips and avoid high-variance spots with bottom-range hands against aggressive opponents who have shown a tendency to punish my opens. Therefore, I will make an exploitative fold.

I am the chip leader in the Small Blind with a suited Queen. Since the Button has folded, I am only competing with Minni in the Big Blind. Queen-Four suited is a strong enough hand to attempt a steal or play a pot head-up in a three-handed game. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the Big Blind while maintaining control over the pot size if called.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB. I have Q♠ T♥ in the BB — a playable hand but marginal out of position. With deep stacks and the button (SB) retaining postflop position, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a hand that can be dominated. A 3‑bet or a tiny reraise is unattractive: I’d still be OOP and would risk tough spots vs stronger broadways or a 4‑bet. Calling is cheap enough to realize equity and keep the pot controllable. Plan: call to see the flop (total commitment 300); if I hit top pair or a strong draw I’ll play aggressively, otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to large pressure or a big 4‑bet.

I have the betting lead after raising pre-flop with Qc4c. The flop of Tc8d5d is relatively neutral. I don't have a made hand, but as the chip leader in a three-handed game, a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard play to put pressure on Minni and potentially end the hand right here. If I get called, I have a backdoor flush draw and two overcards to the middle of the board.

I have top pair (T) with a Q kicker on Tc‑8d‑5d. Flash’s bet is small (300 into a 900 pot) and the board has two diamonds — there are draws but nothing that crushes my hand yet. Out of position and with deep stacks I want pot control and to keep worse hands and bluffs in; a raise here only bloats the pot and risks a tough spot vs stronger tens or a 3‑bet. Folding is too weak. I’ll call the 300 to realize equity, see a turn, and re‑evaluate: if I improve or he shows weakness I’ll press; if he barrels a big bet on a scary turn (diamond/overcard) I’ll be prepared to fold.

The board has paired on the turn with the 8h. After Minni called my flop continuation bet, it's likely she has at least a piece of the board or a draw (like a diamond flush draw or a straight draw). While the pairing of the 8 is a card that theoretically favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, my hand (Queen-high with no draw) has very little equity if called again. Since I am the chip leader, I will check here to see if I can get a free river or evaluate based on her action. If she checks behind, I might have a chance to bluff or improve on the river; if she bets, I can safely fold without losing more chips with a weak hand.

I have top pair (T) with a Queen kicker on Tc‑8d‑5d‑8h. Villain (SB) c‑bet the flop small and now checked the turn — a clear sign of weakness or a float/diamond draw. Checking behind just gifts free cards to the two‑diamond draw and lets weaker hands improve cheaply. Out of position I still don’t want to bloat the pot, but I should both extract value from worse Tens/Tx hands, Qx and some pairs, and charge draws. I’ll make a value/protection bet of about half the pot (600 into 1200). If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I can re‑evaluate on the river; if he check‑raises big I’ll assume a very strong hand (sets/88) and fold to sizable aggression.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Eight of Hearts. After I checked, Minni led out with a bet of 600 into an 1800 pot. My hand, Queen-Four of clubs, has no pair and no real draw left, as the backdoor club potential has vanished. Minni's lead on the turn after calling pre-flop and on the flop suggests she either has a Ten, an Eight, or perhaps a diamond flush draw that she is now betting aggressively. Given my hand has almost no equity and no showdown value, calling is not an option. While I am the chip leader, bluff-raising into a board that likely hit her range (or where she's showing strength) is unnecessarily risky. The most disciplined play here is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with Ace-Four offsuit. This is a standard opening hand for the Button. Opening to 3x the big blind is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take control of the pot or win the blinds immediately. With an Ace in my hand, I have good showdown potential if called and a strong blocker to my opponents' continuing ranges.

I’m in the small blind with K♦5♦ facing a BTN open to 300. Pot is 450 and it’s 250 to call. K5s is a marginal, often-dominated hand and plays poorly out of position — calling 250 will put me OOP vs both opponents and lead to many difficult postflop spots. A tiny reraise to 500 buys very little fold equity and just invites cheap calls or a squeeze from the BB. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left, I’ll conserve chips for better spots. Fold.

The opponent Flash opens to 300 (3bb) from the Button. I am in the Big Blind with 3s9s. The pot is currently 450 (300 + 50 + 100), and I need to call 200 to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of roughly 30.7%. Although my hand is suited, 93s is a very weak holding (a 'trash' hand) that is disconnected and dominates very little of the opponent's range. Playing 93s out of position against a 3x open is generally expected to be -EV, as I will struggle to realize my equity and will often be dominated or face difficult decisions on later streets. Looking at my history, I have folded stronger or similar hands (like J9o, 94o, 56o) against similar opens from Flash. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct play.

Three-handed table: button should open up. K♠2♣ is a marginal hand but has a king-blocker and is worth using to try and steal the blinds and play in position. Open to a standard 3x size to apply pressure without committing a meaningful portion of my deep stack. If I face a substantial 3-bet I’ll fold K2o — I don’t want to play a big OOP pot with a dominated hand. If called, I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and keep the pot manageable while extracting value when appropriate.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit facing a 3x raise (300) from Minni on the Button. While an Ace has some showdown value, A6o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Calling would require investing 250 chips into a pot that will be difficult to navigate, and it invites the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall. 3-betting is a potential bluffing option but carries significant risk against the chip leader. Given the stack depths and positional disadvantage, the disciplined play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Nine-Five offsuit. Minni has raised from the button to 300. While I am getting decent odds to call (200 to win a pot of 450), 9-5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent. Since I currently hold a stack lead and we are approaching the final stages of the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand and risk chips in a post-flop situation where I will be at a positional disadvantage.

I hold Ten-Six offsuit (6h Tc) on the Button. This is a very weak hand, well below the standard opening range for the button, especially given the aggressive tendencies of the blinds (Flash and Minni) who have significant chip leads. With 100BB deep stacks, there is no desperate need to steal with trash hands. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with T8o. The action folded to me, and I need to decide whether to call, raise, or fold. With only 3 players left and a relatively large stack (over 260bb), T8o is a decent hand to play from the Small Blind against the Big Blind (Minni), who has been quite active. Raising to 300 is a standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind and try to win the pot pre-flop. Folding is too tight given the current table dynamics and the fact that I am in the Small Blind. Calling is an option, but raising allows me to take the initiative.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the SB with A♣8♦ in the big blind. Flash has opened to 300 a lot in previous hands, so his range is likely wide. I’m deep-stacked (≈234BB) but I’ll be out of position postflop (heads-up vs the SB), and A8o is worth defending but is marginal as a 3-bet candidate (I’d be dominated by better Axs and bloat the pot OOP). Pot odds are good (pot 400, to call 200), so I’ll defend and see a flop. Plan: call to keep the pot controllable; if I hit an Ace I’ll extract value, with draws I can continue selectively, otherwise control the pot and fold to large aggression.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I am leading out with a continuation bet on this Ace-high board. Ace-high boards are favorable for the range of the raiser, and a 50% pot bet (300) puts pressure on Minni to have a piece of the board or a draw to continue. While I only have Ten-high, this is a standard spot to represent the Ace and take down the pot before the board gets more complicated with the existing flush and straight draws.

I have top pair (A♣) with an 8 kicker on A‑6‑5 (two diamonds). Flash is a frequent, wide SB opener and his flop stab is very small (300 into this pot) — that line contains lots of bluffs and weak A‑highs. I want to both extract value from worse A‑hands and small pairs and deny cheap cards to obvious turn/river runouts (4/7 straights, diamonds). Raising also prices out pure floaters while keeping the pot reasonable given deep stacks. I’ll make a value/protection raise to a size that gets called by worse A‑hands and charges draws but is still foldable if he shows heavy aggression. Action: raise to 1200 total on the flop; if he folds I take it down, if he calls I proceed to the turn and re-evaluate, and I’ll fold to a large check‑raise/shove unless I pick up clear equity.

On this Ace-high board (5d-Ah-6d), my continuation bet of 300 was a standard attempt to represent an Ace and take down the pot. However, Minni's 4x raise to 1200 indicates significant strength or a strong draw (like a flush draw or 78/47 for a straight draw). Holding only Ten-high with no pair and no real draw, I have zero showdown value and very little equity to continue. Even if I call, I will likely face more aggression on the turn. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack, as this is not a profitable spot to attempt a bluff against an active opponent like Minni.

I am on the button with 9-3 offsuit. While I have been playing aggressively and opening many pots from the button, 9-3 is near the bottom of my range. With both opponents possessing reasonable stacks and Minni (SB) showing a tendency to defend or 3-bet, this hand is too weak to attempt a steal. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead.

Button folded — I'm heads-up vs the big blind (Pro) with pocket eights (8h8c). Pot is 150, I need 50 to call. With deep effective stacks (≈240BB vs ≈98BB) 88 is a strong, playable hand that I want to take the initiative with rather than just completing and playing OOP. A standard heads-up open to ~3×BB (raise to 300 total) gives solid fold equity, builds value when I'm ahead, and preserves implied-odds/set-mining potential if called. Sizing is big enough to discourage weak floats but small enough that I can fold to a large 3‑bet or call a modest 3‑bet for implied odds. Plan: raise to 300; if called proceed OOP postflop looking for sets and favorable runouts; if 3‑bet large, re-evaluate (often fold), if 3‑bet small consider calling.

The opponent Minni opens to 3BB (300) from the Small Blind. I am in the Big Blind with 7h6d (76o). While 76o has some connectivity, it is an offsuit hand and I will be playing out of position. The 3x raise size offers worse pot odds (requires ~33% equity) compared to a min-raise, and realizing equity with weak offsuit connectors OOP is difficult. Reviewing recent history (e.g., Hand 130, 115), I have folded hands like A6o and 54o to this same action, indicating a tighter defensive range against this 3x sizing. Therefore, folding is the consistent and prudent play to preserve the stack.

Three-handed, button with A♥9♥ is a clear open — suited ace, good blockers and strong postflop play in position. Pot is only 150 and both opponents are deep-stacked (Pro ~98bb, Flash ~258bb), so I want to seize initiative and isolate rather than limp. I’ll open to ~3bb (300 total) — large enough to generate fold equity and discourage free multiway flops but small enough to keep SPR reasonable and allow postflop play if called. Plan: continue with position on most flops; call small 3-bets to play postflop, but respect large 3-bets and fold if the price and ranges are unfavourable.

In a 3-handed game, facing a 3x button raise (300) from the active chip leader (Minni), holding Pocket Deuces (22) in the Small Blind presents a difficult spot. While deep enough (98BB) to set mine, the 3x sizing worsens the pot odds, and being out of position against an aggressive opponent makes realizing equity difficult without hitting a set. Calling leads to a 'fit-or-fold' scenario where I likely check-fold most flops. 3-betting is risky as 22 has poor post-flop playability if called. Conserving chips and avoiding a marginal out-of-position spot is the prudent decision here.

In a 3-handed game, button opening ranges are extremely wide. Minni has been very active from the button. I am holding Qd 2d in the Big Blind. The price to call is 200 chips to win a pot that will be 700 chips, giving me 3.5:1 pot odds. While Q2s is a weak hand, it has suited value and high-card potential in a short-handed environment. Defending here prevents the button from profitably stealing with any two cards, though I will have to play the rest of the hand out of position.

On a flop of 9d-3d-4h, I have a flush draw with the Queen of diamonds. I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the standard and most flexible play here, allowing me to see if Minni continues her aggression. If she bets, I have a strong enough draw to comfortably call or even consider a check-raise depending on her sizing, but for now, checking to the raiser is the best starting move.

I have A♥9♥ on 9♦ 3♦ 4♥ — top pair with the nut kicker and I’m in position. Flash (BB) checked, which is consistent with a wide calling/floating range that includes weaker nines, small pairs, and diamond draws. There are two diamonds on the board so denying free cards and charging diamond draws is important. I want value from worse pairs and to protect my hand, while keeping the pot manageable given the deep stacks. Betting ~300 (~45% pot) hits both objectives: it gets called by worse hands and charges draws, but doesn’t commit my stack if I face heavy resistance. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn and continue for value on safe cards; if I face a large check-raise I’ll give credit to sets/strong draws and fold to very large aggression, maybe call small sizing depending on reads.

On a flop of 9d-3d-4h, I have a Queen-high flush draw. The pot is currently 950 and the bet from Minni is 300, meaning I need to call 300 to potentially win a total pot of 1250. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.17:1. Since a flush draw with 9 outs has roughly 36% equity against a wide range, I have more than enough equity to continue. Calling is the preferred line here to realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position, keeping Minni's bluffs in her range and allowing me to evaluate the turn card.

I have successfully made a Queen-high flush on the turn with the Ten of Diamonds appearing, creating a four-flush board. Since I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop and flop aggressor, I will check. Four-flush boards are extremely coordinated and often slow down action. If I bet, I likely only get called by a better diamond (Ace or King) or potentially the Jack of diamonds. By checking, I allow Minni to continue a bluff if she doesn't have a diamond or to bet for value if she has a lower diamond, while also controlling the pot size in case she is holding the Ace or King of diamonds.

Board: 9♦ 3♦ 4♥ (T♦ on turn). I hold A♥9♥ — top pair, top kicker. Flash (BB) called my flop bet and now checked the turn. The turn is a fairly hostile card: it brings a third diamond and an overcard Ten. Many hands that called the flop (two-diamond combos) now have a completed flush and some turn Tens also beat me. Betting here would build a pot where I have poor equity vs completed diamond flushes and could get check‑raised or committed facing large action. With deep stacks and a marginally vulnerable top pair, I prefer pot control and to avoid bloating the pot on a card that helps the opponent’s draw-heavy calling range. I’ll check back the turn, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the river — calling small river bets for value against worse pairs and folding to large, polarized raises that represent flushes or tens.

I have a Queen-high flush on a board with four diamonds. While a flush is normally a very strong hand, the four-diamond board significantly devalues non-nut flushes because any single diamond in the opponent's hand makes a flush. Minni checked back on the turn, which could indicate she either has a very strong diamond (Ace or King) and is playing for pot control/inducing bluffs, or she lacks a high diamond and is afraid of the board. By checking the river, I control the size of the pot and avoid a difficult decision if raised. I am looking to get to a cheap showdown or call a standard bet, as I likely have the best hand, but betting myself might only get called by hands that beat me (the Ad or Kd).

Check. I bet the flop for value/protection and checked the turn for pot control when the T♦ and an extra diamond made the board hostile — that card helps Tens and gives anyone holding two diamonds a made flush. The river 2♥ doesn’t improve those hands and I’m still top pair with the nut kicker, but a river bet would only extract thin value from a few worse hands while exposing me to a check‑raise or a larger jam from the small set of hands that beat me (T× or the rare two‑diamond flush). Pot is only 1,250 and stacks are deep, so I prefer to realize my equity and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Checking to showdown is the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play here.

I am on the Button with Qc 5s, which is a very weak hand (Queen-high, offsuit). Although I have the positional advantage, the hand quality is too poor to justify an opening raise, especially against two large stacks in the blinds who can easily call or 3-bet. My stack is 98 BBs, so I am not desperate to make moves. Preserving chips for better spots is the optimal strategy here.

In this three-handed situation, I am in the Small Blind with 8-4 suited. While a relatively weak hand, its suited nature provides some playability. Pro has folded on the button, leaving only Minni in the Big Blind. Raising to 300 is a standard aggressive play to attempt to win the blinds immediately or take control of the hand. As the chip leader, I want to maintain pressure on my opponents, especially since we are nearing the final hands of the tournament.

I have 2♦-4♥ — a very weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with almost no playability. Flash (SB) opened to 300; the pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call, which requires roughly 33% equity to break even right now. 2-4 offsuit has far less equity versus a normal opening range, and I'd be playing the rest of the hand out of position with no blockers or realistic fold equity. The min‑raise to 500 is an option but 2-4o is not a hand I want to 3‑bet bluff with deep stacks and another chance to get it in. Fold and wait for a better spot.

62o is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While I am on the button in a 3-handed game, which normally allows for a wider opening range, this hand is too weak to justify a raise, especially given that Minni (SB) has shown a high level of aggression and 3-betting frequency. With a comfortable stack lead and only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to take unnecessary risks with 'trash' hands. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

Button folded; heads-up against the BB. Kc7d is playable and I want initiative rather than passively defending. A raise to 300 (3×BB) gives strong fold equity (the BB must call +200) and lets me take the lead postflop with a c-bet on many favorable textures. If I get a small 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (fold to large/committed 3-bets without a strong holding); if called I’ll continue selectively with top-pair combos or on dry boards, and exercise pot control otherwise.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 2s, facing a raise to 300 (3bb) from the Small Blind (Minni). My hand is one of the weakest starting hands (42 offsuit). I have position on the Small Blind post-flop, but I need to call 200 to win a pot of 400, offering 3:1 odds (25% equity required? No, 200/(400+200) = 33%). My equity against a wide stealing range is likely just below or around 33%, but the poor playability of 42o makes realizing that equity difficult. With a stack of 97bb, I should preserve chips and wait for better spots to defend, rather than calling with the bottom of my range.

Button in a 3-handed game with deep stacks — position is my biggest advantage. T8o is marginal but profitable to open from the button: it has decent playability and fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to take the initiative, steal the blinds or isolate the big blind, while keeping the pot size manageable versus Flash’s big stack. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if I get called I’ll use position to c-bet good flops and pressure weak ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with J8o facing a 3bb open raise from the Button (Minni). Although Button opening ranges are wide in 3-handed play, J8o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling would require realizing significant equity which is difficult OOP without initiative. A 3-bet bluff is risky given the stack depths and lack of blockers. The raise size of 3bb makes the price to call unattractive for this marginal holding. Folding is the standard and most prudent play to preserve my stack.

In 3-handed play, King-Queen offsuit is a premium hand, especially when facing a button open from a frequent raiser like Minni. While calling is an option to keep the pot smaller out of position, 3-betting for value is the superior play here. It allows me to take control of the hand, potentially win the pot immediately, or define ranges for post-flop play. I will raise to 1,000 to put pressure on Minni's wide opening range.

Button with Ts8d — I opened to 300 to take the initiative, and Flash (BB) 3-bet to 1000. In a 3-handed, deep-stacked game position is my biggest edge and Flash’s 3-bet sizing is moderate, consistent with a fairly wide/aggressive range rather than an only-premium range. T8o is marginal but has good postflop playability in position and the price is reasonable: calling 700 (1000 total) gives ~34% pot odds and lets me realize equity and apply pressure on later streets. I won’t 4-bet-bluff with this hand; I’ll call, see the flop, c-bet when the texture and my equity justify it (or use position to probe/float), and fold to strong, committed action (a large 4‑bet or jam).

On this flop of Queen, Nine, Five, I have flopped top pair with a strong King kicker. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, I should follow through with a continuation bet. This board is relatively dry, but I want to extract value from hands like pocket Jacks, Tens, or even middle pairs and gutshot draws (like Jack-Ten) that Minni might have called the 3-bet with. A bet of 850 into the 2,050 pot is approximately 41% of the pot, which is a standard sizing to maintain pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I'm somehow outkicked by Ace-Queen.

Flop: Qh 9s 5c — I’m on the button with Ts8d. Flash 3‑bet pre and now fires a small 850 into a 2900 pot (very small sizing). Calling 850 commits 850 to win a pot that would be ~4600 (pot odds ≈ 850/4600 ≈ 18.5%). Hand equity: Ts8d has 4 Js to a made straight plus 3 tens and 3 eights to pair (naive ~10 outs, ~38% to improve by river, though T/8 are not clean vs Qx). Against a fairly wide, capped/aggressive 3‑bet/c‑bet range (small flop sizing points to many bluffs/weak broadways and some Qx/pairs), my realistic equity comfortably approaches or exceeds the ~18–19% break‑even threshold. With position and deep stacks I prefer to see another card and make a decision on the turn rather than folding away equity or bluff‑raising into a likely stronger range. Plan: call 850, use position on the turn — proceed with bluff/float lines if villain shows weakness, value/raise if I improve to a clean winner, fold to a large committed shove or heavy turn action when I haven’t improved.

The Seven of Diamonds on the turn is a relatively safe card. Having flopped top pair with a strong kicker (TPKK), I am likely still ahead of Minni's range, which includes weaker Queens like QJ or QT, pocket pairs like Jacks and Tens, and straight draws such as JT or 86. I will continue with a second barrel for value. A bet of 1,800 into the 3,750 pot (approximately 48%) is a solid sizing to extract value from those weaker made hands while charging draws to see the river.

3-handed, deep stacks. Pot is 5,550 and Flash has just bet 1,800 on the turn (Qh 9s 5c 7d). I hold Ts8d. Required equity to call = 1,800 / (5,550+1,800) ≈ 24.5%. My hand has 8 clean straight outs (any J or 6) and 6 additional pair outs (three tens, three eights) — not all pair-outs are clean vs a likely Qx/9x value portion of his range, but Flash has been aggressive with a small 3‑bet and tiny flop c‑bet which suggests a wide, partially-bluffing range. I estimate my realistic equity vs his continuing range is comfortably around or above the ~25% threshold given position and the number of river cards that help me. Raising here would be thin: I have little fold equity versus a deep, aggressive 3-bettor and would risk getting re-punished by stronger hands. Folding surrenders too much equity for a marginal price. Plan: call 1,800 to keep weaker hands/bluffs in and use position on the river — if I improve to a clean winner (straight or a defensible pair that plays well) I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll fold to large river aggression but may attempt a river bluff only when the river and sizing justify it.

On the river, the board texture is 9-5-Q-7-6. I have top pair with a King kicker (TPKK). While this was a very strong hand on the flop and turn, the river completes a potential straight for any hand containing an Eight (such as 8-9, 7-8, or T-8, all of which could have called the previous streets). Minni has been 'sticky', calling a 3-bet and two subsequent barrels. By checking, I control the size of the pot and can potentially induce a bluff from missed draws (like KJ or J10) or simply reach a cheap showdown against middle pairs. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I risk being raised by a straight or a set. Checking is the most prudent play to maximize my long-term stack size in this specific spot.

I just hit a straight on the river. Board is 9s-5c-Qh-7d-6d and my Ts-8d makes 5-6-7-8-9 (a 9-high straight). The only realistic hands that beat me are rare combos that hold both a T and an 8 (making a 10-high straight); otherwise Flash’s line (preflop 3-bet, tiny flop c-bet, turn bet then check on the river) looks like Qx, single pairs, missed draws or medium-value hands that will often call a value bet. Checking surrenders value to those medium hands. I want a size that gets called by top-pair/second-pair and pocket pairs but doesn’t overcommit vs the unlikely T8 combos — target about 1/3 pot. Bet 2,500 to extract value from Qx/9x/pairs and to induce calls from worse hands; if Flash raises big I’ll re-evaluate (small/medium raises I’ll likely call, huge shoves I’ll weigh combos and pot odds).

On the river, the board texture is 9-5-Q-7-6. My TPKK is still a very strong hand, but the 8 is now a straight card. I checked to control the pot size and induce bluffs. Minni bet 2,500 into a pot of 7,350, giving me pot odds of approximately 4:1. At these odds, I only need to be right about 20% of the time. Given that Minni is an active player who could be betting for thin value with a weaker Queen (like QJ or QT) or turning a hand like JT into a bluff after I showed weakness by checking, a call is the most mathematically and strategically sound decision. Folding TPKK here would be over-folding against a wide 3-handed range.

I am on the Button with [9c 3h], which is a very weak off-suit hand (9-3o). With 96.5 BBs, my stack is comfortable enough that I do not need to take unnecessary risks by opening with trash hands. My opponents in the blinds cover me and have been active, making a steal attempt with such low equity unprofitable. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with K2o. The action folded to me. I only need to put in 50 more chips to complete, but K2o is a weak hand to play out of position against Minni, who has been active and has a large stack. However, in a 3-handed game, folding the small blind to the big blind is often too tight. That said, I've just come off a hand where I lost a decent pot (Hand #136), so I'll opt for a simple fold here to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position against the chip leader.

Jack-Five offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even in a short-handed (3-player) game. While I am on the button, which is the best position, this hand does not play well post-flop and is easily dominated by the ranges of the Small Blind (Minni) and the Big Blind (Pro). Given that Minni is the chip leader and has been active, and Pro is looking for opportunities to move, folding here is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better spots in the final 13 hands.

7♥3♦ is a very weak hand and I'm out of position, but the button has folded so this is a heads-up decision vs the big blind (Pro). With deep effective stacks and only a 50-chip cost to see the flop, the right exploitative play is to take the initiative and attempt a small steal rather than limp/call OOP or auto-fold. I’ll min-raise to 200 to apply pressure and pick up the pot when Pro folds; the sizing is small relative to our stacks and preserves fold equity. If called, I’ll play the flop very cautiously and fold to heavy resistance without improvement. If I face a sizable 3-bet, I’ll relinquish the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit facing a min-raise from the Small Blind. I am getting excellent pot odds (3:1) requiring only 25% equity to call, and my hand is well ahead of a wide SB opening range. Additionally, I will have position on the raiser post-flop. While a 3-bet is an option to deny equity, calling allows me to play a pot in position against a wide range with a solid hand without bloating the pot unnecessarily with a marginal holding.

I was the preflop aggressor with a complete miss on a dry-ish paired board (2♣ 5♠ 5♣). With 7♥3♦ I have almost no showdown value, so this is a pure bluff/continuation-bet spot to leverage fold equity. Pro’s calling range to a min-raise contains lots of A‑high, broadways and small pairs that will often fold to a small c-bet here. I’ll make a compact c-bet (~35–40% pot) to take down the pot now while keeping the pot controlled. Plan: fold to a large raise or check-raise; if called I’ll give up on most turns unless I pair up or the action clearly changes (villain checks and we see a safe card), and avoid bloating the pot out of position with no equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit on a paired board of [2c 5s 5c]. The opponent (SB) c-bets small (150 into 400). Given the blind-vs-blind dynamic, the opponent's range is wide, and my Ace-high has significant showdown value, likely being ahead of their air and draw bluffs. The price to call is excellent, requiring very little equity. Folding here would be too tight, and raising might isolate me against better hands while folding out worse ones I'm beating. I will call to realize my equity and evaluate the turn.

I hold 7h3d — complete air. I c‑bet a small 150 on the dry paired flop (2c 5s 5c) and Pro just called. The turn K♦ doesn't improve me and actually helps a lot of hands in his calling range (Kx, Ax, broadways). Pot is 700 and action has checked to me. With almost no showdown value and no backdoor equity, I don't want to barrel into a range that now contains many made hands or risk a check‑raise. I’ll check to keep the pot small and conserve chips for better spots. If Villain fires a sizable bet on the turn I will fold; I might consider a very small call to a probing bet but will not invest heavily without improvement.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds arrives, and the Small Blind checks. The board is now paired [2c 5s 5c Kd]. I hold Ace-Eight offsuit, which has reasonable showdown value. The King hits the opponent's range somewhat (Kx hands), but their check suggests they may not have improved or are pot-controlling a marginal made hand. Betting here has little merit: I unlikely get called by worse hands (except perhaps a flush draw), and I fold out hands I am already beating (air). If the opponent has a pair (pocket pair, 2x, Kx), they likely call. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a river card, and potentially win at showdown with Ace-high or improve. I will take the free card.

I improved to a lone pair on the river (7♣ in my hand + 7♦ on board = a pair of 7s). Villain called a small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn, which looks like a fairly wide, often‐weak calling range (A/K‑x, broadways, and medium/small pocket pairs). Very strong hands that absolutely beat me (5x trips/full, 22 full house, quads) are possible but unlikely given the passive turn. With pot = 700 I want a thin value bet to extract from missed overcards and small pairs (A‑high, Q/J/Tx, 66/44/33) while keeping risk manageable. I’ll bet small — 200 (~28% pot) — to target those calls. Plan: fold to a sizable raise (check‑raise represents a near‑locked strong hand here); if called I take it to showdown (I expect to be ahead of a decent chunk of his river calling range).

I am holding Ace-Eight offsuit on a board of [2c 5s 5c Kd 7d]. The action has gone: pre-flop min-raise call, flop small bet call, turn check-check, and now a very small river bet from the Small Blind (200 into 700). The pot odds are extremely favorable, requiring me to be good only about 18% of the time. In a blind-vs-blind scenario, the opponent's range is wide and contains many missed straight and flush draws (clubs) or lower high-card hands that might be taking a stab or blocking. While the opponent could have a small pair (2x, 7x) or a weak King, my Ace-high has enough showdown value against their bluffs to justify calling this small amount. Raising achieves nothing as worse hands fold and better hands call.

Three-handed and on the button I want to take the initiative and try to steal the blinds rather than limp into a multiway pot with J♣3♠ (marginal hand). Both opponents defend reasonably wide from the blinds, but a standard open from the button gets a lot of folds and gives me position postflop. I’ll open to a normal size (3x the big blind / 300 total) to generate fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with this weak hand. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet from either player, or continue vs single caller — c-bet and/or use position to pressure. If heads‑up postflop, play straightforwardly for value/bluff depending on texture.

I hold 7c 2h, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button (Minni). Being out of position with a trash hand against an aggressive chip leader is a clear fold situation. I will conserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 5s. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. Pro has folded from the Small Blind. To call, I need to put in 200 chips into a pot that will be 650. This gives me odds of about 2.25 to 1. While 45o is a very weak hand, in a three-handed game, defending the Big Blind against a button raise can sometimes be justified with a wider range. However, 45o is near the bottom of that range and plays poorly out of position. Given the stage of the tournament (hand 139 of 150) and the relatively large stack sizes, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than bloat a pot out of position with a marginal holding.

I am on the Button with Q3o, which is a very weak hand (bottom 20% of hands). Although I am in position, my opponents in the blinds (Flash and Minni) have significantly larger stacks and have shown aggression and willingness to defend. Opening here risks getting 3-bet or called by dominating hands, and Q3o plays poorly post-flop. To maximize my stack over the final 10 hands, I should conserve chips for higher equity spots. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d7h. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). My current stack is healthy (20,336), and Minni has a larger stack. The cost to complete the bet is only 50 chips into a 150-chip pot. While 97 offsuit is a marginal hand, given the pot odds (3:1) and the fact that we are down to the final few hands of the tournament, completing the blind to see a flop is reasonable. However, Minni has been quite active and aggressive from the Big Blind. Raising to 300 to try and take the pot now is a standard aggressive play, but completing to 100 allows me to see a flop cheaply. Given the tournament context and stack sizes, I'll complete the blind.

We’re heads-up here (Pro folded, only Flash in the SB). Kc4c is marginal but playable heads-up — suited king with backdoor flush potential and good blocker value. With deep stacks and a single opponent I want initiative and fold equity, so I’ll open a standard size rather than check and play passively. Raising to 300 (3x the BB, 300 total committed) puts pressure on a wide SB calling range and keeps pot control reasonable; I’ll c-bet favorable flops and fold to large, polarized 3-bets with this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d7h. After I completed the blind, Minni (the chip leader) raised to 300. Although the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 400 (giving me 2:1 odds), 97 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent. Given that we are at hand 140 of 150 and I have a comfortable second-place stack, it is better to fold now and preserve my chips for better spots rather than calling and likely having to fold to further aggression on the flop.

In 3-handed play, the button should open a very wide range of hands to apply pressure and pick up the blinds. 5c 4c is a solid speculative hand with straight and flush potential that plays well post-flop. I will raise to 300 to stay consistent with my aggressive button play and try to take down the pot early.

J♣3♣ is a marginal hand but it's suited and very cheap to see a flop here. Pot is 450 and I need to call 250 (≈36% pot‑odds) — with my huge stack and deep effective stacks there are strong implied odds if I hit a club or a Jack. 3‑betting out of the small blind OOP with J3s is thin — I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a speculative hand. I'll call the 250 to see the flop, and fold to a sizable 3‑bet (or re‑evaluate only versus a very small cold 3‑bet). Postflop I'll proceed only with clear equity (flush/straight draws, pair of J, or good board to bluff); otherwise check‑fold and preserve my chip lead at this 3‑handed stage.

I am in the Big Blind with T9o. The Button raised to 300 and the Small Blind called. The pot is 700 and I need to call 200, giving me pot odds of 4.5 to 1 (roughly 22% equity needed). T9o connects well enough with boards to justify a call at this price, despite being sandwiched between two players. Folding would be too tight given the excellent odds.

Board is Ks 2c Kc; my hand Jc3c is not a made hand but I have a four‑card club flush draw (9 clubs left = ~35% to complete by the river, ~19% to hit the turn). It checked to me in a 3‑way pot — with the board paired there is a significant chance someone has a King (trips) or a very strong holding, and leading out from out of position with a draw is inviting a raise or bloating a pot I don’t want to be committed to yet. I prefer to control the pot and realize my equity for free: check and see a turn card. Plan: if a club hits I’ll bet for value; if an opponent fires a small turn bet I’ll call to continue with 9 outs, but I’ll fold to a large raise or heavy aggression (likely trips/full house). Checking preserves stack, keeps options open, and avoids getting exploited on this dangerous paired board.

I hold Ten-Nine offsuit on a paired board of Ks-2c-Kc. I have Ten-high with no draw. The Small Blind has checked. With the Button yet to act behind me, and a board that connects well with the Button's opening range (containing many Kings), I have no reason to lead. My hand has very little equity and no showdown value currently. My plan is to check and fold to any significant aggression.

On a paired board of Ks 2c Kc, I have a flush draw with my 5c 4c. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a good spot to continuation bet. The board is relatively dry, and while a King is possible in my opponents' ranges, they both checked to me. A bet here represents strength (like a King or a strong Ace) and allows me to take the pot down immediately if they missed. If called, I still have the equity of the flush draw to improve on the turn.

I call 350. Board Ks 2c Kc with Jc3c gives me a 4‑card club flush draw (9 outs, ~35% to complete by the river) plus a few extra pair outs. Flash’s 350 into a 1250 pot gives me ~21.9% break‑even equity, so a call here is +EV and the cheapest way to realize my equity out of position. Raising OOP on a paired board that can easily hide trips/full houses is unnecessary and would bloat a pot I don’t want to be committed to with a draw. Plan: call to see the turn — if a club hits I’ll start extracting value; if the turn is a blank and someone fires a large bet/raise I’ll fold (I don’t want to continue a big pot as a drawing hand), but I’ll call small turn bets to continue with my 9 outs. Deep stack and implied odds make a flop call correct here.

On the flop of Ks-2c-Kc, I have Ten-high (9h Ts) with no draws. The Button bet 350 into 900, and the Small Blind called. Although the pot odds are attractive (requiring ~18% equity), my hand has virtually no equity against two opponents on this board texture. The Small Blind's call indicates they likely have at least a pair or a King, leaving me drawing nearly dead. I have no showdown value and no realistic way to improve to the best hand. Folding is the only correct play to preserve chips.

Board is Ks 2c Kc 8s; I hold Jc3c — I have a four‑card club flush draw (9 outs) but no pair. After calling the small flop c‑bet, the turn is not a club so my chance to complete on the river is ~9/46 ≈ 19.6%. Pot is 1600 and I’m out of position against Flash. Leading out here would bloat the pot with only a drawing hand and little fold equity versus Kx or club+K hands; if he raises I’ll be priced poorly to continue. Checking lets me realize equity cheaply: I can call a small turn bet and fold to heavy aggression, or see the river for free if he checks back and then re‑evaluate (bet for value if I hit, or fold vs strong action). This preserves stack and follows my earlier plan to only invest more with clear equity. Action: check.

On the turn 8s, Minni checks to me again. I have a flush draw with my 5c 4c. Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a hand she's not willing to fold yet, such as a small pair or a deuce. By checking back, I take a free card to try and complete my flush on the river, avoiding a potential check-raise that would force me to fold my equity. This keeps the pot manageable while I have significant improvement potential.

I missed my flush and have no pair (J♣3♣). The river (K♠ 2♣ K♣ 8♠ 2♥) creates a double‑paired board (K K 2 2), so any single K or 2 in Flash’s range now becomes a full house. Flash opened pre and c‑bet the flop then checked the turn — ambiguous, but his line includes many Kx/2x hands that now crush my hand. I don’t have showdown value and a bluff here is thin: against this opponent’s range a bet will often be called by hands that beat me and I’ll lose more; checking preserves chips. I’ll check and fold to a river bet (no reason to manufacture action with a missed draw on a dangerous board).

The board has double-paired (Ks Kc 2c 2h) with an 8s. My hand is 5c 4c, meaning my best five-card hand is the board's two pair with an 8 kicker (K-K-2-2-8). Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, which suggests she has a piece of the board, an Ace-high hand, or a pocket pair. Since any card in her hand higher than an 8 (9, T, J, Q, A) would beat me, and many of her 'missed' hands like QJ or Ace-high contain these cards, I have very little showdown value. However, Minni has checked the river, indicating she likely doesn't have a King or a Deuce. While a bluff could potentially force out some of her better high-card hands, Minni has shown a tendency to be sticky in previous hands. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 141/150), I will check behind to see the showdown, hoping for a chop against other low-card hands or a win if she was floating with something even lower than a 5.

I’m on the button with 9♦2♦ — a very weak, but suited, hand. With only two opponents and deep stacks this is a spot to be aggressive from the button and try to steal the blinds rather than limp and invite multiway action. A small standard open (2x the BB) keeps risk low while giving good fold equity; if I face a 3‑bet I can comfortably fold and preserve chips, and if I’m called I have positional advantage to play postflop and can fold to heavy action on dry boards. Pot = 150, to‑call = 100, so I’ll raise to the minimum standard open to apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Ad 3d, facing a min-raise (200) from the Button (Minni). My stack is 87 BBs, so I have plenty of room to maneuver. A3s is a strong speculative hand that plays well post-flop, particularly with nut flush potential. Minni is the chip leader and opens very wide from the button. 3-betting is an option to isolate or take it down, but playing a bloated pot out of position against a deep stack who calls frequently is risky. The price to call is excellent (150 more to win 350+), so flat calling to see a flop and keep the pot manageable is the prudent play.

My hand, 2h 6c, is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against two players. Even though I am getting 5:1 pot odds to call the additional 100 chips, the lack of playability and high probability of being dominated or losing more chips on later streets makes a fold the most disciplined play here to protect my stack as the tournament nears its end.

On this paired flop of 5d-5s-4h, I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Deuce) and a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position against the aggressive chip leader (Minni), standard strategy is to check to the raiser. The board texture is dry and paired, often leading to a check-back from the opponent if they missed or have a marginal hand. I have enough equity to continue if faced with a standard c-bet, but leading out (donk betting) is unnecessary and turns my hand into a bluff prematurely. I will check and evaluate Minni's action.

I raised preflop from the button with 9♦2♦ as a steal attempt and the SB (Pro) just checked the flop. Board is 5♦ 5♠ 4♥ — very dry and paired. I have almost no showdown value (only a two-card backdoor diamond possibility), but I have position and initiative as the preflop raiser. This is a good spot to c-bet to pick up the pot or narrow Pro’s range (many overcards and unpaired hands will fold). I’ll keep the bet modest to preserve stack and allow a cheap continuation if called: 50% pot (250 into 500). Plan: bet 250 to apply pressure; if Pro folds we win outright, if called I will proceed cautiously and fold to a significant raise since I have no made hand and poor equity.

I am facing a continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 500 on a 5d-5s-4h board. The pot odds are 3:1 (25% equity required). With Ad3d, I have a gutshot straight draw (needs a 2), a backdoor flush draw, and an overcard (Ace). Against a wide Button opening range and c-bet, my Ace-high often has showdown value against pure bluffs, and my draws provide significant equity against made hands. My estimated equity is well above the 25% required to call. Folding would be too tight given the price and my hand's potential. Raising is risky out of position against the deep-stacked chip leader. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to realize my equity on the turn.

On the turn, the board is 5d 5s 4h 7c. I hold Ad 3d, giving me Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel (needing a 2). The 7c is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture, although it connects with some straight draws (68) or pairs (A7, 78). As the pre-flop caller out of position, and having checked-called the flop, standard play is to check again to the aggressor. Leading out (donking) here would not serve much purpose as it folds out bluffs that I beat and gets called by better hands. My Ace-high has some showdown value if the opponent checks back, and checking gives me the option to see a free river or evaluate a bet if made. Therefore, I check.

I c-bet the dry flop (5♦5♠4♥) and Pro called. I hold 9♦2♦ with only a backdoor diamond draw — essentially zero equity vs a calling range that likely contains at least a pair (4x, overcards that float, or a 5). The turn 7♣ is a blank for my hand and doesn’t give me any meaningful fold equity vs hands that called the flop. With pot = 1000 and deep stacks, a second-barrel bluff here risks too much for little chance of success. I’ll control the pot and avoid getting raised off my feet: check the turn, plan to fold to significant aggression, and only continue to the river vs a very small probe bet where pot odds justify a call.

On the river, the board is 5d-5s-4h-7c-Jd. I hold Ad-3d, giving me a pair of Fives with an Ace-Jack-Seven kicker (best 5 cards: 5-5-A-J-7). Minni's check-back on the turn suggests a capped range, likely consisting of marginal made hands (weak pairs, weak Ax) or missed draws. The river Jack connects with some of her broadway range. Betting here accomplishes little, as worse hands (King-high air) will fold, and better hands (pairs, better kickers) will call. My hand has showdown value against missed draws and some lower equity hands. Checking gives me the best chance to realize this equity at showdown or evaluate if faced with a bet. I check to the aggressor.

Checked to me on the river with 9♦2♦ on 5♦ 5♠ 4♥ 7♣ J♦. I have no pair and essentially zero showdown value — only backdoor diamonds that didn’t complete. Pro called my flop c‑bet then checked turn and river, which looks like a cautious/call‑down or marginal pair range (4x, small pocket pairs, or floats). A river bluff here has limited fold equity against that range and is unlikely to be +EV for only a 1k pot; risking a bet that will often be called (or raised) is unnecessary with my deep stack. I’ll take the safe line: check to showdown. If I face a river bet I’ll fold to meaningful pressure since I have no real equity.

I am on the Button with Kd 4s. The blinds are 50/100. Although it is a 3-handed game where Button opening ranges are wide, K4 offsuit is near the bottom of the range and plays poorly post-flop against calling ranges which will often dominate me (better Kings, pairs). With 93 BBs, I am deep enough to wait for better spots rather than trying to steal with marginal holdings, especially with aggressive opponents in the blinds who cover me. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

With 7s 6c in the Small Blind, I am facing the Big Blind (Minni) who has a significant chip lead. 76o is a weak hand that does not play well out of position. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 143 of 150) and I am currently in second place, I want to avoid unnecessary confrontations with the chip leader where I am likely to be outdrawn or bullied post-flop. Folding here preserves my stack for better opportunities in the final few hands.

In a 3-handed game, King-Eight suited (Kc 8c) on the button is a very strong starting hand. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play the hand with position.

I have Jc7c in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 250 to call). Flash has been opening wide and with deep stacks (I have ~311 BBs) J7s has playable equity (backdoor flush + straight possibilities) and good implied odds — so I prefer to defend rather than fold or make a weak min-raise. A min‑raise to 500 is too small to effectively isolate and a larger 3-bet would be an overcommit with a marginal hand. I'll call (300 total in the pot including my 50 SB) and see a flop; postflop I'll proceed cautiously OOP, fold to heavy 3-bets unless I hit, and loosen up heads-up against Flash where position advantage allows more postflop play.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 7d facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind call. The pot is currently 700, and I need to call 200 more to see the flop. This offers me pot odds of 200/900, or approximately 22.2%. My hand (Jack-Seven suited) has sufficient equity and playability to call profitably at this price, despite being out of position. Closing the action allows me to realize equity relatively well. A 3-bet squeeze is too risky given the hand strength and two opponents, so calling is the most standard and +EV play.

I’m first to act OOP with Jc7c on a T♥ Q♠ T♠ flop in a 3-way pot (pot 900). I have no pair and essentially no meaningful draw (only a very remote backdoor). Against a wide button raiser and a BB who defended, there are lots of Tx/Qx and calling/spade-draw hands in their ranges — my bluff equity is very poor multiway and any c-bet is unlikely to fold enough hands to be profitable. With deep stacks I should conserve chips and see a turn card; plan is to check and fold to meaningful aggression, or if checked through re-evaluate on the turn and only continue if I pick up real equity or the board becomes highly favorable.

I hold Jd 7d on a flop of Th Qs Ts. This board textures heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Flash) or anyone with broadway cards. I have missed the flop completely, having no pair and no immediate straight or flush draws (requiring runner-runner for a straight). Leading out into two opponents on this paired broadway board would be ill-advised as I have zero showdown value and very little fold equity against ranges that likely contain a Ten, Queen, or pocket pair. The correct play is to check and fold to any significant aggression.

On a board of Th-Qs-Ts, I have King-high with no direct draws. This is a very coordinated and paired board that likely connects with the defending ranges of both the Small Blind (Minni) and Big Blind (Pro). Since both opponents checked, and I am in a multi-way pot, a continuation bet is risky as it's unlikely to fold out a Queen or any Ten. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card, control the pot size, and potentially realize some showdown value or improve to a winning hand without risking chips against a potential check-raise.

Check. I remain out of position in a 3‑way pot with Jc7c on T♥ Q♠ T♠ 8♥ — I have no pair, no club draw, and only 4 outs (the 9s) to a straight (~4/46 ≈ 8.7% to hit the river). Multiway with Flash opening wide and deep stacks means my bluff equity is very poor and I should conserve chips. The turn 8h didn’t change my equity meaningfully, so I’ll check and plan to fold to any realistic bet size. (To justify calling on pure equity versus the current pot of 900, an opponent’s river bet would need to be tiny — roughly ≤86 chips — which is extremely unlikely, so I’ll only continue if I actually hit the 9 or if I face an unusually small river price.)

On the turn, the board is Th Qs Ts 8h. The pot is 900. I hold Jd 7d. The action checked around on the flop. The 8h on the turn brings a second flush draw (hearts) and connects with some straight draws (J9 now has a straight). I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9 to make 8-9-T-J-Q), but even if I hit, I lose to K-J and chop with J-9. I have no showdown value. Bluffing into two opponents on a paired board where checking back the flop with marginal showdown value or traps is common for the button is risky. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free if the button checks back, or fold comfortably if faced with a bet, preserving my stack of 90BB.

On the turn, the 8h has improved my hand to a pair of eights. While this is currently bottom pair on a board of Th-Qs-Ts-8h, it holds significant showdown value given that both Minni and Pro have checked twice (on the flop and now on the turn). A bet here might fold out hands I already beat but is unlikely to get a Queen or a Ten to fold, and I risk being check-raised. By checking behind, I control the pot size and move towards a cheap showdown while allowing my opponents to potentially bluff the river with weaker hands or simply check it down.

I check. On T♥ Q♠ T♠ 8♥ 5♣ with Jc7c I have no pair and no completed draw (I missed the only 9 outs). This was a 3‑way pot that checked through the flop and turn, so my multiway bluff equity is very poor and a river bet risks chips unnecessarily against two opponents who can call with any pair or decent showdown hand. Pot is only 900 and there’s no price to see showdown — conserve my deep stack for better spots. I’ll check and fold to any river raise, but with no bet I take it to showdown.

The board runout is Th Qs Ts 8h 5c. I hold Jd 7d, which is Jack-high with no showdown value. The action has been checked around on both the flop and turn. While the opponents likely have weak ranges given the passive action (likely Ace-high, small pairs, or weak draws), betting into two opponents on the river with zero equity is risky. Ace-high is a significant part of the Button's checked-back range and may call a river stab. With a comfortable stack of 90BB and only a few hands left in the tournament session, preserving chips is more important than attempting a low-frequency bluff for a small pot (9BB). I will check and concede the pot if I am beaten.

On a board of Th-Qs-Ts-8h-5c, I have a pair of eights, which makes two pair (Tens and Eights) with a King kicker. This is a solid showdown value hand. After raising pre-flop and having both Minni and Pro check through the flop and turn, their ranges are unlikely to contain a Ten or a strong Queen. However, in a three-way pot, betting here is unlikely to get called by many worse hands, and I risk being check-raised by a slow-played Queen or a marginal pocket pair like 99 or JJ. Checking back is the safest way to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown without further risk.

Button with Kc7h is a marginal but playable hand in position against only the blinds. I want to apply pressure and take the initiative, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total) to steal the blinds or isolate one opponent. With deep stacks I keep the raise modest to avoid bloating the pot with a speculative hand; plan is to c-bet favorable flops and use position to fold to strong resistance. If I face a big 3-bet I’ll usually fold K7o; I may call small defenses to play postflop, but the default is to raise now to 300 to maximize fold equity and postflop edge.

I hold Ten of Clubs and Jack of Hearts (JTo) in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Minni). Minni is the chip leader and has been opening frequently, so their range is wide. However, JTo is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive big stack who can apply significant pressure on later streets. With 145/150 hands played and being the shortest stack (though still ~90BB deep), I should avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots out of position. The 3x sizing makes calling less attractive than a min-raise would. I will fold and look for a better opportunity, likely when I have position next hand.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the strongest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With only a few hands left in the tournament and being in second place, this is a crucial opportunity to significantly increase my stack and potentially challenge Minni for the lead. Minni has been an active and aggressive chip leader, making it likely they will continue to engage in the pot. A 3-bet is the standard and most profitable play here to build the pot immediately and get value. I will raise to 1000, which is a standard sizing (approximately 3.3x the open) to put pressure on Minni's opening range while still leaving room for post-flop play or a 4-bet shove if Minni decides to get aggressive.

Flash has 3-bet me to 1,000 after my open to 300. The pot is 1,350 and calling 700 more would make the pot 2,050, so I need ~700/2050 ≈ 34.1% equity to break even. K♣7♥ will be dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (many Kx, broadways and pairs) and usually has well under that threshold unless Flash is extremely wide. I’m also the big stack (30k+) with only a few hands left in the tournament — no need to gamble marginal chips. The 300 already in is a sunk cost, so I fold and wait for a better spot. If I had a stronger Kx or saw a clear light 3‑bet profile/sizing I’d consider calling or a small 4‑bet, but not with K7o here.

Current hand is 9d 7s on the Button. This is a weak off-suit one-gapper. Although I am in position, the opponents in the blinds (Flash and Minni) have significantly larger stacks (20k and 30k vs my 9k) and have shown aggression (recent 3-bets from Flash). Opening with this hand risks getting 3-bet or playing a weak hand post-flop against deep stacks who can apply maximum pressure. With only 4 hands remaining after this, conserving the stack for a higher equity spot is better than trying to steal blinds with a bottom-range hand. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket threes (3h, 3d) in a 3-handed game. Pocket pairs are relatively strong in this format. Since the button folded, it is just me and Minni in the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or see a flop with the initiative.

Qh3s is a weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand that will be dominated a large portion of the SB’s open-raising range. Flash opened to 300 from the small blind; calling 200 into a 400 pot (~33% required equity) OOP vs a reasonably wide but still strong SB range is not profitable long-term. With deep stacks, calling here puts me in many difficult postflop spots where I’ll face c-bets and pressure; a min-raise to 500 is an unnecessary spew with this holding. I’ll preserve my healthy stack and fold preflop to pick better spots later in the late tournament.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of treys (3c, 3d). In three-handed play, any pocket pair is a strong hand to open with. My stack is currently second, and Pro is the short stack with about 88 BBs left. I will put in my standard opening raise of 300. This allows me to take control of the action and potentially win the blinds, or see a flop with a hand that has set-mining value and some showdown potential against non-pairs.

3-handed, facing a standard button open to 300. I hold 4♣8♣ — a weak, low-suited connector with poor playability out of position. To call I must put in 250 into a 450 pot (requiring ~35.7% equity), and the Big Blind (Pro) still gets to act — often meaning I’ll be OOP against one or both opponents on later streets. This hand is a poor candidate to 3-bet (too weak to bluff and likely dominated/mistreated if called or 4-bet). With deep stacks but a marginal holding and tougher post-flop logistics, the +EV line here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 92o, facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). The Small Blind has folded. The pot is 450, and I need to call 200. This gives me pot odds of roughly 30.7%. Although my raw equity against a wide button range might be close to this number, 92o is one of the worst playing hands in poker, especially out of position. It has poor connectivity and low card strength, meaning my equity realization will be significantly lower than 100%. With 88 big blinds, I am not desperate and should not be defending such weak holdings. Folding is the clear best option to preserve my stack for better spots in these final hands.

7♥4♦ is one of the weakest starting hands — easily dominated and poorly connected. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to play, so a call requires roughly 40% equity to be profitable (100 / (150+100)). Against two opponents (SB and BB) who defend and 3‑bet fairly wide in this game, 7‑4o’s equity is well below that. I could attempt a tiny steal, but both opponents have deep stacks and shown willingness to defend/3‑bet; raising with this junk is high variance and low EV. Preserve my large stack and wait for a much better spot. Folding is the prudent +EV line.

Current hand is J5 offsuit in the Small Blind. The Button folded. It is a Blind vs Blind situation. With only 3 hands left in the tournament and being the short stack (8800 vs 20k+), I need high equity spots to try to double up, but J5o is too weak to play, especially out of position against an aggressive Big Blind. Completing offers little value and raising is too risky with this holding. Preserving chips for the final two hands is preferable to bleeding chips with trash.

Current hand is 149 of 150. I am on the Button with 9d 6d (suited gapper) and a stack of 8800 (88 BB). The game is 3-handed with two large stacks in the blinds (Flash ~20k, Minni ~30k). While 96s is often an open on the button in 3-handed play, the opponents have shown recent aggression (Flash 3-betting in Hand 136, 145). Opening here risks getting 3-bet and forced to fold, wasting chips, or playing a bloated pot with a marginal hand against deep stacks who can apply maximum pressure. The potential gain of 1.5 BBs from stealing does not outweigh the risk of losing chips right before the final hand. I will preserve my stack for the final hand where I will be in the Small Blind.

With only two hands left in the tournament, I am currently in second place. I have [Jc, 6c] in the Small Blind after the Button folded. While this is a marginal hand, it is suited and worth seeing a flop for a small additional investment. Calling (limping) allows me to keep the pot small against the chip leader, Minni, while still giving me the opportunity to win the pot if I hit the flop or if the action remains checked down. Raising out of position against a big stack this late in the tournament carries unnecessary risk given my secure second-place standing.

Facing a limp from the small blind with Qs5s in the big blind. Q5s is marginal but suited and playable heads-up; I want to isolate the limper and avoid a free multi-way flop while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll make a standard isolation raise to 300 total (3x BB) — big enough to gain fold equity and define Flash’s range, but small enough given my hand strength and deep stacks. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet; if called, proceed cautiously out of position — continue with any made pair or strong spade/straight equity, check and control the pot with air or weak pairs, and extract value when I improve.

We are at Hand 149 of 150. My primary goal is to maximize my stack and secure my current second-place position, as I am significantly ahead of Pro and unlikely to catch Minni without a massive confrontation. I completed the small blind with [Jc, 6c], a marginal suited hand, but Minni (the chip leader) responded with a raise to 300. Calling here would put me out of position against an aggressive big stack with a weak hand that is often dominated. Given the tournament is almost over, it is more prudent to fold and preserve my stack rather than risk a larger loss in a post-flop battle where I am at a tactical disadvantage.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am on the button with 9d 5c and a stack of 20,636. I have 50 chips committed as the dealer, and it costs 100 more to call. Given that this is the last hand, I want to preserve my stack and avoid unnecessary risk with a weak starting hand. While it's tempting to play the final hand, 95o is a poor hand to enter a pot with, especially when I can just fold and guarantee my current ranking without any further chip loss. Therefore, I will fold.

Short-handed (only the big blind left) and holding K♥T♦ — a strong, playable hand in this spot. I want to seize initiative and apply pressure from the small blind, so I open to a standard ~3x BB. That size (300 total) gives fold equity, builds a pot where top-pair hands have value, and leaves plenty of stack for postflop play. Plan: c-bet dry flops, play cautiously on coordinated/overcard boards, and fold/re-evaluate if faced with a large 3-bet.

This is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). I am in the Big Blind with K5o. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 300. I have 100 posted, so it costs 200 to call. The pot odds are 200 to win a total pot of 600 (33% equity required). K5o has decent equity (approx 45-50%) against a wide Small Blind stealing range. Since this is the last hand, I should take any positive EV line without worrying about variance or survival. Calling is strictly positive EV given the odds and position advantage post-flop. A 3-bet is unnecessary as it folds out worse hands and isolates me against better ones.

I have top pair (K♥) with a decent Ten kicker on a relatively dry flop (9♣ 2♥ K♣). Opponent (Pro) just cold‑called preflop, so his calling range is wide — Kx, medium/ small pairs (9s, 2s), broadways, suited connectors and some club combos. Betting is the right play: it extracts value from worse kings, 9x/2x, and charges any club draws while also gaining information. I’ll size up to deny correct odds to draws and get value — roughly 2/3 pot. If I get raised big I’ll re‑evaluate (big check‑raise usually denotes a set or better K), but if called I’ll proceed based on the turn card (continue for value on safe cards, be cautious on clubs/overcards).

It is the final hand (150/150). I hold Top Pair (Kings) on a K-9-2 board. My kicker (5) is weak, but against a Small Blind opening range, Top Pair is very strong. The opponent bets 400 into a pot of 600. I have excellent pot odds to call. Raising would likely only isolate me against better Kings or sets, while folding out bluffs and weaker pairs that I am currently beating. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to play the turn in position.

Turn (Jh) pairs a lot of hands that called my flop c‑bet. I have top pair K with a medium Ten kicker on 9c‑2h‑Kc‑Jh — still decent but the J helps many parts of Pro’s preflop/call range (Jx, KJ two‑pair, QJ, AJ, single‑jack combos) and also increases check‑raise/set risk. Pot is 1,400 and Pro has ~8,100 behind; I don’t want to inflate the pot with a vulnerable top‑pair hand into a possible two‑pair/set. I’ll control the pot and gain info by checking the turn. Plan: if he checks back we go to a safe river decision (can value bet thin or showdown). If he bets a small/standard amount (<= ~pot) I’ll call for value/protection; if he ramps up into a large shove I’ll fold to avoid losing a big chunk to two‑pair/set. Checking keeps options and minimizes variance while preserving my chip lead.

Current hand is 150/150 (Final Hand). I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker (5) on a board of [9c 2h Kc Jh]. The opponent (Minni, SB) bet the flop but checked the turn. The pot is 1400. The turn card (Jack) brings potential straight draws (QT) and a second flush draw (Hearts). With a weak kicker, my hand is a marginal value hand or a bluff catcher. Betting allows me to charge draws, but I risk isolating myself against better Kings (KT, KQ, KJ) or Two Pair, and getting check-raised would put me in a difficult spot. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and induce bluffs on the river from missed draws or weaker hands. Since this is the last hand, I want to minimize the risk of being blown off the best hand while navigating to showdown cheaply.

Board: 9♣ 2♥ K♣ J♥ 2♦. I hold K♥T♦ — top pair with a medium kicker. Pro checked the turn, so his range that reached the river by check‑calling the flop is wide: Kx, medium pocket pairs, 9x, some Jx, occasional 2x (which would have been a set on the flop and can now have trips/full house). The river 2 pairs the board and crushes any hand with a deuce, but many of those hands would have shown stronger action earlier; still it's a risk.

Objective: extract thin value from worse Kx, any calling 9x and single‑pair Jx while keeping the pot controlled against stronger holdings. I don’t want to check and give away value, nor bet so large I face a shove and have to commit with a vulnerable kicker. A half‑pot bet (~700) is the right compromise — it gets called by worse pairs and some calls from second‑pair/overcards, but is small enough that a sizable raise is almost certainly strong and can be folded.

Plan: bet 700. If Pro calls we go to showdown and expect to win many of those matchups. If he raises big/ships, fold to preserve our stack (he’s very likely to have trips/two‑pair).

It is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker (5) on a board of [9c 2h Kc Jh 2d]. My opponent, Minni (SB), raised pre-flop, bet the flop, checked the turn, and now bets 700 into a pot of 1400 on the river. The turn check suggests their range is somewhat capped, likely not holding a monster like a set or strong two-pair, although a trap is possible. The river card (2d) pairs the board, which doesn't significantly change the relative hand strengths unless Minni holds a 2 (trips), which is a small part of their range. There are several missed draws on the board, including club flush draws, heart flush draws (from the turn), and straight draws (QT). Minni could be bluffing with these missed draws. My hand beats all bluffs but loses to almost all value bets (better Kings). Given the pot odds of 3:1 (requiring 25% equity) and the BvB dynamic where ranges are wide and bluffs are frequent, folding Top Pair is too conservative. Raising serves no purpose as worse hands fold and better hands call. Therefore, calling is the highest EV play.
